stages.explanation: In SMA type 1, intercostal muscle weakness with relative diaphragmatic sparing produces the characteristic bell-shaped chest and paradoxical breathing, and bulbar weakness causes a poor cough and recurrent aspiration. Respiratory failure—often precipitated by an otherwise mild viral illness—is the leading cause of death, so anticipatory respiratory care (cough assist, non-invasive ventilation, secretion management, RSV prophylaxis, immunizations) and nutritional support are central. The heart is generally spared in classic SMA (unlike Pompe or muscular dystrophies), so cardiomyopathy is not the main threat. Epilepsy is not a feature of SMA. Renal function is normal. Goals-of-care discussions with the family about ventilation and feeding should accompany disease-modifying therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal muscular atrophy type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann disease), homozygous SMN1 deletion with 2 SMN2 copies
pearls: The 'alert but floppy' infant with tongue fasciculations and areflexia is SMA until proven otherwise—anterior horn cell disease spares cognition and sensation.
Diagnosis is confirmed by homozygous SMN1 exon 7 deletion; SMN2 copy number predicts severity (fewer copies = more severe).
SMA is now treatable—onasemnogene abeparvovec, nusinersen, or risdiplam—and earlier initiation dramatically improves outcomes, so do not delay genetic testing.
Respiratory failure from intercostal weakness and aspiration (bell-shaped chest, paradoxical breathing) is the chief cause of death in type 1; proactive respiratory support is essential.

cases.title: A 12-year-old with high arches and frequent ankle sprains
cases.chiefComplaint: Clumsy gait, frequent tripping, and difficulty with fine motor tasks.
cases.history: A 12-year-old boy is referred for several years of slowly progressive 'clumsiness.' He trips often, sprains his ankles repeatedly, and cannot keep up with peers when running. Recently he has had trouble buttoning shirts and turning keys. There is no pain, sensory loss he can describe, bowel or bladder change, or back trauma. He has no headaches, seizures, or cognitive concerns and does well academically. Birth and early development were normal; he walked at 13 months but was always considered 'a bit awkward.' No medications, no allergies. Family history is striking: his father has 'flat feet that are really high-arched,' wears ankle braces, and a paternal aunt uses orthotics for 'foot drop.' On review of systems, he reports cold, sometimes bluish feet. Examination by his pediatrician noted thin lower legs and an abnormal gait, prompting this referral.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative adolescent with normal mentation and cranial nerves. Bilateral pes cavus and hammer toes; distal lower-leg atrophy producing 'inverted champagne bottle' calves. Steppage gait with bilateral foot drop; cannot heel-walk. Distal weakness greatest in foot dorsiflexors/evertors and intrinsic hand muscles; proximal strength normal. Ankle reflexes absent, knee reflexes diminished, upper-limb reflexes reduced. Stocking distribution of mildly decreased vibration and proprioception at the toes; pinprick relatively preserved. Mild distal hand wasting. No spasticity, no Babinski sign, no scoliosis at rest. Romberg mildly positive. Palpable enlarged peroneal nerve appreciated in a thin patient.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The length-dependent distal weakness, absent ankle reflexes, pes cavus, and palpably enlarged nerves in this boy with an affected father best localize the problem to which structure?
options: Peripheral nerve (length-dependent polyneuropathy)
Neuromuscular junction
Spinal cord dorsal columns
Cerebellum
stages.explanation: Chronic, slowly progressive, length-dependent distal weakness and sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes, distal atrophy, pes cavus/hammer toes, and palpably enlarged nerves are classic for a hereditary peripheral polyneuropathy. The 'inverted champagne bottle' legs and high arches develop because chronic distal denervation in a growing child deforms the feet. A neuromuscular junction disorder (e.g., myasthenia) causes fatigable weakness with normal reflexes and sensation, not atrophy or sensory loss. Isolated dorsal column disease would give sensory ataxia without the marked distal motor wasting, foot deformity, or enlarged nerves. Cerebellar disease causes intention tremor, dysmetria, and ataxia with normal reflexes and no atrophy. The dominant family history further supports an inherited neuropathy.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
CK: 130 U/L (normal)
B12, folate, TSH, fasting glucose, HbA1c: normal
Nerve conduction studies: markedly slowed motor conduction velocities (~20 m/s) UNIFORMLY in all nerves tested; reduced amplitudes; prolonged distal latencies
Sensory nerve action potentials: absent in lower limbs, reduced in upper limbs
Genetic testing: duplication of the PMP22 gene on chromosome 17p11.2

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Uniformly slowed motor conduction velocities (~20 m/s) plus a PMP22 duplication establish which diagnosis?
options: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP)
Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A (CMT1A)
Friedreich ataxia
Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies
stages.explanation: A PMP22 duplication on 17p11.2 with diffuse, uniform demyelinating slowing (velocities in the ~20 m/s range) is the genetic and electrophysiologic signature of CMT1A, the most common inherited neuropathy and the most common CMT subtype. CIDP is acquired and immune-mediated, typically has patchy/multifocal slowing and conduction block, responds to immunotherapy, and progresses over weeks to months—not years from childhood with a dominant family history. Friedreich ataxia is a GAA-repeat (FXN) disorder causing a sensory axonal neuropathy plus cerebellar/corticospinal signs and cardiomyopathy, not a uniform demyelinating neuropathy with PMP22 duplication. Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies results from PMP22 deletion (not duplication) and causes recurrent focal palsies, not a symmetric length-dependent polyneuropathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for CMT1A in this adolescent?
options: Lifelong corticosteroids
Plasma exchange
Supportive care: physical/occupational therapy, ankle-foot orthoses, and avoidance of neurotoxic drugs
Intravenous immunoglobulin
stages.explanation: CMT1A has no disease-modifying drug therapy; management is supportive and multidisciplinary. Physical and occupational therapy maintain strength and function, ankle-foot orthoses address foot drop and prevent falls, and orthopedic care manages pes cavus and contractures. Crucially, patients must avoid neurotoxic medications—especially vincristine, which can precipitate severe, sometimes fatal worsening—as well as agents like high-dose pyridoxine and certain others. Corticosteroids, plasma exchange, and IVIG are treatments for the acquired immune neuropathies (CIDP, Guillain-Barré) and have no role in this inherited demyelinating neuropathy. Genetic counseling for autosomal dominant inheritance (50% transmission risk) should also be provided to the family.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents ask which commonly used class of drug must be strictly avoided in their son. Which is the most important to flag?
options: Acetaminophen
Vincristine (and other vinca alkaloids)
Amoxicillin
Loratadine
stages.explanation: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutic that is profoundly neurotoxic and can cause rapid, severe, occasionally irreversible or fatal neuropathy in patients with CMT (especially CMT1A); it is essentially contraindicated, and any oncologic plan must account for the diagnosis. This is the single most important medication warning to communicate, and it should be documented prominently in the chart. Acetaminophen, amoxicillin, and loratadine have no specific neurotoxicity in CMT and are safe at usual doses. Other agents to use cautiously include high-dose pyridoxine, nitrofurantoin, and certain other chemotherapeutics, but vincristine carries the clearest, highest-stakes warning.

cases.diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A (CMT1A) due to PMP22 duplication
pearls: Pes cavus, hammer toes, 'inverted champagne bottle' legs, distal weakness, and absent ankle reflexes in a child with a dominant family history = CMT.
Uniformly slowed motor conduction velocities (~20 m/s) define the demyelinating CMT1 subtypes; PMP22 duplication confirms CMT1A, the most common form.
PMP22 duplication causes CMT1A; PMP22 deletion causes hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies—same gene, opposite dose, different disease.
There is no disease-modifying drug—care is supportive (PT/OT, orthoses)—and vincristine must be strictly avoided to prevent catastrophic neurotoxicity.

cases.title: A teenager whose eyelids droop by evening
cases.chiefComplaint: Drooping eyelids and double vision that worsen as the day goes on.
cases.history: A 15-year-old girl presents with six weeks of intermittent droopy eyelids and double vision. Symptoms are minimal on waking but worsen by evening and after activities like reading or watching screens. Over the past two weeks she reports a tired, nasal-sounding voice after long conversations, occasional choking on water, and difficulty chewing tough foods—she 'has to rest her jaw' mid-meal. She has noticed her arms feel weak when drying her hair. There is no pain, numbness, fever, rash, or bowel/bladder change. Menarche at 12; periods regular. No medications other than occasional ibuprofen; no allergies. Birth and development were normal and she is an honor student. Family history includes a maternal aunt with autoimmune thyroid disease. No recent travel or unusual foods; immunizations up to date. She denies tick exposure and recent infections.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented adolescent. Bilateral asymmetric ptosis that worsens with sustained upgaze; fatigable complex ophthalmoparesis with binocular diplopia; pupils normal and reactive. Sustained upgaze for 60 seconds increases ptosis; ice-pack application transiently improves it. Facial weakness with a 'transverse (myasthenic) smile,' weak eye closure, and nasal speech after counting aloud. Proximal limb weakness that worsens with repetitive testing (e.g., repeated arm abduction); strength partially recovers after brief rest. Reflexes normal and symmetric; sensation fully intact; coordination normal; no atrophy or fasciculations. Single-breath count is mildly reduced. No skin rash, no thyromegaly appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, and proximal weakness that worsen with use and improve with rest—plus normal reflexes and sensation—localize to which structure?
options: Anterior horn cell
Postsynaptic neuromuscular junction
Peripheral sensory nerve
Cerebellum
stages.explanation: Fatigable, fluctuating weakness that worsens with sustained or repetitive activity and improves with rest—prominently affecting ocular, bulbar, and proximal limb muscles—with entirely normal sensation and reflexes is the signature of a neuromuscular junction disorder. In autoimmune myasthenia gravis the defect is postsynaptic (antibodies against the acetylcholine receptor or MuSK). Anterior horn cell disease (e.g., SMA) causes fixed weakness, atrophy, fasciculations, and areflexia, not fatigability with preserved reflexes. Peripheral sensory nerve disease produces numbness and sensory loss, which she lacks. Cerebellar disease causes ataxia and dysmetria with normal strength. The diurnal pattern (better in the morning, worse by evening) and bedside fatigability are classic clues to the neuromuscular junction.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Ice-pack test: ptosis improves after 2 minutes of cooling
Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody: positive (elevated)
Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz: >10% decremental response in the compound muscle action potential
TSH/free T4: normal; ANA negative
CT chest: no thymoma; mild thymic tissue noted
PFTs: forced vital capacity mildly reduced but adequate

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Positive AChR antibodies, a decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, and a positive ice-pack test confirm which diagnosis?
options: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome
Juvenile autoimmune myasthenia gravis
Botulism
Mitochondrial chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia
stages.explanation: Positive AChR antibodies plus a >10% decrement on 3-Hz repetitive nerve stimulation and a positive ice-pack test confirm seropositive (juvenile) autoimmune myasthenia gravis. Lambert-Eaton produces an INcremental response after exercise (presynaptic, anti-VGCC, with autonomic features and depressed reflexes that facilitate after exercise) and is paraneoplastic/rare in children. Botulism causes acute descending paralysis with autonomic features (dilated, poorly reactive pupils), an incremental response on high-frequency stimulation, and a relevant exposure/time course—not fluctuating fatigable weakness over weeks with AChR antibodies. Mitochondrial chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia causes slowly progressive, symmetric, NON-fatigable ophthalmoplegia and ptosis without diplopia, negative AChR antibodies, and no decrement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate first-line symptomatic treatment for her generalized myasthenia gravis?
options: Pyridostigmine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor)
Pancuronium
Botulinum toxin
Levodopa
stages.explanation: Pyridostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is the first-line symptomatic therapy: by reducing acetylcholine breakdown it increases neuromuscular transmission and rapidly improves ptosis, diplopia, and limb weakness. Immunomodulation (corticosteroids, steroid-sparing agents) is added for inadequate control. Pancuronium is a non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker that would worsen weakness and is dangerous. Botulinum toxin blocks acetylcholine release and would precipitate severe weakness—contraindicated. Levodopa treats parkinsonian disorders and has no role at the neuromuscular junction. Patients and families must also be counseled about drugs that exacerbate myasthenia (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers) and warning signs of myasthenic crisis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks later she develops an upper respiratory infection, increasing dyspnea, weak cough, and a falling forced vital capacity. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Increase pyridostigmine dose only and observe at home
Admit for monitoring and treat the crisis with IVIG or plasma exchange, with airway support as needed
Start oral antihistamines and discharge
Administer intravenous magnesium for muscle relaxation
stages.explanation: A respiratory infection precipitating worsening weakness, weak cough, dyspnea, and declining forced vital capacity signals impending myasthenic crisis—a neuromuscular respiratory emergency. The child should be admitted to a monitored setting with serial vital capacity/negative inspiratory force measurements and treated with a rapid immunotherapy—IVIG or plasma exchange—with intubation/ventilatory support if respiratory parameters deteriorate. Simply increasing pyridostigmine at home is unsafe and excess cholinergic load can itself impair the airway with secretions. Antihistamines and discharge ignore a life-threatening trajectory. Intravenous magnesium is contraindicated in myasthenia because it impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate or worsen crisis.

cases.diagnosis: Juvenile autoimmune (AChR-antibody positive) myasthenia gravis
pearls: Fatigable, fluctuating weakness of ocular, bulbar, and proximal muscles that worsens through the day and improves with rest—with normal reflexes and sensation—is myasthenia gravis.
Bedside ice-pack and sustained-upgaze tests support the diagnosis; AChR antibodies and a decremental response on 3-Hz repetitive nerve stimulation confirm it.
First-line symptomatic therapy is pyridostigmine; add immunotherapy for inadequate control and screen for thymoma with chest imaging.
Falling vital capacity with infection signals myasthenic crisis—admit and treat with IVIG or plasma exchange; avoid magnesium, aminoglycosides, and fluoroquinolones.

cases.title: A 3-year-old seizing for twenty minutes in the ED
cases.chiefComplaint: Generalized convulsion that has not stopped.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought by EMS for a generalized tonic-clonic seizure that began at home roughly 22 minutes ago and is ongoing on arrival. His mother reports two days of fever, runny nose, and decreased oral intake. He has a history of epilepsy diagnosed at age 2, on levetiracetam 30 mg/kg/day, though doses were missed yesterday because he 'wasn't keeping anything down.' No prior episodes this long; typical seizures last under 2 minutes. He has no shunt, no recent head trauma, no toxic ingestion at home, and is up to date on immunizations. No known allergies. Birth was at term without complications; mild speech delay otherwise. Family history is notable for febrile seizures in a sibling. Paramedics gave one dose of intramuscular midazolam 8 minutes ago en route without cessation. A point-of-care glucose drawn by EMS was 84 mg/dL.

vitals.RR: 28/min (irregular, with apneic pauses during clonic phase)
vitals.SpO2: 89% on room air, improving to 96% with supplemental oxygen

cases.exam: Unresponsive with ongoing rhythmic clonic jerking of all four limbs, eyes deviated, and oral secretions. Pupils 4 mm and reactive. No nuchal rigidity appreciated during the event. No external signs of trauma; fontanelle closed. Skin warm and flushed, no petechiae or purpura. Lungs with transmitted upper-airway sounds; air entry adequate with positioning and oxygen. Capillary refill under 2 seconds, pulses strong. No focal asymmetry of movement discernible during the generalized activity. Post-EMS midazolam given; IV access being established.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has been seizing continuously for over 20 minutes despite one dose of IM midazolam. What is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE next step (after airway, breathing, oxygen, glucose check, and IV access)?
options: Obtain an emergent MRI brain before any further medication
Administer a benzodiazepine (e.g., IV lorazepam) as the first-line abortive—he is in status epilepticus
Start a maintenance levetiracetam infusion over 60 minutes and wait
Perform an immediate lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Continuous seizure activity for 5 minutes or more (or recurrent seizures without recovery) defines status epilepticus, and this child has been convulsing over 20 minutes. After securing the airway, giving oxygen, checking glucose (normal here), and obtaining IV access, the first-line abortive is a benzodiazepine—IV lorazepam (or additional midazolam if no IV). Benzodiazepines are the most effective initial agents; under-dosing is a common error. MRI is not appropriate while actively seizing and is not the priority. A slow maintenance infusion does not abort an ongoing seizure—he needs a loading dose strategy, not maintenance dosing while waiting. Lumbar puncture is deferred until the seizure stops and the patient is stabilized; it is never the immediate step during active convulsive status.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He receives IV lorazepam (now two total benzodiazepine doses) but continues to seize. Per current status epilepticus pathways, what is the appropriate second-line therapy?
options: A third benzodiazepine dose
A non-benzodiazepine antiseizure medication load (e.g., IV levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate)
Immediate rapid-sequence intubation with no further antiseizure medication
Oral clobazam
stages.explanation: After two adequately dosed benzodiazepines fail, guidelines move to a second-line non-benzodiazepine antiseizure medication given as an IV load: levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate are all acceptable first choices (the ESETT trial showed comparable efficacy). Repeated benzodiazepine doses beyond two add respiratory depression with diminishing returns and are discouraged. Intubation may become necessary for airway protection, especially as third-line agents are used, but you do not abandon pharmacologic seizure termination—the goal is to stop the seizure. Oral clobazam is inappropriate in an actively convulsing child who cannot protect his airway and has a slow onset; the IV route is required in status epilepticus.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose: 84 mg/dL
Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Ca 9.4 mg/dL, Mg 2.0 mg/dL
Levetiracetam level: subtherapeutic (consistent with missed doses)
VBG: pH 7.21, lactate 6.5 mmol/L (resolving after seizure termination)
Non-contrast head CT (after seizure stopped): no acute hemorrhage, mass, or hydrocephalus
Seizure terminates after the second-line load

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Integrating the history (known epilepsy, missed levetiracetam, intercurrent febrile illness, subtherapeutic level) and course, what is the best diagnostic formulation?
options: New-onset bacterial meningitis as the primary cause
Convulsive status epilepticus precipitated by subtherapeutic antiseizure levels and febrile illness in a child with known epilepsy
Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure
Hypoglycemic seizure
stages.explanation: This is convulsive status epilepticus in a child with established epilepsy, precipitated by missed levetiracetam doses (subtherapeutic level) compounded by a febrile viral illness—a classic and common trigger combination. The transient lactic acidosis is expected from prolonged convulsion and resolves. Bacterial meningitis is a key consideration in any febrile seizing child, but there is no nuchal rigidity or rash and a clear precipitant exists; LP can be performed after stabilization if clinically warranted, yet it is not the primary diagnosis here. Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures do not produce sustained tonic-clonic activity with hypoxia, post-ictal lactic acidosis, and a documented EEG-confirmable epilepsy history. Glucose was normal (84 mg/dL), excluding hypoglycemia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the seizure terminates, what is the most appropriate disposition and ongoing plan?
options: Discharge home now that the seizure has stopped
Admit for monitoring, resume/optimize antiseizure medication, treat the underlying illness, and counsel on adherence and a rescue plan
Start lifelong phenobarbital monotherapy and discharge
Transfer to psychiatry for non-epileptic spells
stages.explanation: After prolonged status epilepticus, the child needs admission for neurologic and respiratory monitoring during the post-ictal period and recovery from second-line sedating medications, plus correction of the precipitants: resume/optimize his levetiracetam (consider IV bridging given vomiting), treat the febrile illness, and ensure hydration. Discharging immediately is unsafe after refractory status. Switching to lifelong phenobarbital monotherapy is not indicated; the issue was nonadherence, not drug failure. Psychiatry transfer is wrong because this was true convulsive status epilepticus. Before discharge, the family should receive clear adherence counseling and an at-home seizure rescue plan (e.g., intranasal or rectal benzodiazepine) with instructions on when to call EMS.

cases.diagnosis: Convulsive status epilepticus precipitated by subtherapeutic antiseizure medication levels and febrile illness in a child with known epilepsy
pearls: Status epilepticus = 5+ minutes of continuous seizure (or recurrent seizures without recovery)—treat immediately; do not wait.
First line is a benzodiazepine (give an adequate dose); after two doses fail, load a second-line agent—levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate (all comparable per ESETT).
Always check glucose early; transient lactic acidosis after a prolonged convulsion is expected and resolves with seizure termination.
Missed antiseizure doses plus an intercurrent febrile illness is a classic status trigger—discharge planning must include adherence counseling and a home rescue benzodiazepine plan.

cases.title: An adolescent with worsening balance and a wide-based gait
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive unsteadiness, frequent falls, and slurred speech.
cases.history: A 13-year-old girl presents with about two years of progressive imbalance. She began stumbling and bumping into doorframes, then developed a staggering, wide-based gait and now falls several times weekly. Over the past year her speech has become slurred and her handwriting messy. She tires easily and reports occasional palpitations. There is no headache, vision loss, weakness she can localize, or bowel/bladder change, and cognition is intact. Birth and early development were normal; symptoms began in late childhood and have steadily progressed. No medications; no allergies. Family history: parents are healthy and unaffected, but a maternal uncle reportedly 'used a wheelchair young and died of a heart problem,' and the parents are distant cousins. On review of systems she has had foot deformities noted by a podiatrist and was told her blood sugar was 'borderline' last year. Immunizations up to date.
cases.exam: Alert, articulate but dysarthric adolescent with normal cognition. Gait is wide-based and ataxic; tandem gait is impossible. Marked appendicular ataxia with dysmetria on finger-nose and heel-shin testing. Romberg strongly positive (worse with eyes closed). Deep tendon reflexes are ABSENT at the ankles and knees, yet plantar responses are EXTENSOR (Babinski) bilaterally. Vibration and proprioception are markedly reduced distally; pinprick relatively preserved. Mild distal weakness. Pes cavus and early kyphoscoliosis. Saccadic (jerky) pursuit with square-wave jerks; no nystagmus at rest. No optic atrophy on initial fundoscopy. Cardiac exam: occasional irregular rhythm.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The striking combination of progressive ataxia, ABSENT lower-limb reflexes, EXTENSOR plantar responses, and loss of vibration/proprioception indicates simultaneous involvement of which two systems?
options: Only the cerebellum
Peripheral nerves/dorsal columns AND corticospinal tracts
Only the neuromuscular junction
Only the basal ganglia
stages.explanation: Absent deep tendon reflexes indicate a lower motor neuron/peripheral process (sensory neuropathy and dorsal-column/dorsal-root involvement), while extensor plantar responses (Babinski) indicate an upper motor neuron/corticospinal tract lesion. The coexistence of areflexia WITH upgoing toes—plus sensory ataxia from dorsal column loss and cerebellar/spinocerebellar signs—is a hallmark pattern of a spinocerebellar degeneration affecting multiple long tracts simultaneously. Pure cerebellar disease preserves reflexes and does not cause Babinski signs or dorsal-column sensory loss. A neuromuscular junction disorder causes fatigable weakness with normal reflexes and sensation. Basal ganglia disease causes movement disorders, not this tract-specific constellation. Recognizing 'areflexia plus extensor plantars' is the key examination pearl that narrows the differential dramatically.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: sensory axonal neuropathy (absent/reduced sensory action potentials) with relatively preserved motor velocities
MRI spine: thinning/atrophy of the cervical spinal cord
MRI brain: relatively preserved cerebellum early
ECG: T-wave abnormalities; echocardiogram: concentric (hypertrophic) cardiomyopathy
Fasting glucose: impaired glucose tolerance
Vitamin E level: normal
Genetic testing: homozygous GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in intron 1 of the FXN (frataxin) gene

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A homozygous GAA expansion in FXN with sensory axonal neuropathy, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and impaired glucose tolerance establishes which diagnosis?
options: Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency
Friedreich ataxia
Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A
Spinocerebellar ataxia type 1
stages.explanation: A homozygous GAA repeat expansion in intron 1 of FXN (frataxin) is diagnostic of Friedreich ataxia, the most common inherited ataxia. The phenotype fits perfectly: autosomal recessive progressive ataxia beginning in late childhood/adolescence, areflexia with extensor plantars, dorsal-column sensory loss with a sensory axonal neuropathy, pes cavus and kyphoscoliosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and diabetes/impaired glucose tolerance. Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency can mimic Friedreich ataxia clinically but has a LOW vitamin E level (normal here) and is treatable with supplementation. CMT1A is a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy (PMP22 duplication) without upgoing toes, cardiomyopathy, or diabetes. Spinocerebellar ataxia type 1 is autosomal dominant (CAG/ataxin-1), with a dominant family history and predominantly cerebellar signs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which systemic complication is the leading cause of death in Friedreich ataxia and mandates ongoing cardiology surveillance?
options: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with arrhythmia/heart failure
Status epilepticus
Renal failure
Aspiration pneumonia from bulbar palsy
stages.explanation: Cardiac disease—hypertrophic cardiomyopathy progressing to arrhythmias (including atrial fibrillation) and heart failure—is the leading cause of death in Friedreich ataxia, so serial ECGs and echocardiograms are essential, and her occasional irregular beats warrant cardiology referral. Patients also need monitoring for diabetes mellitus (frataxin deficiency impairs mitochondrial iron handling and beta-cell function). Epilepsy is not a feature of Friedreich ataxia. Renal failure is not characteristic. Although swallowing and speech are affected late, aspiration is not the predominant cause of death; the cardiomyopathy is. The disease-targeted antioxidant omaviloxolone is now approved to slow progression, but multidisciplinary supportive care—cardiology, endocrinology, PT/OT, scoliosis and foot surgery, and genetic counseling—remains the backbone of management.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before finalizing the diagnosis, which readily treatable mimic should always be excluded because it can present identically?
options: Wilson disease
Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (check serum vitamin E)
Multiple sclerosis
Huntington disease
stages.explanation: Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED) clinically mimics Friedreich ataxia almost exactly—progressive ataxia, areflexia, and posterior-column signs—but is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein gene and is TREATABLE with high-dose vitamin E supplementation, which can halt or partially reverse progression. Therefore a serum vitamin E level (normal in this patient) should always be checked in a child with a Friedreich-like syndrome before concluding the workup. Wilson disease causes a movement disorder with hepatic involvement and Kayser-Fleischer rings, a different picture. Multiple sclerosis is demyelinating with relapsing CNS lesions on MRI, not areflexia with a recessive GAA expansion. Huntington disease causes chorea and is autosomal dominant (CAG in HTT)—not this recessive ataxic phenotype.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents (distant cousins, both unaffected) ask about inheritance and recurrence risk for future children. What is correct?
options: Autosomal dominant; 50% of children affected
Autosomal recessive; both parents are carriers and each future child has a 25% risk
X-linked recessive; only sons affected
Mitochondrial; all children affected
stages.explanation: Friedreich ataxia is autosomal recessive. Both unaffected parents are obligate carriers (heterozygous for a GAA expansion), so each future pregnancy carries a 25% chance of an affected child, a 50% chance of a carrier, and a 25% chance of an unaffected non-carrier. Consanguinity (the parents are distant cousins) increases the likelihood of recessive disease, which fits the negative parental but positive extended-family history (the affected maternal uncle). It is not autosomal dominant (parents would typically be affected, 50% transmission). It is not X-linked—girls are affected and inheritance is recessive autosomal. It is not mitochondrial—frataxin is a nuclear-encoded gene despite its mitochondrial function, so transmission follows Mendelian recessive rules, not maternal mitochondrial inheritance.

cases.diagnosis: Friedreich ataxia (homozygous GAA repeat expansion in FXN)
pearls: Areflexia PLUS extensor plantar responses in a child with progressive ataxia is the classic clue to Friedreich ataxia (combined peripheral, dorsal-column, and corticospinal involvement).
It is autosomal recessive due to a GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the frataxin (FXN) gene; pes cavus, kyphoscoliosis, diabetes, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy round out the picture.
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is the leading cause of death—obtain ECG/echocardiogram and follow with cardiology; screen for diabetes.
Always check serum vitamin E to exclude ataxia with vitamin E deficiency, a clinically identical but treatable mimic.

cases.title: A teen who falls asleep mid-conversation
cases.chiefComplaint: Overwhelming daytime sleepiness and sudden episodes of leg buckling.
cases.history: A 16-year-old boy is referred for six months of irresistible daytime sleepiness. Despite sleeping 8-9 hours nightly, he falls asleep in class, while texting, and even mid-sentence; brief naps leave him refreshed for an hour or two. His mother reports that when he laughs hard or is surprised, his head nods, his jaw goes slack, and his knees buckle—once he slumped to the floor briefly but stayed fully aware and recovered in seconds. He describes vivid, dream-like images and a terrifying sense of being unable to move just as he falls asleep. Grades have dropped and he was nearly in a car accident from drowsiness. No snoring or witnessed apneas. No medications, no drug use, no allergies. Birth and development normal. Family history: an uncle with 'a sleep disorder.' Review of systems negative for headache, weakness between episodes, fever, or recent weight loss; he denies depression though he is frustrated by the symptoms.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented between episodes but visibly drowsy, yawning frequently. Full strength (5/5) throughout with normal tone when tested at rest. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Cranial nerves intact; extraocular movements full without ptosis. No dysmetria, normal gait and tandem gait, Romberg negative. Sensation intact. No fasciculations or atrophy. Mood appropriate; no thought disorder. During the visit, a hearty laugh triggers transient head droop and jaw slackening with preserved awareness, resolving in seconds. Cardiac and respiratory exams normal; no tonsillar hypertrophy or retrognathia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The brief episodes of bilateral muscle weakness (jaw drop, head nod, knee buckling) triggered by laughter with FULLY preserved consciousness are best described as which phenomenon?
options: Atonic (drop) seizures
Cataplexy
Syncope
Transient ischemic attacks
stages.explanation: Sudden, brief, bilateral loss of muscle tone triggered by strong emotion (classically laughter) with completely preserved consciousness is cataplexy—a transient intrusion of REM-sleep atonia into wakefulness. It is the most specific feature of narcolepsy type 1. Atonic seizures cause loss of tone but are NOT emotion-triggered and typically involve altered awareness with an abnormal EEG. Syncope involves loss of consciousness from cerebral hypoperfusion, usually with a prodrome and a postictal-free recovery, not retained awareness during the event. Transient ischemic attacks cause focal deficits (e.g., hemiparesis, aphasia) from vascular territory ischemia, are not emotion-triggered, and are exceedingly rare in healthy teens. The retained awareness during emotion-provoked atonia is the defining clue.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDIES
stages.content:
Overnight polysomnography: normal total sleep time, no significant obstructive apnea; shortened REM latency
Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT): mean sleep latency 4 minutes (markedly short); 3 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs)
CSF hypocretin-1 (orexin): low (<110 pg/mL)
Urine toxicology: negative
TSH, ferritin: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Severe sleepiness, cataplexy, a short mean sleep latency with multiple SOREMPs, and low CSF hypocretin-1 confirm which diagnosis?
options: Obstructive sleep apnea
Narcolepsy type 1 (with cataplexy)
Idiopathic hypersomnia
Insufficient sleep syndrome
stages.explanation: Narcolepsy type 1 is defined by excessive daytime sleepiness plus either cataplexy with characteristic MSLT findings (mean sleep latency <=8 minutes with >=2 SOREMPs) or low CSF hypocretin-1 (orexin) levels—all present here. It results from loss of hypothalamic hypocretin/orexin neurons (autoimmune mechanism, HLA-DQB1*06:02 association). Obstructive sleep apnea would show apneas/hypopneas and oxygen desaturation on polysomnography and is excluded here. Idiopathic hypersomnia causes sleepiness with unrefreshing long sleep but NO cataplexy, NO SOREMPs, and normal hypocretin. Insufficient sleep syndrome (chronic sleep deprivation) resolves with adequate sleep and lacks cataplexy and low hypocretin—this boy sleeps 8-9 hours yet remains profoundly sleepy with cataplexy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides scheduled naps and good sleep hygiene, which medication class is first-line for the excessive daytime sleepiness of narcolepsy?
options: A wake-promoting agent (e.g., modafinil/armodafinil) or a stimulant
A benzodiazepine at bedtime
An antipsychotic
A long-acting opioid
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for the excessive daytime sleepiness of narcolepsy is a wake-promoting agent such as modafinil/armodafinil or a psychostimulant (e.g., methylphenidate, amphetamines). For cataplexy, options include sodium oxybate (also improves sleepiness and disrupted nocturnal sleep), pitolisant, or certain antidepressants (SNRIs/SSRIs/venlafaxine) that suppress REM. Behavioral measures—scheduled short naps, regular sleep schedules, and safety counseling about driving—are essential adjuncts. Benzodiazepines worsen daytime sleepiness and do not treat narcolepsy. Antipsychotics are not indicated and may impair alertness. Opioids are inappropriate and dangerous here. Safety counseling about drowsy driving is critical given his near-miss.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most important safety counseling point for this adolescent and his family?
options: Avoid all physical activity permanently
Counsel on drowsy-driving risk and the need for symptom control before and while driving
Restrict fluids in the evening
Begin a strict ketogenic diet
stages.explanation: The dominant safety concern is drowsy driving—he already had a near-miss. Counseling must emphasize that he should not drive until his sleepiness is well controlled, and that cataplexy and sleep attacks pose serious risk behind the wheel; treatment adherence and scheduled naps are part of safe driving. There is no need to forbid all physical activity—he can participate with awareness of cataplexy triggers. Evening fluid restriction addresses nocturia/enuresis, not narcolepsy. A ketogenic diet treats certain refractory epilepsies and GLUT1 deficiency, not narcolepsy. Family education about the chronic but manageable nature of the disorder, school accommodations, and recognizing cataplexy as benign (not seizures) also supports good outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Narcolepsy type 1 (narcolepsy with cataplexy, hypocretin/orexin deficiency)
pearls: Emotion-triggered (especially laughter) brief bilateral atonia with PRESERVED consciousness is cataplexy—the most specific sign of narcolepsy type 1.
The narcolepsy pentad: excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations, and disrupted nocturnal sleep.
Diagnosis: MSLT with mean sleep latency <=8 min and >=2 sleep-onset REM periods, and/or low CSF hypocretin-1; it is associated with HLA-DQB1*06:02.
Treat sleepiness with modafinil/stimulants and cataplexy with sodium oxybate, pitolisant, or REM-suppressing antidepressants—and always counsel on drowsy-driving safety.

cases.title: A toddler with fever, a stiff neck, and a bulging soft spot
cases.chiefComplaint: High fever, irritability, poor feeding, and a tense fontanelle.
cases.history: An 11-month-old boy is brought to the ED for one day of high fever and worsening fussiness. He has fed poorly, vomited twice, and become increasingly lethargic, arching his back and crying inconsolably when handled. He had mild upper respiratory symptoms for two days before. No seizures yet, no rash noticed initially, no recent travel or sick contacts beyond a daycare cold. Birth was at term without complications. Concerningly, his immunizations are DELAYED—he has not received his Haemophilus influenzae type b or pneumococcal conjugate vaccine series due to missed visits. No medications, no known allergies. Family history is non-contributory. Parents deny trauma. On the way in he became more difficult to rouse, prompting immediate triage. There is no known immunodeficiency, but the under-immunized status is emphasized by the parents.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic infant who is difficult to console and resists movement. Anterior fontanelle is full and tense. Nuchal rigidity is present; he resists neck flexion and is irritable when picked up (paradoxical irritability). Pupils equal and reactive; no focal motor deficit appreciated, moves all extremities. No papilledema on limited fundoscopy. Skin warm; a few faint non-blanching petechiae noted on the trunk on closer inspection. Capillary refill 3 seconds; extremities cool. Brudzinski sign appears positive. No focal seizure activity. Lungs clear; heart tachycardic without murmur. No hepatosplenomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After initial stabilization (airway, IV access, fluids for poor perfusion), what is the single most important IMMEDIATE intervention in this febrile, lethargic infant with meningeal signs?
options: Obtain an MRI brain before any treatment
Administer empiric IV antibiotics (do NOT delay for LP) and obtain blood cultures
Give oral acetaminophen and observe for 6 hours
Start antiviral acyclovir alone and wait for cultures
stages.explanation: Suspected bacterial meningitis is a time-critical emergency: prompt empiric IV antibiotics save lives, and antibiotics must NOT be delayed if lumbar puncture cannot be performed immediately or if imaging is needed first. After blood cultures (and ideally LP if it will not delay therapy), start broad empiric coverage—typically a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) plus vancomycin—with consideration of adjunctive dexamethasone. MRI is not the priority and would dangerously delay treatment. Acetaminophen and observation are grossly inadequate for possible bacterial meningitis with shock features. Acyclovir alone treats HSV encephalitis but does not cover bacterial pathogens; it may be added if HSV is suspected, but it cannot substitute for antibacterial therapy here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CSF: WBC 3,200/uL (90% neutrophils)
CSF glucose: 22 mg/dL (serum glucose 96 mg/dL)
CSF protein: 180 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: gram-positive diplococci
Blood culture: pending; CBC WBC 22,000 with left shift
Head CT (obtained due to lethargy, before LP): no mass or obstructive hydrocephalus
CSF culture later: Streptococcus pneumoniae

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, very low glucose, high protein, and gram-positive diplococci. What is the diagnosis?
options: Viral (aseptic) meningitis
Bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae
Tuberculous meningitis
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
stages.explanation: The classic bacterial meningitis CSF profile—markedly elevated WBC with neutrophil predominance, very low CSF glucose (CSF:serum ratio well below 0.6), and high protein—combined with gram-positive diplococci identifies Streptococcus pneumoniae, a leading cause in this age group and especially in an under-immunized child (missed PCV). Viral meningitis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, and only mildly elevated protein with a negative Gram stain. Tuberculous meningitis is typically subacute with lymphocytic pleocytosis, very low glucose, and high protein, but no gram-positive diplococci and a different exposure/time course. Subarachnoid hemorrhage produces xanthochromia and red blood cells, not a purulent neutrophilic CSF with bacteria on Gram stain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding adjunctive corticosteroids in pediatric bacterial meningitis, which statement reflects current practice?
options: Steroids are contraindicated in all children with meningitis
Dexamethasone is most clearly beneficial in reducing hearing loss in Haemophilus influenzae type b meningitis and is generally given with/just before the first antibiotic dose when used
Steroids should be started only after 7 days of antibiotics
Steroids replace antibiotics in pneumococcal meningitis
stages.explanation: Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces sensorineural hearing loss most clearly in Haemophilus influenzae type b meningitis; when used, it should be given with or just before the first dose of antibiotics, because its benefit depends on blunting the inflammatory burst that accompanies bacterial lysis. Its benefit in pneumococcal meningitis in children is less established but is often considered case-by-case. Steroids are not universally contraindicated. Delaying steroids until day 7 negates the point—timing relative to the first antibiotic dose is what matters. Steroids never replace antibiotics; appropriate antibacterial therapy remains the definitive treatment. Audiology follow-up after recovery is essential because hearing loss is the most common neurologic sequela.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most common long-term neurologic sequela of bacterial meningitis that mandates routine post-discharge screening?
options: Permanent hemiplegia
Sensorineural hearing loss
Parkinsonism
Peripheral neuropathy
stages.explanation: Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common long-term complication of bacterial meningitis, so every child needs a formal audiologic evaluation after recovery (and early enough to intervene, since ossification can complicate later cochlear implantation). Other potential sequelae include developmental delay, seizures, hydrocephalus, and focal deficits, but hearing loss is the most frequent and the reason routine audiometry is standard. Permanent hemiplegia can occur but is far less common. Parkinsonism is not a recognized sequela. Peripheral neuropathy is not a typical complication of bacterial meningitis. Public-health counseling should also stress catch-up immunization (Hib, pneumococcal) given the role of under-immunization here, plus chemoprophylaxis considerations for certain pathogens (not pneumococcus).

cases.diagnosis: Bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae in an under-immunized infant
pearls: In a febrile, lethargic infant with a bulging fontanelle, nuchal rigidity, and paradoxical irritability, give empiric IV antibiotics immediately—do NOT delay for LP or imaging.
Bacterial CSF: high WBC with neutrophil predominance, very low glucose, high protein; pneumococcus appears as gram-positive diplococci.
Empiric therapy is a third-generation cephalosporin plus vancomycin; adjunctive dexamethasone (given with/before the first antibiotic dose) most clearly reduces hearing loss in Hib meningitis.
Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common sequela—arrange audiology follow-up for every survivor, and address catch-up vaccination.

cases.title: A school-age boy who can't keep his legs still at night
cases.chiefComplaint: An uncomfortable urge to move his legs at bedtime, disrupting sleep.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is brought in for months of trouble falling asleep. Most evenings, especially when sitting still to do homework or lying in bed, he feels a creepy-crawly, 'need to move' sensation deep in his legs that he can only relieve by stretching, kicking, or walking around. It is worst at night and improves with movement. As a result he takes over an hour to fall asleep, is tired and inattentive at school, and his teacher has raised concerns about restlessness and focus. His mother notes he has always been a picky eater who avoids meat; recent labs from his pediatrician showed borderline anemia. He has no pain, no daytime leg weakness, no joint swelling, and no growth concerns described as 'growing pains' that fit the classic pattern. No medications, no allergies. Family history: his mother has similar nighttime leg discomfort, never formally diagnosed. Birth and development normal; immunizations current.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cooperative boy. Normal mental status and age-appropriate attention during a structured exam. Cranial nerves intact. Full strength throughout with normal tone; deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetric, plantar responses flexor. Sensation intact to light touch, pinprick, and vibration in the legs; no dermatomal deficit. Normal coordination, gait, and tandem gait; Romberg negative. No joint swelling, tenderness, or limited range of motion; no calf tenderness or cords. Mild conjunctival pallor noted. No skin rash or neurocutaneous stigmata. The remainder of the exam, including back and hips, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An urge to move the legs accompanied by uncomfortable sensations, worse at rest and in the evening, and RELIEVED by movement, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Restless legs syndrome (Willis-Ekbom disease)
Nocturnal seizures
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis
Peripheral neuropathy
stages.explanation: The four core diagnostic features of restless legs syndrome are present: (1) an urge to move the legs, usually with uncomfortable sensations; (2) onset or worsening during rest/inactivity; (3) relief with movement; and (4) a circadian pattern worse in the evening/night. This classic constellation, plus a positive family history, makes restless legs syndrome the diagnosis. Nocturnal seizures involve stereotyped movements with altered awareness and EEG correlates, not a relievable urge to move. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis causes joint swelling, morning stiffness, and pain on examination—absent here. Peripheral neuropathy causes sensory loss or fixed paresthesias with possible reflex/strength changes, not a movement-relieved, circadian urge with a normal neurologic exam.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 11.0 g/dL (mildly low)
MCV: 76 fL (microcytic)
Serum ferritin: 18 ng/mL (low)
Transferrin saturation: low
TSH: normal
Basic metabolic panel: normal
Urinalysis: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the clinical picture plus low ferritin and microcytic anemia, what is the most important contributing factor to address?
options: Vitamin B12 deficiency
Iron deficiency (low ferritin)
Hypothyroidism
Hypercalcemia
stages.explanation: Iron deficiency is the most important and treatable contributor to restless legs syndrome in children. Iron is a cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase in dopamine synthesis, and low brain iron impairs dopaminergic signaling implicated in restless legs syndrome; the low ferritin (18 ng/mL) and microcytic anemia in a picky eater who avoids meat fit this mechanism. Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic anemia and a different neurologic picture (subacute combined degeneration), not microcytosis. Hypothyroidism can cause fatigue but the TSH is normal and it does not explain the iron studies. Hypercalcemia is unrelated and not present. Correcting iron is the cornerstone of management here, and ferritin should generally be targeted above 50 ng/mL.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this child's restless legs syndrome?
options: Oral iron supplementation with a goal of raising ferritin (target >50 ng/mL), plus sleep hygiene
Start a dopamine agonist (e.g., pramipexole) immediately
Begin chronic opioids at bedtime
Prescribe a benzodiazepine indefinitely
stages.explanation: First-line management of pediatric restless legs syndrome with low iron stores is iron repletion—oral iron supplementation aiming to raise serum ferritin (commonly targeted above 50 ng/mL)—together with sleep hygiene and reducing aggravators (e.g., caffeine, certain antihistamines, and inadequate sleep). Many children improve substantially with iron alone. Dopamine agonists such as pramipexole are reserved for refractory or severe cases in children (and carry risks like augmentation); they are not first-line, especially when a correctable cause (iron deficiency) is present. Chronic opioids are inappropriate first-line therapy in a child. Indefinite benzodiazepines are not recommended due to tolerance, dependence, and lack of disease modification. Reassessing ferritin after a course of iron guides further management.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which common comorbidity is frequently associated with pediatric restless legs syndrome and worth screening for?
options: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
Type 1 diabetes mellitus
Asthma
Migraine with brainstem aura
stages.explanation: Pediatric restless legs syndrome is frequently associated with ADHD; the two share links to dopaminergic dysfunction and iron status, and untreated restless legs (with consequent sleep disruption) can mimic or worsen inattention and hyperactivity—relevant given this boy's school concerns about focus and restlessness. Screening for and addressing both conditions, including optimizing iron and sleep, can improve daytime symptoms. Type 1 diabetes, asthma, and migraine with brainstem aura are not characteristically linked to pediatric restless legs syndrome. Recognizing the restless legs-ADHD association is high-yield because treating the sleep disorder (and iron deficiency) may meaningfully improve attention before or alongside any ADHD-specific therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Restless legs syndrome (Willis-Ekbom disease) associated with iron deficiency
pearls: The four core criteria: urge to move the legs (with uncomfortable sensations), worse at rest, relieved by movement, and worse in the evening/night.
Check ferritin—iron deficiency is the key modifiable driver; iron is a cofactor for dopamine synthesis and low brain iron worsens restless legs.
First-line therapy in children is iron repletion (target ferritin >50 ng/mL) plus sleep hygiene; reserve dopamine agonists for refractory cases.
Screen for comorbid ADHD—the conditions overlap, and treating restless legs and iron deficiency can improve daytime attention.

cases.title: A jaundiced newborn arching and crying with a high-pitched wail
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe jaundice with lethargy, poor feeding, and abnormal posturing.
cases.history: A 5-day-old term boy is admitted from home for deepening jaundice and abnormal behavior. Born at 38 weeks to a mother with blood type O, the infant is blood type B (ABO setup for hemolysis). He was discharged at 36 hours without a bilirubin check and is exclusively breastfed with suboptimal latch; he has lost 11% of birth weight. Over two days he became progressively yellow to the soles, sleepy, and a poor feeder, with a high-pitched cry. Today his parents noted he arches his back and stiffens. No fever or rash. No family history of significant hemolytic disease known, though a maternal aunt 'had a baby who needed an exchange transfusion.' No medications; not yet on any. No known glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase status. Pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated; no maternal infections. Immunizations not yet due beyond hepatitis B at birth.
cases.exam: Deeply jaundiced infant, yellow extending to palms and soles. Lethargic with poor feeding and a high-pitched cry. Hypertonia with episodic opisthotonic (back-arching) posturing and retrocollis; intermittent fisting. Setting-sun sign with impaired upgaze noted. Moro reflex incomplete and asymmetric; suck weak. Anterior fontanelle soft. No focal motor asymmetry between sides during quiet periods. Pupils reactive. Hepatosplenomegaly mild. No petechiae. The infant alternates between hypotonia when very lethargic and the described hypertonic arching episodes. No dysmorphic features. Sclerae icteric; mucous membranes slightly dry consistent with dehydration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this severely jaundiced, dehydrated neonate with lethargy, a high-pitched cry, and opisthotonic posturing, what is the most urgent initial laboratory test to guide emergency management?
options: Total and direct serum bilirubin
Lumbar puncture
Serum ammonia
Thyroid function tests
stages.explanation: The immediate, decision-driving test is a total (and direct/conjugated) serum bilirubin, because the entire clinical picture suggests acute bilirubin encephalopathy from extreme unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and the bilirubin level relative to age-in-hours nomograms (and neurotoxicity thresholds) determines whether emergent intensive phototherapy and/or exchange transfusion are needed. A lumbar puncture is not the priority and would not change acute management of hyperbilirubinemia. Serum ammonia would be checked if a urea cycle defect/metabolic crisis were suspected, but the jaundice-to-soles plus the ABO setup and poor feeding point to bilirubin neurotoxicity. Thyroid function is part of metabolic screening but is far too slow and nonspecific to guide this emergency. Concurrent hemolysis workup (blood type, Coombs, CBC, smear, reticulocytes) should accompany the bilirubin.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total serum bilirubin: 32 mg/dL (predominantly unconjugated)
Direct bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
Infant blood type: B positive; mother O; direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test: positive
Hemoglobin: 11 g/dL; reticulocytes elevated; smear: spherocytes and polychromasia
Weight loss: 11% from birth; mild hypernatremic dehydration (Na 149 mEq/L)
G6PD: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A total bilirubin of 32 mg/dL with hemolysis and acute neurologic signs (lethargy, high-pitched cry, opisthotonus, setting-sun sign) in a 5-day-old indicates which diagnosis?
options: Benign breast-milk jaundice
Acute bilirubin encephalopathy (evolving toward kernicterus)
Neonatal sepsis with meningitis
Biliary atresia
stages.explanation: Extreme unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (32 mg/dL) with the classic neurologic signs—lethargy alternating with hypertonia, high-pitched cry, opisthotonic posturing/retrocollis, and the setting-sun sign—is acute bilirubin encephalopathy, the reversible early phase that, if untreated, leads to kernicterus (chronic, permanent bilirubin encephalopathy). The ABO incompatibility (O mother, B infant) with a positive Coombs test, spherocytes, and reticulocytosis explains the hemolysis, compounded by dehydration and inadequate breastfeeding. Breast-milk jaundice is benign, lower-grade, and does not cause encephalopathy. Neonatal sepsis/meningitis is in the differential for an encephalopathic neonate, but the predominantly unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with hemolysis points to bilirubin neurotoxicity. Biliary atresia causes CONJUGATED (direct) hyperbilirubinemia, which is normal here (0.6 mg/dL).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a bilirubin of 32 mg/dL with acute encephalopathy and hemolysis, what is the most appropriate emergent treatment?
options: Intensive phototherapy alone and recheck in 24 hours
Emergent (double-volume) exchange transfusion along with intensive phototherapy and hydration
Ursodeoxycholic acid
Discontinue breastfeeding permanently and observe
stages.explanation: At this bilirubin level with signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy and ongoing hemolysis, emergent double-volume exchange transfusion is indicated to rapidly remove bilirubin and antibody-coated red cells, performed alongside maximal intensive phototherapy and correction of dehydration; IVIG may be considered in isoimmune hemolysis to reduce the need for exchange. Intensive phototherapy alone is insufficient when the infant is already encephalopathic at 32 mg/dL—delay risks permanent kernicterus. Ursodeoxycholic acid is irrelevant to acute unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Stopping breastfeeding is not the emergency treatment, though feeding/hydration must be optimized (lactation support, supplementation as needed). Time is brain in this scenario—escalation to exchange transfusion should not be delayed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If treatment is delayed and chronic kernicterus develops, which classic tetrad of long-term sequelae should be anticipated?
options: Spastic diplegia, epilepsy, microcephaly, and cataracts
Choreoathetoid cerebral palsy, sensorineural (auditory) deficits, gaze palsy (impaired upgaze), and dental enamel dysplasia
Flaccid paralysis, areflexia, anosmia, and diabetes
Ataxia, tremor, optic atrophy, and renal failure
stages.explanation: Chronic bilirubin encephalopathy (kernicterus) classically produces a tetrad reflecting bilirubin's predilection for the basal ganglia (especially globus pallidus), auditory pathways, and oculomotor centers: (1) extrapyramidal movement disorder—choreoathetoid (dyskinetic) cerebral palsy; (2) auditory dysfunction—sensorineural hearing loss/auditory neuropathy; (3) oculomotor impairment—gaze palsy, classically of upward gaze; and (4) dental enamel dysplasia (hypoplasia). The other option sets describe patterns not characteristic of kernicterus: spastic diplegia with cataracts suggests other etiologies; flaccid paralysis with anosmia and diabetes is unrelated; ataxia with renal failure does not fit. MRI in kernicterus characteristically shows bilateral globus pallidus signal changes. This is why aggressive, timely treatment of severe hyperbilirubinemia is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Acute bilirubin encephalopathy evolving toward kernicterus, due to severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from ABO hemolytic disease and inadequate breastfeeding
pearls: Lethargy, high-pitched cry, opisthotonus/retrocollis, and the setting-sun sign in a deeply jaundiced neonate signal acute bilirubin encephalopathy—a neurologic emergency.
Get a total/direct bilirubin immediately and plot against age-in-hours; unconjugated predominance plus a positive Coombs and spherocytes point to ABO hemolysis.
At extreme levels with encephalopathy, escalate beyond phototherapy to emergent exchange transfusion (consider IVIG in isoimmune hemolysis)—time is brain.
Chronic kernicterus tetrad: choreoathetoid CP, sensorineural hearing loss, upgaze palsy, and dental enamel dysplasia, with globus pallidus changes on MRI.

cases.title: A boy with declining grades, jerks, and personality change
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of behavioral change, falling grades, and recurrent body jerks.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought in for several months of cognitive and behavioral decline. Once a strong student, he has become forgetful, irritable, and withdrawn, with deteriorating handwriting and grades. Over the past two months his parents notice repetitive, periodic jerks of his trunk and limbs—rhythmic 'drops' every few seconds that sometimes make him stumble, often during quiet activities. More recently his gait has become clumsy and his speech less clear. There are no fevers, headaches, or focal weakness reported. Critically, he was born abroad, immigrated at age 3, and had measles infection in infancy (around 8 months of age) before he could be vaccinated; vaccination records are incomplete. No current medications; no allergies. Birth and early development were normal until this decline. Family history is unremarkable. He has had no known toxin exposure and no recent travel. Review of systems is otherwise notable only for occasional visual complaints.
cases.exam: Alert but with blunted affect and clear cognitive slowing; oriented to person and place only. Periodic, stereotyped myoclonic jerks of the trunk and limbs recur every 5-10 seconds, sometimes causing brief loss of postural tone. Mild dysarthria. Gait is broad-based and clumsy with poor tandem. Tone is mildly increased; deep tendon reflexes are brisk with bilateral extensor plantar responses. Fundoscopy reveals a focal area of chorioretinitis/maculopathy. No meningismus. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; no focal hemiparesis. Sensation grossly intact. The periodic myoclonus is the most striking and reproducible finding, present at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A previously normal child with subacute cognitive/behavioral decline, periodic myoclonic jerks, and a remote history of early measles infection should raise suspicion for which category of disease?
options: A primary psychiatric disorder
A subacute progressive (slow-virus) encephalopathy
Simple childhood absence epilepsy
Acute bacterial meningitis
stages.explanation: The combination of progressive intellectual and behavioral deterioration, characteristic PERIODIC myoclonus, and a history of early-life measles infection strongly suggests a subacute progressive encephalopathy—specifically a slow-virus disease (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, SSPE), a delayed complication of measles. A primary psychiatric disorder would not produce periodic myoclonus, pyramidal signs (brisk reflexes, extensor plantars), chorioretinitis, and progressive neurologic decline. Childhood absence epilepsy causes brief staring spells (3 Hz spike-wave) WITHOUT cognitive deterioration, myoclonus, or pyramidal signs, and is benign. Acute bacterial meningitis is a fulminant febrile illness over hours to days with meningismus—not a months-long degenerative course in an afebrile child. The periodic myoclonus plus measles history is the pivotal clue.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
EEG: periodic high-amplitude generalized complexes recurring every 5-8 seconds, time-locked to the myoclonic jerks (Radermecker complexes / burst-suppression-like periodicity)
CSF: normal cell count and glucose; elevated IgG with oligoclonal bands
CSF and serum measles (rubeola) antibody titers: markedly elevated, with a high CSF:serum antibody ratio
MRI brain: increasing T2/FLAIR signal in periventricular and subcortical white matter; later basal ganglia involvement
Metabolic and toxic screen: unremarkable

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Periodic EEG complexes time-locked to myoclonus, very high CSF measles antibody titers, and the clinical course establish which diagnosis?
options: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE)
Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy
Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
stages.explanation: SSPE is confirmed by the classic triad: a compatible clinical course (subacute cognitive decline with periodic myoclonus), characteristic periodic EEG complexes time-locked to the jerks, and markedly elevated measles antibody titers in CSF (with an elevated CSF:serum ratio indicating intrathecal synthesis). It is a rare, fatal, persistent measles infection of the CNS appearing years after primary infection, especially when measles occurs in infancy. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease also causes dementia with myoclonus and periodic EEG complexes but is a prion disease of older adults without elevated measles titers—and is essentially not relevant in an 8-year-old with this history. Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy causes myoclonus without dementia, with a normal exam and 4-6 Hz polyspike-wave EEG. Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis causes psychiatric symptoms, seizures, and dyskinesias with NMDA-receptor antibodies, not high measles titers or periodic measles-related complexes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the realistic goal of treatment and the most important public-health implication of this diagnosis?
options: It is fully curable with a short antiviral course; no prevention needed
Treatment is largely supportive/disease-slowing (e.g., isoprinosine and/or intrathecal interferon-alpha) with a generally poor prognosis; SSPE is PREVENTABLE by measles vaccination
High-dose corticosteroids reliably cure the disease
It resolves spontaneously without intervention or prevention
stages.explanation: SSPE has no cure; management is supportive with agents that may slow progression in some patients—oral isoprinosine (inosine pranobex) and/or intrathecal/intraventricular interferon-alpha—plus symptomatic control of myoclonus and seizures (e.g., with appropriate antiseizure medications). The prognosis is generally poor, with most patients progressing to a vegetative state and death over months to a few years. The crucial public-health point is that SSPE is PREVENTABLE by measles vaccination; the disease is far more likely after measles in infancy, and maintaining high vaccination coverage prevents both measles and its devastating late complication. It is not curable by a short antiviral course, not reliably cured by corticosteroids, and does not resolve spontaneously. This case underscores the importance of timely immunization.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The parents ask about the typical clinical trajectory of SSPE. Which best describes it?
options: Rapid full recovery within weeks
Relapsing-remitting course with long stable periods and no progression
Progressive stages: behavioral/cognitive decline, then myoclonus and motor deterioration, then rigidity/autonomic instability, often ending in a vegetative state
Isolated, non-progressive learning disability
stages.explanation: SSPE classically progresses through recognizable stages: stage 1—insidious behavioral and cognitive changes (declining school performance, personality change); stage 2—prominent myoclonus with motor deterioration, ataxia, and seizures; stage 3—increasing rigidity, spasticity, extrapyramidal signs, and decerebrate posturing; stage 4—mutism, autonomic instability, and a vegetative state, typically ending in death. Although the rate varies (and rare patients plateau or have slowly progressive courses), the overall trajectory is relentless deterioration, not rapid recovery, a benign relapsing-remitting course, or a static learning disability. Counseling families about this prognosis, providing supportive and palliative care, and emphasizing vaccination to prevent future cases are central responsibilities in managing SSPE.

cases.diagnosis: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE), a late complication of early-life measles infection
pearls: Subacute cognitive/behavioral decline with PERIODIC myoclonus in a child with early measles infection (often in infancy) is SSPE until proven otherwise.
Diagnostic triad: characteristic periodic EEG complexes time-locked to myoclonus, markedly elevated CSF measles antibody titers (intrathecal synthesis), and the compatible clinical course.
There is no cure—management is supportive with isoprinosine and/or intrathecal interferon-alpha; prognosis is generally poor with stepwise deterioration to a vegetative state.
SSPE is preventable by measles vaccination; the risk is highest after measles acquired in infancy—a powerful argument for maintaining immunization coverage.

cases.title: Depressed and Not Breathing Well After a Difficult Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: Term newborn with low Apgar scores and depressed tone after a delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia.
cases.history: A male infant was born at 39 weeks and 2 days to a 31-year-old G2P2 mother by vaginal delivery. Labor was complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring the McRoberts maneuver and suprapubic pressure, with a head-to-body delivery interval of about 4 minutes. Estimated birth weight is 4180 g. Fetal heart tracing showed late decelerations in the hour before delivery. Apgar scores were 2 at 1 minute, 4 at 5 minutes, and 5 at 10 minutes. The infant required positive-pressure ventilation in the delivery room and was intubated briefly before extubation to CPAP. Cord arterial gas showed pH 6.94 and base deficit 16 mmol/L. The pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated; maternal serologies were negative and Group B Strep was negative. Mother took only a prenatal vitamin and has no chronic illness. There is no family history of neonatal seizures or metabolic disease. The infant is now 2 hours old in the NICU on CPAP, showing reduced spontaneous movement and intermittent lip-smacking.
cases.exam: Lethargic term infant with reduced spontaneous activity. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Pupils 3 mm, sluggishly reactive. Tone is diffusely decreased with poor head lag recovery. Moro reflex is incomplete and suck is weak. Intermittent lip-smacking and bicycling movements of the legs are noted. No clonus of the extremities at rest. No cephalohematoma; mild caput. Right arm held in adduction and internal rotation with reduced spontaneous movement (concern for brachial plexus injury). Heart sounds normal, no murmur. Lungs clear with mild subcostal retractions. Abdomen soft, liver edge 1 cm below costal margin. No dysmorphic features.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical syndrome best characterizes this infant's neurologic presentation?
options: Sarnat stage I (mild) encephalopathy
Sarnat stage II (moderate) encephalopathy
Sarnat stage III (severe) encephalopathy
Benign neonatal hyperexcitability
stages.explanation: Lethargy, hypotonia, weak suck, incomplete Moro, and possible subtle seizures (lip-smacking, bicycling) following a sentinel event with a severely acidotic cord gas describe moderate (Sarnat stage II) encephalopathy. Stage I (mild) shows hyperalertness, normal-to-increased tone, and exaggerated reflexes without seizures. Stage III (severe) is characterized by stupor/coma, flaccidity, absent reflexes, and often suppression on EEG; this infant is lethargic but not flaccid or comatose. Benign neonatal hyperexcitability produces stimulus-sensitive jitteriness with normal mental status and no acidosis, which does not fit a depressed, acidotic infant. Correct Sarnat staging is critical because moderate-to-severe encephalopathy is the central eligibility criterion for therapeutic hypothermia.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS & GASES
stages.content:
Cord arterial pH: 6.94
Cord base deficit: 16 mmol/L
Repeat ABG (1 hr of life): pH 7.10, pCO2 44 mmHg, base deficit 14 mmol/L
Glucose: 48 mg/dL
Lactate: 9.2 mmol/L
Na 138 / K 4.6 / iCa 1.1 mmol/L
Hgb 16.8 g/dL, Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
aEEG: discontinuous background, no clear seizures yet
Head exam: no skull fracture, mild caput

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given moderate encephalopathy at 2 hours of life, what is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Initiate therapeutic hypothermia targeting 33.5 °C core temperature
Begin empiric phenobarbital and observe without cooling
Obtain brain MRI before deciding on any therapy
Withhold cooling until 12 hours of age to confirm persistence
stages.explanation: This term infant (≥36 weeks) with severe perinatal acidosis (pH <7.0, base deficit ≥16), a sentinel event, and moderate encephalopathy meets criteria for therapeutic hypothermia, which must begin within 6 hours of birth to be neuroprotective—so initiating cooling now is correct. Phenobarbital alone treats seizures but ignores the time-sensitive neuroprotective window. MRI is valuable for prognosis but is typically obtained days later and must never delay cooling. Waiting until 12 hours would push the infant past the 6-hour therapeutic window, forfeiting the benefit. Cooling reduces death and major neurodevelopmental disability when started promptly.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: During therapeutic hypothermia, which complication should be most actively monitored?
options: Hyperglycemia from cold-induced insulin resistance
Hyperkalemia from cellular shifts
Sinus bradycardia and thrombocytopenia
Hypertension from peripheral vasoconstriction
stages.explanation: Therapeutic hypothermia predictably causes sinus bradycardia (a heart rate in the 80s–100s is expected and usually benign at 33.5 °C) and thrombocytopenia, so platelets and rhythm must be followed closely along with coagulation studies. Hyperglycemia is not the characteristic metabolic derangement; hypoglycemia is actually more concerning in asphyxiated infants and worsens injury. Hypothermia tends to drive potassium intracellularly causing hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia—rebound hyperkalemia is a rewarming concern. Cooling produces relative hemodynamic stability rather than hypertension. Recognizing expected bradycardia prevents inappropriate cessation of cooling, while tracking platelets guards against bleeding.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which study best predicts long-term neurodevelopmental outcome in this infant?
options: Cord blood pH value alone
Brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging at 4–7 days of life
Head ultrasound on day 1
Serum lactate at 1 hour of life
stages.explanation: Brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging performed around day 4–7 (after cooling and rewarming) best correlates with neurodevelopmental outcome, revealing patterns such as basal ganglia/thalamus and watershed injury that predict motor and cognitive impairment. Cord pH reflects the acute insult severity but correlates poorly with individual long-term outcome. Head ultrasound on day 1 is insensitive for the cortical and deep gray injury of HIE and is mainly useful to exclude hemorrhage. Early lactate indicates acute metabolic stress but does not forecast development. MRI timing matters: too early can underestimate, and DWI pseudonormalizes after about 7–10 days.

cases.diagnosis: Neonatal hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (Sarnat stage II), eligible for therapeutic hypothermia
pearls: Therapeutic hypothermia must begin within 6 hours of birth in term/near-term infants (≥36 weeks) with moderate-to-severe encephalopathy—do not let MRI or other workup delay cooling.

Eligibility combines evidence of perinatal asphyxia (pH ≤7.0 or base deficit ≥16, or a sentinel event plus low Apgars/need for resuscitation) with a clinical exam showing moderate-to-severe encephalopathy.
Sinus bradycardia and thrombocytopenia are expected during cooling; watch glucose closely because hypoglycemia worsens HIE injury.
DWI-MRI at day 4–7 is the best predictor of neurodevelopmental outcome; basal ganglia/thalamic injury carries the worst motor prognosis.

cases.title: A Jittery, Irritable Three-Day-Old Who Won't Settle
cases.chiefComplaint: Three-day-old with tremulousness, irritability, and a high-pitched cry that worsens between feeds.
cases.history: A 3-day-old female born at 38 weeks by vaginal delivery is noted by nursing to be increasingly jittery, with a shrill high-pitched cry, poor sleep, and difficulty feeding with an uncoordinated suck. Symptoms began on the second day of life and have intensified. Birth weight was 2680 g (small for gestational age). The mother is a 26-year-old G3P3 who received late and intermittent prenatal care. She acknowledges taking prescribed buprenorphine for opioid use disorder during pregnancy and admits to occasional illicit oxycodone use. She smoked cigarettes throughout pregnancy. Maternal urine toxicology at admission was positive for opiates and buprenorphine; hepatitis C antibody is positive and HIV is negative. The infant received vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine.
