There is no family history of seizures or metabolic disorders. On exam the infant is awake, hyperalert, sweaty, with frequent yawning and sneezing. Vital signs and a point-of-care glucose are obtained. The nursing team has started serial standardized withdrawal scoring.
cases.exam: Hyperalert, irritable term-appearing infant who is difficult to console. Skin warm and sweaty with mild perioral mottling; excoriation noted over the chin and knees from rubbing. Anterior fontanelle soft. Pupils equal and reactive. Diffusely increased tone with brisk reflexes and a fine, symmetric tremor of all four limbs that stops when the limbs are gently held. Moro reflex exaggerated. Frequent yawning, sneezing, and nasal stuffiness. Suck is disorganized with a hyperactive root. No tongue thrusting, no sustained eye deviation, no lip-smacking, and no apnea observed. Abdomen soft with frequent loose stools; mild diaper dermatitis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most reliably distinguishes this infant's tremor from a true neonatal seizure?
options: The tremor is rhythmic and symmetric
The movements stop when the affected limb is gently held
The movements involve all four limbs
The infant is fully awake during the episodes
stages.explanation: Jitteriness/tremor is stimulus-sensitive and stops with gentle passive flexion or holding of the limb, whereas clonic seizure activity persists despite restraint—this is the single most useful bedside discriminator. Rhythmicity does not separate the two, since both jitteriness and clonic seizures can appear rhythmic. Involvement of all four limbs occurs in both and is not discriminating. Being awake is suggestive of jitteriness, but seizures can also occur with altered or even apparently normal alertness in neonates, so it is less reliable than the suppression-with-holding maneuver. Jitteriness also lacks autonomic features like eye deviation and is provoked by stimulation.

stages.label: BEDSIDE DATA
stages.content:
Point-of-care glucose: 62 mg/dL
Ionized calcium: 1.18 mmol/L
Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Score: 11 (two consecutive scores ≥8)
Infant urine toxicology: positive for opiates and buprenorphine
Meconium toxicology: pending
Exam during scoring: stimulus-sensitive tremor that ceases with limb holding; no eye deviation or apnea

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for this infant's neurologic findings?
options: Hypoglycemia-induced irritability
Hypocalcemic tetany
Neonatal opioid withdrawal (neonatal abstinence syndrome)
Neonatal bacterial meningitis
stages.explanation: The constellation of CNS irritability (high-pitched cry, hypertonia, tremor, exaggerated Moro), autonomic signs (tachycardia, tachypnea, sweating, sneezing, yawning), and GI disturbance (loose stools, disorganized feeding) appearing on day 2–3 in an infant with documented maternal opioid use and positive infant toxicology is classic neonatal abstinence syndrome. Hypoglycemia is excluded by a normal glucose of 62 mg/dL. Hypocalcemic tetany is unlikely with a normal ionized calcium. Meningitis must be considered with irritability and fever, but this infant is afebrile, has a clear withdrawal exposure history, and lacks a bulging fontanelle or toxic appearance—NAS is far more consistent.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for this infant?
options: Start intravenous phenobarbital immediately
Optimize nonpharmacologic care and continue serial Finnegan scoring
Begin oral morphine without further nonpharmacologic measures
Administer naloxone to reverse opioid effects
stages.explanation: First-line management of NAS is nonpharmacologic: swaddling, low-stimulation environment, frequent small feeds, skin-to-skin contact, and rooming-in, with serial standardized scoring (Finnegan or Eat-Sleep-Console) to guide escalation. Pharmacologic therapy—typically oral morphine for opioid withdrawal—is added only when supportive care fails (e.g., persistent high scores, poor feeding, weight loss). Starting morphine without first optimizing supportive care skips the standard stepwise approach. Naloxone is contraindicated: in an opioid-exposed neonate it can precipitate severe acute withdrawal and seizures. Phenobarbital is a second agent reserved for polysubstance or refractory cases, not first-line monotherapy here.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding breastfeeding for this mother on buprenorphine maintenance, what is the most appropriate advice?
options: Breastfeeding is contraindicated because buprenorphine passes into milk
Formula feeding is required because she is hepatitis C positive
Breastfeeding should wait until the infant's withdrawal fully resolves
Breastfeeding is encouraged if she is stable on treatment and abstinent from illicit drugs and not HIV-positive
stages.explanation: Breastfeeding is encouraged for mothers stable on buprenorphine or methadone maintenance who are abstinent from illicit drugs and not HIV-positive, because minimal drug transfers into milk and breastfeeding reduces NAS severity, improves bonding, and shortens treatment. It is not contraindicated simply because trace drug enters milk. Hepatitis C is not a contraindication to breastfeeding unless nipples are cracked or bleeding. Delaying breastfeeding until withdrawal resolves is counterproductive, since breastfeeding itself helps soothe and may lessen withdrawal—however, this mother's admitted ongoing illicit opioid use must be addressed, and continued illicit use would change the recommendation.

cases.diagnosis: Neonatal abstinence syndrome (neonatal opioid withdrawal) with neurologic manifestations
pearls: Jitteriness stops with gentle limb holding and is stimulus-provoked; clonic seizures persist against restraint and may have eye deviation or autonomic features.
NAS combines CNS irritability, autonomic instability, and GI dysfunction, typically peaking on days 2–4 for short-acting opioids (later for methadone).
Nonpharmacologic care (swaddling, low stimulation, rooming-in, frequent feeds) plus standardized scoring (Finnegan or Eat-Sleep-Console) is first-line; oral morphine is escalation therapy.
Never give naloxone to an opioid-exposed neonate—it can precipitate seizures; breastfeeding is encouraged for stable, abstinent mothers on maintenance therapy.

cases.title: A Confused Six-Year-Old Who Stopped Walking After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Six-year-old boy with confusion, unsteadiness, and weakness two weeks after a viral illness.
cases.history: A previously healthy 6-year-old boy is admitted for two days of progressive lethargy, confusion, and difficulty walking. About two weeks ago he had a febrile upper respiratory illness with cough and rhinorrhea that resolved. Over the past 48 hours his parents noted he became increasingly drowsy and irritable, complained of a headache, and began stumbling and dragging his right leg. This morning he was difficult to wake and did not recognize his grandmother. There has been no recent travel, tick exposure, or sick contacts beyond the earlier cold. He is fully vaccinated. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He had a single brief episode of staring and lip-smacking this morning. There is no family history of multiple sclerosis, autoimmune disease, or epilepsy. Birth and development were normal. He lives at home with parents and a younger sibling who is well. On arrival he is somnolent but arousable, with fluctuating attention, and the team is concerned about an evolving multifocal neurologic process superimposed on encephalopathy.
cases.exam: Somnolent boy, arousable to voice, with fluctuating attention and disorientation to place. Neck supple. Cranial nerves: mild left central facial weakness; pupils equal and reactive; no papilledema. Motor: right-sided weakness (4-/5 arm and leg) with increased tone and brisk reflexes on the right; left side normal. Right Babinski sign present. Sensory exam grossly intact but limited by cooperation. Gait ataxic and right-leg-favoring when assisted. No nuchal rigidity, no rash, no joint swelling. Fundi normal without optic disc edema. No organomegaly. Speech is dysarthric but comprehension is intact when alert.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features is most characteristic of the leading diagnosis at this point?
options: Isolated focal deficit without altered mental status
Encephalopathy plus multifocal neurologic deficits following a recent infection
Recurrent stereotyped attacks separated by months
Pure ataxia with no encephalopathy or weakness
stages.explanation: The hallmark of acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is encephalopathy (here, confusion and somnolence)—which is required for the diagnosis—combined with multifocal deficits (hemiparesis, facial weakness, ataxia, possible seizure) occurring days to weeks after an infection or, rarely, vaccination. An isolated focal deficit without encephalopathy points toward a single demyelinating event (e.g., a first MS-type attack) rather than ADEM, because encephalopathy is the distinguishing pediatric criterion. Recurrent stereotyped attacks separated in time suggest a relapsing disorder like MS, not a monophasic post-infectious illness. Pure ataxia without encephalopathy describes acute cerebellar ataxia, a different post-infectious entity.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast:
Multiple large, ill-defined ("fluffy") T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in bilateral subcortical and deep white matter
Lesions in both thalami and the right basal ganglia
One lesion in the left middle cerebellar peduncle
No well-defined ovoid periventricular lesions perpendicular to the ventricles
Patchy incomplete (open-ring) enhancement of several lesions
No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct; no mass effect

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before starting immunotherapy, which next step is most important to exclude a dangerous mimic?
options: Lumbar puncture with CSF cell count, protein, and HSV PCR
Electroencephalogram to characterize the seizure
Serum ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urine copper
Muscle biopsy of the quadriceps
stages.explanation: Herpes simplex encephalitis is the can't-miss mimic of an encephalopathic, febrile child with focal deficits, so lumbar puncture for CSF cell count, protein, and HSV PCR (with empiric acyclovir until results return) is the most important next step. EEG can document the seizure and may show focal slowing but does not exclude HSV or guide acute infectious management. Ceruloplasmin and urine copper evaluate for Wilson disease, which presents subacutely with movement and psychiatric features, not an acute post-infectious encephalopathy. Muscle biopsy is irrelevant to a central demyelinating/encephalitic process. CSF in ADEM typically shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein, usually without oligoclonal bands.

stages.label: CSF & SEROLOGIES
stages.content:
CSF: WBC 38 /µL (88% lymphocytes), RBC 2 /µL
CSF protein: 62 mg/dL
CSF glucose: 58 mg/dL (serum 92 mg/dL)
CSF HSV PCR: negative
CSF oligoclonal bands: negative
Serum MOG-IgG: positive
Serum aquaporin-4 (AQP4) IgG: negative
Opening pressure: 18 cm H2O

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment now that HSV is excluded?
options: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone
Long-term interferon beta therapy
Plasma exchange as the sole initial therapy
Oral acyclovir for 21 days
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for ADEM is high-dose IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone ~20–30 mg/kg/day for 3–5 days) followed by an oral taper, which speeds recovery; IVIG or plasma exchange are reserved for steroid-refractory cases. Interferon beta is a disease-modifying therapy for relapsing MS and has no role in a monophasic post-infectious illness—using it here would be inappropriate and could even worsen MOG-associated disease. Plasma exchange is second-line rescue therapy, not the initial monotherapy. Acyclovir was appropriately given empirically but is stopped once HSV PCR returns negative, since this is an immune-mediated demyelinating process, not viral encephalitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which CSF/imaging feature in this child argues against a first attack of multiple sclerosis?
options: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis
Absence of CSF oligoclonal bands with large, fluffy bilateral lesions and encephalopathy
Elevated CSF protein
Patchy lesion enhancement
stages.explanation: Negative CSF oligoclonal bands together with encephalopathy and large, ill-defined bilateral white-matter lesions (rather than discrete ovoid periventricular Dawson fingers) argue against MS and favor ADEM—MS typically has positive oligoclonal bands, well-demarcated lesions, and no encephalopathy. Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis is nonspecific and can occur in both ADEM and MS, so it does not discriminate. Elevated CSF protein is likewise nonspecific and seen in many inflammatory CNS processes. Patchy or even open-ring enhancement can occur in either demyelinating disorder and does not favor one over the other. The encephalopathy plus the lesion morphology and absent bands are the discriminating features pointing to ADEM.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The positive serum MOG-IgG and negative oligoclonal bands most strongly suggest which clinical course?
options: Almost certain progression to classic multiple sclerosis
A monophasic illness, though relapsing MOG-associated disease is possible
An invariably fatal demyelinating disorder
A purely peripheral nervous system disease
stages.explanation: MOG-IgG positivity with absent CSF oligoclonal bands favors a MOG-antibody-associated, typically monophasic ADEM rather than classic MS, though a subset of MOG-positive children relapse (MOG-associated disease), warranting follow-up. Progression to classic MS is actually less likely when oligoclonal bands are negative and MOG is positive—MS more often has positive bands and MS-pattern lesions, which are absent here. ADEM is generally not fatal and most children recover well, especially with steroids. The disease is central (brain/spinal cord demyelination), not a peripheral nerve disorder, so a purely peripheral course is incorrect. Serial MRI and antibody testing help distinguish monophasic from relapsing disease.

cases.diagnosis: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM), MOG-IgG positive
pearls: Encephalopathy is the defining feature of pediatric ADEM and is what separates it from a first MS attack or clinically isolated syndrome.

MRI shows large, fluffy, poorly marginated bilateral white-matter lesions, often with deep gray (thalamic/basal ganglia) involvement; well-defined ovoid periventricular lesions and positive oligoclonal bands favor MS instead.
Always exclude HSV encephalitis with CSF HSV PCR and empiric acyclovir before attributing an encephalopathic febrile child to ADEM.
First-line therapy is high-dose IV methylprednisolone; MOG-IgG positivity with negative oligoclonal bands favors a monophasic course but mandates relapse surveillance.

cases.title: Recurrent Vision Loss and Double Vision in a Teenager
cases.chiefComplaint: Fifteen-year-old girl with a second episode of painful vision loss, now reporting new double vision and imbalance.
cases.history: A 15-year-old girl presents to neurology clinic with five days of blurred, dim vision in the left eye accompanied by pain on eye movement. Eight months ago she had a similar episode in the right eye that was diagnosed as optic neuritis and improved over several weeks with a steroid course; she did not follow up. Over the past two days she has also noticed intermittent horizontal double vision and a sense of unsteadiness when walking. She denies bowel or bladder symptoms, recent infection, fever, or rash. She is otherwise healthy, takes only a combined oral contraceptive, and has no allergies. Menarche was at age 12 with regular cycles. She lives with her mother; a maternal aunt has Hashimoto thyroiditis, and there is no known family history of MS. She does not smoke or use drugs. Review of systems is notable for fatigue and heat sensitivity that transiently worsens her vision after hot showers. The clinician is evaluating whether her two separated attacks, plus current brainstem signs, fulfill criteria for a chronic demyelinating disorder.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented adolescent, not encephalopathic. Left eye visual acuity 20/80, right 20/20; left relative afferent pupillary defect present; mild left optic disc pallor with subtle temporal pallor on the right (prior episode). Red desaturation in the left eye. On lateral gaze there is impaired adduction of the right eye with abducting nystagmus of the left eye (internuclear ophthalmoplegia). Mild dysmetria on left finger-to-nose and a wide-based gait. Motor strength 5/5 throughout; reflexes brisk with bilateral plantar responses equivocal. Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick; no sensory level. No nuchal rigidity. Mental status entirely normal with intact memory and language.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single concept is most central to diagnosing this patient's condition?
options: Encephalopathy with multifocal deficits after infection
Dissemination of CNS lesions in both space and time
Symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia
A monophasic illness confined to one CNS region
stages.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is defined by dissemination in space (lesions in distinct CNS locations—here optic nerves and brainstem) and dissemination in time (separate attacks or new lesions over time—here episodes eight months apart). This patient's two clinically separated optic neuritis events plus a new brainstem syndrome (internuclear ophthalmoplegia) exemplify both. Encephalopathy after infection describes ADEM, and notably she is NOT encephalopathic, which argues against ADEM. Symmetric ascending areflexic weakness describes Guillain-Barré, a peripheral process inconsistent with her optic and brainstem signs. A monophasic single-region illness would be a clinically isolated syndrome, but her recurrence over time excludes a strictly monophasic course.

stages.label: MRI & CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain: multiple ovoid T2/FLAIR periventricular lesions oriented perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers), a juxtacortical lesion, and a brainstem lesion in the dorsal pons; one lesion shows gadolinium enhancement while others do not
MRI cervical spine: a short-segment (<2 vertebral bodies) cord lesion
Visual evoked potentials: prolonged P100 latency bilaterally
CSF: WBC 6 /µL (lymphocytic), protein 38 mg/dL
CSF oligoclonal bands: positive (4 bands, not in serum)
Serum AQP4-IgG: negative; serum MOG-IgG: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which MRI/CSF feature best distinguishes this disorder from monophasic ADEM?
options: Large fluffy bilateral lesions with deep gray involvement
Well-defined ovoid periventricular lesions with simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions plus positive oligoclonal bands
A long longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesion
Absence of any CSF abnormality
stages.explanation: Discrete ovoid periventricular lesions perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers), the coexistence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions (indicating dissemination in time on a single scan), and positive CSF oligoclonal bands strongly favor MS over ADEM. ADEM characteristically shows large, fluffy, poorly demarcated bilateral lesions with frequent deep gray-matter involvement and usually negative oligoclonal bands, so that pattern points away from MS. A longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis lesion (≥3 segments) suggests neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (AQP4) rather than MS, and her cord lesion is short-segment. Normal CSF would not help distinguish and is not the case here, since oligoclonal bands are present.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Her impaired adduction of one eye with abducting nystagmus of the other on lateral gaze localizes a lesion to which structure?
options: The optic nerve
The oculomotor nerve nucleus
The medial longitudinal fasciculus in the brainstem
The cerebellar vermis
stages.explanation: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia—failure of adduction in one eye with nystagmus of the abducting eye—localizes to the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) in the brainstem, which connects the abducens nucleus to the contralateral oculomotor nucleus; bilateral or unilateral INO in a young patient is a classic MS sign and provides dissemination in space when combined with optic nerve involvement. The optic nerve lesion causes the afferent pupillary defect and vision loss, not the gaze-coordination failure. An oculomotor nucleus lesion would impair multiple extraocular movements, ptosis, and pupil, not produce isolated adduction failure with preserved convergence. A cerebellar vermis lesion causes truncal ataxia and gaze-evoked nystagmus, not the dissociated adduction deficit of an MLF lesion.

stages.label: ACUTE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for her current acute relapse?
options: High-dose IV methylprednisolone for the acute attack
Immediate initiation of interferon beta as acute treatment
Oral prednisone 20 mg daily indefinitely
Therapeutic plasma exchange as first-line for all relapses
stages.explanation: Acute MS relapses are treated with high-dose IV methylprednisolone (typically 1 g/day or ~20–30 mg/kg/day for 3–5 days), which hastens recovery from the attack. Interferon beta is a disease-modifying therapy that reduces future relapses but does not treat the acute event, so it is started after the attack, not as acute rescue. Low-dose chronic oral prednisone is not standard MS therapy and carries cumulative toxicity without proven benefit. Plasma exchange is reserved for steroid-refractory severe relapses, not first-line for every relapse. After stabilization she will need a disease-modifying agent, and her oral contraceptive/smoking risks should be reviewed.

stages.label: LONG-TERM PLAN
stages.question: After the relapse is treated, what is the most appropriate long-term strategy?
options: Observation with no disease-modifying therapy until a third attack
Lifelong daily oral corticosteroids
Start a disease-modifying therapy to reduce relapse frequency and disability accrual
Prophylactic IVIG every month indefinitely
stages.explanation: Pediatric-onset MS warrants early initiation of a disease-modifying therapy because children have high relapse rates and benefit from reducing relapse frequency and slowing disability—so starting a DMT after this confirmed second attack is correct. Waiting for a third attack allows preventable relapses and lesion accrual, which is no longer the standard given clear diagnostic criteria are met. Chronic daily corticosteroids cause major long-term toxicity (growth suppression, osteoporosis) without preventing MS progression. Monthly IVIG is not an established first-line maintenance therapy for pediatric MS. Choice of DMT, adherence support, vitamin D, and smoking cessation counseling round out management.

cases.diagnosis: Pediatric-onset multiple sclerosis (relapsing-remitting)
pearls: MS requires dissemination in space and time; a single MRI showing simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions can satisfy dissemination in time.
Absence of encephalopathy, discrete ovoid periventricular lesions (Dawson fingers), and positive CSF oligoclonal bands distinguish MS from monophasic ADEM.
Uhthoff phenomenon (transient worsening with heat) and an internuclear ophthalmoplegia in a young patient are classic MS clues.
Treat acute relapses with high-dose IV methylprednisolone, then start a disease-modifying therapy early in pediatric MS to limit disability.

cases.title: A Boy Who Can't Climb the Stairs After a Stomach Bug
cases.chiefComplaint: Seven-year-old boy with progressive leg weakness and difficulty walking after a recent diarrheal illness.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is admitted for four days of progressive lower-extremity weakness. Symptoms began with tingling and clumsiness in both feet, followed by difficulty climbing stairs and getting up from the floor; today he can barely stand and complains his hands feel numb. About 12 days ago he had several days of bloody diarrhea and crampy abdominal pain after a family barbecue, which resolved on its own. He denies back pain other than mild low-back aching, fever, or bladder problems. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no history of trauma, tick bites, or travel. He lives with both parents; no family history of neuromuscular disease. On review of systems he notes the weakness is fairly symmetric and has been steadily ascending from his feet upward. The admitting team is monitoring closely because of concern that the weakness could involve the muscles of breathing if it continues to progress.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative boy in no acute distress at rest. Cranial nerves intact with normal facial strength and no ptosis. Motor: symmetric weakness, 3/5 hip flexors and knee extensors, 4/5 ankle dorsiflexion, 4+/5 in the upper extremities (legs weaker than arms). Deep tendon reflexes absent at the knees and ankles and diminished (1+) in the arms. Plantar responses flexor. Sensation: mildly reduced vibration and pinprick in a stocking distribution; no discrete sensory level on the trunk. Tone normal to slightly decreased. Gait: unable to walk unassisted. No bladder distension; rectal tone normal. No neck stiffness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most strongly supports the leading diagnosis?
options: A sharp sensory level at the umbilicus
Symmetric ascending weakness with absent deep tendon reflexes
Spastic tone with up-going plantar responses
Asymmetric weakness with fasciculations and preserved reflexes
stages.explanation: Symmetric, ascending weakness with areflexia following a gastrointestinal infection is the classic picture of Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy, AIDP). A sharp truncal sensory level indicates a spinal cord (myelopathic) process such as transverse myelitis, not a peripheral neuropathy, and is absent here. Spasticity and up-going toes are upper-motor-neuron signs incompatible with the flaccid, areflexic, lower-motor-neuron pattern of GBS. Asymmetric weakness with fasciculations and preserved or brisk reflexes suggests an anterior horn cell or motor neuron process, whereas GBS is symmetric and hyporeflexic. The recent Campylobacter-type diarrheal illness is a well-known antecedent trigger.

stages.label: CSF & STUDIES
stages.content:
Lumbar puncture (day 12 of illness):
CSF WBC 3 /µL
CSF protein 96 mg/dL (elevated)
CSF glucose 60 mg/dL
Opening pressure normal
Nerve conduction studies: prolonged distal latencies, conduction slowing, absent F-waves
Stool culture (retrospective): Campylobacter jejuni
MRI spine: no cord lesion; mild enhancement of cauda equina nerve roots

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF finding of elevated protein with a normal cell count is best described as:
options: Albuminocytologic dissociation
Lymphocytic pleocytosis
Hypoglycorrhachia
Xanthochromia
stages.explanation: Elevated CSF protein with a normal (or near-normal) cell count is termed albuminocytologic dissociation, the classic CSF signature of GBS, often most pronounced after the first week of illness. Lymphocytic pleocytosis (high lymphocyte count) suggests viral meningoencephalitis or, if marked, raises concern for other processes—and is not seen here. Hypoglycorrhachia (low CSF glucose) points to bacterial or fungal meningitis, which is absent given normal glucose. Xanthochromia indicates prior subarachnoid blood and is irrelevant to this presentation. The combination of albuminocytologic dissociation with demyelinating nerve conduction findings and nerve-root enhancement confirms an inflammatory polyradiculoneuropathy.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which bedside parameter is most important to monitor for impending respiratory failure?
options: Continuous pulse oximetry alone
Serial forced vital capacity (and negative inspiratory force)
Peak expiratory flow rate during cough
Arterial blood gas every 12 hours regardless of symptoms
stages.explanation: Serial bedside spirometry—forced vital capacity (and negative inspiratory force)—is the most sensitive way to detect evolving neuromuscular respiratory failure, because it declines before oxygen saturation falls. A common rule is to consider intubation as FVC approaches ~15–20 mL/kg or 30% predicted. Pulse oximetry is reassuringly normal until very late in neuromuscular failure (CO2 retention precedes hypoxemia), so relying on SpO2 alone is dangerous. Peak expiratory flow assesses cough strength and airway clearance but is less standardized for predicting intubation. Routine timed blood gases lag behind real-time deterioration and do not substitute for trended FVC and clinical assessment of bulbar function and secretions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disease-modifying treatment for this child?
options: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
Empiric ceftriaxone for meningitis
Pyridostigmine
stages.explanation: IVIG (typically 2 g/kg over 2–5 days) is a first-line disease-modifying therapy for GBS and shortens recovery time; plasma exchange is an equivalent alternative. Corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may even delay recovery—a key board distinction from many other immune-mediated neuropathies and from ADEM/MS. Empiric ceftriaxone treats bacterial meningitis, which is excluded by the acellular CSF and normal glucose. Pyridostigmine is used for myasthenia gravis, a neuromuscular junction disorder with fatigable weakness and normal reflexes, not the areflexic ascending neuropathy seen here. Supportive care—respiratory monitoring, autonomic surveillance, DVT prophylaxis, and pain control—is equally essential.

cases.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy) post-Campylobacter
pearls: GBS classically presents as symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia after an antecedent infection (Campylobacter jejuni, viral URI).
CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal cell count), often most evident after the first week.
Trend forced vital capacity and negative inspiratory force—neuromuscular respiratory failure precedes any drop in pulse oximetry.
Treat with IVIG or plasma exchange; corticosteroids are ineffective and not used in GBS.

cases.title: Sudden Leg Weakness and Trouble Urinating in a Schoolgirl
cases.chiefComplaint: Eleven-year-old girl with one day of rapidly worsening leg weakness, numbness, and inability to urinate.
cases.history: An 11-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with about 24 hours of progressive weakness and numbness of both legs and a feeling that she cannot empty her bladder. Yesterday afternoon she noticed her legs felt heavy and tingly, and overnight she became unable to walk to the bathroom. She reports a band-like tightness around her lower abdomen and numbness from roughly the umbilicus down. She has not passed urine since the previous evening and feels uncomfortable suprapubic fullness. She had a mild cold about ten days ago. There is no back trauma, no fever currently, and no recent vaccination. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no family history of MS or neuromuscular disease. She is a healthy fifth-grader who plays soccer. She denies headache, vision change, or arm weakness. The triage nurse notes a palpably distended bladder. The team must rapidly localize the lesion and decide on imaging before any lumbar puncture.
cases.exam: Alert, anxious girl in mild discomfort from bladder distension. Mental status normal. Cranial nerves intact. Upper extremities: full strength and normal reflexes. Lower extremities: symmetric weakness 2/5 to 3/5, with increased tone developing and brisk patellar reflexes; ankle clonus present and bilateral Babinski signs. A clear sensory level to pinprick and temperature at approximately T10, with diminished vibration and proprioception in the toes. Suprapubic dullness with palpable distended bladder; reduced rectal tone. No spinal tenderness or deformity, no skin lesion over the spine. No nuchal rigidity. Gait not tested due to weakness.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Which feature best localizes this child's lesion to the spinal cord rather than to peripheral nerves?
options: Symmetric leg weakness
A discrete sensory level with up-going toes and urinary retention
Numbness in the toes
A recent viral illness
Anxiety and discomfort
stages.explanation: A well-demarcated truncal sensory level (here ~T10) combined with upper-motor-neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, bilateral Babinski) and sphincter dysfunction (urinary retention, reduced rectal tone) localizes the lesion to the spinal cord—the hallmark of transverse myelitis. Symmetric leg weakness occurs in both myelopathy and Guillain-Barré, so it does not localize. Distal toe numbness alone is nonspecific. A recent viral illness is a common antecedent for both transverse myelitis and GBS and does not localize. Anxiety/discomfort are nonspecific. Distinguishing cord from peripheral nerve is critical: GBS produces areflexia and down-going toes without a sensory level, the opposite of this UMN myelopathic picture.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Emergent MRI of the spine (and bladder decompression with catheterization)
Start IVIG empirically for Guillain-Barré
Discharge with outpatient neurology follow-up
High-dose oral prednisone at home
stages.explanation: Acute myelopathy with a sensory level and retention is a neurologic emergency requiring emergent MRI of the spine to identify a compressive lesion (which would need urgent surgery) or intrinsic cord inflammation, while bladder decompression by catheterization relieves retention. Performing lumbar puncture before imaging risks missing cord compression and, in the presence of a mass, can be hazardous—imaging should precede LP here. Empiric IVIG presumes GBS, but the UMN signs and sensory level point to a cord lesion, not a peripheral neuropathy. Discharge is unsafe for a rapidly progressive myelopathy. Home oral steroids are inappropriate before diagnosis and imaging; high-dose IV steroids follow confirmation of inflammatory myelitis.

stages.label: MRI & CSF
stages.content:
MRI spine with contrast: T2-hyperintense signal in the central cord spanning T8–T11 with mild cord expansion and patchy enhancement; no compressive mass, no epidural collection
MRI brain: normal, no demyelinating lesions
CSF: WBC 42 /µL (lymphocytic predominance), protein 70 mg/dL, glucose 56 mg/dL
CSF oligoclonal bands: negative
Serum AQP4-IgG: pending; MOG-IgG: pending
Post-void residual by catheter: 380 mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which CSF/MRI feature most distinguishes this condition from Guillain-Barré syndrome?
options: CSF pleocytosis with an intramedullary cord lesion on MRI
Elevated CSF protein
A recent antecedent infection
Symmetric distal weakness
Normal CSF glucose
stages.explanation: CSF pleocytosis (WBC 42, lymphocytic) together with an intramedullary T2 cord lesion on MRI indicates an inflammatory cord process (transverse myelitis), whereas GBS classically shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with a NORMAL cell count) and a normal cord on MRI with possible nerve-root enhancement. Elevated CSF protein occurs in both and does not distinguish them. An antecedent infection is common to both. Symmetric distal weakness can be seen in both. Normal CSF glucose is expected in both inflammatory and most GBS cases and is nonspecific. The combination of pleocytosis plus a focal cord lesion, with UMN signs and a sensory level, firmly establishes transverse myelitis over GBS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this inflammatory myelitis?
options: High-dose IV methylprednisolone
Surgical decompression
Intravenous immunoglobulin alone
Oral acyclovir monotherapy
Observation with physical therapy only
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for acute inflammatory transverse myelitis is high-dose IV methylprednisolone (e.g., 30 mg/kg/day, max 1 g, for 3–5 days), which reduces inflammation and can improve recovery; plasma exchange is added for steroid-refractory or severe cases. Surgical decompression is indicated only for a compressive lesion, which MRI explicitly excluded here. IVIG alone is not the established first-line therapy for transverse myelitis (it is first-line for GBS). Acyclovir would treat viral myelitis, but the clinical and CSF picture indicates an immune-mediated inflammatory myelitis rather than active HSV/VZV infection (though some clinicians cover empirically pending PCR). Observation alone is inappropriate for an acute, potentially reversible cord injury.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If her serum AQP4-IgG returns positive, how does this change long-term management?
options: No change; transverse myelitis is always monophasic
It confirms multiple sclerosis and mandates interferon beta
It indicates neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, warranting long-term immunosuppression to prevent relapses
It confirms Guillain-Barré and mandates IVIG
It rules out any autoimmune disease
stages.explanation: A positive aquaporin-4 (AQP4) IgG indicates neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), which is a relapsing disease requiring long-term immunosuppression (e.g., rituximab, eculizumab, or other approved agents) to prevent devastating recurrent attacks—so it substantially changes management beyond the acute steroids. It does not mean the illness is monophasic; NMOSD is characteristically relapsing. It does not confirm MS, and importantly, classic MS disease-modifying drugs like interferon beta can WORSEN NMOSD, so that choice is harmful. It has nothing to do with Guillain-Barré, a peripheral neuropathy. A positive autoantibody clearly does not rule out autoimmune disease—it defines one. AQP4 and MOG testing therefore critically guides whether chronic preventive therapy is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Acute transverse myelitis (idiopathic/post-infectious)
pearls: A sensory level, up-going toes/hyperreflexia, and sphincter dysfunction localize to the spinal cord—transverse myelitis—whereas GBS gives areflexia, down-going toes, and no sensory level.
Acute myelopathy is an emergency: obtain emergent spine MRI to exclude compression BEFORE lumbar puncture, and decompress the bladder.
CSF in transverse myelitis shows pleocytosis with an intramedullary cord lesion on MRI; GBS shows albuminocytologic dissociation with a normal cord.
Treat with high-dose IV methylprednisolone; add plasma exchange for steroid-refractory cases, and send AQP4/MOG antibodies to assess for NMOSD/MOGAD.

cases.title: A Teen Who Became Paranoid and Started Making Strange Faces
cases.chiefComplaint: Fourteen-year-old girl with three weeks of psychiatric changes, abnormal movements, and now seizures and autonomic instability.
cases.history: A 14-year-old girl is transferred to the ICU after a generalized seizure. Three weeks ago her family noticed personality change—anxiety, paranoia, and uncharacteristic emotional outbursts—initially attributed to school stress; she was briefly seen by psychiatry and started on risperidone. Over the following two weeks she developed memory problems, fragmented speech, insomnia, and then involuntary lip and tongue movements with grimacing. In the past few days she became agitated and confused, had two seizures, and her heart rate and blood pressure have been swinging widely. There is no prior psychiatric or seizure history. She takes risperidone, recently started; no other medications and no known allergies. She had a mild flu-like illness about a month ago. Menarche at 12, regular cycles. No family history of autoimmune or psychiatric disease. No drug or alcohol use per family. The team is concerned the antipsychotic may be masking or mimicking a neurologic process, and that her movements and autonomic swings point beyond a primary psychiatric disorder.
cases.exam: Agitated, intermittently unresponsive adolescent with fluctuating arousal. Orofacial dyskinesias with lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, and grimacing; choreoathetoid movements of the hands. Speech reduced to occasional disorganized phrases (near-mutism at times). Pupils equal, reactive; no focal cranial nerve deficit. Tone variably rigid with episodic posturing. Reflexes brisk and symmetric. No nuchal rigidity. Diaphoresis and labile heart rate and blood pressure noted during the exam. No rash, no organomegaly. Pelvic/abdominal exam unremarkable on initial survey; no palpable mass. Cardiopulmonary exam normal between autonomic surges.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical pattern is most characteristic of the leading diagnosis?
options: Acute symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia
Subacute psychiatric change progressing to dyskinesias, seizures, and autonomic instability
Recurrent optic neuritis with internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia with rapid obtundation
Isolated chorea after a streptococcal infection
stages.explanation: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis classically evolves over weeks from a viral-like prodrome to prominent psychiatric/behavioral change, then memory and speech deterioration, orofacial-limb dyskinesias, seizures, decreased consciousness, and autonomic instability—exactly this trajectory. Ascending areflexic weakness is Guillain-Barré, a peripheral disorder. Recurrent optic neuritis with INO describes multiple sclerosis. Fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia suggest bacterial meningitis, which lacks the weeks-long psychiatric-then-dyskinetic course. Isolated post-streptococcal chorea (Sydenham) causes chorea without the profound psychiatric, seizure, and autonomic features. Recognizing this multistage syndrome—especially when antipsychotics seem to worsen or fail to control symptoms—should prompt evaluation for autoimmune encephalitis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: normal or only subtle medial temporal T2 signal
EEG: diffuse slowing; extreme delta brush pattern in runs
CSF: WBC 28 /µL (lymphocytic), protein 45 mg/dL, glucose normal
CSF HSV PCR: negative
CSF anti-NMDA receptor (NR1) antibody: positive
Serum anti-NMDA receptor antibody: positive
Urine toxicology: negative
Pelvic ultrasound: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most strongly confirms the diagnosis?
options: EEG showing diffuse slowing
Lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis
Normal brain MRI
Positive CSF anti-NMDA receptor (NR1) antibodies
Negative urine toxicology
stages.explanation: Detection of anti-NMDA receptor (NR1 subunit) antibodies, particularly in CSF, confirms anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and is the diagnostic gold standard; CSF testing is more sensitive and specific than serum. Diffuse EEG slowing is supportive and the 'extreme delta brush' is suggestive, but neither is confirmatory on its own. Lymphocytic pleocytosis indicates inflammation but is nonspecific. A normal or near-normal MRI is common in this disease and helps exclude alternatives but does not confirm it. Negative toxicology only rules out an intoxication mimic. Once antibodies confirm the diagnosis, the workup must include a search for an underlying tumor, classically an ovarian teratoma.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which additional evaluation is most important in this adolescent girl?
options: Echocardiography for endocarditis
Pelvic imaging (ultrasound/MRI) to evaluate for an ovarian teratoma
Bone marrow biopsy
Skeletal survey for occult fractures
Thyroid uptake scan
stages.explanation: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is frequently paraneoplastic in young women, most commonly associated with an ovarian teratoma, so pelvic ultrasound and/or MRI is essential—tumor removal both treats the trigger and improves neurologic recovery. Echocardiography for endocarditis does not fit this immune-mediated syndrome. Bone marrow biopsy and a skeletal survey are unrelated to this presentation. A thyroid uptake scan would not address the relevant tumor association. Identifying and resecting a teratoma is a key management step; even when no tumor is found initially, periodic surveillance is recommended because occult or later-appearing teratomas occur.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment strategy?
options: Continue risperidone and add a benzodiazepine only
Empiric long-term antiepileptic monotherapy alone
First-line immunotherapy (high-dose steroids, IVIG or plasma exchange) plus tumor removal if found
Antipsychotic dose escalation as primary therapy
Observation, since the disease is self-limited
stages.explanation: Management is first-line immunotherapy—high-dose corticosteroids combined with IVIG or plasma exchange—plus removal of any identified tumor; second-line agents (rituximab, cyclophosphamide) are added for inadequate response. Continuing/escalating antipsychotics is not disease-directed and is risky: these patients are prone to neuroleptic sensitivity and can develop neuroleptic malignant syndrome–like reactions, so antipsychotics are minimized. Antiepileptics control seizures symptomatically but do not treat the autoimmune process. Antipsychotic escalation as primary therapy ignores the underlying immune-mediated encephalitis. The disease is not benignly self-limited—untreated patients have high morbidity and mortality, whereas early immunotherapy and tumor resection markedly improve outcomes.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature of this illness most directly explains her need for intensive care monitoring?
options: The heliotrope rash
Autonomic instability with labile heart rate and blood pressure, plus central hypoventilation risk
Calcinosis cutis
Albuminocytologic dissociation
Pseudohypertrophy of the calves
stages.explanation: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis frequently causes severe autonomic instability—wide swings in heart rate and blood pressure, hyperthermia, and central hypoventilation—which can be life-threatening and is the main reason these patients require ICU-level monitoring and sometimes mechanical ventilation. A heliotrope rash and calcinosis cutis belong to dermatomyositis, an unrelated inflammatory myopathy. Albuminocytologic dissociation is the CSF hallmark of Guillain-Barré, not this encephalitis. Calf pseudohypertrophy is a feature of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The combination of dysautonomia, status epilepticus risk, and decreased consciousness drives the intensity of supportive care while immunotherapy takes effect over days to weeks.

cases.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis (with workup for ovarian teratoma)
pearls: Suspect anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in a young person with subacute psychiatric change evolving into dyskinesias, seizures, autonomic instability, and decreased consciousness.
CSF NR1 antibodies confirm the diagnosis (more sensitive/specific than serum); EEG may show the 'extreme delta brush' and MRI is often normal.
Always search for an ovarian teratoma in affected girls/women—tumor removal improves recovery and is part of treatment.
Treat with first-line immunotherapy (steroids + IVIG or plasma exchange) and escalate to rituximab/cyclophosphamide if needed; minimize antipsychotics due to neuroleptic sensitivity.

cases.title: A Four-Year-Old Who Walks on His Toes and Struggles to Stand Up
cases.chiefComplaint: Four-year-old boy with toe-walking, frequent falls, and difficulty rising from the floor.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought to clinic for clumsiness and difficulty keeping up with peers. His parents report he has always been a bit of a late walker (walked at 18 months) and now toe-walks, falls frequently, struggles to climb stairs, and uses his hands to push himself up off the floor. He cannot run as fast as other children and tires easily. There is no pain, no recent illness, and no regression in language or social skills; speech is mildly delayed. He has no significant medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He was born at term without complications. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle who used a wheelchair from his teenage years and died young of a 'muscle disease,' though details are unclear. The parents have not noticed weakness in the arms. He is otherwise growing and eating well. The pediatrician obtained baseline labs after noting the rising-from-floor maneuver and enlarged calves on exam.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, interactive boy. He walks on his toes with a lordotic, waddling gait. When asked to stand from sitting on the floor, he turns prone and 'climbs up' his thighs with his hands (Gowers sign). Proximal lower-extremity weakness (hip girdle) is evident; distal strength better preserved. Calves are firm and enlarged (pseudohypertrophy). Deep tendon reflexes are diminished at the knees, with intact ankle jerks early on. Tone is normal to slightly reduced; no spasticity. Sensation grossly intact. No fasciculations. Cardiac exam normal; no murmur. Mild expressive language delay noted; otherwise cognition appropriate for play.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What does the Gowers sign in this boy indicate?
options: Cerebellar ataxia
Proximal (hip girdle) muscle weakness
Distal sensory neuropathy
Upper motor neuron spasticity
stages.explanation: The Gowers sign—using the hands to 'walk up' the thighs when rising from the floor—reflects proximal lower-extremity (hip and thigh girdle) weakness, the pattern seen in muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne. Cerebellar ataxia produces incoordination and a wide-based gait but not the specific maneuver of climbing up one's legs. A distal sensory neuropathy affects sensation and distal strength, not proximal power, and would not produce a Gowers sign. Upper motor neuron spasticity causes increased tone, hyperreflexia, and up-going toes—the opposite of the hyporeflexic, proximally weak, normal/low-tone picture here. Toe-walking, waddling lordotic gait, and calf pseudohypertrophy reinforce a proximal myopathic process.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase (CK): 14,800 U/L (markedly elevated)
AST 280 U/L, ALT 240 U/L (elevated, of muscle origin)
LDH elevated
TSH normal
Lactate normal
CBC normal
ECG: sinus rhythm; tall R waves in V1 and deep Q waves in lateral leads (early findings)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the markedly elevated CK and X-linked family history, what is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
options: Nerve conduction studies and EMG only
Genetic testing for dystrophin gene (DMD) deletions/duplications/mutations
Muscle MRI of the thighs
Repeat CK in 6 months
stages.explanation: A boy with proximal weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, a Gowers sign, markedly elevated CK, and an X-linked family history is highly suspicious for Duchenne muscular dystrophy; genetic testing of the dystrophin (DMD) gene for deletions/duplications (and, if negative, sequencing for point mutations) is the preferred confirmatory test and largely replaces biopsy. Nerve conduction studies/EMG assess for neuropathy or guide myopathy workup but are not confirmatory and are uncomfortable in a young child when genetics can give the answer noninvasively. Muscle MRI can show fatty infiltration but is not diagnostic of the specific genetic defect. Simply repeating CK delays a diagnosis that the markedly elevated value already strongly supports.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy has been shown to slow loss of ambulation and is a mainstay of management?
options: Glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisone or deflazacort)
High-dose intravenous immunoglobulin
Plasma exchange
Lifelong strict bed rest to protect muscles
stages.explanation: Glucocorticoids (prednisone or deflazacort) are a mainstay of Duchenne care; they slow muscle deterioration, prolong ambulation, and delay scoliosis and respiratory decline. IVIG and plasma exchange treat immune-mediated neuromuscular diseases (e.g., GBS, dermatomyositis, myasthenia) and have no role in this genetic dystrophinopathy. Strict bed rest is harmful—immobility accelerates contractures and weakness; instead, children benefit from maintained gentle activity, stretching, and physical therapy. Comprehensive management also includes cardiac surveillance (cardiomyopathy), pulmonary monitoring, bone health, and—where applicable—mutation-specific therapies such as exon-skipping agents.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the leading cause of death and must be monitored lifelong?
options: Renal failure
Hepatic cirrhosis
Recurrent meningitis
Cardiomyopathy and respiratory failure
stages.explanation: Progressive dilated cardiomyopathy and respiratory muscle failure are the leading causes of death in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, mandating routine cardiac (echocardiography/MRI, ECG) and pulmonary surveillance, with early cardioprotective medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers) and ventilatory support as disease advances. Renal failure is not a feature of the disease; the elevated AST/ALT here are of muscle origin, not hepatic, and cirrhosis is not part of DMD. Recurrent meningitis is unrelated. Anticipating cardiomyopathy and respiratory decline—and intervening early—has substantially extended survival in modern DMD care, which is why lifelong multidisciplinary monitoring is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Duchenne muscular dystrophy
pearls: Toe-walking, waddling lordotic gait, calf pseudohypertrophy, and a Gowers sign in a young boy point to Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
CK is markedly elevated (often >10,000 U/L); 'transaminase elevation' is frequently muscle-derived, not hepatic—check CK before chasing liver disease.
Confirm with DMD gene testing (deletion/duplication then sequencing); biopsy is largely reserved for inconclusive genetics.
Glucocorticoids prolong ambulation; cardiomyopathy and respiratory failure are the leading causes of death and require lifelong surveillance.

cases.title: A Floppy Newborn Who Can't Suck and Has Stiff Mother's Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Term newborn with profound hypotonia, facial weakness, poor feeding, and bilateral clubfeet.
cases.history: A term male newborn is admitted to the NICU for marked hypotonia and respiratory distress at birth. The pregnancy was complicated by polyhydramnios and reduced fetal movements. He required brief positive-pressure ventilation and now has weak respiratory effort and a poor suck, unable to feed by mouth. The mother is a 29-year-old G1P1 who, on focused history, reports longstanding difficulty releasing her grip (for example, letting go after a handshake), mild facial weakness, and that she was told she has 'early cataracts.' She was not previously diagnosed with a neuromuscular disorder. There is no consanguinity. The maternal grandfather reportedly had 'weak muscles' later in life. The infant's Apgars were 4 and 7. No exposure to magnesium or sedating maternal medications. On exam the infant is strikingly floppy with a tented upper lip and bilateral talipes equinovarus (clubfeet). The neonatology and genetics teams suspect an inherited neuromuscular disorder with a parent-of-origin pattern.
cases.exam: Profoundly hypotonic ('floppy') infant lying in a frog-leg posture with poor antigravity movement. Facial diplegia with a characteristic inverted-V (tented) upper lip and an open, triangular mouth; weak cry. Suck and gag are weak; pooling of secretions. Reflexes are diminished. Bilateral clubfeet (talipes equinovarus). Respiratory effort shallow with subcostal retractions. No dysmorphic facies beyond the myopathic face; palate intact. Cardiac exam normal. No hepatosplenomegaly. The mother demonstrates delayed hand-grip release (grip myotonia) when examined, and percussion of her thenar eminence produces a sustained contraction (percussion myotonia).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which maternal finding is the most important clue to the infant's diagnosis?
options: A history of polyhydramnios
Maternal grip myotonia (delayed release) plus early cataracts
The maternal grandfather's late-life weakness
Reduced fetal movements in pregnancy
The mother being a primigravida
stages.explanation: Maternal grip and percussion myotonia together with early ('Christmas tree') cataracts strongly indicate that the mother has myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), and a severely affected floppy newborn of such a mother is the classic congenital DM1 picture transmitted maternally. Polyhydramnios and reduced fetal movements are consequences of fetal weakness/poor swallowing but are nonspecific. The grandfather's late-life weakness fits the anticipation pattern of the family but is less directly diagnostic than the mother's overt myotonia and cataracts. Being a primigravida is irrelevant. Recognizing maternal myotonia (which the mother may not realize she has) is often the key that unlocks the neonatal diagnosis.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
Infant CK: mildly elevated (320 U/L)
Infant EMG: limited; neonates often do not show myotonic discharges
Mother's EMG: myotonic discharges ('dive-bomber' sound)
Genetic testing (infant): CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the DMPK gene, >1000 repeats
Genetic testing (mother): DMPK CTG expansion (smaller than infant's)
Newborn echocardiogram: normal
Swallow study: unsafe oral feeding, aspiration risk

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The phenomenon whereby this disease is more severe and earlier-onset in the infant than in the mother is called:
options: Genomic imprinting
Mosaicism
Anticipation due to trinucleotide repeat expansion
Incomplete penetrance
Variable expressivity unrelated to repeat size
stages.explanation: Anticipation describes earlier onset and increasing severity across generations, driven here by expansion of the unstable CTG trinucleotide repeat in DMPK—the infant's much larger expansion explains the severe congenital phenotype, and maternal transmission characteristically yields the largest expansions and the congenital form. Genomic imprinting refers to parent-of-origin gene silencing; while DM1's congenital form is maternally transmitted, the mechanism for worsening severity is repeat expansion (anticipation), not imprinting per se. Mosaicism (mixed cell populations) does not explain the intergenerational pattern. Incomplete penetrance means some carriers are unaffected, which is not the issue here. Variable expressivity exists but, in DM1, severity correlates closely with repeat length.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority for this neonate?
options: Start mexiletine to treat the infant's myotonia
Respiratory and feeding support (ventilatory assistance and tube feeding)
Begin high-dose corticosteroids
Urgent surgical correction of clubfeet
Initiate IVIG
stages.explanation: Congenital DM1 newborns are critically threatened by respiratory insufficiency and feeding/aspiration risk, so the immediate priority is supportive care—ventilatory assistance (CPAP or mechanical ventilation) and safe nutrition via tube feeding given the unsafe swallow. Mexiletine treats symptomatic myotonia in older patients but does not address the neonatal crisis of weakness and respiratory failure, and clinical myotonia is typically absent in neonates. Corticosteroids and IVIG treat inflammatory myopathies (e.g., dermatomyositis) and have no role in this genetic disorder. Clubfeet need orthopedic management (often serial casting) but are not the acute life threat; airway and feeding stabilization come first. Cardiac and respiratory surveillance continue long-term.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which lifelong complication of DM1 warrants ongoing surveillance and can cause sudden death?
options: Nephrotic syndrome
Hemolytic anemia
Inflammatory arthritis
Cardiac conduction abnormalities (heart block and arrhythmias)
Recurrent nephrolithiasis
stages.explanation: DM1 characteristically causes cardiac conduction system disease—progressive heart block and arrhythmias—which can lead to sudden death, so lifelong ECG surveillance (and sometimes pacemaker placement) is essential for both the infant over time and the affected mother. Nephrotic syndrome is not a feature of DM1. Hemolytic anemia is associated with autoimmune disorders like lupus, not myotonic dystrophy. Inflammatory arthritis is not part of the DM1 phenotype. Recurrent nephrolithiasis is unrelated. Other multisystem features of DM1 include cataracts, insulin resistance, hypersomnolence, and (in adults) frontal balding and testicular atrophy—but the conduction disease is the one most directly tied to sudden death and mandates cardiac monitoring.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate genetic counseling point for this family?
options: The disorder is autosomal recessive with low recurrence risk
It is autosomal dominant; the mother carries the gene and future maternally transmitted pregnancies risk congenital disease, often more severe
It affects only males
There is no heritable component
Paternal transmission causes the most severe congenital form
stages.explanation: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 is autosomal dominant; the affected mother has a 50% chance of transmitting the expanded allele to each child, and—because the congenital severe form is characteristically maternally transmitted with further repeat expansion—future pregnancies carry risk of congenital disease that can be as or more severe (anticipation). It is not autosomal recessive, so recurrence risk is substantial, not low. It is not sex-limited; both sexes are affected. There is a clear heritable component. The congenital form is associated with MATERNAL, not paternal, transmission, so attributing the severe congenital form to paternal transmission is incorrect. The family should be offered genetic counseling and the mother evaluated for cardiac conduction disease.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)
pearls: A floppy newborn with facial diplegia (tented upper lip), feeding/respiratory failure, and clubfeet should prompt examining the MOTHER for grip/percussion myotonia and cataracts.
DM1 is autosomal dominant from a CTG expansion in DMPK; anticipation (repeat expansion) explains worsening severity across generations, and the severe congenital form is maternally transmitted.
Neonates usually lack electrical/clinical myotonia; diagnosis relies on family history and DMPK repeat testing rather than infant EMG.
Immediate care is supportive—respiratory and feeding support; long-term surveillance includes cardiac conduction disease and offering family genetic counseling.

cases.title: An Eight-Year-Old With a Rash and Weak Shoulders and Hips
cases.chiefComplaint: Eight-year-old girl with several weeks of progressive muscle weakness and a facial and knuckle rash.
cases.history: An 8-year-old girl is referred to clinic for about six weeks of progressive weakness and fatigue. Her parents note she now has trouble climbing stairs, getting out of the bath, lifting her backpack, and brushing her hair; she tires quickly during play. Over the same period she developed a reddish-purple discoloration around her eyes and scaly red bumps over her knuckles, elbows, and knees. She has had low-grade intermittent fevers and complains of muscle aching but denies joint swelling. There is no preceding trauma. She is otherwise developmentally normal, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no family history of autoimmune or neuromuscular disease. She has had no difficulty swallowing or breathing so far, and no nasal regurgitation of liquids. Review of systems is notable for photosensitivity of the rash. The clinician notes a symmetric proximal weakness pattern and a distinctive rash and is considering an inflammatory myopathy.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative girl. Skin: violaceous (heliotrope) discoloration of the upper eyelids with mild periorbital edema; erythematous scaly papules over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, elbows, and knees (Gottron papules); dilated nailfold capillaries. Muscle: symmetric proximal weakness—neck flexors, deltoids/shoulder girdle, and hip flexors 4-/5—with preserved distal strength; mild muscle tenderness. She rises from the floor with a partial Gowers maneuver. Deep tendon reflexes normal. Sensation intact. No joint effusions or deformities. No subcutaneous nodules or calcium deposits palpable yet. Lungs clear; no dysphonia or weak cough; palatal elevation symmetric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which pair of findings is most specific for the leading diagnosis?
options: Malar rash and oral ulcers
Heliotrope rash and Gottron papules with symmetric proximal weakness
Distal weakness and a stocking-glove sensory loss
Migratory large-joint arthritis and a heart murmur
Ptosis and fatigable diplopia
stages.explanation: A heliotrope (violaceous periorbital) rash and Gottron papules over extensor joint surfaces, together with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, are highly specific for juvenile dermatomyositis. A malar rash with oral ulcers suggests systemic lupus erythematosus, a different connective tissue disease. Distal weakness with stocking-glove sensory loss indicates a peripheral neuropathy, not an inflammatory myopathy (dermatomyositis spares sensation and is proximal). Migratory arthritis with a murmur points to rheumatic fever. Ptosis with fatigable diplopia describes myasthenia gravis, a neuromuscular junction disorder. The combination of the pathognomonic skin findings and symmetric girdle weakness is the classic juvenile dermatomyositis presentation.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase (CK): 1,850 U/L (elevated)
Aldolase: elevated
AST 96 U/L, ALT 72 U/L (muscle origin)
LDH elevated
ANA: positive (1:320)
Myositis-specific antibody panel: anti-NXP2 positive
ESR 38 mm/hr, CRP mildly elevated
MRI thighs: symmetric muscle edema on STIR (myositis)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which study would most safely support the diagnosis while guiding biopsy if needed?
options: Plain radiographs of the thighs
MRI of the thigh muscles showing symmetric muscle edema
Nerve conduction studies alone
Echocardiography
Bone scan
stages.explanation: MRI of the proximal muscles (e.g., thighs) demonstrates symmetric muscle edema/inflammation, supports the diagnosis noninvasively, and can localize an affected site to guide muscle biopsy if confirmation is required—an approach often allowing children to avoid biopsy or EMG. Plain radiographs do not show muscle inflammation (though they can later reveal calcinosis). Nerve conduction studies assess nerves, not the inflammatory myopathy, and are normal in dermatomyositis. Echocardiography evaluates for cardiac involvement but does not establish the muscle diagnosis. A bone scan is not used to diagnose myositis. Combined with the pathognomonic rash, elevated muscle enzymes, and MRI findings, the diagnosis can frequently be made without invasive testing.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: High-dose corticosteroids, usually with methotrexate
Intravenous immunoglobulin as sole therapy
Pyridostigmine
Topical emollients alone
Antibiotics for presumed infection
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for juvenile dermatomyositis is high-dose corticosteroids combined with a steroid-sparing agent—most often methotrexate—to control inflammation, hasten recovery, and reduce steroid toxicity; IVIG is added for refractory or severe (including significant skin or dysphagia) disease. IVIG alone is not standard initial monotherapy. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis, not an inflammatory myopathy. Topical emollients address only the skin and ignore the muscle disease and systemic inflammation. Antibiotics are inappropriate because this is autoimmune, not infectious. Early, adequate immunosuppression is important to control disease activity and lower the risk of complications such as calcinosis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is particularly associated with delayed or undertreated disease in this condition?
options: Calcinosis cutis
Nephrolithiasis
Optic neuritis
Peripheral neuropathy
Hemolytic anemia
stages.explanation: Calcinosis cutis (dystrophic calcium deposits in skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle) is a characteristic complication of juvenile dermatomyositis, more common with delayed diagnosis or inadequately controlled chronic inflammation, and can cause pain, ulceration, and functional limitation—motivating prompt, aggressive treatment. Nephrolithiasis is not a typical feature. Optic neuritis is a demyelinating/inflammatory CNS event unrelated to this myopathy. Peripheral neuropathy is not part of dermatomyositis, which spares sensation and nerves. Hemolytic anemia is associated with lupus rather than dermatomyositis. Other concerns include dysphagia/aspiration and, rarely, interstitial lung disease, but calcinosis is the classic chronic complication tied to disease control.

cases.diagnosis: Juvenile dermatomyositis
pearls: Heliotrope rash plus Gottron papules with symmetric proximal (girdle) weakness is the classic juvenile dermatomyositis picture; sensation and reflexes are preserved.
Muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase, LDH, AST/ALT of muscle origin) are elevated; MRI shows symmetric muscle edema and can guide or obviate biopsy.
First-line therapy is corticosteroids plus a steroid-sparing agent (commonly methotrexate); IVIG for refractory or severe disease.
Calcinosis cutis is the hallmark chronic complication of delayed or undertreated disease—prompt, adequate immunosuppression helps prevent it.

cases.title: Sudden One-Sided Facial Droop in a 10-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: His face has been drooping on the left side since this morning.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother after she noticed his left face drooping when he woke up. He says food and drink dribble from the left corner of his mouth and his left eye will not close fully, causing tearing and irritation. He reports mild pain behind the left ear that began two days ago and a vague change in how food tastes. He has had no headache, vomiting, fever, limb weakness, slurred speech, or trouble walking. He had a mild cold about a week ago. There was no head trauma. He spends summers at his grandparents' rural home in Connecticut and was outdoors frequently over the past month, though no one recalls a tick bite or rash. PMH is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with both parents and a younger sister, attends 5th grade, and is doing well academically. Family history is negative for stroke, neuromuscular disease, or recurrent facial palsy.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing, in no distress. There is flattening of the left nasolabial fold and drooping of the left corner of the mouth. He cannot wrinkle the left forehead or raise the left eyebrow, and the left eye does not close completely with a visible Bell phenomenon. Smile and cheek puff are asymmetric. Hearing is grossly intact; there is mild hyperacusis on the left. Extraocular movements are full, pupils equal and reactive, and the remainder of the cranial nerves are normal. Strength, tone, reflexes, coordination, and gait are normal in all four limbs. No skin rash, no preauricular vesicles, neck supple.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam finding most reliably distinguishes a peripheral (lower motor neuron) facial palsy from a central (upper motor neuron) lesion?
options: Drooping of the corner of the mouth on the affected side
Inability to wrinkle the forehead on the affected side
Flattening of the nasolabial fold
Asymmetry of the smile
stages.explanation: Inability to wrinkle the forehead is the key sign of a peripheral (LMN) palsy. The upper face receives bilateral cortical input, so a central (UMN) lesion spares the forehead, allowing the patient to wrinkle it; a peripheral CN VII lesion paralyzes the entire hemiface including the forehead. Mouth drooping, nasolabial flattening, and smile asymmetry all occur in BOTH central and peripheral palsies because the lower face is predominantly contralaterally innervated and is affected either way, so none of these localize the lesion. The presence of forehead involvement plus ear pain, hyperacusis, and taste change all point to a peripheral CN VII process.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the rural Connecticut summer exposure, which additional step is most appropriate before settling on idiopathic Bell palsy?
options: Obtain a non-contrast CT head to exclude stroke
Send Lyme (Borrelia burgdorferi) serology and ask carefully about rash and tick exposure
Order a stat MRI brain with contrast
Begin empiric IV acyclovir for presumed herpes zoster
stages.explanation: In Lyme-endemic regions, facial nerve palsy is a classic manifestation of early disseminated Lyme disease and is often bilateral; serology (ELISA with reflex Western blot) plus a careful exposure/rash history is the right next step because confirming Lyme changes treatment to oral doxycycline/amoxicillin. A CT head has poor sensitivity for the posterior fossa and is unnecessary in an isolated peripheral palsy with a normal neuro exam. Routine MRI is not required for typical Bell palsy and would delay care. Empiric acyclovir is inappropriate without vesicles (no Ramsay Hunt) and does not address the leading endemic alternative diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Lyme C6 EIA: negative
Western blot: not performed (screen negative)
CBC: within normal limits
Fingerstick glucose: 92 mg/dL
Otoscopy: no vesicles, tympanic membranes normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With Lyme serology negative and a confident diagnosis of idiopathic Bell palsy, what is the most appropriate management?
options: A 10-day course of oral prednisolone plus aggressive eye protection
Observation alone with no medication
Oral doxycycline for 14 days
Surgical facial nerve decompression
stages.explanation: Early oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone ~1 mg/kg/day for 7-10 days), started within 72 hours of onset, improve the likelihood of complete recovery and are the mainstay of Bell palsy therapy; meticulous eye protection (artificial tears, lubricating ointment, taping the lid at night) is critical because the eye cannot fully close. Observation alone misses the benefit of timely steroids. Doxycycline treats Lyme, which was ruled out here. Surgical decompression is not indicated for typical Bell palsy and carries significant risk. Antivirals add little for most cases and are not first-line monotherapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What should you tell the family about prognosis and follow-up?
options: Most children recover poorly and will likely have permanent paralysis
Recovery is usually good, often beginning within 2-3 weeks; return promptly if eye pain, new weakness, or other deficits develop
He must be admitted for IV steroids and continuous monitoring
Recurrence is inevitable and warrants prophylactic daily steroids
stages.explanation: The prognosis of childhood Bell palsy is excellent, with the majority recovering completely, typically with improvement starting in 2-3 weeks and continuing over weeks to months; clear return precautions (eye pain or corneal injury, new or progressive deficits suggesting another process) are essential. Predicting permanent paralysis is inaccurate and needlessly alarming. Admission for IV steroids is unnecessary for an uncomplicated, ambulatory case treated orally. Recurrence is uncommon, and chronic prophylactic steroids are not indicated and would cause harm.

cases.diagnosis: Bell palsy (idiopathic peripheral facial nerve palsy)
pearls: Forehead involvement (cannot wrinkle the brow) marks a peripheral CN VII palsy; forehead sparing implies a central lesion.
In Lyme-endemic areas, always consider Borrelia facial palsy — it is frequently bilateral and treated with antibiotics, not steroids.
Start oral corticosteroids within 72 hours and protect the cornea relentlessly, since incomplete eye closure risks exposure keratopathy.
Preauricular/ear-canal vesicles suggest Ramsay Hunt syndrome (zoster), which warrants antivirals and carries a worse prognosis.

cases.title: A Newborn Who Won't Move One Arm
cases.chiefComplaint: The nurse notes the baby is not moving her right arm during routine care.
cases.history: A 1-day-old female newborn is evaluated in the nursery after staff noticed she keeps her right arm still while moving the left arm and both legs vigorously. She was born at 39 weeks to a 31-year-old G3P3 mother with gestational diabetes. Estimated birth weight is 4,450 g (macrosomic). Delivery was vaginal and complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring the McRoberts maneuver and suprapubic pressure; Apgars were 7 and 9. There was no maternal fever, no prolonged rupture of membranes, and no instrumentation to the head. The infant is feeding well and is otherwise vigorous and pink. Maternal pregnancy was notable for diet-controlled diabetes and appropriate prenatal care. Newborn screen and hearing screen are pending. There is no family history of neuromuscular disease or bleeding disorders. The mother is concerned because the arm 'just hangs there' and asks whether her daughter has had a stroke.
cases.exam: Vigorous, well-perfused term infant. The right arm lies adducted and internally rotated at the shoulder, extended at the elbow, with the forearm pronated and the wrist flexed — the classic 'waiter's tip' posture. There is decreased spontaneous movement of the right upper arm; the asymmetric (right) Moro is absent on that side. The grasp reflex is intact in the right hand, and finger movements are present. Both legs move symmetrically with normal tone. There is no clavicular crepitus or obvious deformity, and no respiratory distress. Pupils equal; no ptosis or miosis. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Hips stable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The 'waiter's tip' posture (arm adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, forearm pronated, wrist flexed) localizes the injury to which nerve roots?
options: C8-T1 (lower plexus)
C5-C6 (upper plexus)
The entire brachial plexus (C5-T1)
The radial nerve only
stages.explanation: The waiter's tip posture reflects an upper brachial plexus injury at C5-C6 (Erb palsy): loss of shoulder abductors/external rotators and elbow flexors and forearm supinators yields the characteristic adducted, internally rotated, pronated, wrist-flexed arm with preserved hand grasp. C8-T1 injury (that option, Klumpke palsy) produces a 'claw hand' with intrinsic hand weakness and may include Horner syndrome, the opposite distribution. A total plexus injury would render the whole limb flaccid including the hand, which is not the case here since grasp is intact. An isolated radial nerve lesion causes wrist drop without this global shoulder/elbow pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
