stages.question: Which delivery factor in this case is the strongest risk factor for this injury?
options: Maternal gestational diabetes alone
Macrosomia with shoulder dystocia
The McRoberts maneuver
Vaginal (non-instrumented) delivery
stages.explanation: Fetal macrosomia leading to shoulder dystocia is the classic and strongest risk factor for neonatal brachial plexus palsy, as lateral traction and stretch on the neck during delivery of the impacted shoulder injures the upper roots. Gestational diabetes contributes only indirectly by causing macrosomia; it is a risk factor for the dystocia, not the direct mechanism. The McRoberts maneuver is a corrective maneuver used to relieve dystocia, not a cause of the palsy. Uncomplicated vaginal delivery is not itself a risk factor; it is the shoulder dystocia and associated traction that matter.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the intact hand grasp, normal pupils, and no respiratory distress, what does the exam help you exclude, and what is the appropriate initial workup?
options: Excludes a fracture; obtain urgent MRI brachial plexus now
No additional concern needed; discharge with no imaging or exam
Reassuring against Horner syndrome and phrenic nerve (C3-C5) involvement; obtain a clavicle/humerus X-ray to evaluate for associated fracture
Suggests Klumpke palsy; order nerve conduction studies today
stages.explanation: Normal pupils argue against an associated Horner syndrome (lower-trunk/T1 sympathetic involvement), and the absence of respiratory distress argues against phrenic nerve (C3-C5) injury causing diaphragmatic paralysis — both important comorbidities to assess in plexus injuries. A plain X-ray of the clavicle and humerus is appropriate because fractures commonly accompany shoulder dystocia and can mimic or coexist with the palsy. Urgent MRI is not first-line in the newborn period. Discharging without an exam or imaging is unsafe. This is an upper-trunk pattern, not Klumpke, and early NCS is not the initial step.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Clavicle/humerus X-ray: no fracture identified
Chest X-ray: both hemidiaphragms at normal level, no elevation
Glucose: 58 mg/dL
No respiratory distress on continued observation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate initial management plan for this C5-C6 palsy?
options: Immediate surgical nerve grafting before discharge
Rigid immobilization of the arm in a sling for 6 weeks
Gentle range-of-motion physical/occupational therapy and observation, with neurology/plexus referral if no recovery by 1 month
Botulinum toxin injection to the deltoid
stages.explanation: Most neonatal brachial plexus palsies are stretch (neurapraxia) injuries that recover spontaneously, so initial management is supportive: gentle passive range-of-motion exercises (often started after the first 1-2 weeks) to prevent contractures, parental education, and close follow-up; persistent deficits beyond about 3-6 months prompt referral to a specialized brachial plexus team for possible microsurgical repair. Immediate surgery is not done in the newborn — recovery is awaited. Rigid prolonged immobilization promotes contractures and is harmful. Botulinum toxin is not a primary therapy and may be considered only later for co-contraction in selected cases.

cases.diagnosis: Neonatal brachial plexus palsy — Erb palsy (C5-C6 upper trunk injury)
pearls: Erb palsy = C5-C6 'waiter's tip' arm with preserved hand grasp; Klumpke palsy = C8-T1 'claw hand' often with Horner syndrome.
Macrosomia with shoulder dystocia is the dominant risk factor; check for associated clavicle/humerus fracture.
Always assess for Horner syndrome (T1) and diaphragmatic paralysis (phrenic C3-C5) as serious comorbidities.
Most cases recover spontaneously with ROM therapy; refer to a plexus surgical team if no recovery by 3-6 months.

cases.title: A 2-Year-Old Who Walks on His Toes and Isn't Keeping Up
cases.chiefComplaint: He started walking late, walks on his toes, and his legs seem stiff.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic for evaluation of delayed walking. He took his first independent steps only recently and walks on his toes with his knees turning inward. His parents note his legs feel 'tight' when they dress him and that he frequently crosses his legs. He was born at 28 weeks' gestation, weighing 1,050 g, and spent 9 weeks in the NICU complicated by respiratory distress syndrome requiring ventilation and a grade II intraventricular hemorrhage; a head ultrasound near discharge raised concern for periventricular changes. He sat at 11 months and crawled late. Fine motor and language are appropriate for age, and he is socially engaged and playful. He has no history of seizures, no loss of skills, and no episodes of regression. He takes a daily multivitamin and has no allergies. He lives with both parents; immunizations are current. Family history is unremarkable, with no consanguinity, no neuromuscular disease, and no relatives with similar gait problems. Parents specifically ask whether his condition will worsen over time.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive, age-appropriate language and play. Lower extremities show increased tone with a catch on rapid passive movement (spasticity), most pronounced in the hip adductors and gastrocnemius. When held upright, his legs scissor and he bears weight on his toes with limited ankle dorsiflexion. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ at the patellae and ankles with sustained ankle clonus and bilateral upgoing toes. Upper-extremity tone, strength, and reflexes are normal, and fine motor manipulation is good. He has no facial weakness, no fasciculations, and no muscle tenderness. There is no truncal ataxia. Head circumference is at the 25th percentile and tracking along its curve.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of leg spasticity, scissoring, brisk reflexes, clonus, and upgoing toes localizes the problem to which level of the nervous system?
options: Lower motor neuron (anterior horn cell or peripheral nerve)
Neuromuscular junction
Upper motor neuron (corticospinal tract)
Muscle (primary myopathy)
stages.explanation: Hypertonia, hyperreflexia, clonus, scissoring, and extensor plantar responses are all upper motor neuron (corticospinal tract) signs, indicating a central, supraspinal lesion affecting the leg fibers. Lower motor neuron disease produces hypotonia, weakness, hyporeflexia, and fasciculations — the opposite pattern. Neuromuscular junction disorders cause fatigable weakness with normal or reduced reflexes, not spasticity. A primary myopathy causes weakness and often hypotonia with normal or diminished reflexes, not the brisk reflexes and clonus seen here. The predominance in the legs over the arms is the classic 'diplegic' pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding would you most expect on brain MRI in this former 28-week preemie?
options: Periventricular leukomalacia (white-matter injury near the lateral ventricles)
Bilateral basal ganglia and thalamic injury
A progressive demyelinating leukodystrophy pattern
A space-occupying posterior fossa tumor
stages.explanation: Periventricular leukomalacia (PVL) — ischemic injury to the periventricular white matter — is the characteristic lesion in premature infants and preferentially damages the corticospinal fibers serving the legs (which pass nearest the ventricles), producing spastic diplegia. Basal ganglia/thalamic injury is more typical of term hypoxic-ischemic injury or kernicterus and tends to cause dyskinetic CP. A progressive leukodystrophy would imply ongoing deterioration and is inconsistent with the static history here. A posterior fossa tumor would cause ataxia, raised ICP, or cranial nerve signs, not isolated spastic diplegia, and is not linked to prematurity.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: bilateral periventricular white-matter volume loss and gliosis adjacent to the trigones of the lateral ventricles; ventricles mildly enlarged with irregular margins
No basal ganglia signal abnormality
No mass, no acute infarct, no demyelination suggesting an active leukodystrophy
Myelination otherwise appropriate for age

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents ask whether his condition will get worse. What is the most accurate counseling about the natural history?
options: It is a progressive, degenerative disease that will steadily worsen
The underlying brain injury is static and non-progressive, though the functional picture can change with growth; ongoing therapy and monitoring are key
He will fully outgrow all symptoms by school age without intervention
Symptoms indicate an evolving metabolic disorder requiring genetic testing first
stages.explanation: Cerebral palsy is defined by a non-progressive (static) injury to the developing brain; the lesion itself does not worsen, although the clinical manifestations can evolve as the child grows (e.g., contractures, changing gait) and orthopedic complications can develop. Calling it progressive/degenerative is incorrect and describes a different category of disease. Expecting complete resolution without intervention is overly optimistic and false. A static MRI consistent with PVL in a documented preemie does not point to an evolving metabolic disorder; regression or atypical features would prompt that workup, but they are absent here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management approach for his spastic diplegia?
options: Immediate selective dorsal rhizotomy
A multidisciplinary program centered on physical and occupational therapy, with orthotics (AFOs) and developmental support
High-dose oral corticosteroids to halt progression
Lifelong bracing of the legs in extension with no therapy
stages.explanation: First-line care for spastic CP is multidisciplinary and conservative: regular physical and occupational therapy to maximize function and prevent contractures, ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) to address toe-walking and improve gait, and developmental/early-intervention services; focal spasticity may be treated with botulinum toxin and, in selected refractory cases, oral agents or surgery. Selective dorsal rhizotomy is reserved for carefully selected children after conservative measures, not as the immediate first step. Corticosteroids have no role — the lesion is static, not inflammatory. Rigid immobilization without active therapy promotes contractures and is harmful.

cases.diagnosis: Spastic diplegic cerebral palsy due to periventricular leukomalacia of prematurity
pearls: Spastic diplegia (legs >> arms) in a former preemie is the classic phenotype of periventricular leukomalacia.
PVL injures the periventricular corticospinal fibers serving the legs because they run closest to the lateral ventricles.
Cerebral palsy is a STATIC encephalopathy; true regression or worsening should trigger a search for an alternative diagnosis.
First-line management is multidisciplinary therapy plus orthotics; reserve botulinum toxin, oral agents, and rhizotomy for refractory spasticity.

cases.title: Pale Skin Patches and Cheek Bumps in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Her parents are worried about light patches on her skin and small bumps on her face.
cases.history: An 18-month-old girl is brought to clinic because her parents noticed several pale, oval patches on her trunk and limbs that were present from early infancy, and more recently a cluster of small reddish bumps across her cheeks and nose. She is developing appropriately — walking, using several words, and interactive. She has had no seizures, no episodes of staring or 'jackknife' spasms, and no developmental regression. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated; a fetal echocardiogram done for a heart murmur late in pregnancy reportedly showed a small cardiac mass, but follow-up was incomplete after the family moved. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with both parents and is up to date on vaccines. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with epilepsy and 'kidney problems,' and the mother recalls being told as a child that she had 'white spots' on her skin but was never formally evaluated. Parents bring photos of the skin findings taken in dim light.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, developmentally on track. Skin shows five well-defined hypopigmented (ash-leaf-shaped) macules on the trunk and lower extremities, more conspicuous under Wood lamp. Across the malar region and nasolabial folds are multiple small, firm, pink-red papules (angiofibromas). There is a slightly raised, leathery plaque over the lumbar back (shagreen patch). Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic murmur without signs of obstruction or failure. Neurologic exam is normal for age: good tone, symmetric reflexes, normal eye movements, and no focal deficits. Abdomen is soft without palpable masses. No retinal abnormality is grossly appreciated on red reflex.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Hypopigmented (ash-leaf) macules best visualized under a Wood lamp, facial angiofibromas, and a shagreen patch together most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Sturge-Weber syndrome
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Neurofibromatosis type 1
Vitiligo with incidental acne
stages.explanation: Ash-leaf hypopigmented macules (Wood-lamp enhanced), facial angiofibromas, and a shagreen patch are three classic cutaneous criteria of tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC); combined with a cardiac mass and family history, TSC is the clear unifying diagnosis. Sturge-Weber features a port-wine (capillary malformation) stain in the V1 trigeminal distribution and leptomeningeal angiomatosis, not hypopigmented macules. NF1 is defined by café-au-lait macules (hyperpigmented), neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules — not ash-leaf spots or angiofibromas. Vitiligo plus acne does not explain the angiofibromas, shagreen patch, or the cardiac mass and ignores the syndromic pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The prenatal report of a cardiac mass most likely represents which TSC-associated tumor, and what is its typical course?
options: Atrial myxoma that requires urgent surgical removal
Cardiac rhabdomyoma, which often regresses spontaneously over time
Rhabdomyosarcoma requiring chemotherapy
Bacterial endocarditis vegetation needing antibiotics
stages.explanation: Cardiac rhabdomyomas are the characteristic cardiac tumor of TSC, are frequently multiple, are often detected prenatally, and characteristically regress spontaneously during infancy and childhood; most are asymptomatic and managed with observation unless they cause obstruction or arrhythmia. Atrial myxoma is associated with Carney complex, not TSC, and is not the typical finding. Rhabdomyosarcoma is a malignant soft-tissue sarcoma unrelated to this presentation. An endocarditis vegetation would present with fever and an infectious picture, which is absent. Recognizing the rhabdomyoma supports the TSC diagnosis and reassures the family about its usually benign, regressing course.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Genetic testing: pathogenic TSC2 variant identified
Brain MRI: multiple cortical tubers and subependymal nodules; no subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA) at this time
Renal ultrasound: two small angiomyolipomas in the right kidney
Echocardiogram: small, non-obstructing ventricular rhabdomyoma, decreased in size from prior
EEG: no epileptiform activity

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed TSC, which ongoing surveillance is most important to detect a treatable, potentially life-threatening complication?
options: Annual skin biopsies of the angiofibromas
Serial brain MRI to monitor for a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA), plus periodic renal imaging for growing angiomyolipomas
Yearly bone marrow biopsy
Monthly serum calcium measurements
stages.explanation: Two of the most important treatable, potentially serious TSC complications are SEGA — which can grow near the foramen of Monro and cause obstructive hydrocephalus — and enlarging renal angiomyolipomas, which can hemorrhage; both are tracked with serial imaging (periodic brain MRI and renal imaging), and mTOR inhibitors (e.g., everolimus) can treat SEGA and AMLs. Skin biopsies are unnecessary since the lesions are clinically diagnostic and benign. Bone marrow biopsy has no role in TSC surveillance. Routine monthly calcium checks are not part of TSC monitoring. Structured neuro-renal imaging surveillance is the standard of care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because this child currently has no seizures, what additional neurologic surveillance is recommended in infants/young children with TSC?
options: No EEG monitoring is needed unless a seizure is witnessed
Periodic surveillance EEG, with caregiver education on recognizing seizures including infantile (epileptic) spasms
Start prophylactic phenobarbital in all TSC children
Begin lifelong vigabatrin regardless of EEG findings
stages.explanation: TSC carries a high risk of epilepsy, especially infantile spasms in the first year; current guidance favors periodic surveillance EEG in young children and thorough caregiver education to recognize spasms early, since prompt treatment (vigabatrin is first-line for TSC-related infantile spasms) improves developmental outcomes. Waiting for an obvious clinical seizure can delay treatment of subtle spasms. Prophylactic phenobarbital for all is not standard and has cognitive downsides. Starting vigabatrin in every child regardless of EEG overtreats and exposes patients to retinal toxicity without indication; therapy is guided by EEG/clinical findings.

cases.diagnosis: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC2)
pearls: Ash-leaf macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patches, and cardiac rhabdomyomas are core TSC features; rhabdomyomas usually regress spontaneously.
Surveillance centers on serial brain MRI for SEGA (foramen of Monro obstruction) and renal imaging for hemorrhage-prone angiomyolipomas.
Epilepsy — especially infantile spasms — is common; surveillance EEG and caregiver education enable early treatment, with vigabatrin first-line for TSC spasms.
mTOR inhibitors (e.g., everolimus) can treat SEGA and angiomyolipomas, reflecting TSC's mTOR-pathway pathophysiology.

cases.title: Lots of Brown Birthmarks and Trouble in School
cases.chiefComplaint: His teacher is concerned about learning difficulties, and his parents note many brown spots on his skin.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy presents for evaluation after his first-grade teacher reported he is struggling to keep up with reading and has trouble paying attention. His parents have also noticed numerous brown birthmarks across his body that have increased in number and size since infancy, plus freckling in his armpits and groin. He has met motor milestones normally and is physically active. There is no history of seizures, headaches, vision complaints, or weakness. He has not had vision screening recently. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with his mother, father, and an older sister; immunizations are current. Family history is significant: his father has 'lots of the same brown spots' and a few soft skin bumps and was told he has a 'genetic skin condition' but never had formal testing. There is no family history of early stroke or cancer. The parents primarily want to know what the brown spots mean and whether the learning issues are related.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cooperative boy. Skin shows six café-au-lait macules, each greater than 5 mm, on the trunk and limbs, plus freckling in both axillae and the inguinal folds. There are no cutaneous neurofibromas yet. Slit-lamp examination by ophthalmology identifies two Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas) in the right eye. Visual acuity, color vision, and visual fields are grossly normal, and the optic discs appear normal on funduscopy. There are no long-bone deformities or scoliosis. Neurologic exam is nonfocal: normal tone, strength, reflexes, coordination, and gait. Head circumference is at the 90th percentile. No abdominal masses or hypertension findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Using the NIH/diagnostic criteria for neurofibromatosis type 1, which combination in this child satisfies the diagnosis?
options: A single café-au-lait macule plus a positive family history
Six or more café-au-lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal), axillary/inguinal freckling, and Lisch nodules
Two neurofibromas and one optic glioma
Café-au-lait macules alone, with no other features
stages.explanation: NF1 requires two or more criteria; this child clearly meets at least three: six or more café-au-lait macules over 5 mm (prepubertal threshold), axillary and inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), and two or more Lisch nodules — a definitive clinical diagnosis, further supported by an affected first-degree relative (his father). A single café-au-lait macule with family history does not meet the two-criteria requirement on skin alone. Two neurofibromas plus an optic glioma would also qualify but are not what this child has. Café-au-lait macules alone without a second feature are insufficient for diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The father's similar café-au-lait macules and skin bumps point to which inheritance pattern, and what does it imply for genetic counseling?
options: Autosomal recessive; recurrence risk only if both parents are carriers
Autosomal dominant; each child of an affected parent has a 50% risk, with variable expressivity
X-linked recessive; only males are affected
Mitochondrial; inherited only from the mother
stages.explanation: NF1 is autosomal dominant (NF1 gene, chromosome 17, encoding neurofibromin), so each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting it; expressivity is highly variable even within a family, while penetrance is essentially complete by adulthood. About half of cases arise from new mutations, but here the affected father indicates familial inheritance. Autosomal recessive is incorrect — a single affected parent transmits the disease. X-linked recessive is wrong; NF1 affects both sexes and the father transmits it. Mitochondrial inheritance is maternal-only and does not fit a paternally transmitted, chromosome-17 disorder.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Ophthalmology: visual acuity and fields normal; optic discs normal; 2 Lisch nodules right iris
Blood pressure: normal for age and height
Genetic testing: pathogenic NF1 variant confirmed
Developmental/educational screen: deficits in reading fluency and sustained attention
No bony dysplasia on skeletal review

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding the learning difficulties, what is the most accurate counseling for this family?
options: Learning disabilities and ADHD are common in NF1; neuropsychological evaluation and school accommodations (e.g., an IEP/504 plan) are recommended
Cognitive problems in NF1 always indicate an underlying brain tumor
Learning issues are unrelated to NF1 and need no further attention
Intellectual disability is universal in NF1 and the prognosis is poor
stages.explanation: Learning disabilities, attention deficits, and executive-function difficulties are among the most common neurologic manifestations of NF1, present in a large fraction of children even with normal overall intelligence; the right step is formal neuropsychological evaluation and securing school supports (IEP/504 accommodations) to help reading and attention. Assuming a brain tumor is causing every cognitive issue is incorrect; cognitive difficulty is usually intrinsic to NF1, not tumor-driven. Dismissing the link entirely misses a well-established association. Universal intellectual disability with poor prognosis is false — most children have normal IQ with specific learning differences.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What routine surveillance is most important in a young child with NF1?
options: Annual whole-body MRI for all patients regardless of symptoms
Yearly ophthalmologic exam (and clinical monitoring) for optic pathway glioma, plus blood pressure checks and growth/development monitoring
Routine screening colonoscopy
Quarterly skin biopsies of café-au-lait macules
stages.explanation: Standard NF1 surveillance in children includes annual ophthalmologic examination (to detect symptomatic optic pathway gliomas, most likely to appear in early childhood), regular blood-pressure monitoring (for hypertension from renal artery stenosis or pheochromocytoma), and ongoing assessment of growth, development, skeletal status, and skin/neurologic findings. Whole-body MRI for everyone is not routine and is reserved for specific concerns. Screening colonoscopy is not part of pediatric NF1 care. Repeated skin biopsies are unnecessary because café-au-lait macules are clinically diagnostic and benign. Symptom-driven imaging is added as indicated.

cases.diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 1
pearls: NF1 needs ≥2 criteria: ≥6 café-au-lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal), axillary/inguinal freckling, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, optic glioma, bony dysplasia, or an affected first-degree relative.
NF1 is autosomal dominant (chromosome 17, neurofibromin) with variable expressivity; ~50% of cases are new mutations.
Learning disabilities and ADHD are the most common neurologic features — pursue neuropsych testing and school accommodations.
Surveillance includes annual eye exams for optic pathway glioma and blood-pressure checks for renovascular hypertension/pheochromocytoma.

cases.title: A 3-Week-Old With a Strange Blistering Rash and Twitching
cases.chiefComplaint: The baby has a streaky blistering rash and episodes of rhythmic limb jerking.
cases.history: A 3-week-old girl is admitted to the pediatric ward after her parents noticed clusters of fluid-filled blisters arranged in lines down her trunk and limbs that have been present since the first days of life, now becoming wart-like in places. Today she had two episodes of rhythmic jerking of the right arm and leg lasting about a minute each, with eye deviation. She was born full-term to a 29-year-old G3P1 mother whose obstetric history includes two prior miscarriages of male fetuses. Birth was uncomplicated; the baby has been feeding adequately. There is no fever and no sick contacts. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. Family history is striking: the mother has subtle 'streaky' areas of skin discoloration on her legs, a few missing/peg-shaped teeth, and patchy scalp hair, and reports that her own mother also lost male pregnancies. Maternal eye exam was reportedly abnormal in childhood. The parents are anxious about whether the rash and seizures are infectious.
cases.exam: Alert between events, normocephalic, anterior fontanelle soft. Skin shows linear vesicles and verrucous (wart-like) papules distributed along curvilinear lines (following the lines of Blaschko) on the trunk and extremities, with some crusting; palms and soles relatively spared. During an observed event, there is rhythmic clonic jerking of the right face and arm with preserved breathing, resolving spontaneously. Pupils equal and reactive; red reflex present bilaterally without obvious retinal lesion on bedside exam. Tone is mildly increased on the right after the event. No hepatosplenomegaly, no oral thrush. Sucking reflex present. Cardiac and respiratory exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A blistering rash that follows curvilinear lines on the trunk and limbs is described as following which pattern, and what does it imply about the underlying process?
options: A dermatomal pattern, implying herpes zoster reactivation
Lines of Blaschko, implying a mosaic (clonal embryonic skin migration) genetic process
A photodistributed pattern, implying a phototoxic drug reaction
A flexural pattern, implying atopic dermatitis
stages.explanation: Lesions arrayed along the lines of Blaschko reflect the migration paths of clonal skin cells during embryogenesis and are the signature of mosaic/X-linked genetic skin disease — here, incontinentia pigmenti. A dermatomal distribution follows a single nerve root and suggests zoster, not these whorled curvilinear lines, and neonatal zoster would be unusual without maternal varicella. A photodistributed pattern spares covered areas and relates to light exposure, irrelevant in a 3-week-old. A flexural eczematous pattern describes atopic dermatitis, which is not vesicular/verrucous and does not follow Blaschko lines. Recognizing Blaschkoid distribution is the diagnostic pivot.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Combining the Blaschkoid vesicular-then-verrucous rash, neonatal seizures, and a maternal history of dental/skin findings plus recurrent male miscarriages, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Neonatal herpes simplex encephalitis
Incontinentia pigmenti (X-linked dominant, IKBKG/NEMO)
Epidermolysis bullosa
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
stages.explanation: Incontinentia pigmenti (IP) is an X-linked dominant disorder of the IKBKG/NEMO gene that is typically lethal in males in utero — explaining the recurrent male miscarriages — and presents in affected females with skin lesions evolving through vesicular, verrucous, hyperpigmented (whorled), and atrophic stages along Blaschko lines, plus dental anomalies, alopecia, ocular and CNS involvement (seizures). Neonatal HSV can cause vesicles and seizures but would not follow Blaschko lines, would not explain the maternal phenotype, and lacks the verrucous evolution and family pattern. Epidermolysis bullosa causes trauma-induced blisters without Blaschkoid distribution or the maternal/genetic pattern. Staph scalded skin is a toxin-mediated peeling, not linear vesicles with this inheritance.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 16.5 ×10³/µL with marked eosinophilia (eosinophils 28%)
Skin biopsy: eosinophilic spongiosis with intraepidermal vesicles and dyskeratotic keratinocytes
HSV PCR (skin/CSF): negative
MRI brain: scattered cortical/subcortical and periventricular areas of restricted diffusion consistent with small ischemic/hemorrhagic foci
EEG: focal sharp waves over the left hemisphere
Genetic testing: IKBKG (NEMO) deletion confirmed

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the confirmed diagnosis and active focal seizures with abnormal MRI/EEG, what is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start IV acyclovir and treat as neonatal HSV
Initiate an antiseizure medication for the neonatal seizures and arrange multidisciplinary care including urgent ophthalmology evaluation
Begin systemic antibiotics for presumed bacterial sepsis only
Provide topical steroids to the rash and discharge home
stages.explanation: Active, recurrent focal neonatal seizures with an abnormal MRI and EEG require prompt antiseizure treatment (e.g., phenobarbital/levetiracetam per neonatal protocols), alongside coordinated multidisciplinary care — and urgent ophthalmology is critical because IP can cause a proliferative retinopathy (similar to ROP) that may lead to retinal detachment and blindness if not detected and treated early. Treating as HSV is wrong given negative HSV PCR and a confirmed genetic diagnosis. Antibiotics alone do not address the seizures or the underlying disorder. Topical steroids with discharge dangerously ignores the seizures and the sight- and life-relevant systemic surveillance the child needs.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which non-cutaneous complication of incontinentia pigmenti most urgently requires early specialist screening to prevent permanent loss of function?
options: Proliferative retinal vasculopathy that can cause retinal detachment and blindness
Pigmentary skin streaks that fade with age
Peg-shaped teeth requiring later orthodontics
Patchy scarring alopecia of the vertex
stages.explanation: The most sight-threatening complication of IP is a proliferative retinal vasculopathy with abnormal neovascularization that can progress to retinal detachment and irreversible blindness; early and serial ophthalmologic screening with prompt laser treatment of avascular/neovascular retina prevents catastrophic vision loss, paralleling ROP management. The whorled hyperpigmented streaks are characteristic but benign and fade over time. Dental anomalies are common and important but addressed electively, not urgently. Scarring alopecia is a recognized feature but does not threaten function in the way the retinopathy does. CNS and ocular involvement drive the morbidity that demands early action.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: How should you counsel the family about inheritance and recurrence risk?
options: It is autosomal recessive; future children are affected only if the father is also a carrier

It is X-linked dominant and usually lethal in males in utero; affected females have a 50% chance of transmitting the mutation, with affected pregnancies skewed toward surviving daughters and male loss
It is sporadic with negligible recurrence risk
It is mitochondrial and affects all offspring equally
stages.explanation: Incontinentia pigmenti is X-linked dominant and typically lethal prenatally in hemizygous males, which explains the recurrent male miscarriages in this family. An affected (heterozygous) mother transmits the mutant X to 50% of offspring; affected male conceptions are usually lost, so surviving children skew toward unaffected sons and affected daughters, and the abnormal X-inactivation pattern shapes severity. Autosomal recessive inheritance is incorrect and would not produce selective male loss. Calling it sporadic with negligible risk ignores the clear maternal transmission. Mitochondrial inheritance would affect all offspring of an affected mother and does not match the X-linked, male-lethal pattern.

cases.diagnosis: Incontinentia pigmenti with neurologic involvement (IKBKG/NEMO mutation)
pearls: Blaschkoid vesicular → verrucous → hyperpigmented → atrophic skin stages with neonatal seizures and blood eosinophilia point to incontinentia pigmenti.
IP is X-linked dominant, typically lethal in males in utero — recurrent male miscarriages are a major clue.
Urgent ophthalmology screening is essential: a proliferative retinopathy can cause retinal detachment and blindness.
CNS involvement (seizures, ischemic/hemorrhagic foci) drives neurologic morbidity; treat seizures and coordinate multidisciplinary care.

cases.title: A Large Red Birthmark Covering Half a Baby's Face
cases.chiefComplaint: Her parents are worried about a large red mark that has grown over much of one side of her face.
cases.history: A 4-month-old girl is brought to clinic because a flat red patch noted at birth on the left side of her face has rapidly grown into a large, raised, bright-red plaque covering the forehead, scalp, cheek, and chin over the past two months. She feeds well and is gaining weight. Parents report no breathing difficulty, no feeding obstruction, and no episodes of weakness, abnormal eye movements, or seizures, though they mention she occasionally seems 'jittery.' She was born full-term by uncomplicated vaginal delivery; the pregnancy was unremarkable. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date for age. She lives with both parents and an older brother. Family history is negative for vascular malformations, stroke in childhood, or congenital heart disease. The parents have been told by relatives that the mark is 'just a strawberry birthmark that will go away,' but the pediatrician was concerned by its very large size and segmental distribution over the face and referred her for further evaluation.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, alert infant. A large, bright-red, plaque-like vascular lesion (>5 cm) occupies a segmental distribution over the left frontotemporal scalp, forehead, upper eyelid margin, cheek, and chin. The lesion is warm and compressible without bruit appreciated. Eyes: extraocular movements full, pupils equal, no proptosis; the upper-eyelid component does not currently occlude the visual axis. Oral and airway exam shows no subglottic/airway lesion or stridor. Cardiac exam normal with no murmur. Neurologic exam is nonfocal: normal tone, symmetric movements, normal primitive reflexes, no focal weakness. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. The remainder of the skin shows no other large hemangiomas.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A large (>5 cm) SEGMENTAL facial infantile hemangioma — as opposed to a small focal one — should raise concern for which associated condition?
options: Sturge-Weber syndrome
PHACE syndrome (posterior fossa malformations, hemangioma, arterial anomalies, cardiac/aortic defects, eye anomalies)
Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon only
An entirely benign hemangioma needing no workup
stages.explanation: A large, segmental facial infantile hemangioma is the hallmark cutaneous flag for PHACE syndrome — Posterior fossa malformations, Hemangioma, Arterial (cerebrovascular) anomalies, Cardiac defects/aortic coarctation, and Eye anomalies — which warrants brain/neck MRI-MRA, echocardiogram, and eye exam. Sturge-Weber involves a capillary malformation (port-wine stain), not a proliferating hemangioma, in the V1 distribution with leptomeningeal angioma. Kasabach-Merritt phenomenon is a consumptive coagulopathy seen with kaposiform hemangioendothelioma/tufted angioma, not typical infantile hemangiomas. Assuming it is entirely benign ignores the size/segmental red flags that mandate evaluation for vascular brain and cardiac anomalies.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging workup given the concern for PHACE syndrome?
options: Non-contrast head CT only
MRI of the brain with MRA of the head and neck, plus echocardiography and ophthalmologic evaluation
Skull radiographs
Carotid ultrasound alone
stages.explanation: PHACE evaluation requires MRI of the brain (to assess for posterior fossa malformations such as Dandy-Walker) with MRA of the head and neck (to detect arterial anomalies — dysplasia, stenosis, aberrant origins — that confer stroke risk), an echocardiogram (with aortic arch assessment for coarctation/aberrant subclavian), and a dilated eye exam. Non-contrast CT cannot adequately characterize posterior fossa structures or the cerebral vasculature. Skull radiographs provide no relevant vascular or parenchymal information. Carotid ultrasound alone is insufficient because it cannot evaluate the intracranial circulation, posterior fossa, heart, or eyes that define the syndrome's organ involvement.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: hypoplasia of the left cerebellar hemisphere (posterior fossa malformation)
MRA head/neck: dysplasia and segmental narrowing of the left internal carotid artery with an aberrant course
Echocardiogram: coarctation of the aorta, mild
Ophthalmology: no posterior segment abnormality; visual axis currently clear
MRI: no acute infarct at this time

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The hemangioma is large and involves the periocular region; systemic treatment is being considered. What is the first-line therapy for a complicated infantile hemangioma, and what caveat applies given the MRA findings?
options: Oral propranolol, but with cardiology/neurology input and caution because cerebral arterial anomalies and coarctation raise concern about blood-pressure/perfusion effects
High-dose systemic corticosteroids as the clear first choice
Immediate surgical excision of the entire lesion
Topical timolol alone, which is sufficient for any size lesion
stages.explanation: Oral propranolol is the first-line systemic therapy for problematic infantile hemangiomas, but in PHACE syndrome the presence of cerebral arterial stenosis/dysplasia and aortic coarctation raises theoretical concern that beta-blockade could lower perfusion pressure across stenotic vessels and precipitate ischemia, so it is started cautiously with cardiology and neurology input (often after vascular assessment, sometimes with slower titration). High-dose systemic steroids are an older second-line option, not the clear first choice. Immediate complete surgical excision is not first-line for a large facial lesion in infancy. Topical timolol is appropriate only for small/superficial lesions, not a large complicated segmental hemangioma.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which serious long-term neurologic complication are children with PHACE and cerebral arterial anomalies at increased risk for?
options: Arterial ischemic stroke (and acute compromise during the arteriopathy course)
Peripheral neuropathy
Spinal muscular atrophy
Benign rolandic epilepsy
stages.explanation: The cerebrovascular arteriopathy of PHACE (stenoses, dysplasia, aberrant/absent vessels, and occasionally a moyamoya-like pattern) places these children at risk for arterial ischemic stroke, which is the most feared neurologic complication and informs decisions about therapy and monitoring. Peripheral neuropathy is unrelated to the central large-vessel pathology of PHACE. Spinal muscular atrophy is an unrelated autosomal recessive motor neuron disease. Benign rolandic epilepsy is an idiopathic focal childhood epilepsy with no link to PHACE arteriopathy. Recognizing stroke risk underlies the cautious approach to blood-pressure-lowering therapy and the need for vascular surveillance.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the appropriate ongoing care plan for this child?
options: Discharge with reassurance that the birthmark will resolve and no follow-up is needed
Coordinated multidisciplinary follow-up (dermatology, neurology, cardiology, ophthalmology) with monitoring of the lesion, treatment of the coarctation, and vigilance for ischemic symptoms
Plan only for cosmetic laser therapy at age 10
Refer solely to dermatology for hemangioma management
stages.explanation: PHACE is a multisystem disorder, so the child needs coordinated multidisciplinary follow-up: dermatology for the hemangioma (including treatment decisions and monitoring of involution), cardiology to manage the aortic coarctation, neurology for the cerebral arteriopathy and stroke-risk surveillance, and ophthalmology for ocular structures and the visual axis. Discharging with reassurance dangerously ignores the cardiac and cerebrovascular anomalies. Deferring everything to cosmetic laser at age 10 neglects the urgent systemic issues. Referring to dermatology alone fails to address the coarctation and stroke risk that define the syndrome's morbidity. Comprehensive, team-based care is the standard.

cases.diagnosis: PHACE syndrome
pearls: A large (>5 cm) SEGMENTAL facial infantile hemangioma is the red flag that mandates evaluation for PHACE syndrome.
Workup: brain MRI + head/neck MRA, echocardiogram (aortic arch/coarctation), and dilated eye exam.
Cerebral arteriopathy confers a real risk of arterial ischemic stroke — temper blood-pressure-lowering therapy accordingly.
Propranolol remains first-line for complicated hemangiomas but is used cautiously in PHACE with cardiology/neurology input.

cases.title: A Toddler Losing Skills With Fast, Heavy Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: He has stopped walking and talking like before and is breathing fast.
cases.history: A 16-month-old boy is admitted after several weeks of losing previously acquired skills. He had been walking with support and saying a few words, but over the past month he has become floppy, stopped pulling to stand, lost words, and feeds poorly with frequent choking. In the last few days he developed rapid, deep breathing and increased sleepiness. He has had recurrent episodes of decompensation during minor illnesses, each with lethargy and fast breathing. Birth and early development were normal until about 12 months. He takes no medications and has no allergies; diet is unremarkable. He lives with both parents, who are first cousins (consanguineous). Family history includes a maternal infant who died young of an unexplained 'metabolic problem.' On review of systems, parents note abnormal eye movements and episodes of staring. There is no fever, no rash, no recent trauma, and no toxic ingestion known. They are frightened by how quickly he is deteriorating and by the breathing pattern.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable, hypotonic infant. Tachypneic with deep (Kussmaul-like) respirations. Eyes: intermittent nystagmus and a dysconjugate gaze; pupils reactive. There is poor head control and truncal hypotonia, but deep tendon reflexes are brisk with bilateral extensor plantar responses, indicating mixed upper-motor-neuron signs. He has decreased spontaneous movement and dystonic posturing of the limbs at times. No hepatomegaly is appreciated. No dysmorphic features. Fontanelle closed. Cardiac and respiratory auscultation clear aside from tachypnea. He has weak suck and an uncoordinated swallow.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a previously normal infant with developmental REGRESSION (loss of milestones), what category of disease should top the differential?
options: Static encephalopathy / cerebral palsy
A progressive (often inherited metabolic/neurodegenerative) disorder
Benign developmental variation
An acquired behavioral disorder such as autism alone
stages.explanation: Loss of previously acquired skills (true regression) is a red flag for a progressive disease process, most commonly an inherited metabolic or neurodegenerative disorder, and demands urgent metabolic and neurologic workup. Static encephalopathy/cerebral palsy is by definition non-progressive — children with CP may gain skills slowly but do not lose them, so regression argues against it. Benign developmental variation never causes loss of established milestones. Isolated autism can involve some early plateauing or loss of social/language skills but does not explain the systemic deterioration, hypotonia with brisk reflexes, abnormal eye movements, and Kussmaul breathing seen here. Regression mandates a metabolic differential.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His deep tachypnea most likely reflects compensation for which laboratory abnormality, and what initial test should you obtain?
options: Respiratory alkalosis from anxiety; obtain a chest X-ray
A metabolic (lactic) acidosis; obtain venous/arterial blood gas and serum lactate
Hypercapnia from airway obstruction; obtain neck films
Hyperglycemia; obtain an HbA1c
stages.explanation: Kussmaul-pattern deep breathing in a deteriorating infant is classic respiratory compensation for a metabolic acidosis, and in the setting of regression with episodic decompensation a lactic acidosis from mitochondrial dysfunction is high on the list — so a blood gas and serum lactate (plus pyruvate, ammonia, glucose) are the key initial tests. Respiratory alkalosis from anxiety does not fit a lethargic, sick infant and a chest X-ray would not explain it. Airway obstruction with hypercapnia would cause distress/stridor, not this metabolic picture. Hyperglycemia/HbA1c is not the mechanism here; the breathing reflects acid-base compensation, and lactate is the targeted test.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Venous blood gas: pH 7.22, HCO3 12 mEq/L (metabolic acidosis)
Serum lactate: 6.8 mmol/L (elevated)
CSF lactate: elevated
Ammonia: normal
Glucose: 78 mg/dL
MRI brain: symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the bilateral basal ganglia (putamen) and dorsal brainstem/periaqueductal gray
MR spectroscopy: lactate peak present

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The clinical picture plus symmetric basal ganglia/brainstem lesions with elevated lactate and a lactate peak on MR spectroscopy is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy)
Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
Herpes simplex encephalitis
Wilson disease
stages.explanation: Leigh syndrome is a mitochondrial neurodegenerative disorder characterized by developmental regression, lactic acidosis, and symmetric necrotizing lesions of the basal ganglia (especially putamen) and brainstem, with a lactate peak on MR spectroscopy — exactly this picture, and consanguinity fits its often autosomal recessive (or mitochondrial/X-linked) inheritance. ADEM causes multifocal, often asymmetric demyelinating white-matter lesions, typically post-infectious and monophasic, not symmetric deep-gray necrosis with lactate. HSV encephalitis characteristically involves the temporal lobes with fever and seizures, not symmetric basal ganglia with lactic acidosis. Wilson disease affects the basal ganglia but presents later, with hepatic and copper abnormalities, not infantile regression with lactic acidosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While confirming the molecular cause, which management principle is most appropriate for Leigh syndrome?
options: Curative gene therapy is available and should be started immediately
Supportive care, treatment of acidosis and intercurrent illness, a mitochondrial 'cocktail'/cofactor trial, and AVOIDANCE of mitochondrial toxins (e.g., valproate); inheritance counseling
High-dose IV steroids and plasma exchange as definitive therapy
A strict ketogenic diet is contraindicated in all mitochondrial disease and never used
stages.explanation: Leigh syndrome has no cure; management is supportive — aggressively treat metabolic acidosis and intercurrent illnesses that trigger decompensation, offer a trial of cofactors/vitamins (the mitochondrial 'cocktail', e.g., thiamine, riboflavin, CoQ10) tailored to the defect, and carefully avoid mitochondrial toxins such as valproate (hepatotoxic in some mitochondrial disease) and minimize fasting; genetic counseling is essential given consanguinity. Curative gene therapy is not standard of care. Steroids and plasma exchange treat inflammatory/demyelinating disease like ADEM, not Leigh syndrome. The blanket claim that ketogenic diet is never used is inaccurate — it can help certain mitochondrial phenotypes (e.g., PDH deficiency) and is decided case by case.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His brainstem lesions and uncoordinated swallow raise concern for which life-threatening acute complication that should drive disposition?
options: Central respiratory failure and aspiration from brainstem involvement, requiring close monitoring of airway/breathing
An acute large-vessel ischemic stroke needing thrombectomy
Status epilepticus that is curative if treated
Acute liver failure from copper accumulation
stages.explanation: Leigh syndrome characteristically attacks the brainstem, and progressive involvement of respiratory control centers plus a weak, uncoordinated swallow places the child at high risk for central respiratory failure (abnormal breathing patterns, apnea) and aspiration — the leading causes of acute deterioration and death — so disposition centers on airway/breathing monitoring, aspiration precautions, and readiness to support ventilation. A large-vessel ischemic stroke needing thrombectomy is not the mechanism; the lesions are symmetric metabolic necrosis, not arterial occlusion. Treating status epilepticus is important supportive care but is not 'curative' of Leigh syndrome. Acute copper-related liver failure describes Wilson disease, not Leigh syndrome. Brainstem respiratory compromise is the dominant acute threat.

cases.diagnosis: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy) — mitochondrial disease
pearls: Developmental REGRESSION signals a progressive disorder — pursue an urgent metabolic/neurodegenerative workup, not a CP label.
Kussmaul breathing + lactic acidosis + symmetric basal ganglia/brainstem lesions with an MRS lactate peak = Leigh syndrome.
Management is supportive: treat acidosis/illness, trial cofactor 'cocktails', and avoid mitochondrial toxins like valproate.
Consanguinity and an affected relative fit Leigh syndrome's heterogeneous inheritance (autosomal recessive, mitochondrial, or X-linked) — counsel the family.

cases.title: A 12-Year-Old With Sudden Confusion, Vomiting, and Vision Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: He suddenly became confused, started vomiting, and says he can't see on one side.
cases.history: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the ED with the acute onset of a severe throbbing headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, and difficulty seeing toward his right side that began a few hours ago. He has had several similar but milder episodes over the past year with headaches and transient visual disturbance, and one prior 'seizure.' His parents note he is notably short for his age and has done poorly in school recently with declining grades. He tires easily and has poor exercise tolerance. He had a documented sensorineural hearing problem. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with his mother and two siblings. Family history is striking on the maternal side: his mother has migraines and diabetes, a maternal aunt has hearing loss and 'muscle problems,' and a maternal uncle died young — the pattern of affected relatives all traces through the mother's lineage. There was no trauma, fever, or ingestion. The visual symptoms do not respect a single arterial territory and have shifted in character during the episode.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, intermittently confused adolescent. He is short-statured and thin. There is a right homonymous visual field deficit (cortical) with preserved pupillary responses; fundi normal. Speech is at times dysarthric and he has mild word-finding difficulty. Motor exam shows subtle right-sided weakness that fluctuates; reflexes symmetric. There is no neck stiffness. He appears generally weak with low muscle bulk. Hearing is reduced bilaterally. Cardiac exam is normal. The deficits localize to the left occipital/parieto-temporal cortex but do not conform neatly to a single major arterial distribution, and the exam findings have waxed and waned during evaluation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An acute focal neurologic deficit (a 'stroke-like episode') in a 12-year-old that does NOT conform to a single arterial territory should make you consider what besides a typical thromboembolic arterial stroke?
options: Only a routine atherosclerotic stroke
A metabolic stroke-like episode (e.g., mitochondrial disease such as MELAS)
Simple migraine with no further workup
Conversion disorder, requiring no imaging
stages.explanation: A stroke-like episode in a child that crosses or does not respect classic vascular territories — especially with short stature, prior similar episodes, seizures, hearing loss, and a maternal-line family history — should raise mitochondrial disease such as MELAS, where the lesions reflect a metabolic energy failure rather than occlusion of a named artery. Routine atherosclerotic stroke is essentially nonexistent at age 12 and would follow a vascular territory. Calling it simple migraine with no workup is unsafe given confusion, field cut, and prior seizure. Conversion disorder without imaging is inappropriate for objective, localizing deficits and would miss a treatable, dangerous process. The non-territorial pattern is the key clue.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of features in this boy most specifically points toward MELAS rather than another etiology?
options: Tall stature, paternal inheritance, and normal lactate
Fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia
Acute hemiparesis after head trauma
Short stature, sensorineural hearing loss, seizures, maternal-line inheritance, and stroke-like episodes
stages.explanation: MELAS (Mitochondrial Encephalomyopathy, Lactic Acidosis, and Stroke-like episodes) classically features short stature, sensorineural hearing loss, seizures, recurrent stroke-like episodes, and maternal (mitochondrial) inheritance — exactly this constellation, with all affected relatives on the maternal side. Tall stature with paternal inheritance and normal lactate is the opposite of the expected profile (mtDNA is maternally inherited; lactate is typically elevated). Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia describe meningitis, which does not fit the chronic relapsing, maternally inherited picture. Acute hemiparesis after trauma implicates a traumatic/vascular injury, not a metabolic mitochondrial disorder. The maternal lineage plus the syndromic features are decisive.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum lactate: 5.4 mmol/L (elevated)
CSF lactate: elevated; CSF cell count normal, no xanthochromia
Glucose: 92 mg/dL
MRI brain (DWI): cortical/subcortical signal abnormality in the left parieto-occipital region not confined to a single vascular territory, with relative sparing of deep structures
MR angiography: major intracranial arteries patent (no large-vessel occlusion)
MR spectroscopy: lactate peak in affected cortex

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which genetic test would most likely confirm the diagnosis?
options: Nuclear NF1 gene sequencing
Mitochondrial DNA analysis for the m.3243A>G (MT-TL1) point mutation
Karyotype for trisomy 21
FMR1 CGG repeat testing for fragile X
stages.explanation: The most common cause of MELAS is the m.3243A>G point mutation in the mitochondrial MT-TL1 gene (encoding tRNA-Leucine), so targeted mitochondrial DNA testing for m.3243A>G (often best detected in tissues such as muscle or urinary sediment due to heteroplasmy) is the confirmatory test. NF1 sequencing tests a nuclear gene for neurofibromatosis, unrelated to this picture. A karyotype for trisomy 21 evaluates a chromosomal aneuploidy that does not cause stroke-like episodes with lactic acidosis. FMR1 testing for fragile X assesses an X-linked cause of intellectual disability, not a mitochondrial stroke-like syndrome. The maternally inherited, energy-failure phenotype points squarely to mtDNA analysis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: During the acute stroke-like episode, which management is most appropriate for MELAS?
options: Immediate IV tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) for thrombolysis
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin as the primary treatment
Supportive care with attention to hydration and acidosis, control of seizures, and IV/oral L-arginine for the acute stroke-like episode
Emergent mechanical thrombectomy
stages.explanation: MELAS stroke-like episodes are metabolic, not thromboembolic, so treatment is supportive — manage hydration and acidosis, aggressively control seizures (which can worsen energy failure), and administer L-arginine, which is used acutely (and as prophylaxis) to improve endothelial/vascular function and may reduce the severity/recurrence of episodes. Thrombolysis with tPA targets a clot that is not present and exposes the child to hemorrhage risk without benefit. Heparin anticoagulation likewise treats thromboembolism, the wrong mechanism. Mechanical thrombectomy requires a large-vessel occlusion, which MRA here shows is absent. Avoiding mitochondrial toxins (e.g., valproate) and supporting energy metabolism round out care.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: How should you counsel the family about inheritance and recurrence?
options: It is autosomal dominant from the father; sons are at highest risk
It follows mitochondrial (maternal) inheritance — passed from an affected mother to all her children, with variable severity due to heteroplasmy; affected males do not transmit it
It is X-linked recessive with no maternal transmission
It is a non-genetic, acquired condition with no recurrence risk
stages.explanation: MELAS is caused by mitochondrial DNA mutations, which are inherited maternally: an affected mother passes the mtDNA to all of her children, while affected males do not transmit it to their offspring; the proportion of mutant mitochondria (heteroplasmy) varies among tissues and individuals, producing variable expression and severity — consistent with the maternal-line clustering here. Autosomal dominant paternal inheritance contradicts mtDNA biology. X-linked recessive inheritance is incorrect and would not produce uniform maternal transmission. Calling it acquired with no recurrence risk is wrong and would misguide reproductive counseling. Heteroplasmy explains why relatives show a spectrum from mild migraines/diabetes to severe encephalopathy.

cases.diagnosis: MELAS syndrome (mitochondrial m.3243A>G mutation)
pearls: A childhood stroke-like episode crossing/ignoring arterial territories, with elevated lactate, suggests MELAS rather than thromboembolic stroke.
MELAS = short stature, sensorineural hearing loss, seizures, lactic acidosis, and maternal-line inheritance; most carry m.3243A>G in MT-TL1.
Acute treatment is supportive plus L-arginine; tPA/anticoagulation/thrombectomy are inappropriate because there is no occlusive clot.
Mitochondrial (maternal) inheritance with heteroplasmy explains variable severity; affected mothers transmit to all children, affected fathers to none.

cases.title: A 9-Month-Old With Delay, Eczema, and a Musty Smell
cases.chiefComplaint: His parents are worried he is behind in development and has a persistent rash and an unusual odor.
cases.history: A 9-month-old boy is brought to clinic for developmental concerns. He is not yet sitting independently, makes little eye contact, and has lost interest in his surroundings; his parents feel he is 'slipping behind.' He has stubborn eczema unresponsive to moisturizers and a peculiar musty or 'mousy' smell to his urine and body that the family has noticed for months. The family recently immigrated from a country without universal newborn metabolic screening, and the boy was born at home with no newborn screen performed. He is breastfed and started purees. He takes no medications; the eczema is treated with emollients. He has had no seizures yet but has occasional jerking movements, and his parents note he is fair-skinned with blue eyes, lighter than his siblings. He lives with both parents and three older siblings who are all healthy. The parents are consanguineous (first cousins once removed). There is no other notable family history. Immunizations are incomplete.
cases.exam: Fair-skinned, blond, blue-eyed infant who is hypotonic and less interactive than expected for age. Head circumference is at the 5th percentile (relative microcephaly). Skin shows diffuse eczematous, dry patches. There is a faint musty odor. Neurologic exam shows axial hypotonia with some appendicular hypertonia and brisk reflexes; he does not sit unsupported and has poor visual tracking. No dysmorphic facies, no hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiac and respiratory exams are normal. He startles to sound, and there is occasional myoclonic jerking of the limbs. No cataracts; red reflex present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of developmental delay, eczema, a musty/mousy odor, and unusually light pigmentation in an UNSCREENED infant should prompt suspicion for which category of disease?
options: An acquired nutritional deficiency only
An inborn error of metabolism (amino acid disorder)
A primary skin disorder
Normal variation in a fair-skinned family
stages.explanation: Developmental delay with eczema, a musty (mousy) body/urine odor, and hypopigmentation in a child who never had newborn screening is the classic teaching presentation of an amino acid inborn error of metabolism — phenylketonuria (PKU). The musty odor comes from phenylacetate accumulation, and tyrosine deficiency (downstream of the blocked phenylalanine hydroxylase) reduces melanin, causing light skin/hair/eyes. An acquired nutritional deficiency alone does not unify the odor and pigment findings. A primary skin disorder ignores the neurodevelopmental and metabolic clues. Attributing it to normal fair-skin variation dangerously dismisses a treatable cause of intellectual disability. The unscreened, consanguineous context heightens suspicion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying biochemical defect in classic PKU, and what test confirms it?
options: Deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) causing elevated blood phenylalanine; confirm with quantitative plasma amino acids
Deficiency of branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase; confirm with urine organic acids
Deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase; confirm with enzyme assay
Deficiency of alpha-galactosidase A; confirm with leukocyte enzyme testing
stages.explanation: Classic PKU results from deficient phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH), the enzyme converting phenylalanine to tyrosine; phenylalanine accumulates (neurotoxic) while tyrosine falls (hence hypopigmentation), and the diagnosis is confirmed by markedly elevated plasma phenylalanine on quantitative amino acid analysis (with screening via blood phenylalanine). Branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency is maple syrup urine disease, which has a sweet/maple odor and different chemistry. Galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency is galactosemia, presenting with cataracts, jaundice, and E. coli sepsis, not a mousy odor. Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency is Fabry disease, an X-linked lysosomal disorder with a completely different phenotype.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Plasma phenylalanine: 1,250 µmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <120)
Plasma tyrosine: low
Urine: positive for phenylketones; mousy odor noted
BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin) studies: pending to exclude a cofactor-deficient variant
Newborn screen: never performed
MRI brain: mild white-matter signal changes, no structural malformation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for classic PKU?
options: A protein-rich, high-phenylalanine diet to support growth
A lifelong phenylalanine-restricted diet (special low-Phe medical formula and restricted natural protein) with monitoring of blood phenylalanine, plus tyrosine supplementation
Liver transplantation for all newly diagnosed patients
A gluten-free diet
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of PKU management is dietary: lifelong restriction of phenylalanine using specialized low-phenylalanine medical formula and tight limits on natural protein, with regular monitoring of blood phenylalanine to keep it in the target range, plus tyrosine supplementation since tyrosine becomes a conditionally essential amino acid; some patients respond to sapropterin (BH4), and BH4-deficient variants need additional therapy. A high-phenylalanine protein-rich diet is exactly wrong and would worsen neurotoxicity. Liver transplantation is not standard therapy for PKU. A gluten-free diet treats celiac disease and is irrelevant to phenylalanine metabolism. Early, sustained dietary control is what preserves the brain.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling about prognosis given that this child was diagnosed late at 9 months?
options: Strict dietary control going forward can prevent FURTHER neurologic damage, though any intellectual disability already incurred from prolonged exposure may not fully reverse — emphasizing why newborn screening and early treatment matter
There is no point starting the diet now since damage is done
He will completely recover all lost function within weeks of starting the diet
Diet can be safely stopped once he reaches school age in all patients
stages.explanation: PKU is the prototype of a diet-reversible cause of intellectual disability when treated from birth; starting (or intensifying) dietary control still protects the developing brain from ongoing high-phenylalanine toxicity and prevents further damage, but neurologic injury already sustained during months of untreated exposure may not be fully reversible — which is the entire rationale for universal newborn screening and early intervention. Refusing to treat because 'damage is done' is wrong; treatment still helps. Promising complete recovery in weeks overstates reversibility of established injury. Telling families the diet can be stopped at school age is incorrect — current guidance favors lifelong phenylalanine control.

cases.diagnosis: Phenylketonuria (untreated classic PKU with neurologic sequelae)
pearls: Developmental delay + eczema + musty (mousy) odor + hypopigmentation in an unscreened infant = think PKU.
Classic PKU is phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency: phenylalanine rises (neurotoxic), tyrosine falls (hence light pigmentation).
Treatment is a lifelong phenylalanine-restricted diet with low-Phe formula, tyrosine supplementation, and blood-Phe monitoring; some respond to BH4 (sapropterin).
PKU is the classic diet-PREVENTABLE intellectual disability — early newborn screening and treatment from birth are what preserve cognition.

cases.title: Infant With a Facial Birthmark and New Twitching Spells
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report repetitive twitching of the right arm and face beginning two weeks ago.
cases.history: An 8-month-old boy is brought to clinic for spells of rhythmic jerking of the right arm and the right side of his face lasting 1-2 minutes, occurring several times daily over the past two weeks. He remains alert during some events and is briefly unresponsive in others, followed by sleepiness. He was born full-term by uncomplicated vaginal delivery and was noted at birth to have a flat reddish-purple birthmark over the left forehead, upper eyelid, and cheek that has not faded. Development had been normal, but parents feel he is now using the right hand less than the left. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. He is exclusively formula-fed and meeting feeding milestones. Family history is unremarkable; there is no family history of epilepsy, similar birthmarks, or neurocutaneous disorders. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, and there was no perinatal distress, fever, or recent illness preceding the spells. Parents deny trauma or ill contacts.
cases.exam: Alert infant with a well-demarcated, non-blanching reddish-purple patch over the left forehead, upper eyelid, and maxillary region (V1-V2 distribution). Conjunctivae clear; eyes appear symmetric but the left globe seems mildly enlarged. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. He tracks objects and babbles. There is subtle decreased spontaneous movement and mildly increased tone of the right arm with an emerging hand preference for the left. Deep tendon reflexes are slightly brisker on the right. No dysmorphism, no hepatosplenomegaly, and skin elsewhere is clear with no hypopigmented macules or café-au-lait spots.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the facial port-wine birthmark in this distribution plus new focal seizures, which feature most strongly predicts intracranial involvement and warrants urgent referral?
options: Involvement of the V1 (forehead and upper eyelid) region by the port-wine stain
Bilateral cheek involvement only
The birthmark fading over the first year of life
Isolated involvement of the lower lip and chin
stages.explanation: Port-wine stains involving the V1 (ophthalmic) distribution—forehead and upper eyelid—carry the highest risk of associated leptomeningeal angiomatosis and glaucoma, the hallmark of Sturge-Weber syndrome. This is why V1 involvement plus seizures mandates prompt neuroimaging and ophthalmologic evaluation. Bilateral cheek-only (V2/V3) involvement without V1 carries much lower CNS risk. A fading birthmark suggests a benign nevus simplex (salmon patch), not a port-wine stain. Isolated lower-lip/chin (V3) involvement is also low risk for intracranial disease. Risk tracks with the ophthalmic dermatome because of shared embryologic vascular development between facial and meningeal/ocular vasculature.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the suspected intracranial vascular abnormality?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Plain skull radiographs
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced brain MRI is the study of choice; gadolinium reveals the leptomeningeal angioma (pial vascular malformation), typically ipsilateral to the facial stain, plus secondary changes like cortical atrophy and abnormal venous drainage. Non-contrast CT may eventually show the classic 'tram-track' gyriform calcifications but is insensitive early, especially in infants, and adds radiation. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial large vessels and has no role for leptomeningeal disease. Skull radiographs may show late calcification but are obsolete for this purpose and cannot assess the brain parenchyma or pial vasculature. MRI also best demonstrates the choroid plexus enlargement and accelerated myelination sometimes seen.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI with contrast: Leptomeningeal enhancement over the left parieto-occipital cortex consistent with a pial angioma
Ipsilateral mild cortical atrophy and enlarged ipsilateral choroid plexus
EEG: Focal slowing and epileptiform discharges over the left hemisphere
Ophthalmology: Elevated intraocular pressure in the left eye (24 mmHg) with increased corneal diameter — consistent with glaucoma
CBC and metabolic panel: within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which first-line antiseizure medication is most appropriate for controlling this infant's focal seizures?
options: Ethosuximide
Oxcarbazepine or levetiracetam
Valproate as monotherapy first-line in this infant
Phenobarbital lifelong as preferred agent
stages.explanation: Focal seizures in Sturge-Weber are best treated with focal-spectrum agents; oxcarbazepine and levetiracetam are reasonable, well-tolerated first-line choices in infants. Ethosuximide treats only absence seizures and has no efficacy against focal seizures. Valproate is broad-spectrum but is generally avoided as first choice in very young children because of hepatotoxicity risk (especially under age 2) and is not preferred here. Phenobarbital can control seizures but carries sedation and adverse neurodevelopmental effects, so it is not a preferred long-term agent. Early, aggressive seizure control matters because uncontrolled seizures worsen the progressive hemispheric injury characteristic of this disorder.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond seizures and glaucoma, which additional long-term complication should be specifically monitored and addressed in this child?
options: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss
Bilateral cataracts from the leptomeningeal angioma
Renal angiomyolipomas
Stroke-like episodes and progressive hemiparesis with cognitive impairment
stages.explanation: Sturge-Weber is a progressive disorder: chronic venous stasis and ischemia from the leptomeningeal angioma cause recurrent stroke-like episodes, worsening hemiparesis, visual field cuts, hemianopia, and cognitive/developmental impairment over time, sometimes with episodic transient neurologic deterioration. Low-dose aspirin is often used to reduce thrombotic stroke-like events, and developmental support, rehabilitation, and seizure control are essential to preserve function. Sensorineural hearing loss is not characteristic of this syndrome. Glaucoma (not cataracts) is the ocular hallmark, driven by elevated episcleral venous pressure and developmental angle anomalies, not by the pial angioma directly. Renal angiomyolipomas are features of tuberous sclerosis, a different neurocutaneous syndrome. The hallmark facial stain, ocular, and CNS findings together define Sturge-Weber.

cases.diagnosis: Sturge-Weber syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis) with leptomeningeal angioma, focal epilepsy, and glaucoma
pearls: A port-wine stain involving the V1 (forehead/upper eyelid) distribution is the strongest predictor of leptomeningeal angiomatosis and glaucoma.
Contrast-enhanced brain MRI is the test of choice; CT 'tram-track' gyriform calcifications appear late.
Sturge-Weber results from a somatic mosaic GNAQ mutation and is not inherited.
Manage with focal-spectrum antiseizure drugs, lifelong glaucoma surveillance, and low-dose aspirin to reduce stroke-like episodes.

cases.title: A Preschooler With Worsening Unsteadiness and Red Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report progressively worsening balance and frequent falls over the past two years.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl presents with progressive gait unsteadiness first noticed around 18 months of age when she began walking with a wide-based, wobbly gait. Over the past two years her balance has deteriorated; she now falls frequently, has difficulty with fine tasks, and her speech has become slurred. Her parents also note odd jerky eye movements and red 'spider-like' streaks on the whites of her eyes appearing over the last year. She has had numerous sinopulmonary infections, including three episodes of pneumonia and recurrent otitis media requiring multiple antibiotic courses. She takes no daily medications and has no known drug allergies. She was born full-term after an uncomplicated pregnancy; early milestones were normal until gait regression. Family history is notable for consanguinity—her parents are first cousins—and a maternal uncle who died young of an unspecified cancer. She attends preschool but has been kept home frequently due to illness. There is no history of toxin exposure, head trauma, or preceding viral illness.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative girl with dysarthric speech. Conjunctivae show prominent dilated bulbar telangiectasias bilaterally. Eye movements reveal oculomotor apraxia and gaze-evoked nystagmus. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Marked truncal and appendicular ataxia with a wide-based, titubating gait; she cannot perform tandem walking. Finger-to-nose testing shows dysmetria and intention tremor. Tone is normal to mildly reduced; deep tendon reflexes are diminished. No focal weakness. Skin shows a few telangiectasias on the ears. No hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. No café-au-lait macules. Romberg cannot be reliably assessed due to baseline imbalance.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings best narrows the diagnosis in this child with progressive ataxia?
options: Ataxia with hepatosplenomegaly and cherry-red macula
Progressive ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and recurrent sinopulmonary infections
Acute ataxia following a viral illness with full recovery expected
Episodic ataxia triggered by stress lasting minutes
stages.explanation: The triad of progressive cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous (conjunctival) telangiectasias, and recurrent sinopulmonary infections from combined immunodeficiency is classic for ataxia-telangiectasia, and the consanguinity here fits an autosomal recessive disorder. Ataxia with hepatosplenomegaly and a cherry-red macula suggests a lysosomal storage disorder (e.g., Niemann-Pick or Tay-Sachs), not this picture. Acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia is self-limited and follows a recent viral illness—incompatible with two years of progression and telangiectasias. Episodic ataxia is paroxysmal, brief, often triggered by stress, and not associated with telangiectasias or immunodeficiency. The combination of progressive neurologic, cutaneous/ocular, and immunologic features, together with oculomotor apraxia, is the key diagnostic pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which single laboratory test is the most useful initial screening study to support this diagnosis?
options: Serum lactate and pyruvate
Serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP)
Serum ceruloplasmin
Very-long-chain fatty acids
stages.explanation: Elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein is found in over 90% of patients with ataxia-telangiectasia and is the simplest, most useful initial screen. Lactate and pyruvate screen for mitochondrial disease, which does not cause conjunctival telangiectasias or this immunodeficiency.
