Ceruloplasmin evaluates Wilson disease, which presents later with movement disorder, Kayser-Fleischer rings, and hepatic dysfunction—not telangiectasias or recurrent infections. Very-long-chain fatty acids screen for peroxisomal disorders such as adrenoleukodystrophy, which causes a leukodystrophy phenotype, not this triad. Persistently elevated AFP after infancy strongly supports the diagnosis, which can then be confirmed by ATM gene testing and absent ATM protein.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum alpha-fetoprotein: 312 ng/mL (markedly elevated; normal <10 for age)
IgA: 28 mg/dL (low)
IgG: 410 mg/dL (low)
IgE: undetectable
Absolute lymphocyte count: 1.1 ×10³/µL (low)
Brain MRI: Cerebellar vermian and hemispheric atrophy
Chromosomal breakage study: increased breakage with 7;14 translocations
Genetic testing: biallelic pathogenic variants in ATM

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because of the underlying defect, which malignancy is this patient at greatest lifetime risk of developing?
options: Osteosarcoma
Lymphoid malignancies (leukemia and lymphoma)
Hepatocellular carcinoma
Medulloblastoma
stages.explanation: The ATM gene encodes a kinase central to DNA double-strand break repair; its loss markedly increases risk of lymphoid malignancies—leukemia and lymphoma—which are the leading cancer-related cause of death in ataxia-telangiectasia. Osteosarcoma is associated with hereditary retinoblastoma and Li-Fraumeni (TP53), not ATM. Hepatocellular carcinoma is unrelated despite the elevated AFP (which here reflects the underlying disorder, not liver tumor). Medulloblastoma is associated with Gorlin syndrome and other pathways, not ATM. Awareness of cancer risk also has practical implications: these patients are radiosensitive, so diagnostic and therapeutic ionizing radiation must be minimized to avoid tissue and DNA damage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which management principle is most important to communicate to this family and her care team?
options: Begin high-dose corticosteroids to halt the ataxia progression
Avoid ionizing radiation when possible and provide immunologic, infectious, and supportive care
Restrict dietary protein to lower the alpha-fetoprotein level
Initiate chelation therapy to reduce tissue copper
stages.explanation: Because ATM deficiency causes profound radiosensitivity, minimizing ionizing radiation (limiting CTs and X-rays, and using cautious, dose-reduced radiotherapy only if cancer arises) is critical, alongside immunoglobulin replacement for hypogammaglobulinemia, aggressive treatment and prophylaxis of infections, pulmonary surveillance and airway clearance, nutritional support, and physical and occupational therapy. Corticosteroids do not halt the progressive cerebellar degeneration of this disorder and are not curative. Dietary protein restriction does not lower AFP and is irrelevant—the AFP elevation reflects the underlying genetic disorder, not diet. Chelation therapy treats Wilson disease (copper overload), which is a different diagnosis entirely. Multidisciplinary supportive care and cancer surveillance, with strict radiation avoidance, define the management approach.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The parents are first cousins. What is the inheritance pattern, and what counseling is most accurate for future pregnancies?
options: X-linked recessive; only sons are at risk
Autosomal dominant; each child has a 50% chance of being affected
Autosomal recessive; each subsequent child has a 25% recurrence risk, and carrier/prenatal testing can be offered
Mitochondrial; risk is transmitted only through the mother to all offspring
stages.explanation: Ataxia-telangiectasia is autosomal recessive, caused by biallelic pathogenic ATM variants; with both parents obligate carriers, each subsequent pregnancy carries a 25% recurrence risk, and the consanguinity (first cousins) increases the chance both parents share the same variant. Carrier testing of relatives and prenatal or preimplantation genetic testing can be offered, along with cancer-risk counseling for carriers (heterozygotes have modestly increased breast cancer risk). X-linked recessive is incorrect—both sons and daughters are affected, and this girl is affected. Autosomal dominant with 50% risk is wrong because the disorder requires two mutated alleles. Mitochondrial inheritance does not apply; ATM is a nuclear gene, not mitochondrial.

cases.diagnosis: Ataxia-telangiectasia (Louis-Bar syndrome) due to biallelic ATM mutations
pearls: Progressive ataxia + conjunctival telangiectasias + recurrent sinopulmonary infections = ataxia-telangiectasia.
Elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein is the best initial screening test (>90% of patients).
ATM defects confer radiosensitivity and a high risk of lymphoid malignancies—minimize ionizing radiation.
Telangiectasias often appear after ataxia begins; their absence in a toddler does not exclude the diagnosis.

cases.title: A Teen With Recurrent Throbbing Headaches Relieved by Sleep
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports recurrent throbbing headaches that force her to lie down in a dark room.
cases.history: A 12-year-old girl presents with a 6-month history of recurrent headaches occurring about twice monthly. Each episode builds over 30 minutes to a moderate-to-severe throbbing pain, usually on one side of the head but sometimes bilateral frontotemporal, lasting 4-8 hours. She feels nauseated, dislikes bright lights and loud sounds, and prefers to lie still in a dark, quiet room; sleep typically aborts the attack. She denies any visual changes, flashing lights, weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty preceding the headaches. There is no early-morning headache, no vomiting on awakening, and no worsening with cough or position change. Attacks are more frequent around menses, after skipping meals, and after poor sleep during exam weeks. She takes occasional acetaminophen with partial relief and has no known drug allergies. She is an honor student with normal growth. Family history is significant: her mother has 'sick headaches' that confine her to bed, and a maternal aunt has migraines. There is no history of head trauma or fever.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable adolescent in no distress between attacks. Visual acuity normal. Fundoscopy shows sharp optic disc margins with no papilledema. Extraocular movements full; pupils equal and reactive. No meningismus. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout with normal tone and symmetric reflexes. Gait, including tandem, is normal; finger-to-nose and rapid alternating movements are intact. No focal sensory deficits. Skin shows no neurocutaneous stigmata. Neck supple, no carotid bruits. The general examination, including blood pressure and growth parameters, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and a normal neurologic exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order an urgent non-contrast head CT
Make a clinical diagnosis and counsel on management without neuroimaging
Obtain a brain MRI with contrast to exclude a tumor
Perform a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
stages.explanation: This presentation—recurrent episodic throbbing headaches with nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, relief with sleep, identifiable triggers, a strong family history, and a completely normal neurologic exam—is diagnostic of pediatric migraine without aura on clinical grounds. Neuroimaging is not indicated when there are no red flags. A head CT exposes the child to radiation and is low-yield here. Brain MRI is reserved for red flags such as abnormal exam findings, an occipital-only pattern, progressive worsening, early-morning vomiting, or signs of raised intracranial pressure. Lumbar puncture would be appropriate for suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension or infection, neither of which is suggested by this normal exam.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature is required to classify these headaches as migraine WITHOUT aura rather than with aura?
options: Presence of unilateral throbbing pain
Absence of fully reversible focal neurologic symptoms preceding or during the headache
Association with nausea and photophobia
Relief of the headache with sleep
stages.explanation: Migraine without aura is defined by recurrent attacks of headache with characteristic features (e.g., throbbing quality, nausea, photophobia/phonophobia) in the absence of an aura—the fully reversible focal neurologic symptoms (most often visual) that precede or accompany migraine with aura. This patient explicitly denies any preceding visual, sensory, motor, or speech symptoms, placing her in the 'without aura' category. Unilateral throbbing pain, nausea/photophobia, and relief with sleep are typical migraine features but are shared by both subtypes and do not distinguish them. The defining distinction is the presence or absence of aura.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute (abortive) therapy to recommend for her attacks?
options: Daily propranolol started immediately
Scheduled opioids for breakthrough pain
Early ibuprofen at attack onset, with a triptan added if NSAIDs are insufficient
Butalbital-containing combination analgesics as first-line
stages.explanation: For acute pediatric migraine, first-line abortive therapy is an NSAID such as ibuprofen taken early and at an adequate dose; if NSAIDs are inadequate, a triptan (several are approved for adolescents) is added. Treating early and limiting use to fewer than ~10-15 days per month prevents medication-overuse headache. Propranolol is a preventive (prophylactic) agent, not an abortive, and daily prophylaxis is reserved for frequent or disabling attacks. Opioids are contraindicated for routine migraine because of poor efficacy, dependence risk, and worsening of headache. Butalbital-containing combinations are avoided in children due to high risk of dependence and medication-overuse headache and are not first-line.

stages.label: HEADACHE DIARY & WORKUP
stages.content:
4-week headache diary: 2 attacks, each 4-6 hours, throbbing, with nausea and photophobia; both relieved by sleep
No visual, sensory, motor, or speech symptoms preceding any attack
Triggers logged: onset of menses, a skipped lunch, and a late night before exams
Acute medication use: ibuprofen on 3 days total this month (no overuse)
Neurologic re-examination: normal, no papilledema; PedMIDAS disability score in the mild range

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which lifestyle counseling point is most appropriate to emphasize for long-term headache reduction?
options: Strict elimination of all dietary fats
Regular sleep, consistent meals/hydration, stress management, and limiting acute medication days
Complete avoidance of physical activity
Routinely treating every headache with combination analgesics to prevent escalation
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of pediatric migraine management is lifestyle optimization: regular sleep schedules, not skipping meals, adequate hydration, stress reduction, and a headache diary to identify triggers—plus limiting acute medication use to avoid medication-overuse headache. Her triggers (menses, skipped meals, poor sleep) are addressable. Eliminating all dietary fat is unsupported and unnecessary; specific trigger foods, if any, should be identified individually. Avoiding physical activity is counterproductive—regular aerobic exercise reduces migraine frequency. Routinely medicating every headache with combination analgesics promotes medication-overuse (rebound) headache and worsens the disorder over time, so it should be avoided.

cases.diagnosis: Migraine without aura (pediatric)
pearls: Episodic throbbing headache with nausea, photophobia/phonophobia, relief with sleep, triggers, and family history plus a normal exam is migraine—no imaging needed.
Migraine without aura lacks the fully reversible focal neurologic symptoms that define aura.
First-line acute therapy is early NSAIDs, adding a triptan if needed; avoid opioids and butalbital.
Lifestyle measures (sleep, meals, hydration, stress) and limiting acute med days are central to prevention.

cases.title: A Teen Who Sees Shimmering Zigzags Before His Headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: He describes shimmering zigzag lights in his vision shortly before a one-sided headache begins.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents with episodes occurring roughly once a month over the past year. Each begins with a small shimmering spot in his central vision that expands over about 15-20 minutes into a crescent of bright, flickering zigzag lines with a blurred center, affecting both eyes; the visual disturbance fully resolves within 30 minutes. Shortly afterward, a throbbing headache develops, usually on the left, with nausea and sensitivity to light. The headache lasts several hours and improves with rest and ibuprofen. He denies weakness, numbness, slurred speech, confusion, loss of consciousness, tongue biting, or incontinence during the episodes, and he remains fully aware throughout. There is no headache on waking and no progressive worsening. He plays soccer, denies recreational drug use, and takes no daily medications; he has no known allergies. Family history is notable for migraine with visual symptoms in his mother. He has no history of head trauma, cardiac disease, or clotting disorders. The visual symptoms are stereotyped and identical from episode to episode.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing adolescent in no distress. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally; visual fields full to confrontation; no field cut. Fundoscopy normal with sharp disc margins and no papilledema. Pupils equal, round, reactive; extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 with normal tone; reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Sensation intact to all modalities. Coordination normal; gait, including tandem, is steady. No carotid bruits, no cardiac murmur, regular rhythm. Skin without neurocutaneous markings. The entire neurologic examination is normal between and outside of episodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which characteristic of his visual symptoms best distinguishes a migraine aura from an occipital seizure or a TIA?
options: Sudden onset and resolution within seconds
Gradual spread (build-up) over minutes with positive visual phenomena, then full resolution, followed by headache
Persistent monocular vision loss lasting hours
Associated tongue biting and postictal confusion
stages.explanation: Migraine aura characteristically develops gradually over 5-20 minutes ('marching' or spreading), features positive phenomena (shimmering, scintillating zigzags/fortification spectra), lasts under 60 minutes, fully reverses, and is typically followed by headache—reflecting the slow spread of cortical spreading depression. Sudden onset/offset within seconds and brief positive symptoms favor seizure, and occipital seizures often produce colored circular shapes plus possible impaired awareness. Persistent monocular vision loss lasting hours suggests an ocular or vascular (e.g., TIA/amaurosis) cause—TIA visual loss is usually negative (loss) and abrupt. Tongue biting and postictal confusion indicate a generalized seizure, which he explicitly lacks. The slow march plus positive scintillations is the key migrainous feature.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management given the typical history and normal examination?
options: Diagnose migraine with aura clinically and counsel; no imaging required
Obtain an emergent CT angiogram to rule out a vascular malformation
Order an EEG to exclude occipital epilepsy
Start anticoagulation for presumed TIA
stages.explanation: Stereotyped visual symptoms that build gradually, fully resolve, and are followed by a throbbing unilateral headache with nausea and photophobia—together with a strong family history and a normal neurologic exam—are diagnostic of migraine with aura clinically; no neuroimaging is required absent red flags. A CT angiogram is unnecessary because there are no features suggesting an arteriovenous malformation (e.g., always same-side symptoms, seizures, focal deficits, or an abnormal exam). EEG is not indicated because the episodes lack ictal features and awareness is preserved with gradual spread. Anticoagulation for 'TIA' is inappropriate and potentially harmful: this is not ischemia, and the clinical picture is clearly migrainous.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For acute treatment of his attacks, which approach is most appropriate?
options: Take a triptan only during the aura, before any headache, to abort it
Treat the headache phase early with an NSAID and/or a triptan; counsel that triptans are taken at headache onset
Use daily amitriptyline as abortive therapy
Administer high-flow oxygen as first-line abortive therapy
stages.explanation: Acute migraine, with or without aura, is treated by addressing the headache phase early—NSAIDs such as ibuprofen and/or a triptan taken at headache onset. Triptans are generally taken at the start of the headache rather than during the aura, where evidence for aborting the attack is limited. Daily amitriptyline is a preventive (prophylactic) agent for frequent or disabling migraine, not an abortive. High-flow oxygen is first-line for cluster headache, not migraine. With monthly attacks that respond to ibuprofen, optimizing acute treatment and lifestyle is appropriate, reserving daily prophylaxis for higher frequency or disability.

stages.label: CLINIC FINDINGS
stages.content:
Aura description: scintillating scotoma building over 15-20 minutes, fully reversible within 30 minutes, then headache
No motor weakness, dysphasia, or impaired awareness during episodes
Repeat neurologic exam and visual fields: normal; fundoscopy without papilledema
Blood pressure normal; no cardiac murmur on auscultation
No neuroimaging performed (no red flags); clinical diagnosis supported

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which counseling point about migraine with aura is most important to convey to this adolescent and his family?
options: He should never participate in contact sports again
Aura itself is benign and reversible, but new or atypical features warrant re-evaluation; reassure and educate on triggers
He requires lifelong anticoagulation to prevent stroke
Combined oral contraceptive use would be strongly recommended for him in the future
stages.explanation: Reassurance is appropriate: typical migraine aura is benign and fully reversible, and his stereotyped pattern with a normal exam is characteristic. Families should be taught to seek re-evaluation for new red flags—aura that suddenly changes, includes weakness (suggesting hemiplegic migraine), is prolonged (>60 min), or is accompanied by an abnormal exam. He need not abandon sports; reasonable activity is encouraged. Lifelong anticoagulation is not indicated—migraine aura is not a thromboembolic event. The combined oral contraceptive point is a distractor: estrogen-containing contraceptives are relatively contraindicated in patients (typically women) with migraine WITH aura due to stroke risk, so it would not be 'strongly recommended.'

cases.diagnosis: Migraine with aura (pediatric)
pearls: Migraine aura builds gradually over minutes with positive visual phenomena (scintillating scotoma), resolves within an hour, and precedes headache.
Abrupt onset favors seizure (often with impaired awareness); abrupt negative vision loss favors TIA—the gradual march of positive symptoms favors aura.
Typical stereotyped aura with a normal exam needs no imaging; new weakness, prolonged aura, or exam abnormalities are red flags.
Estrogen-containing contraceptives are relatively contraindicated in migraine with aura because of increased stroke risk.

cases.title: A Young Girl With Recurrent Bouts of Belly Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report repeated episodes of severe central abdominal pain that make her stop activities and lie down.
cases.history: An 8-year-old girl presents with a 1-year history of recurrent episodes of abdominal pain. Each attack involves dull, sometimes severe, midline periumbilical pain lasting several hours, occasionally accompanied by nausea, anorexia, and pallor. During attacks she becomes quiet and wants to lie down; between attacks she is completely well, eats normally, and grows appropriately. Episodes occur roughly twice a month and are sometimes preceded by stress or skipped meals. She denies diarrhea, blood in the stool, weight loss, fever, joint pain, rash, or urinary symptoms. She has no headache during most attacks but reports occasional mild headache. Stool patterns are normal. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. Growth charts show consistent tracking along the 50th percentile. Family history is striking for migraine in her mother and maternal grandmother, both with 'sick headaches.' Prior evaluation by her pediatrician—including stool studies, urinalysis, celiac screening, and an abdominal ultrasound—was unremarkable. There is no travel history, no new foods, and no school avoidance pattern.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, well-nourished girl in no distress at the visit. Abdomen is soft, non-distended, non-tender, with no organomegaly, masses, or guarding; bowel sounds normal. No periumbilical or right lower quadrant tenderness, no rebound. No costovertebral angle tenderness. No perianal lesions or visible blood. Skin without rashes, petechiae, or purpura; no joint swelling. Neurologic exam is normal, including fundoscopy without papilledema, normal gait, and symmetric reflexes. Growth parameters are appropriate and tracking normally. The general physical examination is entirely unremarkable between episodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a functional/migraine-spectrum cause rather than an organic abdominal pathology?
options: Nocturnal pain that consistently wakes her from sleep
Midline periumbilical pain with complete wellness between stereotyped episodes and normal growth
Significant weight loss and bloody stools
Persistent right lower quadrant tenderness with fever
stages.explanation: Midline (periumbilical) pain that comes in stereotyped, self-limited episodes with complete return to baseline health and normal growth strongly favors a functional/migraine-spectrum disorder such as abdominal migraine. Pain farther from the umbilicus, or 'alarm' features, raises concern for organic disease. Nocturnal awakening pain is an alarm feature suggesting organic pathology (e.g., inflammatory bowel disease, peptic disease). Weight loss and bloody stools are red flags for IBD or other serious disease. Persistent right lower quadrant tenderness with fever suggests appendicitis or another inflammatory/surgical process. The combination of midline location, episodic stereotyped nature, normal interval health, and normal growth is the reassuring functional pattern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of criteria best matches a diagnosis of abdominal migraine in this child?
options: Chronic continuous abdominal pain with daily diarrhea
Paroxysmal episodes of intense midline abdominal pain lasting ≥1 hour, with ≥2 associated features (anorexia, nausea, pallor), normal between episodes
Pain only with defecation that improves after a bowel movement
Epigastric burning relieved by antacids and worse when lying flat
stages.explanation: Abdominal migraine (a Rome IV functional disorder and childhood periodic syndrome) is defined by paroxysmal episodes of intense, acute periumbilical/midline/diffuse abdominal pain lasting ≥1 hour (typically the most severe symptom), occurring in a stereotyped pattern with two or more associated features such as anorexia, nausea, vomiting, pallor, or headache, with complete return to baseline health between episodes and no better explanation after appropriate evaluation. Chronic continuous pain with daily diarrhea suggests an organic or other functional GI disorder. Pain relieved by defecation suggests irritable bowel syndrome. Epigastric burning relieved by antacids and worse when supine suggests gastroesophageal reflux. The paroxysmal, stereotyped midline pattern with associated features fits abdominal migraine.

stages.label: PRIOR WORKUP
stages.content:
CBC: normal (Hgb 12.8 g/dL, WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL)
CRP and ESR: normal
Celiac serology (tissue transglutaminase IgA with total IgA): negative
Stool occult blood and stool studies: negative
Urinalysis: normal
Abdominal ultrasound: normal; no structural abnormality
Growth chart: height and weight tracking at the 50th percentile

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy for her recurrent episodes?
options: Begin a strict gluten-free diet indefinitely
Reassure, identify and avoid triggers, optimize sleep/meals/hydration, and use migraine-directed acute therapy; consider prophylaxis if frequent/disabling
Start chronic proton pump inhibitor therapy
Schedule an exploratory laparoscopy
stages.explanation: Management mirrors migraine care: education and reassurance, trigger identification (stress, skipped meals, poor sleep), lifestyle measures (regular meals, hydration, sleep), and migraine-directed acute therapy; preventive agents (e.g., cyproheptadine, propranolol, amitriptyline, or pizotifen where available) are considered for frequent or disabling attacks. A lifelong gluten-free diet is unjustified given a negative celiac screen and no enteropathy features. Chronic proton pump inhibitor therapy targets acid-peptic disease, which is not suggested here. Exploratory laparoscopy is invasive and inappropriate without surgical indications; her exam, growth, and prior workup are reassuring. Recognizing abdominal migraine prevents unnecessary, repeated, invasive testing.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most useful prognostic counseling point to share with the family?
options: Many children with abdominal migraine go on to develop typical migraine headaches later in adolescence or adulthood
These episodes indicate a high risk of abdominal cancer
The condition is contagious and requires isolation during attacks
Symptoms invariably persist unchanged for life
stages.explanation: Abdominal migraine is part of the migraine spectrum (an 'episodic syndrome that may be associated with migraine'), and a substantial proportion of affected children develop typical migraine headaches later in adolescence or adulthood; the abdominal episodes themselves often improve with age. Reassuring families of this natural history reduces anxiety and unnecessary testing. There is no association with abdominal malignancy. The condition is not contagious—this is a neurologic/functional disorder, not an infection. Symptoms generally do not persist unchanged for life; many children improve, though some transition to headache-predominant migraine. Anticipatory guidance about possible future headaches is practically useful.

cases.diagnosis: Abdominal migraine
pearls: Abdominal migraine = paroxysmal, stereotyped midline abdominal pain ≥1 hour with ≥2 associated features (anorexia, nausea, pallor) and complete wellness between episodes.
It is a diagnosis of exclusion supported by a strong migraine family history and a normal exam, growth, and basic workup.
Treat like migraine: lifestyle measures, trigger avoidance, acute therapy, and prophylaxis (e.g., cyproheptadine) if frequent.
Alarm features—nocturnal pain, weight loss, bloody stool, fever, or pain away from the midline—mandate evaluation for organic disease.

cases.title: A Teen With Band-Like Daily Head Pressure During Exams
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports a daily tight, band-like pressure around her head, worst on school days.
cases.history: A 13-year-old girl presents with a 4-month history of near-daily headaches described as a dull, tight, band-like pressure encircling her head, mild to moderate in intensity. The pain is usually bilateral, builds gradually in the afternoon, and is not throbbing. It does not worsen with routine activity such as walking up stairs, and she has no nausea or vomiting. She occasionally notices mild light sensitivity but no sound sensitivity, no visual aura, and no weakness or numbness. Headaches cluster around tests and increased homework, and she reports poor sleep, skipped lunches, and neck/shoulder tension. She has been taking over-the-counter ibuprofen or acetaminophen most days—about 5-6 days per week—with limited relief. She has no known drug allergies. She is doing well academically but feels stressed and anxious about grades. Family history includes a mother with migraines. There is no early-morning headache, no positional change, no fever, and no trauma. Caffeine intake includes one energy drink daily.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent in no acute distress. Visual acuity normal; fundoscopy shows sharp disc margins without papilledema. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Pericranial and trapezius/cervical paraspinal muscles are tender to palpation bilaterally. No meningismus. Motor strength 5/5; tone and reflexes normal and symmetric. Sensation intact; coordination and gait, including tandem, normal. No focal neurologic deficits. No neurocutaneous lesions. Neck range of motion is full though uncomfortable due to muscle tightness. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of features best supports tension-type headache over migraine in this patient?
options: Unilateral throbbing pain with nausea and worsening on exertion
Bilateral, non-throbbing band-like pressure, no nausea, not aggravated by routine activity, with pericranial tenderness
Severe attacks aborted by sleep with photophobia and phonophobia
Visual scintillations preceding the pain
stages.explanation: Tension-type headache is characteristically bilateral, pressing/tightening (non-pulsatile), mild to moderate, not aggravated by routine physical activity, and lacks nausea/vomiting; pericranial muscle tenderness is a supportive finding. This matches her presentation. Unilateral throbbing pain with nausea worsened by exertion describes migraine without aura. Severe attacks relieved by sleep with prominent photophobia and phonophobia also favor migraine. Visual scintillations preceding pain indicate migraine with aura. The defining contrast is migraine's pulsatile, activity-aggravated, nausea-associated pattern versus the dull, bilateral, pressure-type, activity-neutral pattern of tension-type headache. Mild isolated photophobia can occur in tension-type headache but not both photophobia and phonophobia plus nausea.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the history and a normal neurologic exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order brain MRI with contrast
Diagnose tension-type headache clinically and address contributing factors—no neuroimaging needed
Refer for urgent lumbar puncture
Start prophylactic topiramate immediately
stages.explanation: With a classic tension-type headache history and a completely normal neurologic exam (including normal fundoscopy without papilledema), no neuroimaging is indicated; the diagnosis is clinical and management targets contributing factors. Brain MRI is reserved for red flags—abnormal exam, progressive or positional headache, early-morning vomiting, or signs of raised intracranial pressure—none present here. Lumbar puncture would be appropriate only with concern for infection or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, which her exam does not suggest. Prophylactic topiramate is not first-line for episodic tension-type headache; the priority is correcting the obvious drivers (analgesic overuse, stress, poor sleep, skipped meals) before considering pharmacologic prophylaxis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She uses OTC analgesics about 5-6 days per week. What is the most important consequence to recognize and prevent?
options: Aplastic anemia from acetaminophen
Medication-overuse (rebound) headache from frequent analgesic use
Permanent renal failure after one month of NSAIDs
Salicylate-induced Reye syndrome from ibuprofen
stages.explanation: Using acute analgesics on roughly 5-6 days per week risks medication-overuse headache (rebound), in which frequent analgesic intake paradoxically perpetuates and worsens headaches; simple analgesics are generally implicated at ≥15 days/month and triptans/combination agents at ≥10 days/month. Counseling to limit acute medication days is essential. Aplastic anemia is not a typical acetaminophen effect. One month of intermittent NSAIDs does not cause permanent renal failure in an otherwise healthy adolescent, though caution is warranted. Reye syndrome is associated with aspirin (a salicylate) during viral illness, not ibuprofen; ibuprofen is a non-salicylate NSAID. Recognizing and reversing analgesic overuse is the key step.

stages.label: HEADACHE DIARY
stages.content:
4-week diary: headache on 22 of 28 days, dull bilateral band-like pressure, mild-to-moderate
OTC analgesic (ibuprofen or acetaminophen) taken on 21 days — meets criteria for analgesic overuse
No nausea, vomiting, or aura; pain not worsened by routine activity
Sleep averaging 5-6 hours on school nights; lunch frequently skipped; one energy drink daily
Repeat neurologic exam normal; fundoscopy normal with no papilledema

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate overall management plan?
options: Increase scheduled daily ibuprofen to prevent attacks
Limit acute analgesics, address sleep/meals/stress, encourage exercise, and add behavioral therapy (e.g., relaxation/CBT, physical therapy for neck tension)
Begin chronic opioids for daily pain
Prescribe daily butalbital-caffeine-acetaminophen combination
stages.explanation: Best management of episodic tension-type headache is non-pharmacologic-forward: limit acute analgesic days to prevent medication-overuse headache, regularize sleep and meals, ensure hydration, reduce/cap caffeine, manage stress, and add behavioral approaches such as relaxation training, biofeedback, or cognitive behavioral therapy, plus physical therapy for cervical/pericranial tension. Increasing scheduled daily ibuprofen worsens overuse headache and risks GI/renal toxicity. Chronic opioids are contraindicated for benign headache due to dependence and rebound. Daily butalbital-containing combinations are particularly prone to causing dependence and medication-overuse headache and are avoided, especially in adolescents. Treating the lifestyle and stress drivers addresses the root causes here.

cases.diagnosis: Tension-type headache (episodic, with contributing analgesic overuse risk)
pearls: Tension-type headache: bilateral, pressing/tightening, non-pulsatile, mild-moderate, not worsened by activity, no nausea, often with pericranial tenderness.
A classic history plus a normal exam needs no neuroimaging.
Frequent acute analgesic use causes medication-overuse headache—limit acute med days (simple analgesics ≥15 d/mo, combos/triptans ≥10 d/mo).
First-line treatment is behavioral: sleep, meals, hydration, stress reduction, relaxation/CBT, and physical therapy for neck tension.

cases.title: An Obese Teen With Headaches and Transient Visual Blurring
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports worsening daily headaches with brief episodes of graying-out vision and double vision.
cases.history: A 16-year-old girl presents with 6 weeks of progressively worsening headaches, often present on waking and worsened by coughing, straining, or lying flat. She describes brief episodes (seconds) of dimming or 'graying out' of vision in both eyes when standing, and recently new horizontal double vision worse when looking to the sides. She also reports a 'whooshing' sound in her ears (pulsatile tinnitus). She denies weakness, numbness, seizures, or fever. Her BMI is 34 kg/m². She started an oral isotretinoin-like regimen for acne 2 months ago and uses a combined oral contraceptive; she also took a course of doxycycline. She has no known drug allergies. Menses are regular. She is doing well in school but headaches now interfere with reading. Family history is non-contributory for neurologic disease. There is no history of head trauma. Review of systems is otherwise negative except for the visual and auditory symptoms described and intermittent nausea. She denies recent weight loss or constitutional symptoms.
cases.exam: Alert, obese adolescent in mild discomfort. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral optic disc edema (papilledema) with blurred disc margins and obscured vessels. Visual acuity is preserved at 20/25 bilaterally, but visual fields show enlarged blind spots. There is a left abducens (sixth nerve) palsy with incomplete left lateral gaze and horizontal diplopia. Pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Motor strength 5/5, normal tone and reflexes, sensation intact. Coordination and gait are normal. No meningismus, no focal weakness, no rash. The rest of the neurologic and general examination is otherwise unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After identifying papilledema, what is the most appropriate FIRST diagnostic step before lumbar puncture?
options: Proceed directly to lumbar puncture
Obtain neuroimaging (MRI brain, ideally with MR venography) to exclude a mass lesion and venous sinus thrombosis
Start acetazolamide empirically and discharge
Order a CT of the orbits only
stages.explanation: When papilledema and headache suggest raised intracranial pressure, neuroimaging must precede lumbar puncture to exclude a space-occupying lesion (herniation risk) and, importantly, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, which can mimic idiopathic intracranial hypertension—MR venography is preferred. Proceeding directly to lumbar puncture before imaging risks missing a mass or causing herniation. Empiric acetazolamide with discharge is unsafe without confirming the diagnosis and excluding secondary causes, and her abducens palsy needs evaluation. CT of the orbits alone is inadequate; it does not evaluate the brain parenchyma or dural venous sinuses. Standard workup is neuroimaging first, then lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement once a mass and thrombosis are excluded.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: No mass, hydrocephalus, or structural lesion; flattening of the posterior globes and an empty sella noted
MR venography: Patent dural venous sinuses (no thrombosis)
Lumbar puncture opening pressure: 38 cm H2O (markedly elevated; normal ≤25 cm H2O)
CSF analysis: Normal cell count, protein, and glucose
Visual field testing: Enlarged blind spots bilaterally

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding is essential to confirm the diagnosis of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri)?
options: Elevated CSF protein with pleocytosis
Elevated CSF opening pressure with normal CSF composition and normal neuroimaging (excluding other causes)
A ring-enhancing lesion on MRI
Low CSF glucose
stages.explanation: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension is diagnosed by the modified Dandy criteria: signs/symptoms of raised intracranial pressure (papilledema), an elevated CSF opening pressure (typically >25 cm H2O in adults, with higher thresholds in children), normal CSF composition, and normal neuroimaging that excludes other causes (mass, hydrocephalus, venous thrombosis). Her opening pressure of 38 cm H2O with normal CSF and unremarkable MRI/MRV fits. Elevated CSF protein with pleocytosis suggests infection or inflammation, not IIH. A ring-enhancing lesion indicates a mass (abscess/tumor). Low CSF glucose suggests bacterial or fungal meningitis. The defining feature here is high pressure with otherwise normal CSF and imaging.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this patient?
options: Immediate optic nerve sheath fenestration for all patients
Acetazolamide, weight management, and discontinuation of contributing medications, with serial visual field/fundus monitoring
Lifelong high-dose corticosteroids
Therapeutic phlebotomy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of idiopathic intracranial hypertension is acetazolamide (a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production), combined with weight loss and stopping potential precipitants—here the vitamin A derivative (isotretinoin), tetracyclines (doxycycline), and possibly the estrogen-containing contraceptive. Serial monitoring of visual fields and optic discs is essential to detect threatened vision. Surgical options (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting) are reserved for progressive vision loss despite medical therapy, not routine first-line for all. Chronic high-dose corticosteroids are not standard maintenance therapy and cause rebound and weight gain. Therapeutic phlebotomy treats polycythemia, which is unrelated. Protecting vision and lowering pressure define the approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most serious potential complication of untreated or poorly controlled disease that management aims to prevent?
options: Permanent visual loss from chronic papilledema and optic atrophy
Sensorineural hearing loss
Cerebellar herniation
Cognitive decline from cortical atrophy
stages.explanation: The principal danger of idiopathic intracranial hypertension is permanent vision loss: sustained papilledema leads to progressive optic nerve damage and optic atrophy, which can be irreversible—hence close visual field and fundoscopic surveillance and prompt escalation if vision worsens. The pulsatile tinnitus and sixth nerve palsy are characteristic but the sight-threatening optic neuropathy is the feared outcome. Sensorineural hearing loss is not a typical complication (the tinnitus is pulsatile and pressure-related). Cerebellar herniation is not characteristic of IIH because there is no mass or pressure gradient; this is why lumbar puncture is safe once imaging is normal. Cognitive decline from cortical atrophy is not a feature of this disorder.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri)
pearls: Suspect IIH in an obese adolescent with headache, papilledema, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, and a sixth nerve palsy (a false-localizing sign).
Always image (MRI + MRV) before lumbar puncture to exclude a mass and venous sinus thrombosis.
Diagnosis: elevated opening pressure with normal CSF and normal neuroimaging (modified Dandy criteria).
Treat with acetazolamide, weight loss, and stopping triggers (vitamin A derivatives, tetracyclines); the feared complication is permanent vision loss.

cases.title: A Boy With Morning Vomiting and a Wobbly Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with three weeks of morning headaches and vomiting, now with unsteadiness.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for 3 weeks of headaches that are worst in the early morning and associated with vomiting, often before breakfast, after which he feels temporarily better. Over the past week his parents noticed he is increasingly clumsy, veers to one side when walking, and has fallen several times. He has become more tired and irritable. He has no fever, neck stiffness, or recent trauma. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Birth and development were normal, and he had been healthy and active. He attends second grade; teachers recently noted deteriorating handwriting and balance. Family history is unremarkable, with no neurocutaneous disorders or cancers. There were no preceding illnesses, ingestions, or toxin exposures. The headaches have progressively worsened in frequency and intensity, and the morning vomiting has increased over the last several days, prompting this visit. He has not had seizures or loss of consciousness.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing but arousable boy. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilledema. There is a partial right sixth nerve palsy. He has truncal ataxia with a wide-based, unsteady gait and falls toward the right; finger-to-nose testing shows dysmetria, worse on the right, with dysdiadochokinesia. Tone and strength are grossly preserved; deep tendon reflexes are normal. No meningismus. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; speech mildly scanning. No focal long-tract sensory loss. No neurocutaneous lesions. Head circumference is normal for age. The combination of bradycardia, hypertension, and impaired upgaze raises concern for elevated intracranial pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings most strongly localizes the problem and signals raised intracranial pressure?
options: Peripheral weakness with hyporeflexia and normal mentation
Morning headache/vomiting, papilledema, bradycardia/hypertension, and cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysmetria)
Acute flaccid paralysis after a viral illness
Isolated unilateral facial droop with hyperacusis
stages.explanation: Early-morning headache with vomiting that relieves the pain, papilledema, a sixth nerve palsy (false-localizing), and Cushing-type vital signs (bradycardia, hypertension) indicate raised intracranial pressure, while truncal ataxia, gait instability, dysmetria, and scanning speech localize to the cerebellum/posterior fossa. This pattern in a child is classic for a posterior fossa mass causing obstructive hydrocephalus. Peripheral weakness with hyporeflexia suggests a neuromuscular/peripheral process, not a central posterior fossa lesion. Acute flaccid paralysis after a viral illness suggests Guillain-Barré or acute flaccid myelitis. Isolated facial droop with hyperacusis points to a peripheral facial nerve (Bell) palsy. The cerebellar plus raised-ICP picture is the key localization.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate imaging in the emergency department?
options: Plain skull radiographs
Urgent neuroimaging—CT head now (with MRI brain to follow) to evaluate for a posterior fossa mass and hydrocephalus
Lumbar puncture first to measure opening pressure
Electroencephalogram
stages.explanation: With signs of raised intracranial pressure and posterior fossa localization, urgent neuroimaging is required; a non-contrast head CT is fast and readily available in the ED to detect a mass and hydrocephalus, with MRI brain (and spine) to follow for full characterization and staging. Skull radiographs are inadequate to evaluate the brain. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when raised intracranial pressure from a mass is suspected because of the risk of cerebellar herniation—imaging must come first. EEG evaluates seizures, which are not the issue here. Prompt imaging guides emergent neurosurgical management of the obstructing lesion and hydrocephalus.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: Hyperdense midline cerebellar (vermian) mass filling the fourth ventricle
Obstructive hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles
MRI brain: Heterogeneously enhancing posterior fossa mass arising from the vermis, restricting diffusion
MRI spine: Performed for staging — no drop metastases identified
CSF (obtained later, post-decompression): pending cytology

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority for this child?
options: Discharge with outpatient neurology follow-up
Begin oral chemotherapy in the ED
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed cerebellar abscess and observe
Neurosurgical consultation for management of hydrocephalus and tumor resection, with corticosteroids for edema
stages.explanation: This is a neurosurgical emergency: the child needs urgent neurosurgical involvement to address obstructive hydrocephalus (e.g., external ventricular drain or endoscopic third ventriculostomy as indicated) and to plan maximal safe tumor resection, with corticosteroids (dexamethasone) to reduce peritumoral edema. Discharge is unsafe given raised intracranial pressure and a posterior fossa mass. Chemotherapy is part of multimodality therapy but is determined after surgical diagnosis/staging and is not initiated emergently in the ED. Antibiotics for a presumed abscess are inappropriate—the imaging shows a solid enhancing tumor, not an abscess, and 'observe' would be dangerous. Stabilizing intracranial pressure and securing tissue diagnosis are the priorities.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the imaging, which posterior fossa tumor is the most likely diagnosis in this 7-year-old?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma (cystic with mural nodule, off-midline)
Medulloblastoma (midline vermian, enhancing, fills fourth ventricle, diffusion-restricting)
Brainstem (diffuse intrinsic pontine) glioma
Vestibular schwannoma
stages.explanation: A midline (vermian) heterogeneously enhancing posterior fossa mass filling the fourth ventricle with diffusion restriction (reflecting dense small round blue cells), causing obstructive hydrocephalus in a young school-age child, is classic for medulloblastoma—the most common malignant pediatric brain tumor, which can seed the CSF (hence spine MRI for staging). Pilocytic astrocytoma is typically a cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule, often more lateral and lower grade, without diffusion restriction. Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma arises in the pons with cranial neuropathies and long-tract signs, not a fourth-ventricle vermian mass. Vestibular schwannoma is a cerebellopontine-angle tumor of adults (and NF2), presenting with hearing loss, not this picture.

cases.diagnosis: Medulloblastoma (posterior fossa) with obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Morning headache + vomiting + ataxia + papilledema in a child = posterior fossa mass with raised ICP until proven otherwise.
Image before lumbar puncture when raised ICP from a mass is suspected—LP risks herniation.
Medulloblastoma: midline vermian, enhancing, fills the 4th ventricle, restricts diffusion, and can seed CSF—stage with spine MRI and CSF cytology.
Emergent management addresses hydrocephalus and tumor resection; treatment is multimodal (surgery, craniospinal radiation in older children, chemotherapy).

cases.title: A Girl With Slowly Progressive Clumsiness and Headache
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report several months of worsening clumsiness, headaches, and morning nausea.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl presents with a 4-month history of gradually worsening balance and coordination. She trips often, has trouble with handwriting and buttons, and tends to fall toward the left. She has intermittent headaches, occasionally with mild morning nausea, and her teacher notes she now sits closer to the board. Symptoms have progressed slowly rather than abruptly. She denies fever, weight loss, seizures, or recent illness. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Birth and early development were normal; she was previously athletic. There is no history of trauma or toxin exposure. Family history is unremarkable, with no neurofibromatosis or other neurocutaneous disorders and no early cancers. She is in fourth grade with previously good performance now slipping due to coordination difficulty. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. Parents specifically deny any acute viral prodrome preceding the symptoms, and the course has been steadily progressive over months.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative girl. Fundoscopy shows mild blurring of disc margins suggesting early papilledema. Left-sided appendicular ataxia with dysmetria on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin, and dysdiadochokinesia on the left. Wide-based gait with a tendency to fall to the left; tandem gait impaired. Mild gaze-evoked nystagmus on left lateral gaze. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; no facial weakness. Strength 5/5 with normal tone; reflexes symmetric. Sensation intact. No meningismus, no neurocutaneous stigmata. Speech is normal. The findings localize to the left cerebellar hemisphere, and the slow progression with subtle raised-pressure signs is notable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the clinical course most helps distinguish this presentation from acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia?
options: Symmetric ataxia immediately after a varicella infection
Slowly progressive, lateralized cerebellar signs over months with headache and early papilledema
Complete resolution within 2-3 weeks without treatment
Acute onset over hours with no headache
stages.explanation: A slowly progressive course over months with lateralized (left) cerebellar signs accompanied by headache and early papilledema points to a structural posterior fossa lesion (a cerebellar tumor), not a self-limited inflammatory process. Acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia typically follows a viral illness (classically varicella), is symmetric, has an abrupt onset over hours to days, and resolves spontaneously within weeks without progressive or raised-pressure features. Complete resolution in 2-3 weeks and acute onset without headache also describe the benign post-infectious entity. The progressive, focal, headache-associated pattern with papilledema mandates neuroimaging to evaluate for a mass.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to characterize the suspected lesion?
options: Non-contrast head CT only
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Electroencephalogram
Nerve conduction studies
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced brain MRI is the test of choice to characterize a posterior fossa mass: it defines the cystic and solid components, enhancement pattern, location, and relationship to the fourth ventricle, and assesses for hydrocephalus. Non-contrast CT can detect a mass and hydrocephalus and is useful emergently, but it cannot fully characterize enhancement or the mural nodule and lacks MRI's posterior fossa detail. EEG evaluates seizures, which are not present. Nerve conduction studies assess peripheral nerve/muscle disorders, irrelevant to a central cerebellar lesion. MRI guides the surgical plan and narrows the differential among pediatric posterior fossa tumors.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: Well-circumscribed cystic mass in the left cerebellar hemisphere
Avidly enhancing mural nodule within the cyst wall
Minimal surrounding edema; no diffusion restriction in the solid component
Mild dilation of the ventricles consistent with early/partial obstruction
No leptomeningeal enhancement or spinal drop metastases on staging MRI

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which tumor is most consistent with a cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule and no diffusion restriction?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor
stages.explanation: A well-circumscribed cystic cerebellar mass with an avidly enhancing mural nodule, minimal edema, and no diffusion restriction is the classic appearance of a juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO grade 1), the most common pediatric brain tumor and typically a cerebellar hemispheric lesion. Medulloblastoma is usually a midline vermian, solid, enhancing mass that fills the fourth ventricle and restricts diffusion (densely cellular)—the opposite imaging signature. Ependymoma classically arises within the fourth ventricle and extends through its foramina ('plastic' tumor), often heterogeneous with calcification. Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor occurs in very young children, is aggressive, and restricts diffusion. The cyst-plus-mural-nodule pattern is the hallmark of pilocytic astrocytoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate primary management and the expected prognosis?
options: Upfront craniospinal radiation as the primary treatment
Maximal safe surgical resection, which is often curative given the favorable tumor biology
Observation alone with no intervention despite progressive symptoms
Immediate high-dose multiagent chemotherapy instead of surgery
stages.explanation: The mainstay for pilocytic astrocytoma is maximal safe surgical resection; gross-total resection of this low-grade (WHO grade 1) tumor is frequently curative with excellent long-term survival, and it also relieves mass effect and hydrocephalus. Upfront craniospinal radiation is not primary therapy—pilocytic astrocytoma is not a CSF-seeding embryonal tumor like medulloblastoma, and radiation in young children carries significant neurocognitive and endocrine toxicity, reserved for select unresectable/progressive cases. Observation alone is inappropriate given progressive neurologic deficits and raised-pressure signs. Immediate high-dose chemotherapy is not first-line; chemotherapy (often targeted, e.g., MEK inhibitors in BRAF-altered tumors) is reserved for unresectable, recurrent, or progressive disease, especially when located where surgery is risky.

cases.diagnosis: Pilocytic astrocytoma (juvenile cerebellar, WHO grade 1)
pearls: Cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule and no diffusion restriction = pilocytic astrocytoma (most common pediatric brain tumor).
Contrast MRI is the imaging test of choice; lateralized progressive cerebellar signs with headache warrant urgent imaging.
Gross-total surgical resection is often curative for this WHO grade 1 tumor.
Distinguish from medulloblastoma (midline, solid, fills 4th ventricle, diffusion-restricting, CSF-seeding) and ependymoma (4th-ventricle, 'plastic' extension).

cases.title: A Boy Who Stopped Growing and Keeps Bumping Into Things
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report he has not grown in over a year and seems to miss objects to his sides.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy presents with concerns that he has not grown in clothing or shoe size for more than a year and his height has fallen off his prior growth curve. His parents also note he bumps into door frames and people approaching from the sides and now sits very close to the television. He has had intermittent dull headaches and increased thirst with frequent urination, including nighttime bathroom trips. He has become more fatigued. He denies seizures, weakness, or trauma. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Birth and early development were normal, and he was previously tracking along the 50th percentile for height before plateauing. He is in fifth grade with declining energy. Family history is unremarkable; parents are of average height. Review of systems is notable for cold intolerance and constipation. There is no history of head injury, and he has not yet entered puberty. The growth plateau and visual complaints prompted this referral from his pediatrician.
cases.exam: Short-statured, prepubertal boy appearing younger than his stated age; height now below the 5th percentile after prior 50th-percentile tracking. Visual field testing by confrontation reveals a bitemporal hemianopia. Visual acuity mildly reduced; fundoscopy shows mild optic disc pallor without florid papilledema. Extraocular movements full; remaining cranial nerves intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits; strength 5/5, reflexes slightly delayed in relaxation. Gait normal. Skin dry; no neurocutaneous lesions. Tanner stage 1 genitalia for age. The combination of growth failure, a bitemporal field cut, and signs of hormone deficiency points to a sellar/suprasellar process compressing the optic chiasm and pituitary.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The bitemporal hemianopia localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Optic chiasm (midline compression of crossing nasal retinal fibers)
Left optic tract
Occipital cortex
Single optic nerve
stages.explanation: Bitemporal hemianopia results from compression of the optic chiasm, where the crossing nasal retinal fibers (which carry the temporal visual fields) are affected—classically by a sellar/suprasellar mass pushing on the chiasm from below. This fits a suprasellar tumor with associated pituitary/hypothalamic dysfunction. A single optic tract lesion causes a contralateral homonymous hemianopia, not a bitemporal pattern. Occipital cortex lesions cause homonymous hemianopias (often with macular sparing), not bitemporal defects. A single optic nerve lesion causes monocular vision loss in that eye, not a bilateral temporal field cut. The bitemporal pattern is the hallmark of chiasmal compression and strongly suggests a suprasellar mass.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given growth failure, a chiasmal field defect, and polyuria/polydipsia, what is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Reassurance and repeat growth measurement in 1 year
MRI of the brain/pituitary with contrast plus endocrine and visual evaluation
Plain skull radiograph alone
Empiric growth hormone therapy without imaging
stages.explanation: The combination of growth arrest, a bitemporal hemianopia, and polyuria/polydipsia (suggesting diabetes insipidus) points to a sellar/suprasellar mass; the best next step is a dedicated contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain/pituitary, alongside formal visual field testing and a full endocrine workup (anterior and posterior pituitary axes). Waiting a year is unsafe given progressive vision and endocrine compromise. A skull radiograph might reveal suprasellar calcification but cannot evaluate the chiasm, hypothalamus, or pituitary and is inadequate. Empiric growth hormone without imaging is dangerous—it ignores a likely mass and could be inappropriate or harmful before the lesion and full hormonal status are characterized. Imaging plus endocrine and ophthalmologic assessment is the correct integrated approach.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain/pituitary with contrast: Suprasellar cystic-solid mass with calcification, compressing the optic chiasm and extending toward the hypothalamus/third ventricle
Low IGF-1 and low free T4 with inappropriately low TSH (central hypothyroidism)
Morning cortisol low; ACTH low (central adrenal insufficiency)
Serum Na 148 mEq/L with dilute urine (urine osmolality low) — consistent with central diabetes insipidus
Visual fields: confirmed bitemporal hemianopia

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits a calcified, cystic-solid suprasellar mass causing chiasmal compression and panhypopituitarism in a child?
options: Pituitary microadenoma
Craniopharyngioma
Germinoma without calcification
Rathke cleft cyst (purely cystic, non-calcified)
stages.explanation: A suprasellar mass that is cystic and solid with calcification, compressing the optic chiasm and causing hypopituitarism plus diabetes insipidus in a child, is classic for craniopharyngioma (commonly the adamantinomatous type, which contains calcification and 'machinery oil' cyst fluid). It is the most common non-glial pediatric sellar tumor. A pituitary microadenoma is small, intrasellar, usually hormone-secreting, and does not typically calcify or compress the chiasm at that size. Germinoma can occur in the suprasellar region and cause diabetes insipidus but characteristically lacks the dense calcification and cystic 'motor oil' appearance. A Rathke cleft cyst is a purely cystic, non-calcified, usually asymptomatic lesion. The calcified cystic-solid pattern with endocrine and visual compromise defines craniopharyngioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before any surgical intervention, which management step is most critical for this child?
options: Address endocrine deficiencies—particularly stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage and thyroid/desmopressin management—and coordinate neurosurgery/ophthalmology
Start growth hormone immediately to restore height
Begin chemotherapy as primary therapy
Restrict all fluids to correct the hypernatremia
stages.explanation: Before surgery, the priority is correcting and protecting endocrine function: cortisol (central adrenal insufficiency) must be replaced and stress-dosed to prevent adrenal crisis—glucocorticoids before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating crisis—plus thyroid hormone replacement and careful management of central diabetes insipidus with desmopressin and fluids, coordinated with neurosurgery and ophthalmology. Starting growth hormone first is inappropriate and unsafe while a mass and untreated adrenal/thyroid deficits exist. Chemotherapy is not primary therapy for craniopharyngioma (management centers on surgery with or without radiation). Strict fluid restriction is harmful in central diabetes insipidus, where the kidneys cannot concentrate urine; this risks dangerous hypernatremia and volume depletion—treatment is desmopressin with appropriate fluid balance.

cases.diagnosis: Craniopharyngioma (suprasellar, adamantinomatous type)
pearls: Growth failure + bitemporal hemianopia + diabetes insipidus in a child = suprasellar mass compressing the chiasm and pituitary—think craniopharyngioma.
Imaging hallmark: calcified, cystic-solid suprasellar mass ('motor oil' cyst fluid) on contrast MRI/CT.
Replace glucocorticoids before thyroid hormone and manage central diabetes insipidus with desmopressin before surgery.
Treatment is surgical resection with or without radiation; hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction often persists and needs lifelong endocrine care.

cases.title: A 6-Year-Old Girl With Double Vision and a Crooked Smile
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive double vision, facial droop, and clumsiness over the past six weeks.
cases.history: A previously healthy 6-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her parents for six weeks of progressive symptoms. She first developed intermittent horizontal double vision, then a right-sided facial droop noticed by her kindergarten teacher. Over the last two weeks she has become increasingly clumsy, falling more often and struggling to use a fork with her right hand. Her parents report her speech has become slightly slurred and she occasionally coughs while drinking. There has been no fever, headache that wakes her, vomiting, or seizure activity, though she complains of mild morning headache that improves through the day. Birth and developmental history are normal; she met all milestones on time and is reading at grade level. Immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger brother; no sick contacts, travel, or trauma. Family history is unremarkable, with no neurocutaneous disorders, neurofibromatosis, cancers, or early neurologic disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative for weight loss, rash, or joint pain.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive, in no distress. Cranial nerves: right abduction deficit (CN VI palsy) and right peripheral-pattern facial weakness (CN VII), with a diminished gag and mild palatal asymmetry (CN IX/X). Pupils equal and reactive; fundi without papilledema. Motor: subtle right-sided pronator drift with 4+/5 strength and brisk right-sided reflexes; an upgoing right plantar response. Sensation intact. Cerebellar: dysmetria on finger-to-nose and a wide-based, unsteady tandem gait. Speech mildly dysarthric. No meningismus, no skin café-au-lait macules, no neurocutaneous stigmata. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly localizes the lesion to the brainstem (pons)?
options: Mild morning headache that improves during the day
The combination of multiple cranial neuropathies with contralateral long-tract and cerebellar signs
Falls and clumsiness alone
Slurred speech in isolation
Absence of papilledema on funduscopy
stages.explanation: Crossed findings—ipsilateral cranial nerve palsies (CN VI, VII, IX/X) together with long-tract signs (drift, hyperreflexia, Babinski) and cerebellar dysfunction—are the hallmark of an intra-axial pontine lesion, because the pons packs cranial nerve nuclei alongside descending corticospinal and cerebellar pathways. Morning headache suggests raised intracranial pressure but does not localize. Falls/clumsiness and dysarthria are nonspecific in isolation and could arise from cerebellum, cortex, or peripheral causes. Absence of papilledema actually argues against high pressure and does not localize a lesion; many infiltrating pontine tumors present before frank hydrocephalus. The clustering of several cranial neuropathies with crossed motor and cerebellar signs is what pins the lesion to the pons.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Lumbar puncture for cytology before imaging
Non-contrast head CT only
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium
EEG to evaluate for seizures
Stereotactic brain biopsy of the pons
stages.explanation: MRI with and without contrast is the imaging modality of choice for a suspected brainstem lesion: it characterizes infiltrative pontine pathology far better than CT and guides further management. Lumbar puncture before imaging risks herniation if there is a posterior fossa mass and raised pressure, and CSF cytology is not the first step. Non-contrast CT is insensitive in the posterior fossa due to beam-hardening artifact and would miss or underestimate an infiltrative tumor. EEG is irrelevant without seizures and does not evaluate structural brainstem disease. Upfront stereotactic biopsy is not the immediate next step and historically has been reserved for atypical cases; the diagnosis here is typically radiographic, so imaging comes first.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
T2/FLAIR: diffuse, expansile hyperintense signal infiltrating and enlarging the pons
Tumor involves >50% of the pontine cross-section with engulfment of the basilar artery
T1 post-gadolinium: minimal, patchy enhancement
No discrete cystic or calcified components
Effacement of the fourth ventricle without overt hydrocephalus
No leptomeningeal enhancement; remainder of brain normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture and MRI, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma of the cerebellum
Medulloblastoma
Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered)
Brainstem encephalitis (rhombencephalitis)
Pontine cavernous malformation
stages.explanation: A diffusely infiltrative, T2-hyperintense, pons-expanding mass that engulfs the basilar artery with little enhancement in a 5-7 year old presenting with multiple cranial neuropathies, long-tract signs, and ataxia is classic diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (now classified as diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered). Pilocytic astrocytoma is typically a well-circumscribed, avidly enhancing cerebellar cyst-with-mural-nodule, not an infiltrative pontine mass. Medulloblastoma arises in the midline cerebellum/fourth ventricle as an enhancing, restricting mass, often with hydrocephalus. Rhombencephalitis is usually febrile and post-infectious with a different, often patchy MRI pattern and clinical course. A cavernoma shows a 'popcorn' hemorrhagic appearance with a hemosiderin rim, not diffuse infiltration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard first-line treatment that provides temporary symptom relief and survival benefit?
options: Gross total surgical resection of the pontine mass
Fractionated focal radiation therapy to the brainstem
Whole-brain radiation plus prophylactic intrathecal chemotherapy
Bevacizumab monotherapy
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt alone
stages.explanation: Fractionated focal radiation therapy is the standard of care and the only intervention reliably shown to provide transient clinical improvement and modest survival benefit in DIPG; conventional chemotherapy has not improved outcomes, and the prognosis remains poor (median survival under one year). Surgical resection is not feasible: the tumor diffusely infiltrates eloquent brainstem and cannot be safely removed; biopsy is now done for molecular profiling and trials but is not therapeutic. Whole-brain radiation with intrathecal chemotherapy is the paradigm for leukemic CNS disease/medulloblastoma staging, not DIPG. Bevacizumab is not standard first-line therapy. A VP shunt addresses hydrocephalus only and does not treat the tumor; this patient has no overt hydrocephalus.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The parents ask what they should expect over time. What is the most accurate counseling about prognosis?
options: This tumor is usually curable with radiation alone
Despite an initial response to radiation, DIPG is almost uniformly fatal, with median survival under one year
Most children are cured with chemotherapy after surgery
The tumor is benign and will stop growing on its own
Survival is typically more than ten years with current therapy
stages.explanation: DIPG (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered) carries a grim prognosis: most children show transient symptomatic improvement after radiation but then progress, with median survival under one year and very few long-term survivors—honest, compassionate counseling and early palliative-care involvement are essential. It is not curable with radiation alone; radiation buys time and quality of life but does not eradicate the infiltrative tumor. Surgery plus chemotherapy does not cure DIPG; the tumor is unresectable and chemoresistant. It is a high-grade malignancy, not benign, and will not self-arrest. Survival beyond a decade is exceptionally rare and does not represent the expected course.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered)
pearls: Crossed findings—ipsilateral cranial neuropathies with contralateral long-tract and cerebellar signs—localize a lesion to the brainstem.
DIPG classically presents in children 5-9 with the triad of multiple cranial neuropathies, long-tract signs, and ataxia evolving over weeks.
MRI shows a diffusely expansile, T2-hyperintense pons that engulfs the basilar artery with minimal enhancement; the diagnosis is largely radiographic.
Focal radiation gives temporary benefit but prognosis is dismal; surgery is not an option and conventional chemotherapy does not improve survival.

cases.title: A 5-Year-Old Boy With Café-au-Lait Spots and Fading Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening vision in one eye noted during routine neurofibromatosis follow-up.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) presents to the pediatric neurology clinic for routine surveillance. His mother reports that over the past two to three months he has been sitting closer to the television and bumping into furniture on his left side. He does not complain of pain, headache, or vomiting, and there has been no early-morning emesis or behavior change. He was diagnosed with NF1 at age 2 based on six café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, and a first-degree relative (his father) with the condition. He has met developmental milestones, though he receives speech support at school. He takes no daily medications and has no allergies. He lives with both parents; his father has multiple cutaneous neurofibromas. There is no history of prematurity, perinatal complications, or head trauma. Review of systems is notable only for the vision concern; appetite, growth, sleep, and bowel/bladder function are normal. He has had no precocious puberty signs. Prior ophthalmology exams were normal, and he has not had a brain MRI before this visit.
cases.exam: Alert and cooperative. Skin: eight café-au-lait macules >5 mm, bilateral axillary and inguinal freckling, two small cutaneous neurofibromas. Visual acuity reduced in the left eye (20/80) versus the right (20/25). A left relative afferent pupillary defect is present. Confrontation fields suggest a left temporal field deficit. Funduscopy shows mild left optic disc pallor; no papilledema. Lisch nodules visible on slit-lamp. Extraocular movements full. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Motor, sensory, and cerebellar exams normal; gait normal. No precocious puberty on general exam. No proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The relative afferent pupillary defect and decreased acuity in the left eye localize the problem to which structure?
options: The lens or cornea (refractive media)
The retina or optic nerve (anterior visual pathway)
The extraocular muscles
The occipital cortex
The cavernous sinus
stages.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) signifies asymmetric conduction in the afferent visual pathway—optic nerve or extensive retinal disease—on the affected side; combined with reduced acuity and disc pallor it points to an optic nerve/anterior visual pathway lesion. Refractive media problems (cataract, corneal scar) reduce acuity but do not cause an RAPD. Extraocular muscle problems cause diplopia and motility deficits, not an afferent defect. An occipital cortical lesion causes homonymous field loss with normal pupils and no RAPD because the defect is post-chiasmal and bilateral input to pupils is preserved. The cavernous sinus affects CN III, IV, VI, and V1/V2, producing ophthalmoplegia rather than an isolated afferent defect.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate progressive vision loss in this child with NF1?
options: Reassurance and recheck in one year
MRI of the brain and orbits with and without contrast
CT of the orbits without contrast
Genetic testing for NF1 mutation
Visual evoked potentials only
stages.explanation: Progressive vision loss, an RAPD, and a field defect in a child with NF1 mandate MRI of the brain and orbits with and without contrast to evaluate the optic pathway, the most common location for NF1-associated gliomas. Reassurance is inappropriate when there is objective, progressive deficit. CT exposes a child to radiation, poorly characterizes the optic nerve/chiasm, and is inferior to MRI for soft-tissue pathway tumors. Genetic testing is unnecessary—the NF1 diagnosis is already established clinically and would not address the acute concern. Visual evoked potentials can document conduction delay but do not provide anatomic diagnosis; they cannot replace imaging when a structural pathway tumor is suspected.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & ORBITS
stages.content:
Fusiform enlargement of the left optic nerve extending to the optic chiasm
T2 hyperintense, mildly enhancing lesion along the anterior visual pathway
Kinking/buckling of the left optic nerve within the orbit
No hydrocephalus; hypothalamus not involved
Scattered T2 hyperintensities in basal ganglia and cerebellum (NF1 'unidentified bright objects')
No leptomeningeal disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Optic neuritis
Optic pathway glioma (low-grade pilocytic astrocytoma)
Craniopharyngioma
Optic nerve sheath meningioma
Retinoblastoma
stages.explanation: In NF1, a fusiform, T2-hyperintense, enhancing enlargement of the optic nerve extending toward the chiasm in a young child is the classic optic pathway glioma, almost always a low-grade pilocytic astrocytoma. Optic neuritis presents with acute, often painful vision loss and an enhancing but not fusiform-expansile nerve, and is uncommon in this age/NF1 context. Craniopharyngioma is a suprasellar cystic/calcified mass arising from Rathke pouch remnants, typically causing bitemporal hemianopia and endocrine dysfunction, not fusiform optic-nerve enlargement. Optic nerve sheath meningioma shows 'tram-track' enhancement and occurs in adults. Retinoblastoma is an intraocular mass of early childhood presenting with leukocoria, not a fusiform optic-nerve lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
