stages.question: This child has progressive, symptomatic vision loss from his optic pathway glioma. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Immediate gross total surgical resection of the optic nerve
Focal radiation therapy to the optic pathway
Chemotherapy (e.g., carboplatin/vincristine) or a MEK inhibitor
Observation with serial MRI and no treatment
High-dose corticosteroids and plasma exchange
stages.explanation: For a progressive, vision-threatening optic pathway glioma, chemotherapy—classically carboplatin plus vincristine, or increasingly a MEK inhibitor such as selumetinib in NF1—is first-line, aiming to halt progression and preserve vision while avoiding the toxicities of radiation. Surgical resection of an intrinsic optic nerve/chiasm glioma would sacrifice vision and is reserved for select disfiguring blind-eye or exophytic cases. Radiation is avoided when possible in young children and especially in NF1 because of moyamoya/vasculopathy, secondary tumors, and neurocognitive/endocrine harm. Pure observation is appropriate only for stable, asymptomatic lesions—this child has documented progression. Steroids/plasma exchange treat inflammatory optic neuritis, not a glioma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because the tumor extends to the optic chiasm near the hypothalamic-pituitary region, which additional evaluation should be arranged?
options: Bone marrow biopsy
Endocrine evaluation for hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction (including growth and precocious puberty)
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies
Echocardiogram
24-hour ambulatory EEG
stages.explanation: Chiasmal/hypothalamic optic pathway gliomas can disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, so endocrine evaluation is warranted to detect growth hormone abnormalities, precocious (or delayed) puberty, and other deficits, with ongoing monitoring of growth and pubertal staging—important because chemotherapy and tumor location both affect endocrine function. A bone marrow biopsy is for hematologic malignancy/staging and is irrelevant to a low-grade glioma. EMG/nerve conduction studies assess peripheral nerve/muscle disease, not the optic pathway or hypothalamus. An echocardiogram evaluates cardiac structure and is unrelated here. Ambulatory EEG investigates seizures, which are not part of this presentation; the chiasmal extension specifically raises endocrine concern.

cases.diagnosis: Optic pathway glioma in neurofibromatosis type 1
pearls: Optic pathway glioma is the most common CNS tumor in NF1 and is usually a low-grade pilocytic astrocytoma.
A relative afferent pupillary defect with decreased acuity and disc pallor localizes to the optic nerve/anterior visual pathway.
MRI of brain and orbits shows fusiform, T2-hyperintense enlargement of the optic nerve/chiasm; biopsy is often unnecessary in the NF1 context.
Treat only progressive or vision-threatening tumors; chemotherapy or a MEK inhibitor is first-line, and radiation is avoided in NF1 due to vasculopathy and secondary malignancy risk.

cases.title: A 14-Year-Old Football Player Who Feels 'Foggy' After a Hit
cases.chiefComplaint: Headache and feeling dazed after a helmet-to-helmet collision during a football game.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents and athletic trainer about 45 minutes after a helmet-to-helmet hit during a high school football game. He did not lose consciousness but was briefly slow to get up and appeared 'dazed,' asking the same question repeatedly on the sideline. He now reports a diffuse headache rated 5/10, mild nausea without vomiting, and a sense of mental fogginess and difficulty concentrating. He denies neck pain, weakness, numbness, double vision, or seizure. There has been no worsening drowsiness; he has been conversant the entire time. He has no prior concussions, no bleeding disorder, and takes no medications other than occasional ibuprofen. No allergies. He is an honor-roll student and plays multiple sports. No alcohol or drug use. Family history is unremarkable. Immunizations up to date. He was wearing a properly fitted helmet. There was no penetrating injury, and he did not strike the ground with his head. He is otherwise healthy with no chronic illnesses.
cases.exam: Awake, alert, fully oriented; GCS 15. Pupils equal, round, reactive; extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No focal motor or sensory deficits; strength 5/5 and reflexes symmetric. Cerebellar testing intact; tandem and Romberg slightly off with subjective dizziness. Cervical spine nontender with full painless range of motion; no midline tenderness. No scalp laceration, hematoma, or skull deformity; no raccoon eyes, Battle sign, hemotympanum, or CSF rhinorrhea/otorrhea. Cognitive screen shows mild delayed recall deficit and slowed processing. Remainder of the neurologic and general exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Concussion (mild traumatic brain injury)
Epidural hematoma
Diffuse axonal injury
Cervical spine fracture
Subdural hematoma
stages.explanation: A transient alteration in mental status (dazed, repetitive questioning) after head impact, with headache, fogginess, and a nonfocal exam at GCS 15, is the classic picture of concussion—a functional injury without structural damage. An epidural hematoma typically follows a lucid interval with progressive deterioration and often a focal deficit or blown pupil; this boy has remained alert and nonfocal. Diffuse axonal injury causes prolonged loss of consciousness/coma after high-energy trauma, not a brief dazed state. A cervical spine fracture would produce midline neck tenderness or neuro deficits, both absent here. Subdural hematoma usually involves more severe trauma or anticoagulation and causes progressive decline; nothing here suggests it.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: Using validated pediatric decision rules (e.g., PECARN), what is the most appropriate next step regarding neuroimaging?
options: Obtain a non-contrast head CT immediately
Obtain an MRI of the brain
No neuroimaging; clinical observation is appropriate
Obtain skull radiographs
Obtain CT angiography of the head and neck
stages.explanation: This adolescent has GCS 15, no LOC beyond a brief dazed state, no signs of basilar skull fracture, no focal deficit, no severe mechanism, and no vomiting—so PECARN and similar rules place him at very low risk for clinically important traumatic brain injury, and no imaging is indicated; observation is appropriate. A head CT exposes a child to radiation without expected benefit in low-risk concussion. MRI is not used acutely for uncomplicated concussion and adds no immediate value. Skull radiographs do not assess intracranial injury and are obsolete for this purpose. CT angiography evaluates vascular injury (e.g., suspected dissection) and is unwarranted without focal signs or a concerning mechanism.

stages.label: OBSERVATION & SYMPTOM SCREEN
stages.content:
Serial neuro checks over 2 hours: GCS 15, stable, no new deficits
Headache improved to 3/10 with acetaminophen
No vomiting; tolerated oral fluids
SCAT symptom inventory: headache, fogginess, dizziness, difficulty concentrating
Balance Error Scoring System: mildly increased errors
No deterioration in mental status

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate return-to-play and return-to-learn guidance at discharge?
options: Resume full-contact play immediately since CT was not needed
Strict bed rest in a dark room for one week, then return to play
Relative rest 24-48 hours, then gradual stepwise return to learn and a separate medically supervised return-to-play progression, advancing only when symptom-free
Return to school and sports simultaneously once the headache resolves
No school or physical activity until a normal MRI is obtained
stages.explanation: Current consensus (e.g., Berlin/Amsterdam concussion guidelines) favors a brief period (24-48 hours) of relative rest followed by a gradual, stepwise return to cognitive activity (return-to-learn) and a separate, medically supervised return-to-play progression; the athlete must be symptom-free and off restrictions before full contact, advancing one stage per day and stepping back if symptoms recur. Immediate return to contact risks second-impact syndrome and is contraindicated. Prolonged strict bed rest/'cocooning' in a dark room actually delays recovery. Return-to-learn should precede return-to-play, not occur simultaneously. MRI is not required for uncomplicated concussion and should not gate return to normal activity.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The family asks what catastrophic complication can occur if he returns to contact and sustains a second hit before fully recovering. What should you counsel them about?
options: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy developing within days
Second-impact syndrome with diffuse cerebral edema
Immediate epidural hematoma formation
Permanent retrograde amnesia
Cervical disc herniation
stages.explanation: Second-impact syndrome is a rare but catastrophic event in which a second head injury sustained before recovery from a first concussion triggers loss of cerebral autoregulation and rapid, often fatal, diffuse cerebral edema—this is precisely why premature return to contact is dangerous. Chronic traumatic encephalopathy is a long-term neurodegenerative process linked to repetitive head impacts over years, not a days-long complication. An epidural hematoma is caused by arterial bleeding from skull/dural vessel injury and is not a direct consequence of returning too soon. Permanent retrograde amnesia and cervical disc herniation are not the feared sequela of a too-early second concussive hit.

cases.diagnosis: Concussion (mild traumatic brain injury)
pearls: Concussion is a functional injury: transient altered mental status with a nonfocal exam and GCS 15; structural imaging is normal.
Apply PECARN to spare low-risk children CT; brief dazed state without LOC, vomiting, focal signs, or basilar skull fracture signs needs only observation.
Manage with 24-48 hours relative rest, then stepwise return-to-learn before a separate, supervised return-to-play; advance only when symptom-free.
Second-impact syndrome—rapid, potentially fatal cerebral edema from a second hit before recovery—is why premature return to contact is contraindicated.

cases.title: An 11-Year-Old Who 'Woke Up Fine' Then Became Unresponsive
cases.chiefComplaint: Declining consciousness several hours after a fall that struck the side of his head.
cases.history: An 11-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off his bicycle and striking the left side of his head on a curb. He was not wearing a helmet. According to his older sister, he had a brief loss of consciousness for under a minute, then 'woke up totally fine,' walked home, and even ate a snack while complaining only of a left-sided headache. About two hours later he became progressively drowsy, vomited twice, and then became difficult to arouse, prompting the 911 call. On arrival he is somnolent and confused. There is no reported seizure. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known bleeding disorder or allergies. Immunizations are up to date. He lives with his parents and sister; no prior head injuries. There is no family history of bleeding diatheses. The paramedics note a boggy swelling over the left temple. No other trauma is reported, and there is no neck pain complaint, though assessment is limited by his mental status.
cases.exam: Somnolent, GCS 10 (E2 V3 M5), arousable to pain. Left temporal boggy hematoma. Pupils: left 5 mm and sluggishly reactive, right 3 mm and reactive—an emerging left-sided anisocoria. Funduscopy limited. Right-sided pronator drift with decreased movement of the right arm and leg to stimulation; reflexes brisker on the right with an equivocal right Babinski. Neck not formally cleared. Breathing pattern irregular. No external CSF leak noted. The combination of bradycardia, hypertension, and irregular respirations is noted by the team.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The classic 'lucid interval' in this child—initial transient loss of consciousness, a period of normal behavior, then rapid deterioration—is most characteristic of which injury?
options: Concussion
Epidural hematoma
Subarachnoid hemorrhage from aneurysm
Acute subdural hematoma
Diffuse axonal injury
stages.explanation: A lucid interval—brief LOC, then a normal interval, then deterioration—classically describes an epidural hematoma, where arterial bleeding (often the middle meningeal artery after a temporal blow) accumulates and raises pressure over hours. Concussion does not cause progressive obtundation with a focal deficit and anisocoria. Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage causes sudden thunderclap headache and meningismus, not a trauma-related lucid interval in a child. Acute subdural hematoma tends to follow higher-energy trauma with immediate, persistent depressed consciousness and is more common with bridging-vein tears (or in infants/elderly). Diffuse axonal injury produces immediate prolonged coma after severe acceleration-deceleration, without a lucid interval.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic study?
options: MRI of the brain
Non-contrast head CT
Lumbar puncture
Skull radiographs
Cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the immediate study of choice in acute head trauma with a deteriorating mental status: it is fast, widely available, and excellent at detecting acute extra-axial blood and mass effect, allowing rapid neurosurgical decision-making. MRI takes longer, is less accessible emergently, and is unnecessary to identify an acute hematoma requiring surgery. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a mass lesion and signs of raised intracranial pressure because of herniation risk. Skull radiographs cannot evaluate intracranial hemorrhage and would delay care. Cerebral angiography is reserved for suspected vascular lesions, not the emergent evaluation of a traumatic hematoma.

stages.label: NON-CONTRAST HEAD CT
stages.content:
Biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense extra-axial collection over the left temporoparietal region
Collection does not cross the left coronal suture
Non-displaced left temporal bone fracture overlying the collection
8 mm rightward midline shift with effacement of the left lateral ventricle
Early left uncal herniation; basal cisterns compressed
No contralateral hemorrhage

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the CT findings, anisocoria, and Cushing triad, what is the definitive management?
options: Admit for serial neuro checks and repeat CT in 6 hours
Emergent neurosurgical craniotomy for hematoma evacuation
Start levetiracetam and observe
Administer mannitol and discharge once stable
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
stages.explanation: A large epidural hematoma with midline shift, anisocoria, and Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates impending herniation and requires emergent craniotomy for hematoma evacuation—the definitive, life-saving treatment. Observation with delayed repeat CT is unacceptable for an actively herniating patient. Levetiracetam may be used for seizure prophylaxis but does not address the expanding mass. Mannitol (and hyperventilation) are temporizing measures to reduce intracranial pressure while preparing for surgery—not a substitute for evacuation, and discharge is absolutely contraindicated. Lumbar puncture in the setting of a mass and herniation is contraindicated and would be fatal.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: While arranging the operating room, which temporizing measures are most appropriate to manage his elevated intracranial pressure?
options: Lay him flat and give a fluid bolus
Elevate the head of bed to 30°, ensure airway/oxygenation, and give hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline)
Deeply hyperventilate to a PaCO2 of 20 mmHg for the next several hours
Administer high-dose corticosteroids
Begin aggressive antihypertensive therapy to normalize blood pressure
stages.explanation: Bridging measures for raised intracranial pressure include head-of-bed elevation to ~30° to promote venous drainage, securing the airway with adequate oxygenation and ventilation, and hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline) to draw fluid out of the brain—all while expediting surgery. Lying flat and fluid-bolusing can worsen ICP. Prolonged aggressive hyperventilation to PaCO2 ~20 causes excessive vasoconstriction and ischemia; only brief, mild hyperventilation is used as a last-ditch temporizer. Steroids do not help traumatic brain edema and may be harmful. The hypertension here is a compensatory Cushing response to maintain cerebral perfusion; aggressively lowering it can drop cerebral perfusion pressure and worsen ischemia.

cases.diagnosis: Epidural hematoma
pearls: A lucid interval after temporal head trauma—brief LOC, normal interval, then deterioration—is the classic epidural hematoma story.
Epidural hematomas are biconvex (lens-shaped) on CT, do not cross suture lines, and often arise from middle meningeal artery injury with an overlying temporal fracture.
Anisocoria plus Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) signals impending uncal herniation—a surgical emergency.
Definitive treatment is emergent craniotomy; head-up positioning, airway control, and hyperosmolar therapy are temporizing bridges to the OR.

cases.title: A 4-Month-Old With Vomiting, Seizure, and a Bulging Fontanelle
cases.chiefComplaint: Lethargy, vomiting, and a brief seizure in a previously well infant with no reported trauma.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents for two days of poor feeding and vomiting, then a 2-minute generalized seizure at home today, after which he was limp and difficult to wake. The parents report no falls, trauma, or illness; one parent states the infant 'just stopped acting right.' Birth history was full-term, uncomplicated vaginal delivery. He was previously feeding and developing normally per the parents. He takes vitamin D drops only; no other medications, no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Social history: lives with both parents; mother recently returned to work and a relative provides daytime care. The history is internally inconsistent—the mechanism offered does not match the severity—and there were prior ED visits for 'fussiness.' Family history is noncontributory; no bleeding disorders. There is no fever, diarrhea, or known sick contacts. On further questioning the timeline of the seizure and feeding difficulty remains vague and changes between caregivers, raising concern. The child has been admitted to the pediatric ICU for monitoring.
cases.exam: Lethargic infant with a full, tense anterior fontanelle. Increasing head circumference plotted above prior percentile. Pupils equal and reactive; funduscopy by ophthalmology pending. No external bruising on initial survey, though a faint healing bruise is noted over the left upper arm. No fever or neck retraction beyond fontanelle fullness. Tone mildly decreased with sluggish Moro. No obvious deformities; no oral injuries seen. Anterior chest and abdomen without visible marks on first look. Skin survey otherwise unremarkable at presentation. Respirations slightly irregular. Remainder of exam limited by lethargy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a previously well 4-month-old with seizure, lethargy, a bulging fontanelle, and a history that does not fit the severity, what should be at the top of the differential?
options: Benign febrile seizure
Abusive head trauma (inflicted traumatic brain injury)
Viral gastroenteritis with dehydration
Infantile colic
Simple accidental fall from the couch
stages.explanation: Abusive head trauma must top the differential when an infant presents with serious neurologic findings—seizure, lethargy, bulging fontanelle, expanding head circumference—and a history that is absent, vague, inconsistent, or implausible for the injury severity. A febrile seizure requires fever and is benign and brief, not associated with a tense fontanelle and obtundation. Gastroenteritis can cause vomiting and dehydration but not a bulging fontanelle with seizure and a focal exam. Colic is a benign cry pattern in a well-appearing infant. A simple couch fall does not produce severe intracranial injury and seizure; a discrepant mechanism is itself a red flag for inflicted trauma.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: Which set of investigations is most appropriate to evaluate for inflicted injury and its complications?
options: Head CT only
Head CT/MRI, dilated retinal exam by ophthalmology, and a skeletal survey
Lumbar puncture and blood cultures only
Abdominal ultrasound and stool studies
EEG and reassurance
stages.explanation: A complete abusive-head-trauma workup pairs neuroimaging (CT to detect acute hemorrhage, with MRI to characterize subdural collections of differing ages and parenchymal injury) with a dilated fundoscopic exam by ophthalmology to document retinal hemorrhages, and a skeletal survey to find occult fractures. Head CT alone misses retinal hemorrhages and skeletal injuries that establish the pattern of abuse. LP and cultures address meningitis, which is not the leading concern given the tense fontanelle and trauma signs—and LP is risky with raised pressure. Abdominal ultrasound/stool studies do not evaluate the head. EEG with reassurance fails to investigate the suspected mechanism and abandons the child to ongoing risk.

stages.label: IMAGING, EYE EXAM & LABS
stages.content:
Head CT/MRI: bilateral subdural hematomas of different ages (acute and subacute) over the convexities
No overlying skull fracture; mild cerebral edema
Dilated retinal exam: extensive multilayered retinal hemorrhages extending to the periphery, bilateral
Skeletal survey: healing posterior rib fractures and a classic metaphyseal lesion of the distal tibia
CBC and coagulation studies (PT/PTT/INR, platelets, fibrinogen): within normal limits
No evidence of a bleeding disorder

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given bilateral subdurals of differing ages, multilayered retinal hemorrhages, healing rib and metaphyseal fractures, and normal coagulation, what is the diagnosis?
options: Glutaric aciduria type 1
Benign external hydrocephalus with spontaneous subdural
Abusive head trauma (non-accidental trauma)
Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn (vitamin K deficiency)
Menkes disease
stages.explanation: The constellation of bilateral subdural hematomas of different ages, extensive multilayered retinal hemorrhages, and classic abuse-specific fractures (posterior rib fractures and a metaphyseal corner lesion) with normal coagulation is diagnostic of abusive head trauma; the discrepant history further supports it. Glutaric aciduria type 1 can cause subdurals but typically shows macrocephaly with frontotemporal atrophy and metabolic abnormalities, not abuse-specific fractures and florid retinal hemorrhages. Benign external hydrocephalus may predispose to thin subdurals but not retinal hemorrhages with classic fractures. Vitamin K deficiency bleeding would show abnormal coagulation, which is normal here. Menkes disease presents with hair changes, hypotonia, and metabolic copper abnormalities, not this pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Discharge home with close outpatient neurology follow-up
Stabilize the child medically and file a mandated report to Child Protective Services with a multidisciplinary child-protection consult
Counsel the parents on safe sleep and send home
Order genetic and metabolic testing and defer any reporting
Start empiric meningitis antibiotics and observe
stages.explanation: After medically stabilizing the infant (airway, seizures, intracranial pressure, neurosurgery consult as needed), the clinician is a mandated reporter and must notify Child Protective Services and involve a child-protection/abuse team; the child cannot be discharged to a potentially unsafe environment. Discharging home, or simply counseling on safe sleep, leaves the child at risk and ignores legal duty. Pursuing metabolic/genetic testing is reasonable to exclude mimics but must not delay mandated reporting and protection. Empiric meningitis antibiotics treat an unsupported diagnosis; the imaging, eye exam, and skeletal survey point to inflicted trauma, and protective action is the priority.

cases.diagnosis: Abusive head trauma (inflicted subdural hematoma)
pearls: Suspect abusive head trauma when neurologic findings are severe and the history is absent, vague, inconsistent, or implausible for the injury.
The workup triad is neuroimaging (CT plus MRI), a dilated retinal exam, and a skeletal survey; bilateral subdurals of differing ages plus multilayered retinal hemorrhages are classic.
Posterior rib fractures and metaphyseal corner ('bucket-handle') lesions are highly specific for inflicted injury; always check coagulation to exclude bleeding disorders.
Clinicians are mandated reporters: stabilize the child, consult a child-protection team, and report to Child Protective Services.

cases.title: A 3-Year-Old Who Suddenly Can't Walk Straight
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of a staggering, wide-based gait and inability to sit or stand steadily.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents because he woke up this morning unable to walk normally—he staggers, falls repeatedly, and cannot sit on the floor without toppling over. The change came on over a single day. He is fully alert, talking normally, and recognizes his parents. There has been no fever today, no vomiting, no headache, no neck stiffness, and no recent head trauma. About two and a half weeks ago he had a classic case of chickenpox with the typical crops of vesicles, which have since crusted and resolved; he was otherwise well in the interim. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He is partially immunized—his parents declined the varicella vaccine. He attends daycare, where there was a varicella outbreak. Development was previously normal with age-appropriate gait and speech. Family history is unremarkable. There has been no ingestion of medications or toxins at home, and no possibility of access to alcohol or other substances per the parents.
cases.exam: Alert, playful, and interactive child in no distress; normal mental status. Cranial nerves intact; no nystagmus appreciated at rest, mild end-gaze nystagmus on lateral gaze. Truncal ataxia with titubation; he cannot sit unsupported without swaying. Gait is wide-based and staggering with frequent falls. Limb dysmetria on reaching for toys; intention tremor. Strength is 5/5 throughout with normal, symmetric reflexes and downgoing plantars. Sensation grossly intact. No meningismus, no rash (chickenpox lesions fully crusted/healed), no papilledema. No focal weakness or asymmetry. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the acute truncal and gait ataxia with a normal mental status and a recent varicella infection, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Posterior fossa tumor
Post-infectious (post-varicella) acute cerebellar ataxia
Bacterial meningitis
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Acute ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: Acute cerebellar ataxia is the most common cause of acute ataxia in young children and classically follows a viral illness—particularly varicella—by 1-3 weeks; the abrupt truncal/gait ataxia with a normal sensorium and otherwise nonfocal exam fits perfectly. A posterior fossa tumor usually produces subacute, progressive symptoms with headache, vomiting, and papilledema, not a sudden post-viral picture. Bacterial meningitis causes fever, meningismus, and toxicity—absent here. Guillain-Barré causes ascending weakness with areflexia (and a variant with ataxia), but this child has normal strength and reflexes. Stroke is rare in healthy toddlers and would typically cause focal, lateralized deficits rather than symmetric cerebellar signs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: Which initial step is most important to safely support the clinical diagnosis and exclude dangerous mimics?
options: Reassure and discharge without any evaluation
Neuroimaging (MRI, or CT if MRI unavailable) to exclude a posterior fossa mass, plus consider toxicology screen
Immediate lumbar puncture before any imaging
Start IV antibiotics empirically and admit to the ICU
Nerve conduction studies and electromyography
stages.explanation: Although post-infectious acute cerebellar ataxia is largely a clinical diagnosis of exclusion, neuroimaging (MRI preferred; CT if MRI is unavailable) is important to rule out a posterior fossa tumor or stroke, and a toxicology screen should be considered because accidental ingestion is a leading mimic of acute ataxia in toddlers. Discharging without evaluation risks missing a tumor or ingestion. Lumbar puncture before imaging in a child with possible posterior fossa pathology risks herniation; LP, if done, follows imaging. Empiric antibiotics and ICU admission are unwarranted in an afebrile, well-appearing child without meningismus. Nerve conduction studies evaluate peripheral neuropathy/Guillain-Barré, which the normal strength and reflexes argue against.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
MRI brain: normal; no posterior fossa mass, no stroke, no hydrocephalus
Urine toxicology screen: negative
CBC: WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL, normal differential
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
Lumbar puncture (after normal imaging): mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (8 WBC/µL), protein mildly elevated, glucose normal
CSF Gram stain and bacterial cultures: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging is normal and the picture fits post-varicella acute cerebellar ataxia. What is the most appropriate management and prognosis to convey?
options: High-dose IV acyclovir for life-threatening encephalitis
Supportive care with reassurance; the condition is self-limited and usually resolves over days to weeks
Long-term anticonvulsant therapy
Urgent neurosurgical decompression
Lifelong physical disability is expected
stages.explanation: Post-infectious acute cerebellar ataxia is self-limited; management is supportive (safety from falls, reassurance, observation), and the great majority of children recover completely over days to a few weeks. High-dose acyclovir treats varicella encephalitis—a distinct, more severe entity with altered mental status and a different MRI/CSF picture—not this benign post-viral ataxia in an alert child. Anticonvulsants are not indicated; there are no seizures. Neurosurgical decompression addresses a mass or hydrocephalus, neither present here. The prognosis is excellent, so predicting lifelong disability is incorrect and would inappropriately alarm the family.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: The parents ask how this could have been prevented. What is the best counseling point?
options: Acute cerebellar ataxia cannot be prevented in any way
Routine varicella vaccination markedly reduces chickenpox and its post-infectious complications, including this ataxia
Prophylactic antibiotics during chickenpox prevent ataxia
Avoiding all daycare prevents the condition
Vitamin supplementation prevents post-viral ataxia
stages.explanation: Because this ataxia is a recognized post-infectious complication of varicella, routine varicella immunization—which this child did not receive—is the best preventive measure, sharply reducing both chickenpox and its sequelae. Saying it is wholly unpreventable ignores the role of vaccination. Antibiotics do not affect a viral illness or its immune-mediated post-infectious complications. Avoiding daycare is impractical and does not address susceptibility; an unvaccinated child remains at risk from any exposure. Vitamin supplementation has no role in preventing post-viral cerebellar ataxia. This is a teachable moment to update immunizations.

cases.diagnosis: Post-varicella acute cerebellar ataxia
pearls: Acute cerebellar ataxia is the most common cause of acute ataxia in young children and typically follows a viral illness (classically varicella) by 1-3 weeks.
The hallmark is abrupt truncal/gait ataxia with a normal mental status and an otherwise nonfocal exam.
Workup excludes mimics: neuroimaging for a posterior fossa mass/stroke and a toxicology screen for ingestion; CSF may show mild lymphocytic pleocytosis.
It is self-limited with an excellent prognosis—supportive care only—and is largely preventable with varicella vaccination.

cases.title: An 18-Month-Old With 'Dancing Eyes' and Jerky Movements
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapid chaotic eye movements, body jerks, and new unsteadiness over the past two weeks.
cases.history: An 18-month-old girl is admitted to the pediatric ward for two weeks of progressively abnormal movements. Her parents first noticed rapid, chaotic, multidirectional eye movements that do not stop even when she sleeps briefly, followed by jerky muscle twitches of her limbs and trunk and increasing unsteadiness so that she has stopped walking independently. She has also become extremely irritable, with disrupted sleep and frequent inconsolable crying—a marked change from her usually calm temperament. There has been no fever, no recent vaccination reaction, and no clear preceding illness, though she had a mild cold a month ago. She was born at term and was developing normally, walking by 13 months, with several words. She takes no medications and has no allergies; immunizations are up to date. She lives with both parents and an older sibling. There is no family history of neurologic or movement disorders. Review of systems is otherwise negative for vomiting, diarrhea, weight loss, or rash, though her parents note she seems less interested in feeding lately.
cases.exam: Irritable, intermittently inconsolable toddler. Eyes: continuous rapid, involuntary, chaotic, multidirectional conjugate saccades (opsoclonus) persisting through attempted fixation. Frequent brief, arrhythmic myoclonic jerks of the limbs and trunk. Marked truncal ataxia—she cannot sit steadily or stand. Tone mildly reduced; strength grossly normal; reflexes normal. No focal weakness, no nystagmus of the slow-fast type, no meningismus. Abdomen soft without a palpable mass on this exam. No skin lesions. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. The combination of opsoclonus, myoclonus, and ataxia with behavioral change is the dominant finding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of opsoclonus (chaotic eye movements), myoclonus, and ataxia with marked irritability in a toddler is most consistent with which syndrome?
options: Benign paroxysmal vertigo of childhood
Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome (OMS, 'dancing eyes-dancing feet')
Acute cerebellar ataxia
Infantile spasms
Tic disorder
stages.explanation: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome is defined by the combination of opsoclonus (involuntary, chaotic, multidirectional saccades), myoclonus, and ataxia, frequently accompanied by extreme irritability and sleep disruption in a young child—exactly this presentation. Benign paroxysmal vertigo causes brief recurrent episodes of imbalance/fear with normal eyes between spells, not persistent opsoclonus. Acute cerebellar ataxia features ataxia after a viral illness but lacks opsoclonus and myoclonus and is not associated with this profound irritability. Infantile spasms are clusters of flexor/extensor spasms with hypsarrhythmia on EEG, typically in infants under 12 months, not this triad. Tics are stereotyped, suppressible movements, not chaotic saccades with ataxia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: OMS in a toddler is a paraneoplastic syndrome until proven otherwise. What is the most important next step?
options: Reassurance—this is a benign self-limited condition
Search for an occult neuroblastoma: MRI/CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis plus urine catecholamines (HVA/VMA)
EEG to look for hypsarrhythmia
Start levetiracetam and discharge
Genetic testing for a movement disorder
stages.explanation: OMS in young children is most often a paraneoplastic manifestation of neuroblastoma, so the priority is a diligent tumor hunt: cross-sectional imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis (MRI or CT) to find a neuroblastoma anywhere along the sympathetic chain/adrenal glands, plus urine catecholamine metabolites (homovanillic acid and vanillylmandelic acid). Calling it benign is wrong and would miss a treatable malignancy. EEG/hypsarrhythmia evaluates infantile spasms, not OMS. Levetiracetam and discharge neither treats the syndrome nor finds the tumor. Genetic testing for a primary movement disorder is not the priority when an occult neuroblastoma must be excluded.

stages.label: TUMOR WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI abdomen/pelvis: 2.5 cm paraspinal mass adjacent to the left adrenal gland
CT chest: no thoracic mass; lesion confined to the abdomen
Urine homovanillic acid (HVA): elevated
Urine vanillylmandelic acid (VMA): elevated
MIBG scan: focal uptake in the left paraspinal mass
Brain MRI: no structural lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the imaging and elevated urine catecholamines, what is the underlying tumor driving this paraneoplastic syndrome?
options: Wilms tumor
Neuroblastoma
Hepatoblastoma
Pheochromocytoma
Ganglioglioma of the brain
stages.explanation: A paraspinal/adrenal mass with elevated urine HVA and VMA and avid MIBG uptake in a toddler is classic neuroblastoma, the tumor most commonly associated with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome. Wilms tumor is an intrarenal mass that does not secrete catecholamines or cause OMS. Hepatoblastoma is a hepatic mass with elevated alpha-fetoprotein, not catecholamines. Pheochromocytoma also secretes catecholamines and is MIBG-avid but is rare in toddlers, typically causes paroxysmal hypertension, and is not the usual cause of OMS. A cerebral ganglioglioma is an intracranial tumor and would not explain an adrenal mass with urinary catecholamines.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of the neurologic syndrome itself?
options: Observation alone; the neurologic symptoms always resolve once any tumor is removed
Immunotherapy (corticosteroids/ACTH, IVIG, and/or rituximab) in addition to treating the neuroblastoma
Antiepileptic monotherapy
Antibiotics for presumed post-infectious encephalitis
Deep brain stimulation
stages.explanation: OMS is immune-mediated, so its neurologic symptoms are treated with immunotherapy—corticosteroids or ACTH, IVIG, and/or rituximab—alongside resection/treatment of the underlying neuroblastoma; tumor removal alone often does not resolve the neurologic syndrome and many children have relapsing/chronic neurodevelopmental sequelae requiring ongoing immunomodulation. Observation is inadequate because symptoms frequently persist or relapse and untreated OMS carries a high risk of long-term cognitive and motor impairment. Antiepileptics do not treat the myoclonus/ataxia of OMS. Antibiotics treat bacterial infection, which is not the mechanism. Deep brain stimulation has no role in this immune-mediated paraneoplastic syndrome.

cases.diagnosis: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome secondary to neuroblastoma
pearls: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome ('dancing eyes-dancing feet') is the triad of opsoclonus, myoclonus, and ataxia, often with severe irritability and sleep disruption.
In young children OMS is paraneoplastic until proven otherwise—hunt for occult neuroblastoma with chest/abdomen/pelvis imaging and urine HVA/VMA.
Neuroblastoma is the classic associated tumor: a catecholamine-secreting, MIBG-avid paraspinal/adrenal mass.
OMS is immune-mediated: treat with immunotherapy (steroids/ACTH, IVIG, rituximab) plus tumor therapy; relapses and neurodevelopmental sequelae are common.

cases.title: A 9-Year-Old With Fidgety Movements and Mood Swings
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of involuntary, jerky movements and uncharacteristic emotional outbursts.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is brought to clinic for about six weeks of involuntary movements that her parents and teacher describe as constant fidgeting, dropping objects, and clumsy, 'piano-playing' finger movements. Her handwriting has deteriorated and she has become unusually tearful and irritable, with rapid mood swings and difficulty concentrating in school. The movements are irregular, flow from one body part to another, worsen with stress, and disappear during sleep. There is no headache, fever, weakness, or sensory loss now. About two months ago she had a sore throat that was not tested or treated, which resolved on its own. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications; no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. She lives with her parents; there are no sick contacts currently. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with 'rheumatic heart disease.' Review of systems is negative for recent joint pain or swelling, rash, and chest symptoms, though her mother recalls she complained of one knee hurting briefly a few weeks ago. There is no recent travel or new medication exposure.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative but emotionally labile, tearful at times. Involuntary, irregular, non-rhythmic, flowing movements of the face, tongue, and limbs (chorea) that migrate unpredictably and increase with distraction. 'Milkmaid grip' (inability to maintain a steady handgrip) and a 'spooning' posture of the outstretched hands with pronation. Darting tongue ('jack-in-the-box'). Motor strength normal but with motor impersistence; deep tendon reflexes show a 'hung-up' (pendular) knee jerk. No rigidity, no weakness, no sensory deficit. Cardiac exam: a soft systolic murmur is auscultated. No rash or subcutaneous nodules seen. Gait mildly clumsy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The irregular, flowing, migrating involuntary movements with emotional lability and motor impersistence are best characterized as which movement?
options: Tics
Chorea
Tremor
Myoclonus
Dystonia
stages.explanation: Chorea consists of brief, irregular, non-rhythmic, involuntary movements that flow unpredictably from one body region to another, classically with motor impersistence (milkmaid grip, darting tongue) and worsening with stress—exactly this child's findings. Tics are stereotyped, repetitive, and suppressible, often preceded by an urge, and are not flowing/migratory. Tremor is a rhythmic oscillation, unlike the random pattern here. Myoclonus is a sudden, brief, shock-like jerk, not a continuous flowing movement. Dystonia produces sustained or patterned muscle contractions causing twisting postures, distinct from the fleeting, dance-like quality of chorea.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given chorea, emotional lability, and a recent untreated sore throat in a school-aged girl, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Wilson disease
Sydenham chorea (rheumatic chorea)
Huntington disease
Functional (psychogenic) movement disorder
Tourette syndrome
stages.explanation: Sydenham chorea is a post-streptococcal, immune-mediated movement disorder—a major Jones criterion for acute rheumatic fever—that classically appears weeks to months after group A strep pharyngitis in a school-aged child, with chorea plus emotional lability; this fits perfectly. Wilson disease can cause movement abnormalities but typically presents later with Kayser-Fleischer rings, liver disease, and a more dystonic/tremulous picture. Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant adult-onset disorder (juvenile forms are rigid, not choreic, and have a family history of HD). A functional disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion and would not explain the strep link and cardiac murmur. Tourette involves tics, not chorea.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer: elevated
Anti-DNase B: elevated
Throat culture: negative (infection already resolved)
ESR: 28 mm/hr; CRP mildly elevated
Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation
CBC, electrolytes, and ceruloplasmin: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Sydenham chorea is one of the major criteria used to diagnose which condition, of which it can be the sole presenting manifestation?
options: Systemic lupus erythematosus
Acute rheumatic fever
Kawasaki disease
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: Sydenham chorea is a major Jones criterion for acute rheumatic fever and may be the only manifestation, sometimes appearing months after the inciting strep infection when other features (carditis, arthritis) have resolved; the elevated ASO/anti-DNase B and the echocardiographic mitral regurgitation here support rheumatic involvement. Lupus can cause chorea but would have additional multisystem features and specific autoantibodies. Kawasaki disease is a febrile vasculitis with mucocutaneous findings and coronary risk, not chorea. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is a chronic arthritis without chorea. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis follows strep but presents with hematuria, hypertension, and edema, not a movement disorder.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is an essential long-term management step for this patient, regardless of symptomatic treatment of the chorea?
options: Lifelong levodopa therapy
Long-term secondary antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., penicillin) to prevent recurrent rheumatic fever
Chronic corticosteroids indefinitely
Deep brain stimulation
No follow-up is needed once chorea resolves
stages.explanation: Because Sydenham chorea is a manifestation of acute rheumatic fever, the patient requires long-term secondary antibiotic prophylaxis (typically penicillin) to prevent recurrent group A strep infections and progressive rheumatic heart disease—an essential, guideline-based step independent of treating the chorea (which often resolves over weeks to months and may be managed symptomatically with dopamine antagonists or valproate if disabling). Levodopa is for parkinsonism, not chorea. Indefinite corticosteroids are not standard maintenance. Deep brain stimulation is not used for this self-limited chorea. Saying no follow-up is needed ignores the cardiac risk and the high recurrence rate without prophylaxis.

cases.diagnosis: Sydenham chorea (post-streptococcal, a major criterion for acute rheumatic fever)
pearls: Sydenham chorea is post-streptococcal and is a major Jones criterion for acute rheumatic fever; it may be the sole manifestation, appearing weeks to months later.
Recognize chorea by its irregular, flowing, migratory movements with motor impersistence (milkmaid grip, darting tongue) and emotional lability.
Support the diagnosis with elevated ASO/anti-DNase B and screen the heart with echocardiography for rheumatic carditis.
Long-term secondary penicillin prophylaxis is essential to prevent recurrent rheumatic fever; chorea is often self-limited and treated symptomatically if disabling.

cases.title: A 10-Year-Old With Repetitive Movements and Sounds
cases.chiefComplaint: More than a year of recurrent involuntary movements and throat sounds that wax and wane.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is brought to clinic for involuntary movements and sounds that began about 18 months ago. They started as frequent eye blinking and head jerking, later joined by shoulder shrugging and intermittent throat-clearing and grunting sounds. The movements and sounds wax and wane in type and severity, worsen with stress and excitement, and can be briefly suppressed with effort—after which he feels a buildup of inner tension that is relieved by performing them. He reports a premonitory 'urge' before many of them. He also has repetitive hand-washing rituals and a need for symmetry, plus longstanding inattention and impulsivity noted by teachers. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Development was normal. He lives with both parents; a paternal uncle reportedly had 'nervous twitches' as a child. There is no recent strep infection, fever, or new medication, and no exposure to stimulants or toxins. School performance is affected mainly by inattention and by teasing about the movements. Sleep and appetite are normal. There is no family history of Huntington disease or other progressive neurologic illness.
cases.exam: Alert, pleasant, age-appropriate boy. During the visit he exhibits frequent eye blinking, head jerks, and shoulder shrugs (motor tics) along with throat-clearing and occasional grunting (vocal tics); these briefly diminish when he concentrates and increase when discussing stressful topics. No chorea (movements are stereotyped and suppressible, not flowing), no dystonic posturing, no weakness. Cranial nerves, strength, tone, reflexes, coordination, and gait are all normal. No Kayser-Fleischer rings. Mood is normal; he describes anxiety about the tics. Mental status otherwise intact. The remainder of the neurologic exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: These stereotyped, suppressible movements and sounds preceded by a premonitory urge are best classified as what?
options: Chorea
Tics
Myoclonus
Seizures
Stereotypies of autism
stages.explanation: Tics are stereotyped, repetitive, non-rhythmic movements (motor) or sounds (vocal/phonic) that are typically preceded by a premonitory urge, can be briefly suppressed (with rising inner tension and relief upon release), and wax and wane—precisely this boy's pattern. Chorea is irregular and flowing, not stereotyped or suppressible, and lacks a premonitory urge. Myoclonus is a sudden shock-like jerk without an urge or suppressibility. Seizures involve altered awareness or EEG correlates and are not voluntarily suppressible with a relieving release. Stereotypies (as in autism) are rhythmic, fixed, soothing movements beginning earlier in life and not preceded by an urge, occurring in a different developmental context.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic for more than a year beginning before age 18, what is the diagnosis?
options: Provisional (transient) tic disorder
Tourette syndrome
Persistent (chronic) motor tic disorder
Sydenham chorea
Functional movement disorder
stages.explanation: Tourette syndrome requires multiple motor tics AND one or more vocal/phonic tics (not necessarily concurrently), present for more than one year, with onset before age 18—this boy meets all criteria. A provisional tic disorder is diagnosed when tics have been present for less than one year, which excludes this 18-month course. Persistent (chronic) motor OR vocal tic disorder applies when only one modality (motor or vocal, but not both) is present for over a year; he has both. Sydenham chorea is post-streptococcal flowing chorea, not stereotyped suppressible tics. A functional movement disorder lacks the premonitory urge and suppressibility characteristic of tics.

stages.label: COMORBIDITY SCREEN
stages.content:
Obsessive-compulsive symptoms: hand-washing rituals and need for symmetry (consistent with OCD)
ADHD screen: positive for inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive symptoms
No evidence of recent group A streptococcal infection (negative ASO/anti-DNase B)
Normal metabolic panel and ceruloplasmin
Neurologic exam otherwise normal; no neuroimaging indicated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His tics are bothersome but not disabling, and he has comorbid OCD and ADHD. What is the most appropriate first-line management of the tics?
options: Immediate antipsychotic medication for the tics
Education/reassurance and behavioral therapy—Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics (CBIT/habit reversal)
Deep brain stimulation
Long-term penicillin prophylaxis
Strict avoidance of all stress and removal from school
stages.explanation: First-line management of tics is education and reassurance plus behavioral therapy—Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics (CBIT), which includes habit-reversal training; medication (alpha-2 agonists like guanfacine/clonidine, or antipsychotics for severe, disabling tics) is reserved for when tics are functionally impairing. Starting an antipsychotic immediately is excessive for tics that are bothersome but not disabling and carries metabolic/movement side effects. Deep brain stimulation is reserved for severe, refractory adult cases. Penicillin prophylaxis treats rheumatic fever/Sydenham chorea, not Tourette. Removing him from school and eliminating all stress is impractical, harmful to development, and not evidence-based; comorbid OCD and ADHD should also be addressed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling regarding the natural history of his tics?
options: Tics inevitably worsen and become permanently disabling in adulthood
Tics typically wax and wane, often peaking in early adolescence and frequently improving by late adolescence or adulthood
Tics indicate an underlying neurodegenerative disease
Tics will resolve completely within a few weeks
Tics mean he should avoid all physical activity
stages.explanation: Tics characteristically wax and wane in type and severity, commonly peak in severity around early adolescence (roughly ages 10-12), and then frequently diminish substantially by late adolescence or adulthood—reassuring counseling that supports a conservative approach. Tics are not a sign of neurodegeneration; Tourette syndrome is not progressive or degenerative. They do not inevitably worsen into permanent disability. They will not resolve in a few weeks—this is a chronic, fluctuating course over years, distinguishing it from a provisional tic disorder. There is no need to avoid physical activity; staying active and engaged is appropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Tourette syndrome (with comorbid OCD and ADHD)
pearls: Tics are stereotyped, suppressible movements/sounds with a premonitory urge that wax and wane—distinguishing them from chorea, myoclonus, and stereotypies.
Tourette syndrome = multiple motor tics plus one or more vocal tics for over a year, onset before age 18; OCD and ADHD are common comorbidities.
First-line treatment is education and behavioral therapy (CBIT/habit reversal); medication is reserved for impairing tics, and comorbidities should be treated.
Counsel that tics typically peak in early adolescence and often improve by adulthood; Tourette is not neurodegenerative.

cases.title: A 7-Year-Old With Blinking and Throat-Clearing
cases.chiefComplaint: A few months of excessive eye-blinking and recurrent throat-clearing.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother, who is worried about excessive eye-blinking that began about three months ago, soon followed by frequent throat-clearing. The movements come and go—some days they are barely noticeable and other days more frequent—and they increase when he is tired or excited and lessen when he is absorbed in play. He can stop them briefly if asked. He is otherwise completely well, with no headache, fever, weakness, or change in behavior or school performance. His pediatrician already evaluated and treated him for allergies and 'dry eyes' without improvement, and an eye exam was normal. He has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications, and has no allergies to medications. Immunizations are up to date. He lives with both parents and a younger sister; there are no current sick contacts and no recent strep infection. Development has been normal. Family history is unremarkable except a cousin who 'used to blink a lot' as a child and outgrew it. There is no exposure to stimulant medications or toxins, and no recent emotional trauma beyond starting a new school year.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cheerful boy. During the visit he intermittently blinks repetitively and clears his throat; both briefly stop when he is engaged in a task and increase when attention is drawn to them. No flowing/migratory movements (not chorea), no dystonia, no weakness. Conjunctivae clear without injection or discharge; eyes otherwise normal. Cranial nerves, strength, tone, reflexes, coordination, and gait normal. Mood and affect normal; no obsessive-compulsive behaviors or significant inattention reported or observed. The remainder of the neurologic and general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His repetitive eye-blinking and throat-clearing that wax and wane and can be briefly suppressed are best described as what—and what was the most likely earlier misattribution?
options: Allergic conjunctivitis and postnasal drip
Motor and vocal tics, earlier misattributed to allergies/dry eyes
Focal seizures
Chorea from rheumatic fever
Habitual stereotypies of autism
stages.explanation: Repetitive blinking (a motor tic) and throat-clearing (a vocal/phonic tic) that fluctuate, worsen with fatigue/excitement, and are briefly suppressible are tics; eye-blinking tics are very commonly first misattributed to allergies or dry eyes, and throat-clearing to postnasal drip—as happened here, with no response to allergy treatment and a normal eye exam. Allergic conjunctivitis would show injected/itchy eyes and respond to treatment. Focal seizures involve altered awareness/EEG changes and are not voluntarily suppressible. Rheumatic chorea is flowing and migratory, not stereotyped blinking/throat-clearing. Autistic stereotypies are rhythmic, self-soothing, begin earlier, and occur with other developmental features absent here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He has had these tics for about three months with otherwise normal development and exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Tourette syndrome
Provisional (transient) tic disorder
Persistent (chronic) motor tic disorder
Sydenham chorea
Conversion disorder
stages.explanation: A provisional (transient) tic disorder is diagnosed when motor and/or vocal tics have been present for less than one year (with onset before age 18); this boy's three-month course fits. Tourette syndrome requires both multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic persisting for more than one year, which he has not reached. Persistent (chronic) tic disorder also requires more than one year of tics. Sydenham chorea is post-streptococcal flowing chorea, not brief stereotyped tics, and there is no strep history. Conversion (functional neurological) disorder lacks the typical premonitory-urge/suppressibility profile of tics and is a diagnosis of exclusion not supported here.

stages.label: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Duration of tics: ~3 months (less than 1 year)
Neurologic exam: normal
No OCD or ADHD symptoms identified
No recent streptococcal infection; no concerning red-flag features
No neuroimaging or laboratory testing indicated for a typical presentation
Development and school performance: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His tics are mild and not interfering with function. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start an antipsychotic immediately
Reassurance, education, and watchful waiting; avoid drawing attention to the tics
Begin levetiracetam for seizure control
Order brain MRI and EEG now
Start long-term penicillin prophylaxis
stages.explanation: For mild, non-impairing tics—especially a provisional tic disorder—the best management is reassurance, education for the family and child, and watchful waiting; most transient tics resolve on their own, and caregivers should be advised not to call attention to them, as that can transiently worsen tics and increase the child's distress. Starting an antipsychotic is unwarranted for mild tics and risks significant side effects. Levetiracetam treats seizures, which this is not. Routine MRI and EEG are not indicated for a typical tic presentation with a normal exam and no red flags. Penicillin prophylaxis is for rheumatic fever/Sydenham chorea, not tics.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The mother asks about prognosis. What is the most accurate statement?
options: These tics will certainly progress to Tourette syndrome
Most provisional tics resolve within a year; persistence beyond a year would prompt reclassification, but the overall outlook is favorable
These tics indicate a serious progressive brain disease
He will need lifelong medication
Tics this age never improve
stages.explanation: Most provisional (transient) tics resolve spontaneously within a year and carry a favorable prognosis; only if tics persist beyond one year would the diagnosis be reclassified (e.g., to a persistent tic disorder or Tourette syndrome if both motor and vocal tics persist). It is incorrect to say progression to Tourette is certain—the majority do not. Tics are not a marker of progressive brain disease, and a normal exam without red flags is reassuring. Lifelong medication is not required for mild, self-limited tics. Saying tics never improve at this age is wrong; transient tics commonly remit.

cases.diagnosis: Provisional (transient) tic disorder
pearls: Eye-blinking and throat-clearing tics are frequently misattributed to allergies, dry eyes, or postnasal drip before being recognized as tics.
Provisional (transient) tic disorder = motor and/or vocal tics present for less than one year with onset before age 18.
Mild, non-impairing tics need only reassurance, education, and watchful waiting; do not draw attention to them, and avoid unnecessary imaging or medication.
Most transient tics remit within a year; persistence beyond a year prompts reclassification, but the overall prognosis is favorable.

cases.title: A 5-Day-Old With Poor Feeding and Arching
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive poor feeding, lethargy, and abnormal posturing in a previously well newborn.
cases.history: A 5-day-old term male, born at 39 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery to a healthy 28-year-old G2P2 mother, was discharged home at 48 hours feeding well. Over the past 48 hours he has become increasingly sleepy, latching poorly and taking only a few minutes at the breast before falling asleep. Parents report two episodes of stiff back-arching and intermittent jerking of the limbs today. He has had no fever, cough, or diarrhea. Birth was uncomplicated; Apgars were 8 and 9. He received vitamin K and erythromycin eye ointment. The newborn screen was drawn at 36 hours but results are not yet back. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. The mother notes a peculiar sweet smell to his diapers. The parents are first cousins of Mennonite descent. A prior sibling died at 10 days of life from an unexplained "infection." There is no maternal history of diabetes or fever during labor.
cases.exam: Lethargic neonate with reduced spontaneous movement. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat, not bulging. Pupils equal and reactive. Marked hypertonia of the trunk with intermittent opisthotonic posturing alternating with episodes of hypotonia and "boxing/bicycling" movements. Deep tendon reflexes brisk. Moro incomplete. No dysmorphic features, no hepatosplenomegaly, no rash. Capillary refill 2 seconds. A distinctly sweet, burnt-sugar odor is noted on the skin and diaper. No cardiac murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: While obtaining cultures, what is the most appropriate immediate next step for this encephalopathic neonate?
options: Begin empiric ampicillin and gentamicin and await blood culture results before further workup
Obtain a stat point-of-care glucose, ammonia, venous blood gas, and basic metabolic panel
Perform an urgent lumbar puncture and start acyclovir for presumed HSV encephalitis
Order an EEG and start phenobarbital for the abnormal movements
stages.explanation: An encephalopathic neonate with poor feeding and a metabolic-sounding history requires immediate screening for treatable metabolic emergencies: bedside glucose, ammonia, venous gas (for anion-gap acidosis), and electrolytes can be drawn within minutes and direct therapy. Sepsis must always be covered, but antibiotics alone ignore the inborn-error clues (consanguinity, sibling death, sweet odor). LP/acyclovir is reasonable for sepsis/HSV but is not the single most urgent step, and HSV rarely presents with opisthotonus and a sweet odor. EEG/phenobarbital treats a symptom without diagnosing the cause and may worsen a metabolic crisis. Rapid metabolic labs are both diagnostic and life-saving here.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Glucose 64 mg/dL
Ammonia 95 µmol/L (ref <50)
Venous pH 7.28, HCO3 14 mEq/L, anion gap 22
Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.6 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L
Lactate 1.9 mmol/L (normal)
Urine dipstick: ketones 3+
Urine DNPH (dinitrophenylhydrazine) test: positive
CBC: WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 16.4 g/dL, Plt 240 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The high anion-gap metabolic acidosis with marked ketonuria, near-normal lactate, only mildly elevated ammonia, and a positive urine DNPH test in a consanguineous neonate most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Propionic acidemia
Urea cycle defect (OTC deficiency)
Maple syrup urine disease
Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency
stages.explanation: Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is a branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase defect causing accumulation of leucine, isoleucine, and valine. The hallmark is a high-anion-gap ketoacidosis with prominent ketonuria, the characteristic sweet/maple-syrup odor, and a positive DNPH (detects branched-chain ketoacids). Ammonia is typically normal or only mildly elevated, and lactate is normal—distinguishing it from organic acidemias. Propionic acidemia usually causes higher ammonia, hyperglycinemia, and cytopenias without the sweet odor. OTC deficiency causes severe hyperammonemia WITHOUT acidosis or ketosis (often respiratory alkalosis). MCAD presents with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, not florid ketosis. Plasma amino acids showing elevated leucine and presence of alloisoleucine confirm MSUD.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which single laboratory finding is most specific for confirming this diagnosis?
options: Markedly elevated plasma leucine with detectable alloisoleucine
Elevated urine methylmalonic acid on organic acid analysis
Elevated plasma glutamine with low citrulline
Elevated octanoylcarnitine (C8) on acylcarnitine profile
stages.explanation: Plasma amino acid analysis showing markedly elevated leucine (and isoleucine and valine) plus the presence of alloisoleucine—a stereoisomer of isoleucine that is essentially pathognomonic—confirms MSUD. Alloisoleucine is the single most specific marker because it is not seen in other conditions. Elevated urine methylmalonic acid defines methylmalonic acidemia. High glutamine with low citrulline points to a urea cycle defect such as OTC deficiency. Elevated C8 acylcarnitine is the marker for MCAD deficiency. Newborn screening flags an elevated leucine, but the plasma amino acid panel with alloisoleucine is the confirmatory test.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides stopping all protein intake and providing high-calorie dextrose with insulin to drive anabolism, what is the definitive treatment to acutely remove the toxic metabolite when leucine is critically elevated with worsening encephalopathy?
options: Intravenous sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate scavengers
High-dose intravenous biotin and hydroxocobalamin
Hemodialysis (or continuous renal replacement therapy)
Exchange transfusion with packed red blood cells
stages.explanation: When leucine is critically elevated with worsening encephalopathy/cerebral edema, hemodialysis (or CRRT) is the most effective way to rapidly clear branched-chain amino acids; it markedly outperforms peritoneal dialysis or exchange transfusion. Concurrently, anabolism is promoted with high dextrose, intralipid, insulin, and a BCAA-free formula, plus isoleucine and valine supplementation to prevent deficiency. Nitrogen scavengers treat hyperammonemia in urea cycle defects, not MSUD. Biotin/B12 are cofactors for biotinidase deficiency and B12-responsive methylmalonic acidemia, not MSUD. Exchange transfusion is far less efficient than dialysis for clearing leucine. Thiamine (a BCKDH cofactor) is given as an adjunct, but dialysis is the definitive acute removal therapy in crisis.

stages.label: LONG-TERM CARE
stages.question: After the crisis resolves, what is the cornerstone of chronic management to prevent recurrent metabolic decompensation?
options: Lifelong dietary restriction of branched-chain amino acids with a specialized BCAA-free formula and frequent leucine monitoring
A high-protein diet to maintain growth
Daily prophylactic hemodialysis
Strict avoidance of all carbohydrates
stages.explanation: Chronic MSUD management centers on lifelong dietary restriction of branched-chain amino acids—using a specialized BCAA-free medical formula with carefully titrated natural protein—plus frequent monitoring of plasma leucine and a 'sick-day' protocol to prevent catabolic crises during illness. Some patients are thiamine-responsive. A high-protein diet would precipitate toxic BCAA accumulation. Daily prophylactic dialysis is unnecessary and not how chronic disease is managed; dialysis is reserved for acute crises. Avoiding all carbohydrates is exactly wrong—adequate calories (including carbohydrate) are essential to maintain anabolism and prevent catabolism. Liver transplantation is an option that can liberalize the diet in select patients.

cases.diagnosis: Maple syrup urine disease (classic, presenting in neonatal metabolic crisis)
pearls: Classic MSUD presents in the first week with poor feeding, lethargy, opisthotonus/encephalopathy, ketoacidosis, and a maple-syrup/burnt-sugar odor.
The biochemical signature is high-anion-gap ketoacidosis with NORMAL lactate and only mild hyperammonemia; alloisoleucine on plasma amino acids is pathognomonic.
Acute crisis: stop protein, push anabolism with high-calorie dextrose/lipids/insulin and BCAA-free formula; use hemodialysis/CRRT to rapidly clear leucine.
Consanguinity and Mennonite/Old Order Amish ancestry markedly raise the pretest probability; an unexplained prior neonatal death is a red flag.

cases.title: A 5-Month-Old With Irritability and Stiffening
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing irritability, stiffness, and loss of skills over the past two months.
cases.history: A 5-month-old girl is brought to the neurology clinic for inconsolable crying and progressive stiffness. She was the product of an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery and developed normally until about 3 months, when she began smiling, cooing, and reaching for objects. Over the past 6–8 weeks she has become extremely irritable, crying for hours with minimal provocation, and stopped reaching and social smiling. Parents note she startles and stiffens dramatically to noise and touch, and her arms and legs feel rigid. Feeding has become difficult with frequent gagging and poor suck, and she has had several episodes of unexplained fever without infection. There is no vomiting or diarrhea. She is exclusively breastfed with vitamin D supplementation; no other medications; no known allergies. Family history: a maternal uncle died in infancy with a "brain disease." Parents are of Ashkenazi Jewish descent and are not knowingly related. Newborn screening in their state did not include this disorder.
cases.exam: Irritable, fisted infant who is difficult to console. Head circumference at 50th percentile but crossing downward from prior visits. Marked appendicular hypertonia with scissoring of the legs and brisk patellar and biceps reflexes; sustained ankle clonus. Paradoxically, ankle jerks are difficult to elicit and there is reduced neck tone with poor head control, suggesting a superimposed peripheral neuropathy. Exaggerated startle to sound. Optic discs show early pallor. No hepatosplenomegaly, no cherry-red macular spot, no coarse facies, no skin lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An infant with normal early development followed by regression, extreme irritability, and hypertonia raises concern for a neurodegenerative disorder. What is the most useful next diagnostic test?
options: Serum ammonia and plasma amino acids
Brain MRI with attention to white matter
Electroencephalogram (EEG)
Serum lactate and pyruvate
stages.explanation: Regression with hypertonia and irritability in infancy suggests a leukodystrophy; brain MRI is the highest-yield initial study and will reveal symmetric white-matter signal abnormality, often with thalamic/dentate involvement and later atrophy, framing the differential toward demyelinating versus other neurodegenerative causes. Ammonia/amino acids screen for acute metabolic crises, but this is subacute regression without acidosis. EEG is useful if seizures are suspected but does not localize the white-matter process. Lactate/pyruvate screen for mitochondrial disease, lower on the differential given the demyelinating phenotype and peripheral neuropathy. MRI both narrows the differential and guides targeted enzyme/genetic testing.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
Brain MRI: symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the periventricular and deep cerebellar white matter and corticospinal tracts; involvement of the dentate nuclei; early volume loss
Nerve conduction studies: markedly slowed conduction velocities consistent with a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy
CSF protein: 145 mg/dL (elevated)
Fundus exam: optic atrophy, no cherry-red spot
Brainstem auditory evoked responses: abnormal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The combination of a demyelinating leukodystrophy on MRI, a peripheral demyelinating neuropathy, elevated CSF protein, irritability, and optic atrophy in a 5-month-old is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Tay-Sachs disease
Krabbe disease (globoid cell leukodystrophy)
Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease
Metachromatic leukodystrophy (late-infantile)
stages.explanation: Krabbe disease (galactocerebrosidase/GALC deficiency) classically presents in early infancy with marked irritability, hypertonia, and regression, plus a peripheral demyelinating neuropathy and elevated CSF protein—features highlighted here. The CENTRAL plus PERIPHERAL demyelination with high CSF protein strongly points to Krabbe (and MLD). Tay-Sachs features a cherry-red macular spot, hyperacusis/startle, and macrocephaly WITHOUT peripheral neuropathy and with normal CSF protein. Pelizaeus-Merzbacher is X-linked, presents with nystagmus and hypotonia, and lacks neuropathy. Metachromatic leukodystrophy shares neuropathy and high CSF protein but typically presents later (late-infantile ~1–2 yr) with gait regression rather than the very early severe irritability; enzyme assay distinguishes them.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which accumulating substance underlies the toxicity to myelin-forming cells in this disease?
options: GM2 ganglioside
Sulfatides
Psychosine (galactosylsphingosine)
Glucocerebroside
stages.explanation: In Krabbe disease, deficiency of galactocerebrosidase leads to accumulation of psychosine (galactosylsphingosine), which is highly toxic to oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells, producing both central and peripheral demyelination and the characteristic multinucleated globoid cells. GM2 ganglioside accumulates in Tay-Sachs disease. Sulfatides accumulate in metachromatic leukodystrophy due to arylsulfatase A deficiency. Glucocerebroside accumulates in Gaucher disease. The psychosine hypothesis explains why both the CNS white matter and peripheral nerves are affected, accounting for this infant's combined upper- and lower-motor-neuron findings.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: What is the confirmatory diagnostic test for this disorder?
options: Arylsulfatase A enzyme activity in leukocytes
Hexosaminidase A enzyme activity in serum
Galactocerebrosidase (GALC) enzyme activity in leukocytes or fibroblasts
Very-long-chain fatty acid levels in plasma
stages.explanation: Deficient galactocerebrosidase (GALC) activity in leukocytes or cultured fibroblasts confirms Krabbe disease, with GALC gene sequencing for the specific mutation. Arylsulfatase A is the enzyme deficient in metachromatic leukodystrophy. Hexosaminidase A confirms Tay-Sachs disease. Very-long-chain fatty acids screen for X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy/peroxisomal disorders, which present differently (often older boys, adrenal insufficiency). Pathologically, GALC deficiency leads to accumulation of psychosine, which is toxic to oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells and produces the characteristic globoid cells.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The family asks about treatment. Which statement most accurately reflects current management of this condition?
options: Enzyme replacement therapy fully reverses neurologic deterioration
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant can slow progression but is most effective only when performed PRESYMPTOMATICALLY
A galactose-restricted diet halts disease progression
Gene therapy is curative and standard of care for symptomatic infants
stages.explanation: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) can slow progression and improve outcomes in Krabbe disease, but the benefit is largely limited to PRESYMPTOMATIC infants (identified by newborn screening or family history); once symptoms appear in the infantile form, transplant does not reverse established damage—hence the symptomatic infant here has a poor prognosis and care is largely supportive. ERT does not cross the blood-brain barrier effectively and does not reverse CNS disease. Dietary galactose restriction does not treat Krabbe (galactocerebroside, not dietary galactose, is the issue). Gene therapy is investigational, not curative standard of care. This underscores why newborn screening for Krabbe is so important.

cases.diagnosis: Krabbe disease (infantile globoid cell leukodystrophy, GALC deficiency)
pearls: Infantile Krabbe disease: early irritability, hypertonia, and regression with BOTH central demyelination and a peripheral demyelinating neuropathy plus elevated CSF protein.
Psychosine accumulation from GALC deficiency is toxic to myelin-forming cells and produces pathognomonic globoid cells.
Confirm with deficient galactocerebrosidase (GALC) activity; distinguish from MLD (arylsulfatase A) and Tay-Sachs (cherry-red spot, no neuropathy).
HSCT helps only PRESYMPTOMATIC infants—an argument for newborn screening; symptomatic infantile disease is managed supportively.

cases.title: A 9-Year-Old With Falling Grades and Clumsiness
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening school performance, clumsiness, and slurred speech over the past year.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is referred for a one-year history of declining academic performance, frequent falls, and increasingly slurred speech. He was previously an A/B student; now he struggles to read aloud and his handwriting has deteriorated. Parents describe new clumsiness, difficulty looking down to climb stairs, and occasional sudden buckling of the knees when he laughs hard, sometimes nearly falling. There is no history of seizures, headache, or trauma. As an infant he had prolonged neonatal jaundice that resolved. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He has no toxic exposures. Family history: a paternal great-aunt with "early dementia"; parents are non-consanguineous of French-Canadian descent.
