Review of systems is notable for slow eye movements noticed by his teacher and a sense that his abdomen looks fuller. Developmental milestones were normal until this past year.
cases.exam: Alert boy with mildly dysarthric speech. Cranial nerves: striking impairment of VERTICAL gaze—he cannot voluntarily look down, though the oculocephalic (doll's-eye) maneuver moves the eyes fully, indicating a supranuclear palsy; horizontal gaze preserved. Mild generalized dystonia of the hands and an action tremor. Gait is wide-based and ataxic with impaired tandem walking. Dysmetria on finger-to-nose. Tone mildly increased. Reflexes brisk. Abdomen: spleen palpable 4 cm below the costal margin; liver edge palpable. No Kayser-Fleischer rings reported, no cherry-red spot.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding is the most specific clue pointing toward the underlying diagnosis in this boy with regression, ataxia, and splenomegaly?
options: Wide-based ataxic gait
Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (impaired downgaze with intact doll's-eye)
Brisk deep tendon reflexes
Action tremor of the hands
stages.explanation: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy—loss of voluntary vertical (especially downward) saccades with preserved reflexive eye movements on the oculocephalic maneuver—is a hallmark and relatively specific clue for Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC), localizing the lesion above the ocular motor nuclei. Combined with organomegaly and neuroregression, it strongly narrows the differential. Ataxic gait, brisk reflexes, and action tremor are all nonspecific and seen across many neurodegenerative and cerebellar disorders. Recognizing VSGP at the bedside often makes the diagnosis of NPC long before confirmatory testing.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
CBC: mild thrombocytopenia (Plt 118 ×10³/µL)
LFTs: mildly elevated AST/ALT
Abdominal ultrasound: splenomegaly (spleen 14 cm), mild hepatomegaly
Brain MRI: mild cerebellar atrophy, otherwise unremarkable
Slit-lamp exam: no Kayser-Fleischer rings; serum ceruloplasmin normal
Bone marrow aspirate (obtained for cytopenia): foamy lipid-laden macrophages and sea-blue histiocytes
Plasma oxysterols: markedly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Childhood-onset neuroregression with vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, ataxia, dystonia, splenomegaly, foamy macrophages/sea-blue histiocytes, and elevated plasma oxysterols is diagnostic of which disorder?
options: Wilson disease
Niemann-Pick disease type C
Gaucher disease type 3
Juvenile Huntington disease
stages.explanation: This constellation is classic for Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC), a defect in intracellular cholesterol/lipid trafficking (NPC1 or NPC2 genes) causing accumulation of unesterified cholesterol. Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy plus splenomegaly plus neuroregression, with sea-blue histiocytes/foamy macrophages and elevated oxysterols, is essentially diagnostic. Wilson disease causes movement disorder and liver disease but features Kayser-Fleischer rings and low ceruloplasmin (both excluded here). Gaucher type 3 has Gaucher cells and a HORIZONTAL supranuclear saccade palsy, not vertical, and lacks the foamy-cell oxysterol pattern. Juvenile Huntington causes rigidity and decline but not organomegaly, gaze palsy of this type, or the lipid-laden marrow.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED FEATURE
stages.question: The episodes of sudden knee-buckling and falling when the boy laughs hard are best explained by which phenomenon associated with this disease?
options: Atonic (drop) seizures
Gelastic cataplexy (laughter-induced loss of muscle tone)
Syncope from cardiac outflow obstruction
Vasovagal episodes triggered by emotion
stages.explanation: Gelastic cataplexy—a sudden loss of muscle tone triggered by strong emotion, classically laughter—is a characteristic and relatively distinctive feature of Niemann-Pick type C, reflecting hypothalamic/brainstem involvement of hypocretin pathways. Atonic seizures cause drop attacks but are not specifically provoked by laughter and would show ictal EEG changes. Cardiac outflow syncope is exertional, not laughter-triggered, and there is no murmur here. Vasovagal syncope involves prodromal symptoms and recovery patterns unlike abrupt cataplexy. Recognizing laughter-induced cataplexy in a child with neuroregression and gaze palsy is a strong pointer to NPC.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which test best confirms the diagnosis after the suggestive biomarker and marrow findings?
options: Molecular genetic testing for biallelic NPC1 (or NPC2) pathogenic variants
Serum ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urinary copper
Hexosaminidase A activity
Huntingtin (HTT) CAG repeat analysis
stages.explanation: After suggestive biomarkers (elevated plasma oxysterols/lyso-sphingomyelin) and foamy macrophages/sea-blue histiocytes, the diagnosis is confirmed by molecular genetic testing demonstrating biallelic pathogenic variants in NPC1 (about 95% of cases) or NPC2; filipin staining of fibroblasts (showing impaired cholesterol esterification) is a supportive functional assay. Ceruloplasmin/urinary copper evaluate Wilson disease, already excluded by normal ceruloplasmin and absent KF rings. Hexosaminidase A tests for Tay-Sachs, which does not cause splenomegaly or vertical gaze palsy. HTT CAG repeat analysis diagnoses Huntington disease, which lacks organomegaly and the foamy-cell/oxysterol pattern. Genetic confirmation also enables family counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which disease-specific therapy is used to slow neurologic progression in Niemann-Pick type C?
options: Penicillamine (copper chelation)
Miglustat (a substrate-reduction glucosylceramide synthase inhibitor)
Enzyme replacement with imiglucerase
Low-copper diet and zinc
stages.explanation: Miglustat, an inhibitor of glucosylceramide synthase (substrate reduction therapy), is used to slow neurologic progression in NPC and is approved for neurologic manifestations in many countries; supportive/multidisciplinary care addresses dystonia, seizures, and cataplexy (the latter often responsive to anticholinergics/protriptyline). Penicillamine and low-copper diet/zinc treat Wilson disease. Imiglucerase ERT treats Gaucher disease but does not address the CNS lipid-trafficking defect of NPC. There is no curative therapy for NPC; miglustat plus comprehensive symptom management is the current standard.

cases.diagnosis: Niemann-Pick disease type C (juvenile/childhood neurovisceral form)
pearls: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (impaired downgaze with intact doll's-eye) plus splenomegaly and school decline should prompt evaluation for Niemann-Pick type C.
Gelastic cataplexy—loss of tone triggered by laughter—is a distinctive NPC clue.
Plasma oxysterols and lyso-sphingomyelin are useful biomarkers; sea-blue histiocytes/foamy macrophages support the diagnosis; confirm with NPC1/NPC2 genetics.
Miglustat (substrate reduction) can slow neurologic decline; always exclude Wilson disease (KF rings, low ceruloplasmin) in a child with movement disorder plus liver findings.

cases.title: A 2-Year-Old Who Stopped Using Her Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Loss of previously acquired skills and constant hand movements.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is brought in by her parents because she "isn't the same as she was." She was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and met early milestones normally—she sat at 6 months, crawled, pulled to stand, said a few words around 12 months, and used a pincer grasp to feed herself. Between 15 and 24 months her parents noticed she gradually stopped saying words, lost interest in toys, and no longer uses her hands purposefully. Instead she makes near-constant midline hand-wringing and hand-to-mouth movements. She has become less social, with reduced eye contact, and walks with an unsteady, stiff gait. Parents also report episodes of breath-holding and air-swallowing, and occasional staring spells. She has no history of birth complications, no toxic exposures, and takes no medications. No known allergies. Family history is unremarkable; she is an only child and there is no consanguinity. Her appetite is adequate but parents note her head "hasn't grown as fast."
cases.exam: Interactive only intermittently with poor eye contact. Head circumference has decelerated, crossing from the 50th to below the 5th percentile over 18 months (acquired microcephaly). Near-continuous stereotypic hand-wringing and hand-mouthing at the midline; she no longer reaches for or manipulates offered objects. Truncal ataxia with a wide-based, somewhat rigid gait. Tone mildly increased in the lower extremities; reflexes brisk. Episodes of breath-holding alternating with hyperventilation observed during the visit. No organomegaly, no dysmorphic facies, no skin findings, no focal weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A previously normally developing girl who regresses with loss of purposeful hand use, acquired microcephaly, and stereotypic hand movements—what is the single most characteristic feature pointing to the diagnosis?
options: Truncal ataxia
Stereotypic midline hand-wringing with loss of purposeful hand use
Episodic breath-holding
Brisk lower-extremity reflexes
stages.explanation: Loss of acquired purposeful hand skills replaced by stereotypic midline hand-wringing/washing movements is the most characteristic and near-diagnostic feature of Rett syndrome. Together with developmental regression after a period of normal development and deceleration of head growth, it forms the core diagnostic picture. Truncal ataxia, breath-holding/hyperventilation, and brisk reflexes all occur in Rett but are nonspecific and seen in many neurologic conditions; it is the hand stereotypies plus regression that make the bedside diagnosis. Recognizing this pattern prevents misdiagnosis as idiopathic autism.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Autism spectrum disorder
Angelman syndrome
Rett syndrome
Infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy
stages.explanation: Normal early development followed by regression between 6–18 months, loss of purposeful hand use with hand-wringing stereotypies, acquired microcephaly, gait abnormalities, and breathing irregularities in a girl is classic Rett syndrome. Autism involves social/communication deficits but typically without the loss of purposeful hand use, hand-wringing stereotypies, or acquired microcephaly, and is not characteristically regressive after age 1 with deceleration of head growth. Angelman syndrome causes severe speech impairment, ataxia, and a happy demeanor with paroxysmal laughter but not the specific hand-wringing-after-regression pattern. Infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy presents earlier with hypotonia and visual failure, lacking the hand stereotypies.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
options: MECP2 gene sequencing/deletion analysis
Methylation analysis of chromosome 15q11-13
Fragile X (FMR1 CGG repeat) testing
Plasma very-long-chain fatty acids
stages.explanation: Classic Rett syndrome is caused by mutations in MECP2 (methyl-CpG-binding protein 2) on Xq28 in the majority of cases; MECP2 sequencing with deletion/duplication analysis is the confirmatory test. Chromosome 15q11-13 methylation diagnoses Angelman/Prader-Willi syndromes. FMR1 CGG repeat testing diagnoses Fragile X. Plasma VLCFA screens for peroxisomal disorders such as adrenoleukodystrophy. While Rett remains a clinical diagnosis based on consensus criteria, a pathogenic MECP2 variant confirms it; absence of a variant does not exclude clinically classic Rett.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The parents ask about management. Which statement is most accurate?
options: A specific medication can reverse the neurologic regression
Care is multidisciplinary and supportive; girls warrant ECG screening for prolonged QT and monitoring for scoliosis and seizures
A ketogenic diet is curative for Rett syndrome
Because it is X-linked dominant, affected girls have a normal life expectancy with no cardiac risk
stages.explanation: Management of Rett syndrome is multidisciplinary and supportive: physical/occupational/speech therapy, seizure management, nutritional support, and surveillance for complications. Importantly, girls are at risk for prolonged QT interval and sudden death, so periodic ECG screening is recommended, along with monitoring for scoliosis (very common) and seizures. No drug reverses the regression—though trofinetide is approved to improve some symptoms, it is not a cure. A ketogenic diet may help refractory seizures but is not curative. That option is wrong because cardiac risk (long QT) is real and life expectancy can be reduced. Anticipatory, organ-specific surveillance is the cornerstone of care.

cases.diagnosis: Rett syndrome (MECP2-related)
pearls: Rett syndrome: normal early development, then regression (6–18 mo) with loss of purposeful hand use, midline hand-wringing stereotypies, and acquired microcephaly—almost exclusively in girls.
Confirm with MECP2 sequencing/deletion analysis; it is X-linked dominant (lethal in most males).
Screen for and monitor prolonged QT (sudden-death risk), scoliosis, seizures, and breathing dysregulation.
Distinguish from autism (no loss of purposeful hand use or acquired microcephaly) and Angelman syndrome (15q methylation, happy/ataxic phenotype).

cases.title: A 5-Year-Old With Speech Delay and Behavioral Concerns
cases.chiefComplaint: Developmental delay, poor eye contact, and hyperactivity.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy presents for evaluation of global developmental delay and behavioral difficulties. He was born at term without complications but was a late talker, saying his first words at 2.5 years; he now speaks in short, repetitive phrases. He has trouble in preschool with sitting still, transitions, and following directions, and his teacher reports he avoids eye contact, flaps his hands when excited, and is hypersensitive to loud noises and certain textures. He bites his hand when frustrated. There were no perinatal infections and no regression—his skills have slowly progressed, just delayed. He takes no medications, has no allergies, and has no toxic exposures. Family history is notable: a maternal uncle has intellectual disability and lives in a group home, and the maternal grandfather developed a late-onset tremor and gait problems ("ataxia") in his 60s. The mother reports she experienced early menopause at 38. There is no consanguinity.
cases.exam: Active, fidgety boy with poor eye contact and intermittent hand-flapping. Dysmorphology: long, narrow face; prominent forehead and jaw; large, protuberant ears. Palate high-arched; joints hyperextensible (notably at the fingers); flat feet. Genitourinary exam reveals enlarged testes for age (macroorchidism). Neurologic exam: no focal deficits, normal strength and reflexes, no ataxia or stereotypic hand-wringing. Mild hypotonia. No hepatosplenomegaly, no neurocutaneous lesions, no cardiac murmur. Speech is echolalic with perseveration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given intellectual disability with autistic features, a long face with large ears, and macroorchidism, what is the single most appropriate first-line genetic test?
options: Chromosomal microarray (CMA)
FMR1 CGG repeat (Fragile X) testing
Whole-exome sequencing
Karyotype
stages.explanation: The phenotype—intellectual disability with autistic behaviors, a long narrow face, large protuberant ears, and macroorchidism, plus a suggestive family history (maternally inherited ID, a grandfather with late-onset tremor/ataxia, mother with early menopause)—is classic for Fragile X syndrome, so targeted FMR1 CGG repeat testing is the most appropriate first-line test. Chromosomal microarray is recommended first-line for unexplained ID/autism generally, but given the strongly specific Fragile X phenotype here, FMR1 testing is the highest-yield directed test (and CMA will NOT detect the CGG triplet expansion). Exome sequencing is broader and not first-line for this classic presentation. Karyotype misses Fragile X because it is a triplet-repeat expansion, not a visible chromosomal abnormality.

stages.label: GENETIC RESULTS
stages.content:
FMR1 CGG repeat analysis: full mutation, >200 CGG repeats with aberrant methylation
Karyotype: 46,XY (normal)
Lead level: <2 µg/dL
Thyroid function: normal
Maternal carrier testing (subsequently performed): premutation, 85 CGG repeats

stages.label: GENETICS
stages.question: Which statement about the genetics of this condition is correct?
options: It is autosomal recessive and requires two affected parents
It is an X-linked CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion in FMR1; a full mutation (>200 repeats) silences FMR1 and causes loss of FMRP
It results from a maternal 15q11-13 deletion
It is caused by mitochondrial DNA point mutations
stages.explanation: Fragile X syndrome results from expansion of a CGG trinucleotide repeat in the 5' untranslated region of the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. A full mutation (>200 repeats) leads to hypermethylation and transcriptional silencing of FMR1, with loss of the FMRP protein needed for synaptic regulation. It is X-linked (males more severely affected). That option (autosomal recessive) is wrong. A maternal 15q11-13 deletion causes Angelman syndrome. Mitochondrial mutations cause maternally inherited mitochondrial disorders, not Fragile X. The maternal premutation (55–200 repeats) here explains the family history and her early menopause (FXPOI).

stages.label: FAMILY COUNSELING
stages.question: The maternal grandfather's late-onset tremor/ataxia and the mother's early menopause are best explained by which premutation-associated phenomenon?
options: Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) in the grandfather and primary ovarian insufficiency (FXPOI) in the mother
Coincidental Parkinson disease and natural early menopause
Anticipation, meaning all premutation carriers develop full mutations
Mosaic Down syndrome in both relatives
stages.explanation: Premutation carriers (55–200 CGG repeats) are at risk for distinct disorders: older male carriers can develop Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS)—intention tremor, cerebellar ataxia, cognitive decline—explaining the grandfather's late-onset tremor/gait problem; female carriers are at risk for Fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency (FXPOI), explaining the mother's menopause at 38. That option dismisses these as coincidence, missing the unifying premutation. Anticipation means the repeat can EXPAND across generations (premutation to full mutation), but not that ALL premutations expand. Down syndrome is unrelated. Recognizing FXTAS/FXPOI is key to counseling the extended family and identifying at-risk carriers.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach for this child?
options: Curative gene therapy is available and should be initiated
Multidisciplinary supportive care—early intervention, speech/occupational/behavioral therapy, and treatment of comorbid ADHD/anxiety—with family genetic counseling
A low-phenylalanine diet to prevent further decline
Lifelong chelation therapy
stages.explanation: There is no cure for Fragile X; management is supportive and multidisciplinary: early intervention, individualized education, speech/language and occupational therapy, behavioral therapy, and pharmacologic treatment of comorbid ADHD (stimulants/alpha-agonists) and anxiety/aggression as needed, plus genetic counseling for the family (the mother is an obligate carrier and relatives should be offered testing). Gene therapy is not available/standard. A low-phenylalanine diet treats PKU, not Fragile X. Chelation treats heavy-metal toxicity; the lead level here is normal. Comprehensive developmental and behavioral support optimizes function and quality of life.

cases.diagnosis: Fragile X syndrome (FMR1 full mutation)
pearls: Fragile X: most common inherited cause of intellectual disability—long face, large ears, macroorchidism, autistic features, and hand-flapping/biting.
Caused by a CGG repeat expansion in FMR1; full mutation (>200 repeats) silences the gene and eliminates FMRP. Test with FMR1 repeat analysis (microarray and karyotype miss it).
Premutation carriers risk FXTAS (older males: tremor/ataxia) and FXPOI (females: early ovarian insufficiency)—key for family counseling.
Management is multidisciplinary supportive care plus treatment of comorbid ADHD/anxiety and cascade genetic counseling.

cases.title: A 3-Year-Old Who Cannot Speak and Laughs Constantly
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe global developmental delay with no speech, an unsteady gait, and frequent unprovoked laughter.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is referred for severe developmental delay. She was a full-term infant who fed somewhat poorly and was a bit floppy as a newborn but had no perinatal catastrophe. She sat late and only began walking at 28 months with a stiff, jerky, wide-based gait. She has no words and minimal receptive language, though she is very sociable. Her parents describe an unusually happy demeanor with frequent bouts of laughter and smiling, often without clear cause, and excitable hand-flapping. She has a short attention span and is fascinated by water and crinkly objects. Over the past six months she has had two episodes concerning for seizures—staring with jerking. She sleeps poorly, waking repeatedly at night. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Birth and family history are unremarkable; no consanguinity. Parents have noticed she drools and keeps her mouth open with frequent tongue protrusion. Her head seems small relative to peers.
cases.exam: Cheerful, highly sociable toddler with frequent spontaneous laughter and hand-flapping. Microcephaly (head circumference below 2nd percentile). Midface with a wide mouth, widely spaced teeth, prominent chin, and frequent tongue protrusion; relatively fair hair and blue eyes compared with parents. Neurologic: appendicular and truncal ataxia with a stiff, puppet-like, wide-based gait and jerky limb movements; tremulousness of the limbs; increased tone with brisk reflexes. No focal weakness. No organomegaly, no neurocutaneous lesions, no cataracts. She makes no babble or words. Strikingly happy affect throughout the exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Severe speech impairment, ataxic 'puppet-like' gait, microcephaly, and a happy demeanor with paroxysmal laughter point to a specific syndrome. What is the most appropriate first-line confirmatory test?
options: Routine karyotype
MECP2 sequencing
DNA methylation analysis of the chromosome 15q11-13 (Prader-Willi/Angelman) region
Plasma amino acids and urine organic acids
stages.explanation: The phenotype—near-absent speech, ataxic puppet-like gait, microcephaly, easily provoked laughter, and a happy sociable affect with seizures—is classic Angelman syndrome, which results from loss of function of the maternally inherited UBE3A on chromosome 15q11-13. DNA methylation analysis of the 15q11-13 region is the recommended first-line test: it detects ~80% of cases (deletions, uniparental disomy, imprinting defects), and a normal methylation pattern is then followed by UBE3A sequencing. A karyotype lacks resolution for these microdeletions/imprinting defects. MECP2 sequencing tests for Rett syndrome (girls who regress with hand-wringing—different from this child's nonregressive happy/ataxic picture). Metabolic studies are not the targeted test for this clinical syndrome.

stages.label: GENETIC & EEG RESULTS
stages.content:
DNA methylation analysis (15q11-13): abnormal, consistent with loss of the maternal allele
Follow-up FISH/microarray: 5–7 Mb DE NOVO deletion of the MATERNAL chromosome 15q11-13
UBE3A: within the deleted region (loss of maternal UBE3A expression)
EEG: high-amplitude rhythmic 2–3 Hz (delta) activity with notched/triphasic waves and posterior spike-and-slow-wave on eye closure—a characteristic Angelman pattern
Brain MRI: mild nonspecific findings, no structural malformation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Before the genetic result returned, which diagnosis was the leading consideration, and which feature best distinguishes it from its main mimics?
options: Rett syndrome, distinguished by developmental regression with hand-wringing
Angelman syndrome, distinguished by a happy/excitable demeanor with paroxysmal laughter, ataxic gait, and near-absent speech without true regression
Cerebral palsy, distinguished by a clear perinatal hypoxic-ischemic event
Autism spectrum disorder, distinguished by intact gross motor and speech milestones
stages.explanation: The leading diagnosis is Angelman syndrome, best distinguished by its hallmark happy, excitable demeanor with frequent paroxysmal laughter, an ataxic 'puppet-like' gait, microcephaly, and severely impaired (often absent) expressive speech—in a child who did NOT show a clear period of normal development followed by regression. Rett syndrome involves true regression with loss of purposeful hand use and stereotypic hand-wringing, almost exclusively in girls, and is caused by MECP2 mutations—a different course. Cerebral palsy follows a static perinatal insult, absent here, and lacks the characteristic laughter and EEG pattern. Autism does not typically cause this degree of motor impairment, ataxia, or the distinctive EEG. The behavioral-motor signature points to Angelman.

stages.label: GENETICS
stages.question: Which mechanism explains why a deletion in this chromosomal region causes Angelman syndrome (rather than Prader-Willi syndrome)?
options: Loss of the PATERNALLY inherited 15q11-13 region
Loss of the MATERNALLY inherited 15q11-13 region, eliminating maternal UBE3A expression in neurons (genomic imprinting)
Trisomy of chromosome 15
A CGG repeat expansion silencing UBE3A
stages.explanation: Angelman syndrome arises from loss of function of the MATERNALLY inherited UBE3A allele within 15q11-13. UBE3A is imprinted in the brain—only the maternal copy is expressed in neurons (the paternal copy is silenced)—so loss of the maternal allele (by deletion, paternal uniparental disomy, imprinting defect, or UBE3A mutation) leaves neurons without functional UBE3A. Loss of the PATERNAL 15q11-13 region causes Prader-Willi syndrome instead (hypotonia, hyperphagia/obesity, hypogonadism)—the same locus, opposite parent of origin. Trisomy 15 is not the mechanism. A CGG repeat expansion describes Fragile X, not Angelman. This locus is the textbook example of genomic imprinting.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is most characteristic of this syndrome and warrants proactive management?
options: Hyperphagia and morbid obesity
Epilepsy (often beginning in early childhood) with a characteristic EEG, plus severe sleep disturbance
Progressive hepatosplenomegaly
Premature ovarian insufficiency
stages.explanation: Epilepsy is very common in Angelman syndrome, usually beginning in early childhood with multiple seizure types (atypical absence, myoclonic, atonic, generalized tonic-clonic) and a characteristic high-amplitude EEG; severe sleep disturbance is also a hallmark and major management challenge. Both warrant proactive treatment (valproate, levetiracetam, clobazam, or other agents; sleep hygiene and melatonin). Hyperphagia/obesity characterizes Prader-Willi syndrome, the imprinting counterpart, not Angelman. Progressive hepatosplenomegaly suggests a storage disease, not Angelman. Premature ovarian insufficiency is associated with the Fragile X premutation, unrelated here. Recognizing and treating the seizures and sleep problems substantially improves quality of life.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate overall management plan for this child?
options: Curative gene therapy, now standard of care
Multidisciplinary supportive care—seizure control, communication/augmentative therapy, PT/OT, sleep management—plus genetic counseling about recurrence risk
A protein-restricted diet to prevent metabolic crises
Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
stages.explanation: Angelman syndrome management is multidisciplinary and supportive: anti-seizure medications tailored to the seizure types (avoiding agents that can worsen them), augmentative/alternative communication and speech therapy (since expressive speech rarely develops), physical and occupational therapy for gait and motor function, behavioral and sleep interventions (melatonin), and genetic counseling—recurrence risk depends on the molecular mechanism (de novo deletions carry low recurrence, whereas imprinting-center defects or UBE3A mutations can carry higher risk). Gene therapy is investigational, not standard of care. A protein-restricted diet treats aminoacidopathies/urea cycle defects, not Angelman. HSCT has no role here. Comprehensive, anticipatory care optimizes function and safety.

cases.diagnosis: Angelman syndrome (maternal 15q11-13 deletion)
pearls: Angelman syndrome: severe speech impairment, ataxic 'puppet-like' gait, microcephaly, easily provoked laughter, and a happy sociable affect, frequently with epilepsy.
It is caused by loss of the MATERNAL UBE3A on 15q11-13 (imprinted gene); loss of the PATERNAL region at the same locus causes Prader-Willi syndrome.
First-line test is 15q11-13 DNA methylation analysis; characterize the mechanism (deletion, UPD, imprinting defect, UBE3A mutation) because recurrence risk varies.
Manage seizures (characteristic EEG), severe sleep disturbance, and communication/motor needs with multidisciplinary supportive care; expressive speech rarely develops.

cases.title: A 2-Year-Old With Vomiting and Wobbly Walking
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of vomiting, lethargy, and unsteadiness with new clumsiness.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the ED for three days of intermittent vomiting, increasing sleepiness, and a new wobbly, unsteady gait. His parents say he has been more irritable and constipated over several weeks and seems to be "losing words" he previously used. There is no fever, diarrhea, head trauma, or recent travel. The family lives in a house built in the 1920s that is undergoing renovation, with peeling and chipping paint; the boy is described as a picky eater who mouths and chews on non-food items including painted windowsills. He was born full-term with normal early development. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. Immunizations are up to date. Diet is low in iron-rich foods and high in cow's milk. Family history is unremarkable. An older sibling reportedly had "anemia" diagnosed last year. The parents have not noticed seizures but report he had one brief episode of staring and twitching this morning.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable toddler, intermittently irritable. Anterior fontanelle closed. No nuchal rigidity. Pupils equal and reactive; no papilledema appreciated on limited fundoscopy. Pallor of the conjunctivae. Neurologic: truncal and gait ataxia with frequent falls; mild generalized weakness; deep tendon reflexes present and symmetric; no focal motor deficit. He has lost the few words he previously spoke. Abdomen mildly distended, soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. No rash. A faint bluish-gray line is noted along the gingival margin.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the subacute encephalopathy with ataxia, regression, anemia, pica, and an old-housing exposure, which initial test is most likely to reveal the underlying cause?
options: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
Venous blood lead level with CBC and peripheral smear
Serum ammonia and plasma amino acids
Stool studies for enteric pathogens
stages.explanation: The combination of a toddler with pica, residence in pre-1978 housing under renovation, microcytic anemia history, abdominal pain/constipation, regression, ataxia, and a gingival lead line strongly suggests lead poisoning; a venous blood lead level (the confirmatory standard over capillary screening) plus a CBC with smear (microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling) is the highest-yield initial workup. LP may be needed later if encephalopathy worsens or to exclude infection, but it does not address the obvious toxic exposure and carries risk if intracranial pressure is elevated. Ammonia/amino acids screen for metabolic disease, less likely given the exposure history. Stool studies address gastroenteritis, which does not explain regression, ataxia, anemia, or the lead line.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Venous blood lead level: 78 µg/dL
Hgb 8.9 g/dL, MCV 68 fL (microcytic)
Peripheral smear: basophilic stippling of erythrocytes
Ferritin: low
Reticulocyte count: mildly elevated
Abdominal radiograph: radiopaque flecks in the bowel lumen (ingested paint chips)
Non-contrast head CT: diffuse cerebral edema, no hemorrhage or mass

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis, and what does the blood lead level of 78 µg/dL with encephalopathy signify?
options: Iron deficiency anemia alone; the lead is an incidental finding
Lead encephalopathy—a medical emergency requiring urgent chelation
Acute cerebellitis from a viral illness
Thalassemia trait with coincidental developmental delay
stages.explanation: A blood lead level of 78 µg/dL with neurologic symptoms (encephalopathy, ataxia, seizure, regression) and cerebral edema constitutes lead encephalopathy, a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate chelation. Iron deficiency coexists (and increases lead absorption) but does not explain encephalopathy or the very high lead level. Acute cerebellitis is post-viral, typically without anemia, basophilic stippling, a lead line, or this exposure history. Thalassemia trait causes microcytosis but with a normal/high RBC count, no basophilic stippling or lead line, and does not cause encephalopathy. The radiopaque flecks confirm recent paint-chip ingestion. Lead encephalopathy carries high morbidity and demands prompt, aggressive treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For lead encephalopathy with a level of 78 µg/dL, what is the recommended chelation regimen?
options: Oral succimer (DMSA) monotherapy as an outpatient
Dimercaprol (BAL) followed by CaNa2-EDTA, given parenterally and inpatient
Oral D-penicillamine alone
Deferoxamine infusion
stages.explanation: For lead encephalopathy or levels ≥70 µg/dL, the standard is parenteral dual chelation: dimercaprol (BAL) started first, followed by CaNa2-EDTA, administered inpatient with supportive care for cerebral edema and seizures. BAL is given before EDTA because EDTA alone can transiently redistribute lead into the brain and worsen encephalopathy. Oral succimer/DMSA monotherapy is appropriate for moderate elevations (~45–69 µg/dL) WITHOUT encephalopathy, not for this emergency. D-penicillamine is a third-line oral agent, not used for acute encephalopathy. Deferoxamine chelates iron, not lead. Removing the child from the lead source and abdominal decontamination (whole-bowel irrigation for retained flecks) are essential adjuncts.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most important long-term intervention after stabilization, including for the sibling with prior 'anemia'?
options: Lifelong chelation therapy for the patient
Identify and remediate the environmental lead source and screen exposed household contacts
Switch to a high-cow's-milk diet to bind lead in the gut
Routine head CT every 3 months indefinitely
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of lead poisoning management is eliminating ongoing exposure: professional lead abatement/remediation of the home, removing the child during renovation, and reporting to public health, plus screening all exposed household contacts (the anemic sibling almost certainly needs a lead level). Iron and calcium repletion and a balanced diet reduce lead absorption. Lifelong chelation is not done; chelation is episodic and guided by levels and symptoms. A high-milk diet is counterproductive—excess cow's milk worsens iron deficiency, which increases lead absorption. Serial head CT is not routine surveillance. Without source remediation, levels rebound after chelation; prevention is paramount.

cases.diagnosis: Lead encephalopathy (severe childhood plumbism)
pearls: Suspect lead poisoning in a toddler with pica, pre-1978 housing/renovation, microcytic anemia, constipation/abdominal pain, regression, and a gingival lead line.
Smear shows basophilic stippling; abdominal film may show radiopaque paint flecks; venous (not capillary) lead level confirms.
Lead encephalopathy or level ≥70 µg/dL: emergency parenteral chelation with dimercaprol (BAL) FIRST, then CaNa2-EDTA, to avoid worsening CNS lead redistribution.
Iron deficiency increases lead absorption; the definitive long-term fix is environmental remediation plus screening household contacts.

cases.title: A 4-Year-Old With Morning Headaches and Vomiting
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of progressively worsening morning headaches with vomiting and new unsteadiness.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl is admitted for six weeks of headaches that are worst on waking and often relieved after she vomits. Initially attributed to a stomach virus, the vomiting now occurs several mornings a week without diarrhea and she has become increasingly tired and irritable. Over the past two weeks her parents noticed she tilts her head to one side, walks unsteadily, and has occasional double vision. There is no fever, neck pain, rash, or recent travel. She was born at term with normal development and is fully immunized. She takes no medications and has no allergies. There is no family history of neurologic disease or cancer. Review of systems reveals decreased appetite and a recent fall while running. The pediatrician noted her vision seems off and she squints. No history of seizures. She has had no infections, weight loss, or night sweats.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing but cooperative girl holding her head tilted to the right. Funduscopy: bilateral papilledema. Cranial nerves: bilateral abducens (CN VI) palsies with impaired lateral gaze; otherwise intact. Truncal ataxia with a wide-based gait and difficulty with tandem walking; dysmetria on finger-to-nose bilaterally; nystagmus on lateral gaze. Strength normal and symmetric; no long-tract signs. No neck stiffness. No organomegaly, no skin lesions, no lymphadenopathy. The combination of papilledema, CN VI palsies, and head tilt suggests raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given papilledema, abducens palsies, and cerebellar signs concerning for a posterior fossa mass with raised ICP, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Immediate lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
Brain MRI with and without contrast (CT if MRI not immediately available)
Empiric oral analgesics and outpatient ophthalmology follow-up
Electroencephalogram (EEG)
stages.explanation: Signs of elevated intracranial pressure with focal cerebellar findings demand urgent neuroimaging—MRI of the brain with and without contrast is the test of choice to characterize a posterior fossa mass and assess hydrocephalus; non-contrast CT is acceptable if MRI is not immediately available. Lumbar puncture is CONTRAINDICATED before imaging when a posterior fossa mass and obstructive hydrocephalus are suspected, due to risk of fatal cerebellar/tonsillar herniation. Outpatient analgesics dangerously delay diagnosis of a progressive intracranial process. EEG evaluates seizures, which are not the issue here and would not localize the mass. Imaging is both safe and diagnostic in this scenario.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI: a heterogeneously enhancing mass arising from the floor of the FOURTH ventricle, extending through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie ('plastic' growth pattern) toward the cerebellopontine angles and upper cervical canal
Obstructive hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles
No leptomeningeal enhancement seen on initial imaging
Diffusion: less restriction than typically seen with medulloblastoma
Spine MRI (staging): no drop metastases identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A fourth-ventricle floor mass that extrudes ('squeezes') through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie in a young child is most characteristic of which posterior fossa tumor?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
Brainstem (diffuse intrinsic pontine) glioma
stages.explanation: An ependymoma classically arises from the ependymal lining of the fourth ventricle floor and grows as a 'plastic' tumor, extruding through the outlet foramina (Luschka and Magendie) and wrapping around structures—the feature highlighted here. Medulloblastoma typically arises in the cerebellar vermis/roof of the fourth ventricle, is densely cellular with marked diffusion restriction, and readily seeds CSF (drop mets). Pilocytic astrocytoma is usually a cerebellar hemisphere cystic mass with an enhancing mural nodule, not a fourth-ventricle floor lesion threading through foramina. DIPG diffusely expands the pons without a discrete enhancing fourth-ventricle mass. The foraminal extrusion pattern is the key discriminator favoring ependymoma.

stages.label: ACUTE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While planning definitive treatment, the child develops worsening lethargy from obstructive hydrocephalus. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: High-dose IV mannitol alone and continued observation
CSF diversion (external ventricular drain or endoscopic third ventriculostomy) to relieve hydrocephalus
Emergent lumbar puncture to drain CSF
Start whole-brain radiation immediately
stages.explanation: Symptomatic obstructive hydrocephalus from a fourth-ventricle tumor is relieved by CSF diversion—an external ventricular drain or endoscopic third ventriculostomy—often as a bridge to definitive tumor resection (or hydrocephalus may resolve with resection itself). Corticosteroids reduce peritumoral edema as an adjunct. Mannitol alone is a temporizing measure and does not relieve the obstruction. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a posterior fossa mass (herniation risk). Radiation is part of postoperative therapy for residual/high-grade disease but is not the acute intervention for life-threatening hydrocephalus and is generally limited/avoided in very young children. Maximal safe surgical resection is the cornerstone of ependymoma treatment.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the most important determinant of prognosis and the primary definitive treatment for posterior fossa ependymoma?
options: Chemotherapy alone with avoidance of any surgery
Extent of surgical resection—gross total resection followed by adjuvant radiation
Stem cell transplant
A ketogenic diet
stages.explanation: The single most important prognostic factor in ependymoma is the extent of resection: maximal safe gross total resection followed by adjuvant conformal radiation therapy offers the best outcomes; the tumor's tendency to encase the brainstem and cranial nerves via foraminal extension can make complete resection difficult. Chemotherapy alone is inadequate as primary therapy (ependymoma is relatively chemoresistant; chemo is mainly used to delay radiation in infants). Stem cell transplant has a limited role and is not the standard. A ketogenic diet is not a treatment for ependymoma. Aggressive, careful surgery plus radiation defines modern management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because the tumor extends through the fourth-ventricle outlet foramina and abuts the brainstem and lower cranial nerves, which postoperative complication should the team specifically anticipate?
options: Lower cranial nerve dysfunction causing dysphagia and risk of aspiration
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Acute glomerulonephritis
Peripheral facial nerve palsy from parotid involvement
stages.explanation: Posterior fossa ependymomas growing through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie wrap around the brainstem and lower cranial nerves (IX, X, XII), so resection in this region risks injury to these nerves, producing dysphagia, impaired gag, and aspiration risk—an important anticipated complication requiring swallow evaluation and airway/feeding precautions; posterior fossa syndrome (cerebellar mutism) is also recognized after such surgery. Carpal tunnel syndrome is a wrist median neuropathy, anatomically irrelevant. Acute glomerulonephritis is a renal process unrelated to posterior fossa surgery. A peripheral facial palsy from parotid involvement does not arise from a fourth-ventricle tumor; any facial weakness here would be from brainstem/CN VII nucleus involvement, not the parotid. Anticipating lower-cranial-nerve deficits guides safe postoperative care.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior fossa (fourth-ventricle) ependymoma
pearls: Posterior fossa ependymoma arises from the fourth-ventricle floor and grows as a 'plastic' tumor extruding through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie—a distinguishing feature.
Morning headache, vomiting, papilledema, CN VI palsies, head tilt, and cerebellar signs signal raised ICP; image with MRI and NEVER LP before excluding a posterior fossa mass.
Differentiate from medulloblastoma (vermian, marked diffusion restriction, CSF seeding) and pilocytic astrocytoma (cystic hemispheric mass with mural nodule).
Extent of resection is the key prognostic factor; treat with maximal safe surgery plus adjuvant radiation, with CSF diversion for obstructive hydrocephalus.

cases.title: A 7-Year-Old With Back Pain and a Curving Spine
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of progressive back pain, a worsening spinal curve, and a change in walking.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is referred for back pain and progressive scoliosis noted by his school nurse. For about four months he has complained of dull mid-back pain that is worse at night and sometimes wakes him from sleep. His parents noticed his right shoulder is higher than the left and his gait has become slightly stiff and clumsy, with new tripping. He has had occasional urinary urgency and one episode of daytime wetting, unusual for him. There is no history of trauma, fever, or weight loss. He was born at term with normal development; no prior surgeries. He takes only occasional ibuprofen, with partial relief, and has no allergies. Immunizations are current. Family history is negative for scoliosis, neurofibromatosis, or cancer. Review of systems is otherwise negative. The combination of night-time/painful scoliosis with neurologic change is notable, as typical idiopathic scoliosis is painless.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative boy. Spine: a left-convex thoracic curve with rib asymmetry on Adams forward-bend test; paraspinal tenderness over the mid-thoracic spine. Neurologic: mild spasticity in both lower extremities with brisk patellar and Achilles reflexes; bilateral ankle clonus; equivocal Babinski signs. Mild weakness of hip flexion; gait is stiff/spastic. Sensory exam suggests a subtle decline in pinprick below the mid-trunk. Abdominal reflexes asymmetric. Upper extremities normal. No café-au-lait macules or neurocutaneous stigmata. Anal tone slightly reduced.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature in this child most strongly distinguishes his presentation from typical adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and mandates further workup?
options: Presence of a thoracic curve
Painful, atypical (left-convex) scoliosis with night pain and upper motor neuron signs
Rib hump on forward-bend testing
Shoulder height asymmetry
stages.explanation: Idiopathic scoliosis is typically painless, right-convex in the thoracic spine, occurs in adolescents, and lacks neurologic findings. Red flags for a secondary (neurogenic/structural) cause include young age, PAIN especially at night, a LEFT-convex curve, and upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus) plus bowel/bladder changes—all present here—warranting urgent neuroimaging for an intraspinal lesion. A thoracic curve, a rib hump, and shoulder asymmetry are all common in benign idiopathic scoliosis and are not in themselves alarming. It is the constellation of pain, atypical curve direction, and long-tract signs that signals a cord pathology.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Plain spine radiographs: left-convex thoracic scoliosis, no vertebral destruction
MRI of the entire spine with contrast: a fusiform, expansile INTRAMEDULLARY mass widening the spinal cord, spanning multiple thoracic segments
Long-segment (holocord) T2 hyperintensity within the cord with associated cysts/syrinx above and below the solid component
Patchy heterogeneous enhancement of the solid portion
No extramedullary or bony lesion; brain MRI normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An expansile INTRAMEDULLARY cord mass with holocord T2 signal, associated syrinx, and patchy enhancement in a 7-year-old is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Spinal cord (intramedullary) astrocytoma
Schwannoma of a nerve root
Vertebral osteoid osteoma
Leptomeningeal metastasis (extramedullary)
stages.explanation: In children, the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor is astrocytoma (ependymoma is more common in adults). The MRI shows an expansile, INTRAMEDULLARY (within the cord) mass with long-segment/holocord T2 signal, tumoral cysts/syrinx, and patchy enhancement—classic for a pediatric cord astrocytoma. A schwannoma is an intradural-EXTRAmedullary nerve-sheath tumor, not within the cord. Osteoid osteoma is a painful bony lesion (often causing painful scoliosis) but arises in the vertebra/posterior elements, not within the cord, and shows a nidus with reactive sclerosis. Leptomeningeal metastasis is extramedullary surface enhancement, not an expansile intramedullary mass. The intramedullary location and holocord signal point to astrocytoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Because this intramedullary tumor has no family history of neurofibromatosis, which additional evaluation is still most appropriate to complete the workup?
options: A skin and ophthalmologic exam (and brain MRI) to screen for neurofibromatosis-associated features, since cord astrocytomas can be associated with NF1
Immediate bone marrow biopsy
24-hour urinary catecholamines
Echocardiography to look for cardiac rhabdomyomas
stages.explanation: Although this child has no known family history, low-grade gliomas (including spinal cord astrocytomas) can be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1, so a careful skin exam for café-au-lait macules, axillary/inguinal freckling, and neurofibromas, an ophthalmologic exam for Lisch nodules/optic glioma, and brain MRI are appropriate to evaluate for NF1 (which can arise de novo). A bone marrow biopsy is unnecessary—there is no hematologic malignancy suspected. Urinary catecholamines screen for pheochromocytoma/neuroblastoma, not indicated by this presentation. Echocardiography for cardiac rhabdomyomas screens for tuberous sclerosis, which is not suggested by an intramedullary astrocytoma. Identifying an underlying tumor-predisposition syndrome guides surveillance and counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the primary treatment approach for a pediatric low-grade intramedullary spinal cord astrocytoma?
options: Immediate spinal fusion to correct the scoliosis first
Maximal safe surgical resection (debulking) with intraoperative neuromonitoring; adjuvant therapy reserved for high-grade or progressive disease
Upfront high-dose craniospinal irradiation in all cases
Observation alone with serial imaging and no surgery
stages.explanation: For pediatric intramedullary astrocytomas, maximal SAFE surgical resection/debulking with intraoperative neurophysiologic monitoring is the mainstay; because these tumors are infiltrative, the goal is balancing tumor removal against preservation of cord function. Adjuvant chemotherapy or radiation is reserved for high-grade histology, residual/progressive low-grade tumor, or unresectable disease (radiation is used cautiously in young children). Correcting scoliosis first is inappropriate—the curve is secondary to the cord tumor, which must be addressed; fusion may be considered later for residual deformity. Upfront craniospinal radiation in all cases is excessive and toxic, not standard for low-grade tumors. Observation alone is inadequate given progressive neurologic deficits. Histology guides any adjuvant therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If untreated and the tumor continues to expand, which complication is the patient at greatest risk of developing?
options: Progressive spastic paraparesis with sensory level and bowel/bladder dysfunction (myelopathy)
Lower motor neuron facial weakness
Acute meningitis
Carpal tunnel syndrome
stages.explanation: An expanding intramedullary cord tumor produces a progressive MYELOPATHY: worsening spastic paraparesis below the lesion, an ascending sensory level, hyperreflexia/clonus, and bowel/bladder dysfunction—exactly the trajectory hinted at by this boy's spasticity, sensory change, and new urinary symptoms. Untreated, this can progress to paraplegia. Lower motor neuron facial weakness reflects CN VII pathology, unrelated to a thoracic cord lesion. Acute meningitis is an infectious/inflammatory process, not a consequence of a cord tumor. Carpal tunnel syndrome is a peripheral median neuropathy at the wrist, anatomically irrelevant here. Recognizing the myelopathic progression underscores the need for timely diagnosis and surgical decompression.

cases.diagnosis: Intramedullary spinal cord astrocytoma (thoracic, low-grade)
pearls: Painful, atypical (left-convex), or rapidly progressive scoliosis with night pain and upper motor neuron signs is NOT idiopathic—image the whole spine with MRI.
Astrocytoma is the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor in children; MRI shows an expansile cord mass, often holocord T2 signal with a syrinx and patchy enhancement.
Distinguish intramedullary (astrocytoma/ependymoma) from intradural-extramedullary (schwannoma/meningioma) and bony lesions (osteoid osteoma causing painful scoliosis).
Treatment is maximal safe resection with neuromonitoring; adjuvant therapy is reserved for high-grade or progressive disease. Untreated tumors cause progressive myelopathy.

cases.title: A 9-Year-Old With Fever, Headache, and a Seizure
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of worsening headache and fever culminating in a focal seizure with arm twitching.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the ED after a witnessed seizure with twitching of the left arm and face that lasted two minutes, followed by left-sided weakness. For about a week he has had progressively worsening headaches, low-grade fevers, and increasing lethargy; today he vomited and seemed confused. He has a history of unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease (tetralogy of Fallot with a right-to-left shunt) and chronic resting hypoxemia with baseline clubbing; surgical repair was delayed due to family circumstances. He had a dental cleaning three weeks ago. He takes no daily cardiac medications currently and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no recent travel or sick contacts. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he is admitted to the ICU. Parents note he has been less active and more irritable than usual over the past two weeks, and his baseline oxygen saturation runs in the high 80s.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, drowsy boy, arousable but confused (postictal). Central cyanosis with digital clubbing. Neurologic: left-sided hemiparesis (4-/5) with a left facial droop sparing the forehead; left hyperreflexia and an extensor plantar response on the left; right side normal. Mild neck stiffness. Funduscopy: early papilledema. Cardiac: a harsh systolic murmur at the left sternal border; no new regurgitant murmur appreciated. Lungs clear. No skin rash or splinter hemorrhages noted. The focal deficit plus fever and a known right-to-left shunt is highly suggestive of a focal intracranial infection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this cyanotic-heart-disease child with fever, headache, focal seizure, and a new focal deficit, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Lumbar puncture for CSF studies before any imaging
Contrast-enhanced brain MRI (or CT) to evaluate for a focal intracranial lesion
Empiric oral antibiotics and discharge with follow-up
Electroencephalogram as the first study
stages.explanation: Fever plus a focal seizure and focal neurologic deficit in a child with a right-to-left cardiac shunt is classic for a brain abscess; contrast-enhanced neuroimaging (MRI is most sensitive; CT if MRI is unavailable) is the urgent first step to identify a ring-enhancing lesion and assess mass effect. Lumbar puncture BEFORE imaging is contraindicated when a space-occupying lesion with raised ICP is suspected, due to herniation risk—and CSF is often nondiagnostic in abscess. Oral antibiotics with discharge is dangerously inadequate for a possible CNS abscess. EEG first characterizes seizures but does not identify the structural lesion and delays definitive diagnosis. Imaging both confirms the abscess and guides neurosurgical planning.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Contrast brain MRI: a 2.8 cm ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontoparietal region with central diffusion restriction and surrounding vasogenic edema, causing mild midline shift
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL with neutrophilia
CRP 9.5 mg/dL, ESR elevated
Blood cultures: pending (two sets drawn)
Echocardiogram: tetralogy of Fallot physiology with right-to-left shunt; no valvular vegetation seen
Diffusion-weighted imaging: restricted diffusion centrally (favors abscess over necrotic tumor)

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why are children with cyanotic congenital heart disease and a right-to-left shunt at particularly high risk for brain abscess?
options: The shunt allows bacteria to bypass the pulmonary capillary filter and seed the brain directly, aided by polycythemia and relative cerebral hypoxia
Cyanotic heart disease causes immune deficiency identical to HIV
Right-to-left shunts cause direct bacterial contamination of CSF
The murmur mechanically damages the blood-brain barrier
stages.explanation: In a right-to-left shunt, venous blood bypasses the lungs, so bacteria (e.g., from transient bacteremia after dental work) escape the normal pulmonary capillary filtration and travel arterially to the brain, where chronic hypoxemia and secondary polycythemia (with sludging and microinfarction) create a nidus favoring abscess formation—the classic septic-embolus pathophysiology. That option is incorrect: cyanotic heart disease does not cause HIV-like immunodeficiency. Another option is wrong: the shunt does not directly contaminate CSF; the route is hematogenous arterial seeding of brain parenchyma. A third option is incorrect: a murmur reflects turbulent flow and does not damage the blood-brain barrier. This is why endocarditis prophylaxis and timely cardiac repair matter in these children.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen while awaiting cultures and neurosurgical evaluation?
options: Oral amoxicillin alone
Vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone) plus metronidazole
Acyclovir monotherapy
Fluconazole monotherapy
stages.explanation: Empiric therapy for a pyogenic brain abscess must cover the likely polymicrobial flora—streptococci (including Streptococcus anginosus/viridans, common after dental sources), staphylococci, gram-negatives, and anaerobes—so the standard regimen is vancomycin (staph, including MRSA) plus a third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime (streptococci, gram-negatives) plus metronidazole (anaerobes). Oral amoxicillin is inadequate in spectrum and route for a CNS abscess. Acyclovir treats HSV encephalitis, not a ring-enhancing pyogenic abscess. Fluconazole targets fungi, not the expected bacterial pathogens here. Definitive care typically combines prolonged IV antibiotics with neurosurgical aspiration/drainage (which also yields a microbiologic diagnosis), plus seizure management and ICP control.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE CARE
stages.question: Given a 2.8 cm abscess with mass effect and midline shift, what is the most appropriate definitive intervention alongside antibiotics?
options: Continue antibiotics alone indefinitely without any procedure
Stereotactic or open neurosurgical aspiration/drainage of the abscess
Immediate corrective cardiac surgery before addressing the brain lesion
Whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: For a brain abscess larger than ~2.5 cm, causing mass effect/midline shift, or when a microbiologic diagnosis is needed, neurosurgical aspiration/drainage (stereotactic or open) combined with prolonged IV antibiotics is the standard of care; aspiration relieves pressure and identifies the organism to tailor therapy. Antibiotics alone may suffice for small (<2.5 cm) abscesses without mass effect, but not for this larger lesion with shift. Corrective cardiac surgery, while ultimately needed to eliminate the shunt and prevent recurrence, is not the immediate priority over a life-threatening intracranial lesion. Radiation has no role in treating a pyogenic abscess. Seizure prophylaxis and ICP management are important adjuncts during recovery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After drainage and several weeks of IV antibiotics, what is the most appropriate plan to reduce the risk of future brain abscesses in this patient?
options: No further intervention is needed once the abscess resolves
Refer for surgical correction of the cyanotic heart defect and ensure appropriate dental/endocarditis prophylaxis
Place him on lifelong oral antibiotics indefinitely
Begin long-term anticoagulation
stages.explanation: The root cause of recurrent brain abscess in this child is the persistent right-to-left shunt allowing bacteria to bypass the lungs; definitive risk reduction comes from surgical correction of the cyanotic congenital heart defect, together with good dental hygiene and appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis before high-risk procedures. Doing nothing leaves the underlying shunt and high recurrence risk unaddressed. Indefinite oral antibiotics are not standard, promote resistance, and do not fix the shunt. Long-term anticoagulation does not prevent septic embolic seeding through a shunt and adds bleeding risk—it is not indicated here. Correcting the anatomy is the durable solution; abscess care also includes follow-up imaging to confirm resolution.

cases.diagnosis: Brain abscess in cyanotic congenital heart disease (right-to-left shunt)
pearls: Fever, headache, and a new focal deficit/seizure in a child with a right-to-left cardiac shunt = brain abscess until proven otherwise; image first, do NOT LP before imaging.
Right-to-left shunts let bacteria bypass the pulmonary filter; chronic hypoxemia and polycythemia favor abscess formation—often after a dental or other bacteremic source.
MRI shows a ring-enhancing lesion with central diffusion restriction (distinguishes abscess from necrotic tumor); Streptococcus anginosus group is a frequent pathogen.
Treat with vancomycin + ceftriaxone + metronidazole plus neurosurgical drainage for lesions >2.5 cm or with mass effect; correct the cardiac defect to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: A Boy with Falling Grades and Fading Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening school performance, clumsiness, and trouble seeing the board over the past 5 months.
cases.history: A previously healthy, mainstream first-grader is brought in by his mother for a 5-month decline. He was an A/B student who now struggles to read, forgets instructions, and has become withdrawn and irritable with frequent emotional outbursts that teachers initially attributed to ADHD. Over the last 2 months he developed clumsiness, tripping, and difficulty hearing the teacher, often asking her to repeat herself. He has had two episodes of bumping into furniture on his left side. His handwriting has deteriorated. Birth and early development were normal; he walked at 12 months and spoke in sentences by 2 years. No seizures, no headaches, no recent illness or trauma. Medications: a methylphenidate trial started 6 weeks ago by his pediatrician with no benefit. No allergies. He lives with his mother and younger sister. Family history: a maternal uncle died in his teens of an unexplained progressive neurologic illness once labeled 'a type of MS'; mother has occasional constipation and fatigue but no diagnosis.
cases.exam: Alert but inattentive boy with flattened affect and slowed responses. Cranial nerves: bilateral hearing reduced to finger rub; left homonymous visual field defect on confrontation; fundi normal. Motor: mild spasticity and brisk reflexes in both legs with bilateral upgoing toes; subtle right-hand dysmetria on finger-to-nose. Gait mildly wide-based and unsteady with difficulty tandem walking. No tremor, no organomegaly, skin without neurocutaneous stigmata, no Kayser-Fleischer rings. Mental status notable for impaired short-term recall and dysgraphia. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best argues against a primary psychiatric or ADHD diagnosis and warrants neurologic workup?
options: Irritability and emotional outbursts at school
Withdrawn behavior and declining grades
Upper motor neuron signs with a left homonymous field defect and hearing loss
Lack of response to a 6-week methylphenidate trial
stages.explanation: Objective focal neurologic findings, bilateral upgoing toes, spasticity, a homonymous visual field defect, and hearing loss point to structural CNS disease, not a behavioral disorder. Behavioral/ADHD diagnoses are diagnoses of exclusion and never explain UMN signs or visual field cuts. Irritability/outbursts and withdrawal/declining grades are nonspecific and overlap heavily with psychiatric conditions, which is exactly why this boy was misdiagnosed. Failure of stimulant therapy is suggestive but alone is weak evidence; many true ADHD patients do not respond to a first agent. The combination of cognitive regression with hard localizing signs mandates neuroimaging.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic study?
options: MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Lumbar puncture for oligoclonal bands
EEG with sleep deprivation
Noncontrast head CT
stages.explanation: Progressive cognitive regression with multifocal UMN signs in a young boy demands brain MRI with contrast, which is most sensitive for white matter disease and shows characteristic patterns plus active demyelination via enhancement. LP for oligoclonal bands targets MS, which is rare at age 7 and would not be pursued before imaging. EEG is unhelpful without seizures and would not reveal the underlying structural pathology. Noncontrast CT is insensitive for early white matter disease and lacks the contrast information needed to assess the demyelinating edge; MRI is far superior here.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Symmetric confluent T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the parieto-occipital periventricular white matter
Involvement of the splenium of the corpus callosum
Peripheral rim of contrast enhancement at the leading (advancing) demyelinating edge
Sparing of subcortical U-fibers; no mass effect or restricted diffusion centrally
Involvement of corticospinal tracts, auditory pathways, and posterior visual pathways

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the posterior-predominant demyelination with an enhancing edge in a 7-year-old boy, which test confirms the underlying diagnosis?
options: Serum lactate and pyruvate
Plasma very-long-chain fatty acids (VLCFA)
Serum ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urinary copper
Leukocyte arylsulfatase A activity
stages.explanation: Symmetric parieto-occipital demyelination crossing the splenium with a contrast-enhancing advancing margin in a boy is the classic picture of childhood cerebral X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy; plasma VLCFA (elevated C26:0 and C26:0/C22:0 and C24:0/C22:0 ratios) is the screening/confirmatory biochemical test. Lactate/pyruvate screen for mitochondrial disease, which usually shows gray-matter or basal ganglia involvement and lactate elevation. Ceruloplasmin/copper evaluate Wilson disease, which targets basal ganglia and liver, not symmetric posterior white matter. Arylsulfatase A diagnoses metachromatic leukodystrophy, which typically lacks the enhancing leading edge and posterior predominance.

stages.label: BIOCHEMISTRY & ENDOCRINE
stages.content:
Plasma C26:0 markedly elevated
C26:0/C22:0 ratio elevated
C24:0/C22:0 ratio elevated
ABCD1 gene: pathogenic hemizygous variant confirmed
Morning ACTH elevated; 8 a.m. cortisol low; cosyntropin-stimulated cortisol blunted

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The Loes severity score is low-to-moderate and the lesions enhance. What intervention offers the best chance to halt cerebral disease progression, and what comorbidity must be treated now?
options: Lorenzo's oil and a low-fat diet; no additional therapy needed
High-dose corticosteroids for demyelination; observe the white matter
Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (or approved gene therapy) for early cerebral disease, plus glucocorticoid replacement for adrenal insufficiency
Plasma exchange followed by rituximab for the enhancing lesions
stages.explanation: For early childhood cerebral ALD with low Loes score and active (enhancing) demyelination, allogeneic HSCT or approved ex-vivo gene therapy can arrest cerebral progression; benefit is greatest when performed early before extensive damage. Concurrent primary adrenal insufficiency (elevated ACTH, blunted cortisol) is common in ALD and is life-threatening, so physiologic glucocorticoid replacement is mandatory now. Lorenzo's oil normalizes VLCFA but does not stop established cerebral disease. Corticosteroids/observation is wrong; this is not inflammatory demyelination like MS and steroids will not halt it. PLEX/rituximab treats autoimmune demyelination, not a peroxisomal disorder.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What additional counseling and screening is essential for this family given the diagnosis?
options: No family testing is needed because the mutation arose de novo
Genetic counseling for X-linked inheritance, testing of the mother (likely carrier) and at-risk male relatives, and lifelong adrenal monitoring for affected/at-risk males
Only the patient's sisters need neurologic monitoring
Reassure that female carriers are never affected and require no follow-up
stages.explanation: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy is inherited in an X-linked pattern, so the mother is very likely a carrier (consistent with the affected maternal uncle), and at-risk male relatives should be identified and screened with VLCFA and ABCD1 testing; presymptomatic males need ongoing surveillance for both cerebral disease (serial MRI) and adrenal insufficiency, because early detection enables curative HSCT/gene therapy and lifesaving steroid replacement. Assuming a de novo mutation with no family testing ignores the clear maternal-lineage history. Monitoring only sisters neurologically misses at-risk boys, who develop the severe cerebral phenotype. Saying female carriers are never affected is false: carriers can develop adrenomyeloneuropathy-type symptoms in adulthood and warrant follow-up.

cases.diagnosis: Childhood cerebral X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ABCD1 mutation) with adrenal insufficiency
pearls: Behavioral/cognitive regression plus hard UMN or sensory signs in a school-age boy = leukodystrophy until proven otherwise; do not anchor on ADHD.
Cerebral ALD classically shows symmetric parieto-occipital demyelination crossing the splenium with an enhancing advancing edge; screen with plasma VLCFA and confirm with ABCD1.
Always evaluate adrenal function in any boy with ALD-spectrum disease; primary adrenal insufficiency may precede or accompany neurologic disease and is fatal if missed.
HSCT or gene therapy can halt early cerebral ALD when the Loes score is low and lesions enhance, so prompt recognition is time-critical.

cases.title: A Toddler Who Forgot How to Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive loss of walking and worsening floppiness over 4 months, now admitted for failure to thrive and frequent falls.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is admitted after months of motor decline. She walked independently at 13 months and ran by 18 months, but over the past 4 months she became increasingly unsteady, started falling, and now can no longer stand without support. Her parents report she seems weak and 'floppy,' tires quickly, and has become irritable and less interactive. More recently they note stiffening of her legs when picked up, occasional toe-walking, and difficulty feeding with coughing on liquids. No fevers, no seizures, no trauma. Birth was term and uncomplicated; early milestones were normal. Medications: none. No allergies. Diet: previously good, now poor with weight loss. She is the second child of healthy first-cousin parents of Middle Eastern descent. Family history: a paternal cousin reportedly died young of a 'nerve disease.' An older sibling, age 5, is developing normally. Immunizations up to date. Developmental history confirms clear regression rather than static delay.
cases.exam: Thin, irritable toddler. Cranial nerves grossly intact; fundi without cherry-red spot; tracks faces. Tone is mixed: appendicular hypotonia with head lag and poor truncal control, yet brisk patellar and ankle reflexes with sustained ankle clonus and bilateral Babinski signs. Cannot bear weight; legs scissor and stiffen on suspension. No fasciculations. Strength difficult to assess but antigravity movement preserved proximally. No organomegaly. Gag intact but coughs with thin liquids. No skin findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of peripheral hypotonia with hyperreflexia, clonus, and upgoing toes most specifically localizes the disease to which process?
options: Pure anterior horn cell (lower motor neuron) disease
Combined central (UMN) and peripheral nerve (LMN) involvement
Isolated cerebellar degeneration
Neuromuscular junction disorder
stages.explanation: Brisk reflexes, clonus, and extensor plantar responses indicate central/upper motor neuron (CNS white matter) disease, while appendicular hypotonia, head lag, and feeding difficulty point to peripheral nerve involvement (demyelinating neuropathy). This central-plus-peripheral pattern is the hallmark of a disorder affecting both CNS and PNS myelin. Pure anterior horn cell disease (that option, e.g., SMA) gives hypotonia with absent reflexes, not clonus. Isolated cerebellar degeneration causes ataxia and hypotonia but not pyramidal signs. A neuromuscular junction disorder causes fatigable weakness with normal or reduced reflexes, never UMN signs. Recognizing mixed UMN/LMN signs narrows the differential to leukodystrophies with neuropathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial diagnostic study will best characterize the suspected disease process?
options: Creatine kinase and muscle biopsy
MRI of the brain plus nerve conduction studies
Acetylcholine receptor antibodies
Genetic panel for spinal muscular atrophy
stages.explanation: Mixed central and peripheral signs are best evaluated with brain MRI (to demonstrate white matter disease) plus nerve conduction studies (to confirm a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy); together they capture both compartments and strongly suggest a leukodystrophy with neuropathy. CK/muscle biopsy evaluates myopathy, which would not explain UMN signs. ACh receptor antibodies test for myasthenia, inconsistent with clonus and Babinski. An SMA panel targets a pure LMN disorder that causes areflexia, not the brisk reflexes seen here. Pairing CNS imaging with peripheral electrophysiology is the most efficient way to localize this dual-compartment disease.

stages.label: MRI & ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Brain MRI: symmetric confluent T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in periventricular and central white matter with a radiating 'tigroid'/leopard-skin pattern of spared perivascular regions
Sparing of subcortical U-fibers early; no contrast enhancement
Nerve conduction studies: markedly slowed conduction velocities with prolonged distal latencies (demyelinating sensorimotor polyneuropathy)
CSF: protein elevated; cells normal

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Symmetric non-enhancing demyelination with a tigroid pattern PLUS a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy and elevated CSF protein points to which confirmatory enzyme assay?
options: Leukocyte hexosaminidase A
Plasma very-long-chain fatty acids
Leukocyte arylsulfatase A (with urinary sulfatides)
Leukocyte galactocerebrosidase
stages.explanation: Tigroid non-enhancing white matter changes with a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy and elevated CSF protein is classic metachromatic leukodystrophy; deficient leukocyte arylsulfatase A with elevated urinary sulfatides confirms it. Hexosaminidase A tests for Tay-Sachs, which causes gray-matter disease and a cherry-red spot, not peripheral neuropathy. VLCFA screen for adrenoleukodystrophy, which lacks neuropathy and shows an enhancing posterior edge. Galactocerebrosidase diagnoses Krabbe disease, an important mimic that also has neuropathy, so urinary sulfatides and the enzyme assay distinguish MLD from Krabbe. Because pseudodeficiency alleles exist, urinary sulfatides and genetic confirmation support the diagnosis.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY LABS
stages.content:
Leukocyte arylsulfatase A activity: markedly reduced
Urinary sulfatide excretion: elevated
ARSA gene: two pathogenic variants identified (compound heterozygous)
Galactocerebrosidase activity: normal (Krabbe excluded)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This child has late-infantile MLD with established motor regression, spasticity, and dysphagia. What is the most appropriate counseling regarding disease-modifying therapy?
options: Hematopoietic stem cell transplant will rapidly reverse her current deficits
Disease-modifying options (HSCT or gene therapy) help mainly in pre-/early-symptomatic disease; she is now symptomatic, so care is largely supportive, and presymptomatic siblings should be tested
Enzyme replacement therapy fully crosses the blood-brain barrier and is curative
A high-fat ketogenic diet halts demyelination in MLD
stages.explanation: In late-infantile MLD, disease-modifying therapies (allogeneic HSCT, and approved ex-vivo gene therapy) provide benefit primarily when given before or very early in symptom onset; once a child has overt motor regression and spasticity, these therapies generally cannot reverse damage, so management becomes multidisciplinary supportive care (spasticity management, feeding/aspiration precautions, seizure care). Critically, the asymptomatic older sibling should be tested, because early identification could allow timely therapy. HSCT reversing deficits is false. ERT crossing the BBB is false; current ERT does not effectively treat CNS disease. A ketogenic diet has no role in MLD.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: As her disease advances, which complication should the team proactively manage to maintain comfort and reduce morbidity?
options: Recurrent aspiration from progressive bulbar dysfunction and dysphagia
Hyperthyroidism
Polycythemia
Premature puberty
