stages.explanation: Progressive MLD causes worsening spasticity, seizures, and bulbar/swallowing dysfunction; recurrent aspiration is a major source of morbidity and mortality, so proactive management (swallow evaluation, thickened feeds or gastrostomy, secretion control, aspiration precautions) is essential, alongside spasticity and seizure management and palliative planning. Hyperthyroidism, polycythemia, and premature puberty are not features of metachromatic leukodystrophy and are distractors. Anticipating bulbar decline and protecting the airway/nutrition directly improves quality of life in this progressive neurodegenerative disorder, and is a core component of multidisciplinary supportive care for affected children.

cases.diagnosis: Late-infantile metachromatic leukodystrophy (arylsulfatase A deficiency, ARSA mutations)
pearls: Peripheral hypotonia with brisk reflexes, clonus, and Babinski signs (mixed UMN/LMN) signals a leukodystrophy with neuropathy such as MLD or Krabbe.
MLD shows symmetric non-enhancing tigroid white matter changes, a demyelinating peripheral neuropathy, and elevated CSF protein; confirm with low arylsulfatase A plus elevated urinary sulfatides.
Pseudodeficiency alleles can lower ARSA activity without disease, so urinary sulfatides and genetic testing are needed to confirm true MLD.
Disease-modifying therapy (HSCT/gene therapy) works best presymptomatically; always test at-risk siblings while they are still asymptomatic.

cases.title: An Infant Who Startles and Slips Backward
cases.chiefComplaint: Loss of previously acquired skills and an exaggerated startle to sound over the past 2 months.
cases.history: A 10-month-old boy is brought in for developmental regression. He sat unsupported at 6 months and reached for toys, but over the past 2 months he has become less interactive, stopped sitting steadily, and no longer reaches purposefully. His parents describe a dramatic whole-body jerk in response to sudden noises, even soft sounds, that has worsened. He seems to look past people and tracks less. Feeding has become slower. No fevers, vomiting, or seizures noted, though parents wonder if some 'jerks' are seizures. Pregnancy and delivery were normal; newborn screen in their state did not include this disorder. Medications: vitamin D drops only. No allergies. He is an only child. Family history: both parents are of Ashkenazi Jewish descent and are healthy; a maternal great-aunt reportedly had a baby who died before age 3 of a 'brain disease.' No consanguinity. Social: lives with both parents; no toxic exposures. Growth parameters were normal at prior visits with a head circumference now tracking upward across percentiles.
cases.exam: Apathetic infant with poor visual engagement. Striking exaggerated startle (hyperacusis) to a hand clap. Generalized hypotonia with head lag and loss of head control; reflexes brisk with bilateral upgoing toes. No organomegaly; abdomen soft, liver and spleen not enlarged. No coarse facial features. Fundoscopy shows a bright red spot at the center of each macula surrounded by a pale halo. No skin lesions. Increasing head circumference compared with prior plots. He does not fix and follow consistently and shows roving eye movements.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single examination finding most powerfully narrows the diagnosis in this regressing infant?
options: Generalized hypotonia with head lag
Bilateral cherry-red maculae with marked startle response and NO hepatosplenomegaly
Brisk reflexes with upgoing toes
Increasing head circumference
stages.explanation: The dyad of bilateral cherry-red spots plus an exaggerated startle (hyperacusis) in a regressing infant WITHOUT hepatosplenomegaly is highly specific and points to a GM2 gangliosidosis (Tay-Sachs). The absence of organomegaly is the key discriminator that separates Tay-Sachs from storage diseases that enlarge the liver/spleen (e.g., Niemann-Pick, Sandhoff often with organomegaly, GM1). Hypotonia, brisk reflexes, and macrocephaly are all consistent with neurodegeneration but are nonspecific and occur in many leukodystrophies and storage disorders. The cherry-red macula reflects ganglioside accumulation in retinal ganglion cells with a normal, contrasting foveal center.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory test will most directly establish the diagnosis?
options: Leukocyte/serum hexosaminidase A activity
Urine organic acids
Plasma very-long-chain fatty acids
Lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase
stages.explanation: A cherry-red spot with hyperacusis and regression in an Ashkenazi infant without organomegaly is classic Tay-Sachs disease, caused by deficient hexosaminidase A (HEXA); measuring hexosaminidase A enzyme activity is the most direct diagnostic test. Urine organic acids screen for organic acidemias, which present with metabolic acidosis and decompensation, not a cherry-red spot. VLCFA evaluate peroxisomal/adrenoleukodystrophy and would be normal. Acid alpha-glucosidase tests for Pompe disease, which causes a hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and prominent weakness, not a cherry-red macula. Hex A deficiency with normal/elevated total hexosaminidase (due to preserved Hex B) is diagnostic; molecular HEXA testing confirms it.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this infant had presented with the same findings PLUS hepatosplenomegaly, which alternative diagnosis would move to the top of the differential?
options: Tay-Sachs disease (still the best fit)
Sandhoff disease (hexosaminidase A and B deficiency) or Niemann-Pick disease
X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy
GLUT1 deficiency syndrome
stages.explanation: Organomegaly is the key discriminator: a cherry-red spot with regression PLUS hepatosplenomegaly suggests Sandhoff disease (deficiency of BOTH hexosaminidase A and B, so total hexosaminidase is low) or Niemann-Pick disease, rather than Tay-Sachs, in which the liver and spleen are characteristically spared. So adding organomegaly makes Tay-Sachs less likely. Adrenoleukodystrophy is a later-onset white-matter disease in boys without a cherry-red spot and without this storage-organomegaly picture. GLUT1 deficiency causes fasting-dependent seizures and movement disorder with low CSF glucose, not a cherry-red macula or organomegaly. Recognizing the cherry-red-spot differential by organomegaly and enzyme pattern is high-yield.

stages.label: ENZYME & GENETIC TESTING
stages.content:
Hexosaminidase A activity: markedly deficient
Total hexosaminidase (Hex A + Hex B): normal-to-elevated (Hex B preserved)
HEXA gene: two pathogenic variants identified (compound heterozygous)
Brain MRI: bilaterally symmetric T2 hyperintensity of the basal ganglia and thalami; progressive cerebral and cerebellar volume changes
No hepatosplenomegaly on abdominal ultrasound

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next several months, which clinical course should the family be counseled to expect?
options: Plateau of symptoms with eventual partial recovery once treatment begins
Progressive neurodegeneration with seizures, increasing macrocephaly, loss of vision, and death typically in early childhood
Predominantly hepatic failure requiring liver transplant
Stabilization with enzyme replacement therapy that crosses the blood-brain barrier
stages.explanation: Infantile Tay-Sachs disease is relentlessly progressive: continued regression, refractory seizures, progressive macrocephaly (from neuronal storage and reactive gliosis), blindness, increasing spasticity, and death usually by 2 to 4 years. Symptom plateau with recovery does not occur. Hepatic failure is wrong; the liver is characteristically spared in Tay-Sachs (key contrast with other storage diseases). An effective BBB-penetrating ERT does not currently exist for Tay-Sachs; care is supportive. Honest anticipatory counseling allows the family to plan for seizure management, feeding support, and palliative care.

stages.label: FAMILY COUNSELING DATA
stages.content:
Both parents confirmed as HEXA carriers on targeted testing
Recurrence risk for future pregnancies: 25%
Carrier frequency notably higher in Ashkenazi Jewish, certain French-Canadian, Cajun, and Old Order Amish populations
Preconception/prenatal carrier screening and options (prenatal diagnosis, PGT) available

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond supportive care for this child, what is the most appropriate next step for the family?
options: Begin both parents on a special low-fat diet to reduce carrier risk
Refer for genetic counseling with discussion of 25% recurrence risk and reproductive options including carrier screening and prenatal diagnosis
Reassure them that future children are not at increased risk
Start the infant on miglustat to cure the disease
stages.explanation: Tay-Sachs is autosomal recessive; both parents are obligate carriers, giving a 25% recurrence risk in each pregnancy. The appropriate step is genetic counseling covering recurrence risk and reproductive options (carrier screening of relatives, prenatal diagnosis, preimplantation genetic testing, donor gametes). A parental diet cannot change germline carrier status and is nonsensical. Reassurance of no increased risk is factually wrong and harmful. Miglustat is a substrate-reduction agent studied in some gangliosidoses but is not a cure and does not alter infantile Tay-Sachs outcome; offering it as curative misleads the family. Counseling and supportive/palliative care define management.

cases.diagnosis: Infantile Tay-Sachs disease (GM2 gangliosidosis, hexosaminidase A deficiency, HEXA mutations)
pearls: Cherry-red macula + hyperacusis/exaggerated startle + regression WITHOUT hepatosplenomegaly = Tay-Sachs (organomegaly suggests Sandhoff, Niemann-Pick, or GM1 instead).
Diagnose with deficient hexosaminidase A; total hexosaminidase is normal/elevated because Hex B is preserved.
Infantile Tay-Sachs is fatal by early childhood with progressive macrocephaly, seizures, and blindness; treatment is supportive.
It is autosomal recessive with higher carrier rates in Ashkenazi Jewish and certain founder populations; offer genetic counseling and carrier screening.

cases.title: A Teen with a New Tremor and Slurred Speech
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of worsening hand tremor, slurred speech, and clumsiness, with falling grades.
cases.history: A 13-year-old boy presents with 3 months of progressive tremor of both hands, slurred and slower speech, drooling, and clumsy handwriting. Teachers note his grades have dropped and he seems irritable and tearful, with one episode of inappropriate laughter. His parents report he occasionally adopts odd, sustained hand and neck postures. No headaches, seizures, fevers, or recent illness. He had transient jaundice attributed to a viral illness 1 year ago that resolved. Medications: none, no recreational drugs, no neuroleptics. No allergies. He is a 7th grader; no developmental concerns previously. Family history: a paternal aunt had 'liver problems' and a movement disorder in young adulthood; parents are non-consanguineous. Review of systems notable for easy bruising and mild fatigue. No prior surgeries. Diet normal. On further questioning, his mother recalls he has seemed 'emotionally off' for several months, predating the motor symptoms.
cases.exam: Alert teen with hypomimic face and dysarthric, slightly slurred speech with drooling. A coarse postural and kinetic tremor of both hands, described as wing-beating with arms outstretched and elbows flexed. Mild dystonic posturing of the neck and right hand. Cogwheel rigidity at the wrists; bradykinesia on rapid alternating movements. Gait mildly unsteady. Reflexes normal, plantars downgoing. Slit-lamp examination reveals golden-brown rings at the corneal limbus bilaterally. Mild hepatomegaly; no splenomegaly. A few scattered bruises. No Kayser-Fleischer-related visual loss.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An adolescent with a new movement disorder (tremor, dystonia, parkinsonism) PLUS neuropsychiatric change and a history of transient jaundice should first raise suspicion for what category of disease?
options: Primary psychiatric illness with functional movements
A treatable inherited metabolic disorder affecting the basal ganglia and liver
Juvenile Parkinson disease
Multiple sclerosis
stages.explanation: New extrapyramidal signs combined with neuropsychiatric symptoms and a hepatic history in a teenager should immediately suggest a treatable inherited metabolic disorder involving both the brain (basal ganglia) and liver, most notably Wilson disease, which is crucial to catch because it is fatal if untreated but treatable. A primary psychiatric diagnosis cannot explain dystonia, rigidity, KF rings, or hepatomegaly. Juvenile Parkinson disease is rare and would not explain the liver disease or behavioral change. MS causes CNS demyelination but not parkinsonism with a hepatic history and is uncommon at this age; it does not cause KF rings. The brain-plus-liver combination is the key cognitive trigger.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of screening tests is most appropriate to pursue the suspected diagnosis?
options: Serum ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urinary copper, and slit-lamp exam
Serum iron studies and ferritin
Plasma amino acids and ammonia only
Serum copper level alone
stages.explanation: The best screen for Wilson disease is low serum ceruloplasmin, elevated 24-hour urinary copper excretion, and a slit-lamp exam for Kayser-Fleischer rings (already present here). These three together have strong diagnostic value. Iron studies evaluate hemochromatosis or neurodegeneration with brain iron, not copper overload. Plasma amino acids and ammonia screen for urea cycle/aminoacidopathies, which do not cause KF rings or this movement profile. Total serum copper alone is unreliable: it is bound to ceruloplasmin and may be normal or low even though free (non-ceruloplasmin) copper is high. Combining ceruloplasmin, urinary copper, and ophthalmology is the standard initial approach.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum ceruloplasmin: low (18 mg/dL; reference ~20-35)
24-hour urinary copper: markedly elevated
Serum non-ceruloplasmin (free) copper: elevated
AST 96 U/L, ALT 110 U/L; total bilirubin mildly elevated; INR 1.3
Slit-lamp: Kayser-Fleischer rings confirmed bilaterally
Brain MRI: T2 hyperintensity of the putamen and midbrain with a 'face of the giant panda' sign

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Ceruloplasmin is borderline-low and urinary copper is elevated. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm and stage the diagnosis?
options: Start penicillamine empirically without further testing
Liver biopsy for quantitative hepatic copper and/or ATP7B genetic testing
Repeat ceruloplasmin in 6 months
Brain biopsy of the basal ganglia
stages.explanation: When the diagnosis is strongly suspected but not yet definitive (KF rings present, low-normal ceruloplasmin, high urinary copper), confirmation is best achieved with quantitative hepatic copper on liver biopsy and/or ATP7B molecular testing; the liver biopsy also stages hepatic injury. Starting penicillamine empirically commits the patient to a chelator with significant side effects without confirmation and can transiently worsen neurologic symptoms; confirmation should precede or accompany treatment when feasible. Waiting 6 months is unsafe in a progressive treatable disease. Brain biopsy is invasive, unnecessary, and not used to diagnose Wilson disease. Confirmation guides therapy and family screening.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY TESTING
stages.content:
Liver biopsy: hepatic copper concentration markedly elevated; steatosis and early fibrosis
ATP7B gene: two pathogenic variants confirmed (compound heterozygous)
CBC: mild thrombocytopenia (hypersplenism); Coombs-negative
Neurologic exam stable, no encephalopathy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has neurologic Wilson disease with compensated liver involvement. What is the most appropriate initial treatment strategy?
options: High-dose oral copper supplementation
Copper chelation (e.g., trientine or penicillamine with pyridoxine) and/or zinc, plus a low-copper diet and lifelong adherence, with monitoring for paradoxical neurologic worsening
Urgent liver transplantation as first-line therapy
Deep brain stimulation of the globus pallidus
stages.explanation: Wilson disease is treated by removing excess copper and preventing reaccumulation: chelators (trientine, often preferred for neurologic disease; or penicillamine, given with pyridoxine) and/or zinc (which blocks intestinal copper absorption), combined with a low-copper diet (avoid liver, shellfish, nuts, chocolate) and strict lifelong adherence. Patients are monitored for paradoxical early neurologic worsening with chelation. Copper supplementation is exactly backwards and dangerous. Liver transplant is reserved for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis, not compensated disease responsive to medical therapy. DBS is not standard treatment for Wilson disease. First-degree relatives should also be screened.

cases.diagnosis: Wilson disease (ATP7B mutation) with neurologic and hepatic involvement
pearls: Any adolescent with a new movement disorder plus neuropsychiatric change and/or liver disease must be screened for Wilson disease, a treatable cause that is fatal if missed.
Screen with low ceruloplasmin, elevated 24-hour urinary copper, and slit-lamp for Kayser-Fleischer rings; confirm with hepatic copper quantification and/or ATP7B testing.
Total serum copper can be misleadingly normal; free (non-ceruloplasmin) copper and urinary copper are more informative.
Treat with chelation (trientine/penicillamine) and/or zinc plus a low-copper diet for life; watch for paradoxical neurologic worsening and screen first-degree relatives.

cases.title: A Preschooler with Seizures, Jerky Movements, and Delay
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodes of staring and jerking that began in infancy, now with an unsteady gait and slowed development, worse before meals.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is referred for recurrent seizures and developmental delay refractory to two antiseizure medications. Seizures began around 4 months of age as brief staring spells and eye deviations; they have proven hard to control. Parents notice that her events and her general functioning are worse in the morning before breakfast and after fasting, and improve after she eats. They also describe intermittent abnormal movements, including a wobbly gait with episodes of jerkiness and dystonic posturing of the limbs that come and go, sometimes triggered by exertion or hunger. Her speech and motor milestones are delayed; head growth has slowed with a relatively small head. No regression of skills, no organomegaly. Birth and pregnancy normal. Medications: levetiracetam and oxcarbazepine, with limited benefit. No allergies. She is an only child of non-consanguineous parents; a parent reports occasional 'paroxysmal' movement spells in childhood that were never diagnosed. Diet is normal; symptoms are notably state-dependent and meal-related.
cases.exam: Interactive but globally delayed girl with mild acquired microcephaly (head circumference now below prior percentiles). Cranial nerves intact; no cherry-red spot. Tone mildly low axially with intermittent dystonic posturing of the right arm; transient choreiform movements observed. Gait is ataxic and wide-based. Reflexes normal, plantars flexor. No hepatosplenomegaly; no coarse features; skin normal. During the visit she becomes more unsteady and irritable as lunchtime approaches and improves after a snack, a striking state-dependent fluctuation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation is the strongest clue to the underlying mechanism?
options: Onset of seizures in infancy
Symptoms (seizures, movement disorder, alertness) that worsen with fasting and improve with eating
Delayed milestones
Intermittent dystonia and chorea
stages.explanation: The hallmark clue is the clear fasting-dependence: seizures, paroxysmal movements, and cognition worsen before meals/after fasting and improve with food, implicating an energy-supply problem to the brain, specifically impaired glucose transport across the blood-brain barrier (GLUT1 deficiency). Infantile seizure onset, developmental delay, and movement abnormalities are all present but are nonspecific and seen in countless epileptic encephalopathies; they do not point to a mechanism. The state-dependent, meal-related fluctuation is the discriminating feature that should trigger CSF glucose testing and the consideration of a glucose-transporter defect.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the single most important diagnostic test to order, and how must it be performed?
options: Random serum glucose only
Lumbar puncture for CSF glucose with a SIMULTANEOUS (paired) blood glucose after a 4-6 hour fast, calculating the CSF:blood glucose ratio
EEG alone
Brain MRI with spectroscopy
stages.explanation: GLUT1 deficiency is identified by a low CSF glucose (hypoglycorrhachia) with a low CSF:blood glucose ratio in the setting of NORMAL blood glucose; the LP must be done with a simultaneous (paired) blood glucose, ideally after a 4-6 hour fast, with normal CSF cell count and lactate. A random serum glucose is normal in GLUT1 deficiency and misses the diagnosis entirely. EEG may show generalized epileptiform activity but is nonspecific and does not confirm the defect. MRI/spectroscopy is typically normal or nonspecific in GLUT1 deficiency. The paired CSF/blood glucose with ratio calculation is the diagnostic cornerstone, followed by SLC2A1 testing.

stages.label: CSF & GENETICS
stages.content:
CSF glucose: 35 mg/dL (low)
Simultaneous blood glucose: 90 mg/dL (normal)
CSF:blood glucose ratio: 0.39 (low; normal >0.6)
CSF lactate: low-normal
CSF cell count and protein: normal
SLC2A1 gene: heterozygous pathogenic variant identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the low CSF:blood glucose ratio and SLC2A1 mutation, what is the first-line treatment?
options: High-dose levetiracetam monotherapy
A ketogenic diet to provide ketones as an alternative brain fuel
Continuous intravenous dextrose infusion
Acetazolamide
stages.explanation: GLUT1 deficiency impairs glucose transport into the brain, so the treatment is to supply an alternative fuel: a ketogenic diet provides ketone bodies that cross the blood-brain barrier via monocarboxylate transporters and bypass the defective glucose transporter, improving seizures, movement symptoms, and alertness. It is the established first-line therapy and should be started early. Levetiracetam and other standard antiseizure drugs are often ineffective because they do not address the energy deficit. IV dextrose raises blood glucose but cannot overcome the transporter defect and is not a chronic solution. Acetazolamide is not first-line. Certain drugs (e.g., barbiturates, caffeine) that inhibit GLUT1 should be avoided.

stages.label: TREATMENT RESPONSE
stages.content:
Ketogenic diet initiated with ketosis achieved (serum beta-hydroxybutyrate elevated)
Seizure frequency markedly reduced within weeks
Paroxysmal exertion-induced dyskinesia improved
Alertness and gait improved; fewer pre-meal exacerbations
Growth, lipids, and renal stones monitored; carnitine and micronutrients supplemented

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While on the ketogenic diet, which monitoring and counseling point is most important for this patient and family?
options: Encourage frequent high-carbohydrate snacks to maintain energy
Monitor for nephrolithiasis, dyslipidemia, and growth; maintain ketosis and avoid carbohydrate loads and GLUT1-inhibiting substances
Discontinue the diet once seizures stop, as the defect is self-limited
Add valproate to potentiate the diet
stages.explanation: Children on a ketogenic diet need monitoring for kidney stones, dyslipidemia, acidosis, growth/nutrition, and micronutrient/carnitine status, while maintaining consistent ketosis; carbohydrate loads break ketosis and worsen symptoms, and GLUT1-inhibiting substances (e.g., caffeine, some barbiturates, alcohol) should be avoided. High-carb snacks would defeat the therapy by abolishing ketosis. Stopping the diet when seizures resolve is wrong; GLUT1 deficiency is a lifelong genetic disorder and the diet is typically continued at least through brain development and often longer. Valproate is generally not used to potentiate the diet and can complicate metabolic monitoring. Genetic counseling for autosomal-dominant inheritance is also appropriate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The parent reports their own undiagnosed childhood paroxysmal movement spells. What does this most likely indicate about inheritance and family management?
options: It is coincidental; GLUT1 deficiency is always a de novo mutation
GLUT1 deficiency is most often autosomal dominant with variable expressivity, so the parent may be mildly affected; offer family testing and genetic counseling with ~50% transmission risk
It confirms autosomal recessive inheritance with 25% recurrence risk
It indicates the child was misdiagnosed
stages.explanation: GLUT1 deficiency syndrome is most commonly inherited in an autosomal dominant manner (often de novo, but also transmitted) with variable expressivity; the parent's history of paroxysmal exertional movement spells suggests a mild phenotype and possible carriage of the same SLC2A1 variant. Appropriate management is genetic counseling with cascade testing of the parent and at-risk relatives and discussion of roughly a 50% transmission risk per pregnancy for an affected individual. Saying it is always de novo is incorrect. Calling it autosomal recessive with 25% risk is the wrong inheritance pattern. The parent's symptoms support, rather than refute, the child's diagnosis, so that option is wrong.

cases.diagnosis: GLUT1 deficiency syndrome (SLC2A1 mutation)
pearls: Suspect GLUT1 deficiency when epilepsy, a paroxysmal movement disorder, and developmental delay fluctuate with fasting and improve after meals.
Diagnose with a low CSF glucose and low CSF:blood glucose ratio (paired sample, normal blood glucose); confirm with SLC2A1 testing.
The ketogenic diet is first-line: ketones bypass the defective glucose transporter to fuel the brain; standard antiseizure drugs often fail.
On the ketogenic diet, monitor for nephrolithiasis, dyslipidemia, acidosis, and growth, and avoid carbohydrate loads and GLUT1-inhibiting substances like caffeine.

cases.title: A Newborn Whose Seizures Won't Stop
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent seizures beginning on the first day of life that have not responded to standard antiseizure medications.
cases.history: A 3-day-old term boy is in the NICU for refractory neonatal seizures. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery with Apgars of 8 and 9 and no perinatal distress. On the first day of life he developed multifocal clonic jerks, lip-smacking, and tonic posturing with apneas. Seizures recurred despite loading doses of phenobarbital and then a benzodiazepine, and a subsequent fosphenytoin load. He has had episodes of irritability and abnormal eye movements between seizures. No fever; sepsis workup including blood and CSF cultures is pending/negative so far; cranial ultrasound shows no major hemorrhage. Mother reports that in a prior pregnancy she felt unusual rhythmic intrauterine movements late in gestation. Family history: parents are consanguineous (first cousins); a sibling died in the neonatal period of intractable seizures of unknown cause. No maternal substance use. The infant is on continuous EEG monitoring with frequent electrographic seizures, and the team has exhausted first- and second-line agents.
cases.exam: Intubated, sedated neonate with intermittent multifocal clonic seizures and tonic posturing despite therapy. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Pupils equal and reactive; intermittent roving and abnormal conjugate eye movements during events. Tone fluctuates between hypotonia and episodic stiffening; reflexes variable. No dysmorphic features, no hepatosplenomegaly, no rash, no cardiac murmur. Glucose at bedside normal; no jitteriness suggestive of hypoglycemia or hypocalcemia. Continuous EEG demonstrates a burst-suppression pattern with recurrent electrographic seizures unresponsive to standard agents.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Standard antiseizure loads have failed and common metabolic culprits are excluded. In refractory neonatal seizures, what crucial therapeutic trial must be considered?
options: Add a third conventional antiseizure drug and observe for 48 hours
Administer a trial of IV pyridoxine (vitamin B6) with EEG and cardiorespiratory monitoring
Begin therapeutic hypothermia
Start broad-spectrum antivirals empirically
stages.explanation: Neonatal seizures refractory to standard agents, especially with normal glucose/calcium and a negative initial sepsis workup, mandate a trial of IV pyridoxine (with EEG and cardiorespiratory monitoring, because apnea/hypotension can occur) to test for pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy, a treatable inborn error. Simply adding another conventional drug wastes time in a vitamin-responsive disorder and risks ongoing brain injury. Therapeutic hypothermia is for hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, and this infant had no perinatal asphyxia. Empiric antivirals target HSV, reasonable to consider, but they do not treat a metabolic vitamin-dependent epilepsy; the pyridoxine trial is the pivotal, potentially diagnostic-and-therapeutic maneuver here.

stages.label: PYRIDOXINE TRIAL
stages.content:
IV pyridoxine administered during continuous EEG with monitoring
Electrographic seizures cease within minutes
EEG background improves from burst-suppression toward continuity
Transient hypotonia and brief respiratory depression noted, managed supportively
No recurrence of seizures over the next several hours on maintenance pyridoxine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The seizures stopped with IV pyridoxine. Which biomarker and gene best confirm the classic (ALDH7A1-related) form of this disorder?
options: Elevated urine orotic acid; OTC gene
Elevated alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA) and pipecolic acid; ALDH7A1 (antiquitin) gene
Elevated very-long-chain fatty acids; ABCD1 gene
Low CSF glucose; SLC2A1 gene
stages.explanation: Classic pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy results from antiquitin (ALDH7A1) deficiency; the biochemical hallmark is accumulation of alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA) and pipecolic acid (in urine, plasma, and/or CSF), and ALDH7A1 sequencing confirms it. Urine orotic acid with OTC indicates a urea cycle defect (hyperammonemia), not a B6-dependent epilepsy. Elevated VLCFA/ABCD1 indicate adrenoleukodystrophy, a childhood white-matter disease, not neonatal vitamin-responsive seizures. Low CSF glucose/SLC2A1 is GLUT1 deficiency, treated with a ketogenic diet, not pyridoxine. Measuring AASA/pipecolic acid plus ALDH7A1 testing distinguishes the classic antiquitin form from pyridoxal-5'-phosphate-responsive and folinic-acid-responsive seizures.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY BIOCHEMISTRY
stages.content:
Urine alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA): markedly elevated
Plasma and CSF pipecolic acid: elevated
ALDH7A1 (antiquitin) gene: homozygous pathogenic variant (consanguineous parents)
Brain MRI: structurally near-normal with nonspecific findings
Glucose, calcium, magnesium, ammonia: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate ongoing management for this infant?
options: Stop pyridoxine once seizures resolve, as it was only needed acutely
Lifelong daily oral pyridoxine supplementation, with consideration of a lysine-restricted diet and arginine supplementation as adjuncts
Switch to long-term phenobarbital monotherapy and discontinue pyridoxine
Begin a ketogenic diet as primary therapy
stages.explanation: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy requires lifelong daily pyridoxine; stopping it leads to seizure recurrence, so that option is dangerous. Adjunctive strategies aimed at reducing toxic lysine-pathway metabolites, namely a lysine-restricted diet and arginine supplementation (triple/adjunct therapy), can improve neurodevelopmental outcomes and are considered alongside pyridoxine. Phenobarbital monotherapy does not control these vitamin-dependent seizures and abandoning pyridoxine would be harmful. A ketogenic diet is the treatment for GLUT1 deficiency, not this disorder. Even with good seizure control, developmental delay can occur, so early intervention and developmental monitoring are important, and genetic counseling for autosomal-recessive recurrence risk (25%) is indicated, especially given consanguinity.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Even after seizures are well controlled on pyridoxine, what outcome should the family be counseled to anticipate and monitor for?
options: Complete certainty of normal neurodevelopment because the seizures are controlled
A significant proportion of children have intellectual disability or developmental delay despite good seizure control, so early developmental surveillance and intervention are warranted
Progressive hepatic failure from chronic pyridoxine therapy
Inevitable blindness from optic atrophy
stages.explanation: Although pyridoxine controls the seizures, many children with pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy still have varying degrees of developmental delay or intellectual disability, so families should be counseled to expect this possibility and the child should receive ongoing developmental surveillance with early intervention (PT/OT/speech); adjunct lysine-reduction strategies aim to improve these outcomes. Promising certain normal development is false. Pyridoxine at therapeutic doses does not cause hepatic failure; very high chronic doses can cause a sensory neuropathy, which is monitored, but not liver failure. Optic atrophy with inevitable blindness is not a defining feature of this disorder. Honest developmental counseling guides realistic expectations and proactive support.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before discharge, what counseling is most appropriate for this consanguineous family with a prior neonatal death from intractable seizures?
options: Reassure them that recurrence risk in future pregnancies is negligible
Provide genetic counseling explaining autosomal-recessive inheritance with 25% recurrence risk and discuss options including prenatal diagnosis and antenatal maternal pyridoxine in future pregnancies
Advise that the condition is not heritable since it responded to a vitamin
Recommend no further testing for relatives
stages.explanation: Classic pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy is autosomal recessive; with both parents carriers (supported by consanguinity and a prior affected neonate), the recurrence risk is 25% per pregnancy, so families should receive genetic counseling. Options such as prenatal diagnosis (ALDH7A1 testing/biomarkers) and, in some cases, antenatal maternal pyridoxine supplementation in future at-risk pregnancies can be discussed. Telling them recurrence risk is negligible or that the disorder is non-heritable because it is vitamin-responsive is factually wrong and unsafe. Declining relative testing ignores carrier identification. Accurate counseling addresses the prior sibling death and informs reproductive planning.

cases.diagnosis: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (antiquitin/ALDH7A1 deficiency)
pearls: Neonatal seizures refractory to standard antiseizure drugs (with normal glucose/calcium) warrant an IV pyridoxine trial under EEG and cardiorespiratory monitoring, since apnea/hypotension can occur.
Classic disease is due to antiquitin (ALDH7A1) deficiency; confirm with elevated urine/plasma/CSF alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde and pipecolic acid plus ALDH7A1 testing.
Treatment is lifelong daily pyridoxine; lysine restriction and arginine supplementation are adjuncts that may improve developmental outcome.
It is autosomal recessive (25% recurrence); offer genetic counseling and discuss prenatal diagnosis and antenatal pyridoxine for future at-risk pregnancies.

cases.title: An Infant Whose Head Is Growing Too Fast
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly enlarging head, poor feeding, and abnormal downward eye position noted by parents over 2 weeks.
cases.history: A 1-month-old boy is admitted for evaluation of an abnormally fast-growing head. His parents noticed his head looked large and that he keeps his eyes pointed downward, with the whites visible above the colored part. Over 2 weeks he has fed poorly, vomited intermittently, and become more irritable then increasingly sleepy. He was born at term; pregnancy was complicated by limited prenatal care, and a third-trimester ultrasound reportedly showed enlarged ventricles. Birth was vaginal without instrumentation. No fevers; no known trauma; immunizations current for age. Medications: none. No allergies. He is the first child of non-consanguineous parents; no family history of neural tube defects or hydrocephalus. Feeding has declined and the parents report he arches and cries with handling. Serial head circumference plots from the pediatrician show measurements crossing upward across multiple percentile lines over the past 3 weeks, prompting referral.
cases.exam: Irritable infant with a large head. Head circumference is well above the 97th percentile and has crossed percentile lines. Anterior fontanelle is full, tense, and bulging; scalp veins are distended; cranial sutures feel splayed. Eyes show a 'sunsetting' sign with forced downgaze and visible sclera above the iris; poor upgaze. The cry is high-pitched. Tone is mildly increased in the legs; Moro present. No dysmorphism, no spinal defect on back exam, no hepatosplenomegaly. Transillumination of the skull is increased. He is consolable only briefly and tires with feeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation in this 1-month-old most strongly indicates raised intracranial pressure from hydrocephalus?
options: Poor feeding and intermittent vomiting alone
Macrocephaly crossing percentiles with a bulging fontanelle, split sutures, distended scalp veins, and the sunsetting sign
Irritability and high-pitched cry alone
Increased leg tone
stages.explanation: In an infant, the specific signs of elevated intracranial pressure from hydrocephalus are an accelerating head circumference that crosses percentiles, a bulging/tense anterior fontanelle, splayed sutures, dilated scalp veins, and the sunsetting eyes (impaired upgaze from dorsal midbrain/tectal pressure). Together these are diagnostic of hydrocephalus with raised ICP. Poor feeding/vomiting, irritability/high-pitched cry, and increased leg tone are each consistent with raised ICP but are nonspecific and occur in many illnesses; only the combined macrocephaly-plus-fontanelle-plus-sunsetting picture localizes the problem to expanding ventricles. Because infant sutures are open, the head enlarges before classic Cushing changes appear.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study in this infant with an open fontanelle?
options: Cranial ultrasound through the anterior fontanelle
Lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
Skull radiographs
Catheter cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: With an open anterior fontanelle, cranial ultrasound is the ideal first imaging study: it is fast, bedside, radiation-free, requires no sedation, and reliably demonstrates ventricular size and intraventricular hemorrhage. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated as an initial step when obstructive hydrocephalus and raised ICP are suspected, due to risk of herniation, and it is not how hydrocephalus is diagnosed here. Skull radiographs may show split sutures but cannot assess the ventricles or cause. Cerebral angiography is invasive and not a first-line test. Ultrasound guides whether and what further imaging (e.g., MRI) is needed to define the etiology and level of obstruction.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Cranial ultrasound: marked dilation of the lateral and third ventricles
Fourth ventricle relatively normal in size (suggesting aqueductal-level obstruction)
No large intraventricular hemorrhage; no cystic posterior fossa malformation seen
Follow-up brain MRI: triventricular hydrocephalus with aqueductal stenosis; thinned cortical mantle; no tumor

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI shows triventricular hydrocephalus from aqueductal stenosis. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Serial lumbar punctures to remove CSF
Acetazolamide and furosemide as long-term definitive therapy
CSF diversion: ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement (or endoscopic third ventriculostomy in selected cases)
Observation with repeat ultrasound in 3 months
stages.explanation: Progressive obstructive hydrocephalus with raised ICP requires CSF diversion. A ventriculoperitoneal shunt is the standard definitive treatment; in selected patients with obstructive (e.g., aqueductal) hydrocephalus, endoscopic third ventriculostomy (sometimes with choroid plexus cauterization) is an alternative that avoids hardware. Serial LPs do not relieve obstructive hydrocephalus (the obstruction is above the spinal space) and risk herniation. Acetazolamide/furosemide only transiently reduce CSF production and are not definitive, with notable side effects; they do not replace surgery. Observation is unsafe given progressive ventriculomegaly, a thinning cortical mantle, and symptomatic raised ICP. Definitive surgical diversion protects the developing brain.

stages.label: POST-OP COURSE
stages.content:
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed without complication
Fontanelle softens; sunsetting sign resolves; feeding improves
Head circumference growth velocity normalizes on serial plots
Post-op imaging: decreased ventricular size with catheter in good position
Parents educated on shunt malfunction and infection warning signs before discharge

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before discharge, which counseling point about the shunt is most important for the parents?
options: The shunt is permanent and will never need revision
Recognize signs of shunt malfunction or infection (vomiting, lethargy, bulging fontanelle, fever, irritability) and seek urgent care, as revisions are common over a lifetime
Antibiotic prophylaxis is needed daily for life
The child should avoid all physical activity permanently
stages.explanation: Shunts frequently malfunction or become infected over a patient's lifetime, so parents must learn the warning signs (in an infant: vomiting, increasing head size or bulging fontanelle, lethargy/irritability, poor feeding, sunsetting eyes, and fever suggesting infection) and seek urgent evaluation, because untreated shunt failure can be life-threatening. Telling them it is permanent and never needs revision is false; revisions are common. Daily lifelong antibiotics are not standard. Permanent avoidance of all activity is unnecessary and harmful to development. Empowering parents to detect failure early is the single most important discharge teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital obstructive hydrocephalus due to aqueductal stenosis
pearls: In infants, hydrocephalus presents as accelerating head circumference crossing percentiles, a bulging fontanelle, split sutures, scalp vein distension, and sunsetting eyes (impaired upgaze).
With an open fontanelle, cranial ultrasound is the first-line imaging study; avoid LP when obstructive hydrocephalus with raised ICP is suspected.
Aqueductal stenosis causes triventricular hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and third ventricles with a normal fourth ventricle).
Definitive treatment is CSF diversion (VP shunt, or ETV in selected obstructive cases); counsel families on shunt malfunction/infection signs because revisions are common.

cases.title: A Shunted Child with Headache and Vomiting
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening headache, repeated vomiting, and increasing sleepiness over the past day.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed in infancy for congenital hydrocephalus is brought to the ED by his parents. For 1 day he has had a progressively worsening frontal headache, several episodes of nonbilious vomiting, and increasing drowsiness so that he is now difficult to keep awake. He has had no fever, no diarrhea, no sick contacts, and no recent trauma. His last shunt revision was 3 years ago. He has been otherwise developmentally appropriate and attends first grade. Parents note he has 'looked like this once before' when his shunt was blocked. Medications: none regularly. No allergies. Immunizations up to date. He has eaten little today and vomited his breakfast. On the way to the hospital he became more lethargic and complained that the light hurt his eyes. There is no neck stiffness reported and no rash. Parents brought his shunt card listing the valve type and prior surgeries.
cases.exam: Sleepy boy who arouses to voice but quickly drifts off. Pupils equal and reactive but with limited upgaze (impaired vertical gaze). Mild papilledema on fundoscopy. No focal limb weakness; reflexes symmetric. Neck supple, no meningismus, no rash. The scalp shows a palpable shunt reservoir and tubing tracking down the neck; the reservoir does not refill briskly after compression. Abdomen soft, nontender, no peritoneal signs. The relative bradycardia and hypertension with declining alertness are concerning for raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a child with a VP shunt presenting with headache, vomiting, and lethargy, what should be the leading working diagnosis until proven otherwise?
options: Viral gastroenteritis
Migraine headache
Shunt malfunction (obstruction) with raised intracranial pressure
Bacterial meningitis
stages.explanation: Any shunted child with the classic triad of headache, vomiting, and lethargy must be assumed to have shunt malfunction with raised intracranial pressure until proven otherwise; this is a neurosurgical emergency. The bradycardia with hypertension, impaired upgaze, papilledema, and a poorly refilling reservoir all support raised ICP. Viral gastroenteritis would not explain papilledema, impaired upgaze, or bradycardia, and anchoring on it risks a fatal miss. Migraine does not cause papilledema or progressive obtundation. Bacterial meningitis is a consideration (shunts can become infected), but there is no fever or meningismus, and obstruction is the most immediate threat; both can be evaluated, but malfunction leads the differential.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate workup?
options: Noncontrast head CT (or rapid MRI) and a shunt series (radiographs of the shunt tract)
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Abdominal ultrasound only
Reassurance and discharge with antiemetics
stages.explanation: Suspected shunt malfunction is evaluated with neuroimaging to assess ventricular size, a noncontrast head CT or rapid (limited) MRI compared with the child's baseline, plus a shunt series (plain radiographs of the entire shunt from valve to distal catheter) to look for disconnection, fracture, kinking, or migration. Lumbar puncture first is dangerous with possible obstruction and raised ICP and could precipitate herniation. Abdominal ultrasound alone might detect a distal pseudocyst but does not evaluate the ventricles or proximal hardware and is insufficient. Discharge with antiemetics is unsafe and could be fatal. Imaging plus a shunt series rapidly localizes the problem.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast head CT: ventricles enlarged compared with the patient's prior baseline study
Shunt series: discontinuity of the catheter in the neck region (disconnected/fractured tubing)
No intracranial hemorrhage; effaced sulci consistent with raised ICP
Proximal catheter tip in ventricle; distal catheter retracted

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms increased ventricular size with a fractured/disconnected shunt and the child is becoming more obtunded. What is the definitive management?
options: Admit for observation and serial neuro checks only
Start IV mannitol and discharge once headache improves
Emergent neurosurgical intervention to revise the shunt (and temporize raised ICP, e.g., tapping the shunt/external drainage as needed)
Begin oral acetazolamide and outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: A confirmed shunt malfunction with enlarging ventricles and a deteriorating level of consciousness requires emergent neurosurgical shunt revision; temporizing measures (e.g., tapping the shunt reservoir to drain CSF or placing an external ventricular drain, head-of-bed elevation, and treating raised ICP) bridge to surgery. Observation alone is unsafe in a child who is declining. Mannitol then discharge does not fix the mechanical failure and would lead to deterioration. Outpatient acetazolamide cannot relieve mechanical obstruction and dangerously delays care. The fix is mechanical: restore CSF flow surgically. Untreated, this progresses to herniation and death.

stages.label: OPERATIVE COURSE
stages.content:
Taken emergently to the OR; fractured distal catheter replaced and continuity restored
Intraoperative findings confirm catheter disconnection; CSF flow re-established
Post-op: alertness returns to baseline; headache and vomiting resolve
Follow-up CT: ventricles decreased toward baseline
Discharged with reinforced shunt-failure precautions

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful revision, what is the most important counseling for the family?
options: Shunt failure will not happen again after this revision
Recurrence of headache, vomiting, lethargy, vision changes, or fever should prompt immediate return, since shunt malfunction and infection can recur and are emergencies
Future episodes can be safely managed at home with antiemetics
The child should avoid school indefinitely
stages.explanation: Shunts can fail or become infected again at any time, so families must be taught to return immediately for the warning signs of malfunction (recurrent headache, vomiting, lethargy, irritability, vision changes, or in younger children a bulging fontanelle/increasing head size) and of infection (fever, redness/swelling along the tract). Promising it will never recur is false. Managing future episodes at home with antiemetics is dangerous and could mask a lethal obstruction. Indefinite school avoidance is unnecessary and harmful. The key message is rapid re-presentation, because timely recognition of recurrent shunt failure is lifesaving.

cases.diagnosis: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt malfunction (catheter fracture/disconnection) with raised intracranial pressure
pearls: In any shunted child, headache + vomiting + lethargy = shunt malfunction with raised ICP until proven otherwise; it is a neurosurgical emergency.
Work up with neuroimaging compared to baseline plus a shunt series (full-tract radiographs) for disconnection, fracture, kink, or migration; avoid LP when obstruction is suspected.
Bradycardia with hypertension, impaired upgaze, and papilledema signal raised ICP and impending decompensation.
Definitive treatment is emergent surgical shunt revision; counsel families that shunt failure and infection can recur and require immediate return.

cases.title: A Newborn with an Open Defect on Her Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Open lumbar spinal defect noted at delivery with exposed neural tissue.
cases.history: A newborn girl is in the operating room for management of an open spinal defect identified immediately at birth. She was delivered at 38 weeks; prenatal care was limited and the mother did not take folic acid before or during early pregnancy. A mid-gestation ultrasound had suggested a spinal abnormality and ventriculomegaly, but the family did not pursue further fetal evaluation. At delivery there is a midline lumbar sac with exposed, flat neural tissue (placode) leaking clear fluid, without skin covering. She moves her hips but has reduced movement at the ankles and feet, and there is a constant dribble of urine. Apgars were 7 and 9. No other gross anomalies. Family history: a maternal cousin had spina bifida. No maternal diabetes documented; no known teratogen exposure besides lack of folate. The defect has been kept moist and covered with sterile saline dressings, the infant nursed prone, and broad-spectrum antibiotics started to reduce infection risk pending closure.
cases.exam: Term newborn nursed prone. A midline lumbosacral sac with a flat, reddish neural placode and CSF leakage, no overlying skin. Lower-extremity exam: antigravity hip flexion present but absent ankle dorsiflexion/plantarflexion and reduced movement of the feet; decreased response to stimulation below the level of the lesion. Anal tone diminished; continuous urinary dribbling suggesting a neurogenic bladder. Head circumference upper-normal with a soft, slightly full fontanelle. No clubfoot deformity noted. Upper extremities normal. The exposed neural tissue is kept moist with sterile saline; latex precautions in place.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before surgical closure, what is the most important immediate management of the exposed neural placode in the delivery room/OR?
options: Apply firm dry gauze and position the infant supine
Keep the placode covered with moist sterile (saline) nonadherent dressing, nurse the infant prone, use latex precautions, and give antibiotics to prevent infection and desiccation
Vigorously clean the placode with antiseptic scrub
Delay any covering until the surgeon arrives
stages.explanation: An open myelomeningocele exposes neural tissue and CSF, so the priorities before closure are preventing desiccation and infection: cover the placode with a moist, sterile, nonadherent (saline) dressing, position the infant prone to avoid pressure on the sac, institute latex precautions (these children have high latex-allergy risk), maintain thermoregulation, and start antibiotics. Dry gauze with supine positioning would damage and compress the placode and risk drying it out. Vigorous antiseptic scrubbing injures fragile neural tissue. Leaving it uncovered until the surgeon arrives risks infection (meningitis/ventriculitis) and drying. These measures protect neurologic function pending early closure.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended timing of surgical closure for an open myelomeningocele diagnosed at birth (when fetal repair was not performed)?
options: Within roughly the first 24-72 hours of life
After 1 month to allow the infant to grow
Only if the child develops meningitis
Closure is not necessary if the sac is intact
stages.explanation: For a postnatally diagnosed open neural tube defect, early surgical closure (generally within about 24-72 hours of life) is recommended to reduce the risk of CSF infection (meningitis/ventriculitis) and to protect exposed neural tissue, while preserving residual function. Waiting a month markedly increases infection risk and is not standard. Closing only after meningitis develops is backwards, the goal is to prevent infection. Saying closure is unnecessary is incorrect for an open lesion with CSF leak; even a covered sac generally requires repair. (Note: prenatal in-utero repair is an option offered to selected pregnancies, but here the defect is being managed postnatally.)

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
Head ultrasound/MRI: ventriculomegaly with a small posterior fossa and downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils/brainstem (Chiari II malformation)
Spinal MRI: lumbosacral myelomeningocele with low-lying neural placode
Renal/bladder ultrasound: bladder distension; monitoring for neurogenic bladder
Motor level approximately mid-lumbar (preserved hip flexion, absent ankle movement)

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Imaging shows ventriculomegaly and a Chiari II malformation. Which associated complication is most strongly linked to myelomeningocele and must be anticipated after closure?
options: Congenital cataracts
Hydrocephalus (frequently requiring CSF diversion such as a VP shunt), associated with the Chiari II malformation
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
Cleft palate
stages.explanation: Open myelomeningocele is strongly associated with the Chiari II malformation (hindbrain herniation with a small posterior fossa), and the great majority of these infants develop hydrocephalus, often requiring CSF diversion (VP shunt or ETV) around or shortly after back closure. Therefore hydrocephalus must be anticipated and monitored (serial head circumference, fontanelle, ventricle size). Congenital cataracts, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and cleft palate are not characteristic associations of myelomeningocele. Recognizing the Chiari II-hydrocephalus link allows timely shunting; symptomatic Chiari II can also cause brainstem signs (stridor, apnea, swallowing difficulty) that require attention.

stages.label: POST-OP COURSE
stages.content:
Myelomeningocele closed within the first 2 days of life without wound complication
Serial head circumference rising; fontanelle increasingly full; ventricles enlarging
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed for progressive hydrocephalus
Neurogenic bladder managed with clean intermittent catheterization
Multidisciplinary follow-up (neurosurgery, urology, orthopedics, PM&R) arranged

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What counseling regarding PREVENTION is most important for this mother for future pregnancies?
options: No prevention is possible; neural tube defects are purely random

Periconceptional folic acid supplementation substantially reduces recurrence risk; given a prior affected child, a higher (high-dose) folic acid regimen is recommended before and during early pregnancy
Avoiding all dairy products prevents recurrence
Folic acid should be started only after the first trimester
stages.explanation: Neural tube defects are substantially preventable with periconceptional folic acid; because neural tube closure occurs in the first 3-4 weeks, supplementation must begin before conception and continue through early pregnancy. With a prior affected pregnancy, recurrence risk is increased and a higher-dose folic acid regimen is recommended preconceptionally. Saying prevention is impossible is wrong and removes a proven intervention. Avoiding dairy has no role. Starting folic acid after the first trimester is too late, the neural tube has already closed. Counseling on high-dose periconceptional folate, plus prenatal screening (MSAFP, ultrasound) in future pregnancies, is the key prevention message.

cases.diagnosis: Open lumbosacral myelomeningocele with Chiari II malformation and hydrocephalus
pearls: Protect an open myelomeningocele before closure: moist sterile saline dressing, prone positioning, latex precautions, thermoregulation, and antibiotics.
Close postnatally diagnosed open neural tube defects early (about 24-72 hours) to reduce CSF infection risk and protect neural tissue.
Myelomeningocele is associated with the Chiari II malformation and hydrocephalus that frequently requires CSF diversion; anticipate and monitor for it.
Periconceptional folic acid prevents neural tube defects; after a prior affected child, use high-dose folate started before conception.

cases.title: A Girl Whose Headache Spikes When She Coughs
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent severe headaches at the back of the head brought on by coughing and straining for several months.
cases.history: An 11-year-old girl presents with several months of recurrent occipital (back-of-head) headaches that are characteristically triggered by coughing, sneezing, laughing, and straining (Valsalva). The headaches are brief but intense, lasting seconds to minutes, and are sometimes accompanied by neck pain. Over the past 2 months she has also noticed intermittent numbness and tingling in her hands and reports occasionally not feeling temperature normally in her arms, once burning a fingertip on a stove without noticing immediately. No vision loss, no morning vomiting, no fever, no trauma. School performance is normal. Medications: ibuprofen as needed with little relief. No allergies. She is otherwise healthy with normal development. Family history is unremarkable for neurologic disease. On review, she denies clumsiness of gait but reports occasional dizziness with position changes. The cough-induced, Valsalva-triggered occipital headaches plus suspended sensory symptoms in the arms prompted neurologic referral and imaging.
cases.exam: Well-appearing girl in no distress. Mental status normal. Cranial nerves intact; no papilledema; normal eye movements without nystagmus at rest, though brief downbeat nystagmus is noted on downgaze. Motor strength normal. Sensory exam reveals decreased pain and temperature sensation over the shoulders and upper arms in a 'cape-like' distribution, with preserved light touch and proprioception (suspended dissociated sensory loss). Reflexes slightly reduced in the upper extremities, normal in the legs; plantars flexor. Gait normal, tandem intact. No scoliosis appreciated today. Neck supple. The dissociated sensory loss suggests a central cord syrinx.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Cough/Valsalva-triggered occipital headaches plus a cape-like loss of pain and temperature with preserved light touch most suggest what localization?
options: A peripheral polyneuropathy
A craniocervical junction abnormality with an associated central cervical cord syrinx
Migraine with aura
A primary psychiatric/somatic complaint
stages.explanation: Occipital headaches reliably provoked by cough/Valsalva point to the craniocervical junction (transient pressure dissociation across the foramen magnum), and a suspended, dissociated sensory loss (decreased pain/temperature with preserved light touch in a cape distribution) localizes to a central cervical cord lesion, classically a syrinx, because crossing spinothalamic fibers in the central cord are affected while dorsal columns are spared. This combination strongly suggests a Chiari I malformation with syringomyelia. A peripheral polyneuropathy gives length-dependent, stocking-glove loss, not a cape pattern. Migraine with aura is not Valsalva-locked and lacks dissociated sensory loss. A psychiatric/somatic label ignores objective dissociated sensory findings and downbeat nystagmus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic study?
options: MRI of the brain and entire spine
Noncontrast head CT
Nerve conduction studies and EMG
Plain cervical spine radiographs
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain (to assess the posterior fossa and cerebellar tonsillar position relative to the foramen magnum) plus the spine (to detect a syrinx and define its extent) is the diagnostic study of choice for suspected Chiari I malformation with syringomyelia. CT poorly visualizes the posterior fossa soft tissue and cord and would miss a syrinx. Nerve conduction studies/EMG evaluate peripheral nerves and would be normal in this central cord process. Plain radiographs cannot show tonsillar herniation or a syrinx. MRI both confirms the diagnosis and guides surgical decision-making by measuring the degree of tonsillar descent and syrinx size.

stages.label: MRI
stages.content:
Cerebellar tonsils herniate below the foramen magnum (peg-like, >5 mm) consistent with Chiari I malformation
Crowded foramen magnum with effacement of CSF spaces posteriorly
Cervical cord syrinx (syringomyelia) extending over several segments
No hydrocephalus; no posterior fossa mass; brainstem otherwise normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has symptomatic Chiari I malformation with a cervical syrinx (cough headaches plus dissociated sensory loss). What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance and no treatment, since Chiari I is always incidental
Posterior fossa decompression (suboccipital craniectomy with C1 laminectomy, typically with duraplasty) to restore CSF flow and treat the syrinx
Lifelong opioids for headache control
Immediate ventriculoperitoneal shunt
stages.explanation: A symptomatic Chiari I malformation, especially with neurologic deficits and a syrinx, is treated surgically with posterior fossa (suboccipital) decompression, usually a suboccipital craniectomy and C1 laminectomy, commonly with duraplasty, to relieve crowding at the foramen magnum, restore normal CSF flow, and allow the syrinx to collapse and symptoms to improve. Reassurance/no treatment is wrong because, although incidental Chiari I can be observed, this child is symptomatic with a syrinx and deficits. Chronic opioids do not address the mechanical problem and carry dependency risk. A VP shunt is not the primary treatment for Chiari I without hydrocephalus; decompression is the targeted procedure.

stages.label: POST-OP COURSE
stages.content:
Posterior fossa decompression with duraplasty performed
Cough-induced occipital headaches resolved
Follow-up MRI: improved CSF flow at the craniocervical junction with reduction in syrinx size
Upper-extremity sensory symptoms gradually improving
Routine surveillance imaging planned

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement best guides counseling about management of Chiari I malformation more broadly?
options: All patients with tonsillar herniation on MRI require surgery regardless of symptoms
Asymptomatic Chiari I found incidentally (without a syrinx or deficits) can often be observed, whereas symptomatic disease or an associated syrinx warrants surgical evaluation
Surgery guarantees complete and immediate resolution of all symptoms
Syrinxes never improve after decompression
stages.explanation: Management is symptom- and finding-driven: incidental, asymptomatic Chiari I without a syrinx or neurologic deficits is frequently managed with observation and may need no surgery, while symptomatic patients (e.g., Valsalva headaches, brainstem/lower-cranial-nerve signs) or those with a syrinx or progressive deficits warrant surgical evaluation for decompression. Saying all herniation requires surgery overtreats benign incidental findings. Promising complete, immediate resolution overstates outcomes; improvement is common but variable and may be gradual. Claiming syrinxes never improve is false, decompression often reduces syrinx size. Individualized, finding-based counseling is the correct approach.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic Chiari I malformation with syringomyelia
pearls: Cough/Valsalva-triggered occipital headaches are the classic symptom of Chiari I malformation due to transient craniocervical pressure dissociation.
A cape-like dissociated sensory loss (impaired pain/temperature, preserved light touch) signals a central cord syrinx (syringomyelia).
MRI of the brain and spine is the test of choice: it shows tonsillar herniation (>5 mm) and defines the syrinx.
Treat symptomatic Chiari I or Chiari I with a syrinx by posterior fossa decompression (often with duraplasty); incidental, asymptomatic Chiari I without a syrinx can be observed.

cases.title: Leg Weakness and New Wetting in a 5-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: New daytime urinary accidents and dragging of the right foot over the past 3 months.
cases.history: A previously toilet-trained 5-year-old girl is brought in by her mother for 3 months of new daytime urinary incontinence and intermittent right foot dragging. She had been fully dry day and night since age 3. The mother reports occasional nighttime wetting has returned and the child now trips frequently and complains her legs feel tired after walking. There is no fever, recent illness, or trauma. Bowel habits show new constipation with occasional soiling. Birth history was uncomplicated, term, vaginal delivery; developmental milestones were normal. PMH is unremarkable. No medications; no known allergies. She lives with both parents and attends kindergarten. Family history is negative for spinal or neurologic disorders. On undressing, the mother mentions a small dimple over the lower back that has been present since birth and a tuft of hair her pediatrician once noted. Growth parameters are normal. There is no recent regression in language or social skills, and she has no headache or visual complaints.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative girl. Cranial nerves intact. Over the lumbosacral midline there is a deep sacral dimple above the gluteal cleft with an overlying hair tuft and a small subcutaneous lipoma. Lower-extremity exam shows 4/5 strength in the right ankle dorsiflexors with mild right foot drop; left leg 5/5. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk at the knees but the right ankle jerk is reduced. Mild bilateral pes cavus and a high right arch. Sensation to light touch is diminished over the right lateral foot and perineum. Gait reveals a subtle right steppage pattern. Upper extremities are normal. Anal wink is sluggish. No spinal tenderness, no scoliosis, no meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature in this presentation is most concerning for an underlying structural spinal cord abnormality?
options: The constellation of cutaneous lumbosacral markers with new lower-limb and bladder/bowel dysfunction
Return of nighttime wetting alone
Mild pes cavus in isolation
Brisk knee reflexes
stages.explanation: Cutaneous lumbosacral stigmata (deep sacral dimple above the cleft, hair tuft, subcutaneous lipoma) combined with new neurologic deficits — foot drop, an asymmetric absent ankle jerk, perineal sensory loss, and bladder/bowel dysfunction — point to an occult spinal dysraphism with cord involvement, classically tethered cord syndrome. Isolated nighttime wetting is common and nonspecific in young children. Pes cavus alone is a clue to a chronic neuropathic process but is not diagnostic by itself. Brisk knee reflexes are a nonspecific finding and, in isolation, do not localize. The key is the combination of skin findings, asymmetric motor/sensory loss, and sphincter dysfunction emerging together.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to evaluate this child?
options: Plain lumbosacral radiographs
CT of the lumbar spine without contrast
MRI of the total spine with and without contrast
Spinal ultrasound
stages.explanation: MRI of the spine (with attention to the lumbosacral region) is the gold standard for evaluating suspected tethered cord and occult dysraphism. It defines conus position, a thickened or fatty filum terminale, lipomas, and associated anomalies, and it images the whole cord to exclude syrinx or additional lesions. Plain films may show spina bifida occulta or sacral anomalies but cannot assess the cord itself. CT shows bone well but poorly characterizes the cord and filum and adds radiation. Spinal ultrasound is useful only in young infants (under ~3 months) before posterior ossification; at age 5 the bony posterior elements preclude adequate acoustic windows, making MRI the correct choice.

stages.label: MRI SPINE
stages.content:
MRI lumbosacral spine:
Conus medullaris terminates at L4 (normal ≤ L1–L2)
Thickened filum terminale measuring 3 mm with fatty infiltration
Small intradural lipoma at the filum
Low-lying cord tethered to a posterior dural attachment
No syrinx; no Chiari malformation
Spina bifida occulta at S1–S2

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given symptomatic tethered cord with a low-lying conus and progressive deficits, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in 12 months
Start oxybutynin and refer to urology only
Neurosurgical referral for surgical untethering
Physical therapy alone and reassess in 6 months
stages.explanation: A symptomatic tethered cord with progressive motor, sensory, and sphincter deficits is a surgical problem: neurosurgical referral for prophylactic/therapeutic untethering (release of the filum/lipoma) is indicated to halt progression and, ideally, recover function. Deficits already present may not fully reverse, so timely surgery is key. Observation is inappropriate when neurologic decline is documented and progressive. Oxybutynin or urology referral alone treats a symptom without addressing the tethering cord that is causing ongoing injury. Physical therapy is supportive but does not address the underlying traction on the cord; delaying surgery risks irreversible deficits, particularly to bladder function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which of the following is the most important reason to intervene before deficits become long-standing in tethered cord syndrome?
options: Risk of malignant transformation of the filum lipoma
Bladder dysfunction and motor deficits may become irreversible once chronic
High likelihood of developing hydrocephalus
Inevitable progression to complete paraplegia within weeks
stages.explanation: The central rationale for early untethering is that neurogenic bladder dysfunction and motor/sensory deficits from chronic cord traction may become permanent if allowed to persist; recovery correlates inversely with symptom duration. Filum lipomas are benign and do not undergo malignant transformation, so cancer risk is not the driver. Hydrocephalus is associated with Chiari II/open myelomeningocele and is not a typical consequence of an isolated low-lying tethered cord. Progression is usually insidious over months to years rather than producing complete paraplegia within weeks; the urgency is about preventing accumulating, irreversible disability rather than an acute catastrophic course.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional baseline assessment is most useful before surgery to document and later track the child's neurogenic bladder?
options: Renal biopsy
Voiding diary alone with no further testing
Serum creatinine trend in isolation
Urodynamic studies (with renal-bladder ultrasound)
stages.explanation: Urodynamic studies, paired with a renal-bladder ultrasound, objectively characterize detrusor function, bladder capacity, and any upper-tract risk, giving a baseline to compare against after untethering and guiding bladder management — important because bladder dysfunction is the deficit least likely to fully recover. A renal biopsy is invasive and not indicated; there is no intrinsic renal parenchymal disease to sample. A voiding diary is helpful but subjective and insufficient on its own to quantify detrusor behavior or detect dangerous high-pressure storage. A creatinine trend alone is a late, insensitive marker of upper-tract damage and does not characterize bladder physiology. Urodynamics best documents and monitors the neurogenic bladder.

cases.diagnosis: Tethered cord syndrome (symptomatic, with low-lying conus and fatty thickened filum terminale)
pearls: Cutaneous lumbosacral markers (deep dimple above the gluteal cleft, hair tuft, lipoma, hemangioma) should prompt evaluation for occult spinal dysraphism.
A conus terminating below L2 with a thickened (>2 mm) or fatty filum supports tethered cord on MRI.
New bladder/bowel dysfunction plus asymmetric leg findings in a previously continent child is tethered cord until proven otherwise.
Symptomatic tethering warrants surgical untethering; recovery — especially of bladder function — declines the longer deficits persist.

cases.title: An Oddly Shaped Head in a 3-Month-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents are worried that their baby's head looks long and narrow with a bump along the top.
cases.history: A 3-month-old boy is brought in by both parents who feel his head shape is abnormal — long front-to-back, narrow side-to-side, with a palpable ridge running along the top of the skull. They first noticed it around 6 weeks and think it has become more pronounced. He was born at 39 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery without instrumentation; pregnancy was uncomplicated. He feeds well, is gaining weight, smiles socially, coos, and tracks faces and objects. He sleeps supine per safe-sleep guidance and spends limited time in tummy position. There is no preference for turning the head to one side and no flattening of the back of the head. No vomiting, irritability, or seizures. He has no developmental concerns. PMH is unremarkable; he is up to date on vaccines. No medications; no known allergies. Family history is negative for craniofacial syndromes or syndromic features, and there are no affected siblings. He lives at home with parents; no tobacco exposure. Both parents have normal head shapes.
cases.exam: Vigorous, alert infant with appropriate tone and social smile. Head is elongated front-to-back and narrow biparietally (scaphocephaly/dolichocephaly), with a prominent frontal bossing and palpable bony ridge along the midline vertex. A firm, raised ridge is felt over the sagittal suture; the anterior fontanelle is small and the suture line feels closed there. The ears are symmetric and not anteriorly displaced. There is no occipital or frontal flattening and no parallelogram deformity. Posterior fontanelle closed. Eyes well aligned; red reflexes present. No dysmorphic facial features, normal palate. Neurologic exam age-appropriate: good head control developing, symmetric movements, normal tone and reflexes. No papilledema visible on attempted fundoscopy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which head-shape pattern is most consistent with this infant's examination?
options: Posterior unilateral flattening with ipsilateral ear displacement (parallelogram)
Bilateral occipital flattening with a tall, wide head (brachycephaly)
Long, narrow head with a palpable ridge over the sagittal suture (scaphocephaly)
Frontal flattening on one side with contralateral occipital flattening
stages.explanation: The infant has an elongated, narrow (scaphocephalic/dolichocephalic) head with a palpable ridge over the sagittal suture and frontal bossing — the hallmark of premature fusion of the sagittal suture (sagittal craniosynostosis), the most common single-suture synostosis. A posterior parallelogram with ipsilateral anterior ear displacement describes positional (deformational) plagiocephaly. Bilateral occipital flattening with a tall, wide head describes positional brachycephaly. Frontal flattening with contralateral occipital flattening is a positional pattern, not a suture-fusion pattern. The palpable bony ridge along a suture is a key feature distinguishing true synostosis from positional molding.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes craniosynostosis from positional (deformational) plagiocephaly?
options: Improvement of head shape with repositioning and tummy time
A palpable bony ridge over the affected suture with a fixed, progressive shape
Presence of a head-turning preference
Flattening that is worse on one side of the occiput
stages.explanation: A palpable, often ridged, prematurely fused suture producing a fixed and frequently progressive cranial deformity is the defining feature of craniosynostosis. Positional plagiocephaly, by contrast, typically improves with repositioning, increased tummy time, and time off the flattened area, and is associated with a head-turning preference (often from torticollis) and occipital flattening that does not cross a fused suture. Repositioning improving the shape argues for positional, not synostotic, deformity. A head-turning preference and asymmetric occipital flattening are classic for positional plagiocephaly. The combination of a ridge and a non-correcting shape points to true suture fusion.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
