options: MRI brain with sedation
Reassurance and repeat clinical exam in 6 months only
Cranial CT with 3D reconstruction (or limited low-dose CT) to confirm suture fusion
Genetic panel as the first test
stages.explanation: When the exam suggests single-suture synostosis, cranial CT with 3D reconstruction is the confirmatory standard, clearly demonstrating the fused suture and bony bridging; many centers use low-dose protocols or, increasingly, head ultrasound or rapid MRI to limit radiation, but CT remains definitive. MRI is useful for associated brain anomalies but is not the first-line tool to confirm suture fusion and often requires sedation. Reassurance alone is inappropriate when a true synostosis is suspected, because untreated fusion progresses and may restrict skull growth. Genetic testing is reserved for syndromic features or multiple-suture involvement; isolated nonsyndromic sagittal synostosis usually does not require it first.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Cranial CT with 3D reconstruction:
Complete fusion of the sagittal suture with bony ridging
Scaphocephalic calvarial shape with biparietal narrowing and frontal bossing
Coronal, lambdoid, and metopic sutures patent
No intracranial mass or hydrocephalus
Normal ventricles

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for nonsyndromic sagittal craniosynostosis confirmed at 3 months?
options: Referral to craniofacial/neurosurgery team for surgical correction
Helmet therapy alone, as for positional plagiocephaly
Observation indefinitely with no intervention
Immediate high-dose corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Confirmed craniosynostosis is treated surgically; referral to a craniofacial/neurosurgical team is the correct step. Early intervention (e.g., endoscopic strip craniectomy with postoperative helmeting in young infants, or open cranial vault remodeling in older infants) corrects the shape and allows normal brain/skull growth, optimally performed in the first months of life. Helmet therapy alone treats positional molding, not a fused suture, and will not correct true synostosis. Indefinite observation risks cosmetic deformity and, rarely with single-suture disease, raised intracranial pressure. Corticosteroids have no role. The earlier endoscopic approach is feasible precisely because this infant presents at 3 months.

cases.diagnosis: Sagittal (nonsyndromic) craniosynostosis with scaphocephaly
pearls: Sagittal synostosis is the most common single-suture craniosynostosis and produces a long, narrow scaphocephalic head with a palpable midline ridge.
A fixed, ridged, non-correcting head shape suggests true synostosis; positional plagiocephaly improves with repositioning and tummy time.
Cranial CT with 3D reconstruction confirms suture fusion; reserve genetics for syndromic or multisuture cases.
Refer early — endoscopic strip craniectomy with helmeting is feasible in the first few months, with open vault remodeling for older infants.

cases.title: Sudden Arm and Leg Weakness in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute onset of right-sided weakness and a facial droop noticed by his mother this morning.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED after his mother noticed he could not grip his spoon with his right hand at breakfast and the right side of his face was drooping. The deficits came on over less than an hour. He has been dragging his right leg when walking. He is alert but his speech sounds slightly slurred. There was no trauma, no fever today, and no headache that he can describe. Notably, he had chickenpox (varicella) about 6 weeks ago with a typical vesicular rash that has since resolved; he was otherwise healthy and unvaccinated against varicella due to parental choice. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; normal development and prior milestones. No prior strokes, no known cardiac disease. No regular medications; no known allergies. He lives with his parents and a younger sibling; no tobacco exposure. Family history is negative for early stroke, clotting disorders, or sickle cell disease. There is no recent vomiting, neck stiffness, or recent vaccinations beyond routine. He has had no recent dehydration or chest crises.
cases.exam: Awake, mildly anxious boy. Speech mildly dysarthric, comprehension intact. Right central facial weakness (forehead spared). Right upper extremity 2/5 strength, right lower extremity 3/5; left side 5/5. Tone increased on the right with an extensor plantar response on the right. Deep tendon reflexes brisker on the right. Sensation grossly intact. No neck stiffness, no photophobia. Pupils equal and reactive; no nystagmus; visual fields full to confrontation. No vesicular rash currently; healed varicella scars noted. Cardiac exam regular without murmur. No bruits. Gait testing deferred due to weakness. No papilledema seen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in this child with acute focal hemiparesis?
options: Outpatient EEG within the week
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Emergent neuroimaging with a stroke protocol (urgent MRI/MRA, or CT/CTA if MRI unavailable)
Oral aspirin and discharge with follow-up
stages.explanation: Acute focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, central facial droop, dysarthria) over minutes to an hour represent a 'brain attack' and require emergent neuroimaging via a pediatric stroke protocol — ideally MRI with MRA/diffusion, or CT/CTA when MRI is not rapidly available — to identify infarct and arteriopathy and exclude hemorrhage. EEG is for suspected seizures and would delay critical evaluation. Lumbar puncture before imaging is unsafe with a focal deficit and risk of mass effect, and is not the priority. Discharging on aspirin without imaging is dangerous: you must first define the lesion and rule out hemorrhage before any antithrombotic decision.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with DWI:
Acute restricted diffusion in the left lenticulostriate territory (basal ganglia/internal capsule)
No hemorrhage on GRE
MRA: focal narrowing and irregularity (banding) of the distal left internal carotid and proximal middle cerebral artery
No evidence of dissection
CBC normal; hemoglobin electrophoresis pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the recent varicella and the MRA findings of focal proximal MCA/distal ICA narrowing, what is the most likely mechanism of this stroke?
options: Focal cerebral arteriopathy of childhood (post-varicella vasculopathy)
Cardioembolism from undiagnosed congenital heart disease
Sickle cell-related large-vessel vasculopathy
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
stages.explanation: Unilateral focal narrowing of the distal ICA and proximal MCA in a young child weeks to months after varicella is classic for focal cerebral arteriopathy (post-varicella arteriopathy), a leading cause of childhood arterial ischemic stroke affecting the basal ganglia via lenticulostriate vessels. Cardioembolism typically arises with known structural heart disease and produces a different vascular pattern, and his cardiac exam is normal. Sickle cell vasculopathy occurs in HbSS patients; his CBC is normal and electrophoresis is pending, and the history lacks sickle features. CVST presents with headache, seizures, and venous (not arterial) findings, and imaging here shows arterial narrowing with arterial-territory infarction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After hemorrhage has been excluded, what is the most appropriate initial antithrombotic management for this childhood arterial ischemic stroke?
options: Intravenous tPA thrombolysis as first-line
Aspirin antiplatelet therapy
No antithrombotic therapy at all
High-dose warfarin to INR 3-4
stages.explanation: For most childhood arterial ischemic strokes without cardioembolic source or dissection — including focal cerebral arteriopathy — aspirin is the recommended initial antithrombotic agent to prevent recurrence once hemorrhage is excluded. IV tPA is not standard in young children with AIS because of unproven benefit, narrow/uncertain time windows, and frequent delayed presentation; this child also presents outside a clear window. Doing nothing leaves the child at risk of recurrent infarction. Full-intensity warfarin/anticoagulation is reserved for cardioembolic sources or dissection, not typical arteriopathy. Many post-varicella arteriopathy protocols also add a short course of corticosteroids, but the core antithrombotic is aspirin.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional intervention is most appropriate for confirmed post-varicella focal cerebral arteriopathy?
options: Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation
Immediate decompressive craniectomy
Plasma exchange
A course of corticosteroids plus consideration of antiviral therapy, with vascular follow-up imaging
stages.explanation: Post-varicella focal cerebral arteriopathy is an inflammatory vasculopathy; adding corticosteroids to dampen arterial wall inflammation (often with consideration of acyclovir) plus serial vascular imaging to monitor for progression or stabilization is appropriate, alongside aspirin. Lifelong anticoagulation is unnecessary and not indicated for this arteriopathy. Decompressive craniectomy is reserved for large infarcts with malignant edema and mass effect, which this small deep infarct is not. Plasma exchange has no established role here. Most focal cerebral arteriopathy is monophasic and stabilizes over months, so structured follow-up imaging guides duration of therapy and detects the minority that progress.

cases.diagnosis: Pediatric arterial ischemic stroke due to post-varicella focal cerebral arteriopathy
pearls: Acute focal deficits in a child are a stroke until proven otherwise — obtain emergent stroke-protocol imaging (MRI/MRA preferred).
Focal cerebral arteriopathy, often post-varicella, is a leading cause of childhood AIS and targets the basal ganglia via lenticulostriate vessels.
MRA showing unilateral distal ICA/proximal MCA narrowing supports focal cerebral arteriopathy rather than cardioembolism or moyamoya.
Manage with aspirin after excluding hemorrhage; corticosteroids (± antiviral) and follow-up vascular imaging are used for the inflammatory arteriopathy.

cases.title: Sudden One-Sided Paralysis in a Boy with Known Anemia
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute inability to move the left arm and leg with a facial droop, developing over the past hour.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) is brought to the ED for sudden left-sided weakness and a left facial droop noticed by his father about an hour ago. He has been more tired than usual for two days and had a low-grade illness last week. He has had several prior vaso-occlusive pain crises and one episode of acute chest syndrome at age 5. He has never had a stroke before and has not had recent transcranial Doppler screening because the family missed appointments. His medications include hydroxyurea (which adherence has been inconsistent) and folic acid; he is penicillin-prophylaxis age-appropriate and fully vaccinated including pneumococcal. No known drug allergies. He lives with his father and grandmother. Family history is significant for sickle cell trait in both parents and a cousin with sickle cell disease. There is no trauma, no current fever measured at home, no headache he can localize, and no seizure activity reported. He has had no recent blood transfusions and his baseline hemoglobin runs around 8 g/dL.
cases.exam: Alert but frightened boy. Speech mildly slurred. Left central facial droop with forehead sparing. Left upper extremity 1/5 strength, left lower extremity 2/5; right side full strength. Increased tone on the left with a left extensor plantar response and brisk left reflexes. Sensation reduced on the left. Scleral icterus present; conjunctivae pale. No neck stiffness or photophobia. Pupils equal and reactive. Heart regular with a soft systolic flow murmur; lungs clear. Abdomen soft, no splenomegaly. No focal long-bone tenderness today. No rash. Capillary refill brisk. No papilledema appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this child with HbSS and acute hemiplegia, what is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Begin a simple top-up transfusion to a hemoglobin of 12 g/dL right away
Administer IV mannitol empirically for cerebral edema
Obtain an outpatient transcranial Doppler study
Emergent neuroimaging (stroke protocol) and urgent hematology involvement while preparing for transfusion
stages.explanation: Acute hemiplegia in a child with HbSS is an overt stroke until proven otherwise and a medical emergency: obtain emergent stroke-protocol neuroimaging to confirm infarct and exclude hemorrhage, and involve hematology urgently to arrange definitive treatment. A simple transfusion that overshoots hemoglobin is dangerous because raising hematocrit above ~10–11 g/dL increases viscosity and can worsen sickling/ischemia; exchange transfusion is preferred for overt stroke. Empiric mannitol is not indicated without evidence of herniation. Outpatient TCD is a screening tool to predict stroke risk, not a step in managing an acute stroke; it must not delay emergent imaging and treatment.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL
Reticulocytes 12%
HbS fraction 78%
Platelets 360 ×10³/µL
WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL
MRI brain with DWI: acute infarct in the right MCA territory
MRA: stenosis of the distal right internal carotid artery
No intracranial hemorrhage on GRE

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive acute treatment for overt ischemic stroke in this child with HbSS?
options: Simple transfusion to hemoglobin 14 g/dL
Exchange transfusion to reduce HbS to below ~30% while avoiding hyperviscosity
IV heparin anticoagulation
Hydroxyurea dose increase alone
stages.explanation: The definitive acute treatment for overt stroke in sickle cell disease is exchange (erythrocytapheresis) transfusion to rapidly lower the HbS fraction to below approximately 30% while keeping total hemoglobin near 10 g/dL to avoid hyperviscosity. This reduces the sickling burden in the cerebral circulation and limits further infarction. A simple transfusion to a high hemoglobin raises viscosity and can extend the stroke. Heparin anticoagulation is not the primary therapy for sickle arteriopathic stroke and adds bleeding risk. Increasing hydroxyurea is part of long-term prevention but does not address the acute event; exchange transfusion is the immediate priority once stroke is confirmed.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After recovery, what is the cornerstone of secondary stroke prevention in this child?
options: Daily aspirin alone
Warfarin to an INR of 2-3
A chronic (regular) transfusion program to maintain HbS below ~30%
No specific therapy; routine TCD screening only
stages.explanation: After an overt stroke in HbSS, chronic monthly transfusion therapy to keep HbS below about 30% is the established cornerstone of secondary prevention, dramatically reducing recurrence; hydroxyurea may later substitute in selected patients (per TWiTCH), and hematopoietic stem cell transplant is curative for eligible children. Aspirin alone is inadequate for sickle arteriopathy. Warfarin does not address the underlying sickling mechanism and is not used for this indication. TCD screening identifies primary-prevention candidates (abnormal velocities >200 cm/s) but is not a treatment; a child who has already had a stroke needs active transfusion-based prevention, not screening alone.

stages.label: SCREENING
stages.question: What is the rationale for transcranial Doppler (TCD) screening in children with HbSS?
options: Elevated cerebral artery flow velocities (>200 cm/s) identify children at high risk for first stroke who benefit from prophylactic transfusion
It detects silent venous sinus thrombosis
It measures hemoglobin oxygen saturation in the brain
It confirms the diagnosis of acute infarction better than MRI
stages.explanation: TCD measures blood-flow velocity in the large intracranial arteries; abnormally high time-averaged mean velocities (≥200 cm/s) in the distal ICA/MCA mark children at high risk of first (primary) stroke. The STOP trial showed that starting chronic transfusion in these high-velocity children markedly reduces first-stroke risk, which is why annual TCD screening from about ages 2–16 is standard in HbSS. TCD does not detect venous sinus thrombosis and does not measure tissue oxygenation. It is a risk-stratification/screening tool, not a diagnostic test for acute infarct — MRI/MRA remains superior for confirming and characterizing an established stroke.

cases.diagnosis: Overt arterial ischemic stroke in homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) with cerebral arteriopathy
pearls: Acute hemiplegia in HbSS is an overt stroke emergency — image immediately and treat with exchange transfusion to HbS <30%.
Avoid simple over-transfusion above ~10–11 g/dL; hyperviscosity can extend the infarct.
Annual TCD screening (velocities ≥200 cm/s) identifies high-risk children for primary prevention with chronic transfusion (STOP trial).
Chronic transfusion is the cornerstone of secondary prevention; hydroxyurea (TWiTCH) or stem cell transplant are longer-term options.

cases.title: Recurring Spells of Weakness After Hard Crying
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated brief episodes of right-sided weakness and slurred speech, often after crying or blowing out birthday candles.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is admitted for recurrent stereotyped episodes of transient right arm and leg weakness with slurred speech, each lasting 5-20 minutes and fully resolving. Her mother notices the spells frequently follow vigorous crying, blowing balloons, or running. Over two months there have been at least eight episodes, increasing in frequency. She also reports intermittent headaches and one episode of transient blurred vision. Development has been normal, though her teacher recently noted she seems more distractible and has had a slight decline in handwriting. No fever, trauma, or recent infection. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. No regular medications; no known allergies. She was adopted; biological family history is unknown, though her records note Asian ancestry. She lives with her adoptive parents. No tobacco exposure. There have been no convulsions, no loss of consciousness, and no clear post-event confusion lasting beyond the weakness. Between episodes she is at her neurologic baseline. She has not had any recent dehydration, and there is no history of cardiac disease or sickle cell disease.
cases.exam: At baseline between events she is alert and interactive. Cranial nerves intact; speech fluent. Strength 5/5 in all extremities with symmetric reflexes and downgoing toes when examined at baseline. During an observed spell after she became upset, she developed transient right facial weakness, right arm drift, and dysarthria that resolved within 15 minutes with rest. No neck stiffness, no bruits over the neck. No skin neurocutaneous stigmata. Cardiac exam normal, no murmur. Fundoscopy without papilledema. Gait normal at baseline. No nystagmus. Cognitive screen shows mild slowing but is grossly intact. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The episodes are reproducibly triggered by crying, exertion, and blowing — what physiologic mechanism links these triggers to the transient deficits?
options: Hyperventilation-induced hypocapnia causing cerebral vasoconstriction in a brain with marginal collateral flow
Vagal bradycardia reducing cardiac output
Hyperglycemia from stress
Migraine aura without any vascular substrate
stages.explanation: Crying, exertion, and blowing all induce hyperventilation, which lowers PaCO2; hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction. In a child whose major intracranial arteries are progressively stenosing with fragile collateral circulation, this drop in perfusion precipitates transient ischemia (TIAs) — a classic clue to moyamoya. Vagal bradycardia causes syncope/lightheadedness, not focal hemiparesis with preserved consciousness. Stress hyperglycemia does not cause focal transient deficits. While the headaches could suggest migraine, migraine aura does not explain reproducible exertion/hyperventilation-triggered focal weakness in a child with cognitive decline; the hyperventilation–vasoconstriction link in a steno-occlusive arteriopathy is the unifying mechanism here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging to evaluate the suspected cerebrovascular disorder?
options: Carotid ultrasound only
Non-contrast head CT alone
MRI brain with MRA of the intracranial circulation (with catheter angiography as the gold standard)
EEG with photic stimulation
stages.explanation: MRI brain with intracranial MRA evaluates for ischemic changes and the characteristic stenosis of the distal internal carotid and proximal anterior/middle cerebral arteries, while catheter (digital subtraction) angiography remains the gold standard, demonstrating the basal collateral 'puff of smoke' (moyamoya) network. Carotid ultrasound assesses the extracranial neck vessels and would miss the intracranial steno-occlusive disease. Non-contrast CT may show prior infarcts but cannot characterize the arteriopathy. EEG evaluates seizures; these spells are vascular TIAs, not epileptic events, so EEG would not establish the diagnosis. Vascular imaging is essential to define moyamoya and plan revascularization.

stages.label: ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
MRA and catheter angiography:
Bilateral stenosis of the distal internal carotid arteries extending to the proximal ACA and MCA
Prominent basal collateral vessels producing a 'puff of smoke' (moyamoya) appearance
MRI: small chronic infarcts in bilateral watershed zones; FLAIR 'ivy sign' over sulci
No aneurysm or hemorrhage
No evidence of vasculitis on lab workup

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management for symptomatic moyamoya disease with recurrent TIAs and watershed infarcts?
options: Surgical revascularization (e.g., pial synangiosis/EDAS or direct STA-MCA bypass), with aspirin as adjunct
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation
Chronic corticosteroids
Blood pressure-lowering therapy to reduce flow
stages.explanation: Symptomatic moyamoya with recurrent ischemia is treated definitively with surgical revascularization — indirect procedures such as pial synangiosis/EDAS (favored in children) or direct STA-MCA bypass — to recruit new collateral blood supply and prevent future strokes; antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) is a common adjunct. Anticoagulation does not address the progressive stenosis and adds hemorrhage risk, especially relevant in moyamoya. Corticosteroids treat vasculitis, which was excluded here. Lowering blood pressure would further reduce already marginal cerebral perfusion and could precipitate ischemia. Avoiding hyperventilation/dehydration and maintaining normotension and hydration are also key supportive measures perioperatively.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During hospitalization, which supportive measure is most important to prevent further ischemic events in this child?
options: Encourage deep breathing exercises and incentive spirometry
Restrict fluids to reduce intracranial pressure
Allow permissive hypotension to lower bleeding risk
Maintain euvolemia and normotension, avoid hyperventilation and hypotension, and provide adequate analgesia to prevent crying spells
stages.explanation: Because hypocapnia from hyperventilation and any drop in cerebral perfusion can trigger ischemia in moyamoya, supportive care centers on maintaining euvolemia and normotension, avoiding hyperventilation and dehydration, and minimizing crying/agitation (good analgesia, a calm environment). Encouraging deep-breathing/incentive spirometry would promote hyperventilation and hypocapnia — exactly what precipitates her TIAs — so it is harmful here. Fluid restriction causes hypovolemia and reduced perfusion, worsening ischemia. Permissive hypotension lowers cerebral blood flow and risks watershed infarction. Keeping the child well hydrated, comfortable, and normotensive protects the marginal collateral circulation until revascularization.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Moyamoya can be idiopathic (moyamoya disease) or syndromic (moyamoya syndrome). Which associated condition should be screened for given the pattern of disease?
options: Sickle cell disease, neurofibromatosis type 1, Down syndrome, and prior cranial radiation
Cystic fibrosis
Congenital hypothyroidism
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency
stages.explanation: Moyamoya arteriopathy is associated with several conditions (moyamoya syndrome), including sickle cell disease, neurofibromatosis type 1, trisomy 21 (Down syndrome), and a history of cranial irradiation; screening for these guides etiology and counseling, and is especially relevant in children. Cystic fibrosis is a respiratory/exocrine disorder with no link to this cerebral arteriopathy. Congenital hypothyroidism is unrelated to moyamoya, though autoimmune (Graves) thyroid disease has a reported association in some populations. G6PD deficiency causes hemolysis, not a steno-occlusive cerebral arteriopathy. Identifying an underlying associated condition distinguishes moyamoya syndrome from idiopathic moyamoya disease and informs broader management.

cases.diagnosis: Moyamoya disease with recurrent transient ischemic attacks and watershed infarcts
pearls: Hyperventilation triggers (crying, blowing, exertion) provoking transient focal deficits are a classic moyamoya clue via hypocapnic vasoconstriction.
Angiography shows distal ICA/proximal ACA-MCA stenosis with a basal collateral 'puff of smoke'; MRI may show the 'ivy sign.'
Surgical revascularization (indirect pial synangiosis/EDAS in children, or direct bypass) is definitive; aspirin is adjunctive.
Avoid hyperventilation, dehydration, and hypotension — they reduce already marginal cerebral perfusion and precipitate ischemia.

cases.title: Worsening Headache and Seizures After a Stomach Bug
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of progressively severe headache, now with a witnessed seizure.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is admitted after 4 days of worsening, constant headache that is worse when lying flat and on waking, with two episodes of vomiting today and a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure in the ED. He had a viral gastroenteritis last week with diarrhea and poor oral intake; his parents say he 'wasn't drinking much' for several days. The headache has not responded to acetaminophen. He has had intermittent blurred vision and saw double briefly this morning. PMH is unremarkable. No regular medications; recently took ibuprofen and acetaminophen for the headache. No known allergies. He plays soccer; no recent head trauma. He lives with his parents and is fully immunized. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with deep vein thrombosis and a grandfather with 'a clot in the lung.' There has been no fever today, no neck trauma, and no recent ear infection. He has not had prior headaches of this severity. He has had decreased urine output consistent with dehydration during his illness. No rash.
cases.exam: Drowsy but rousable, oriented when awake. Mild papilledema bilaterally on fundoscopy. Partial left sixth-nerve palsy with horizontal diplopia on left gaze. No focal limb weakness; reflexes symmetric, toes downgoing. Neck supple without meningismus. Mucous membranes dry; capillary refill slightly delayed. Pupils equal and reactive. No rash or petechiae. Cardiac exam regular (relative bradycardia). Lungs clear. Postictal mild confusion has resolved. No nuchal rigidity. Gait deferred. No cerebellar signs. Skin shows reduced turgor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests raised intracranial pressure from a venous outflow problem rather than a primary headache?
options: Photophobia and nausea only
Positional (worse lying flat/on waking) headache with papilledema, a sixth-nerve palsy, seizure, and a prothrombotic family history after dehydration
Unilateral throbbing headache relieved by sleep
Headache with normal fundoscopy and no neurologic signs
stages.explanation: Headache that worsens lying flat and on waking, with papilledema, a sixth-nerve (false-localizing) palsy, a seizure, and a strong family history of thrombosis emerging after dehydration, points to elevated intracranial pressure from impaired venous drainage — cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST). Dehydration is a recognized prothrombotic trigger in children. Photophobia and nausea alone are nonspecific. A unilateral throbbing headache relieved by sleep suggests migraine, not raised ICP. A headache with a normal exam argues against a serious secondary cause; here the abnormal fundoscopy, cranial nerve palsy, and seizure mark a dangerous process requiring urgent venous imaging.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast CT head alone
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
CT or MR venography (CTV/MRV) of the cerebral venous sinuses
Plain skull radiographs
stages.explanation: CVST is confirmed by dedicated venous imaging — CT venography or MR venography — which directly shows the thrombosed sinus and absent flow. A non-contrast CT may show indirect signs (a hyperdense sinus, the 'cord sign,' or the 'empty delta sign' when contrast is given), but a normal non-contrast CT does not exclude CVST, so venography is required. Carotid Doppler evaluates extracranial arterial flow and cannot assess the dural venous sinuses. Plain skull films provide no useful information about intracranial venous thrombosis. MRV/CTV both confirms the clot and defines its extent, guiding anticoagulation decisions.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
CT with contrast: 'empty delta sign' in the superior sagittal sinus
MR venography: thrombosis of the superior sagittal and right transverse sinuses with absent flow
MRI: venous congestion; small right parietal venous infarct without significant hemorrhage
Na 134 mEq/L
BUN 28 mg/dL, Cr 0.7 mg/dL (prerenal pattern)
Platelets 290 ×10³/µL; INR 1.0; D-dimer elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given CVST with a small non-hemorrhagic venous infarct, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Aspirin alone
Withhold anticoagulation indefinitely because of the venous infarct
Immediate surgical thrombectomy as first-line
Therapeutic anticoagulation (low-molecular-weight heparin) plus aggressive rehydration
stages.explanation: The standard treatment for pediatric CVST is therapeutic anticoagulation, typically with low-molecular-weight heparin, even in the presence of a small venous infarct, because preventing clot propagation improves outcomes; this is paired with correcting the precipitant — here, aggressive rehydration. Aspirin is insufficient for an established sinus thrombosis. Withholding anticoagulation is appropriate only with significant or expanding intracranial hemorrhage; a small non-hemorrhagic venous infarct is not a contraindication. Endovascular/surgical thrombectomy is reserved for clinical deterioration despite anticoagulation, not first-line therapy. Seizures are treated with antiseizure medication, and intracranial pressure is monitored.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond acute treatment, which work-up is most appropriate given his presentation and family history?
options: Evaluation for an underlying prothrombotic (thrombophilia) state and counseling on hydration/triggers
No further evaluation is needed
Lifelong aspirin only with no testing
Bone marrow biopsy
stages.explanation: A child with CVST and a strong family history of venous thromboembolism warrants evaluation for an inherited or acquired thrombophilia (e.g., factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, protein C/S or antithrombin deficiency, antiphospholipid antibodies), which informs duration of anticoagulation and family counseling, plus education on avoiding dehydration. Doing nothing misses a treatable, heritable risk factor. Aspirin alone without testing neither treats the acute clot adequately nor clarifies recurrence risk. A bone marrow biopsy is not indicated; there is no cytopenia or marrow process suggested. Identifying a thrombophilia guides how long to anticoagulate and screening of at-risk relatives.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite anticoagulation his headache persists with worsening papilledema. Which complication threatens permanent harm and must be monitored and treated?
options: Acute renal failure from the contrast load
Persistently elevated intracranial pressure causing progressive optic nerve injury and vision loss
Rebound hypertension from rehydration
Drug-induced hepatitis from heparin
stages.explanation: Impaired venous drainage in CVST can sustain raised intracranial pressure even after anticoagulation begins; the most feared consequence is progressive papilledema causing optic nerve damage and permanent vision loss, so serial fundoscopy/visual assessment (and acetazolamide, CSF diversion, or in refractory cases optic nerve sheath fenestration) may be needed. Contrast-induced renal failure is uncommon in a child with normal baseline renal function and is not the dominant ongoing risk. Rehydration corrects the precipitant and would not be expected to cause harmful rebound hypertension here. Heparin can rarely affect platelets (HIT) but drug-induced hepatitis is not a characteristic CVST complication; the pressing issue is intracranial pressure control to protect vision.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (superior sagittal and transverse sinuses) precipitated by dehydration
pearls: Headache worse lying flat/on waking with papilledema and a sixth-nerve palsy signals raised ICP — consider CVST, especially after dehydration.
The 'empty delta sign' on contrast CT and absent flow on MR/CT venography confirm sinus thrombosis; a normal non-contrast CT does not exclude it.
Treat pediatric CVST with therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH) plus rehydration, even with a small non-hemorrhagic venous infarct.
Work up for thrombophilia, especially with a family history of venous thromboembolism, to guide anticoagulation duration.

cases.title: Worst Headache of His Life Then Collapse
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden explosive headache while playing, followed by vomiting and a brief loss of consciousness.
cases.history: A previously healthy 13-year-old boy collapsed during a basketball game after grabbing his head and screaming about a sudden, severe headache he described as 'the worst ever,' reaching maximal intensity within seconds. He vomited and briefly lost consciousness, then became confused. He was brought to the PICU after intubation for declining mental status. There was no preceding trauma or collision. He has had occasional headaches over the past months that he did not report. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. No prior seizures or neurologic disease. He lives with his parents and a sibling; no substance use known. Family history is unremarkable for aneurysms or bleeding disorders, though detailed history is limited by the acute situation. No fever or recent illness. There is no history of bleeding tendency, easy bruising, or anticoagulant use. EMS reported a brief generalized convulsion en route. He has had no recent dehydration or infection.
cases.exam: Intubated, sedated; off sedation, opens eyes to pain and localizes with the left arm. Pupils 4 mm and reactive on the left; right pupil 5 mm and sluggish. Right-sided weakness with extensor posturing on the right to stimulation. Bilateral papilledema not yet developed; fundi without acute hemorrhage on limited exam. Neck stiff to passive flexion. Brisk reflexes on the right with an extensor plantar response. Cardiac exam shows relative bradycardia; blood pressure elevated. No external head trauma or hematoma. Skin without petechiae. The combination of hypertension and bradycardia is noted. No cerebellar testing possible. Gag present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A sudden 'worst headache of life' (thunderclap) with vomiting, transient LOC, and meningismus in an adolescent most urgently requires which immediate study?
options: MRI brain with contrast scheduled for the next morning
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Emergent non-contrast head CT to detect intracranial hemorrhage
EEG to evaluate the convulsion
stages.explanation: A thunderclap headache with vomiting, transient loss of consciousness, and neck stiffness is hemorrhage until proven otherwise; an emergent non-contrast head CT is the fastest, most sensitive immediate test for acute intracranial/subarachnoid blood. A next-day contrast MRI dangerously delays diagnosis of a potentially expanding bleed. Lumbar puncture before imaging is contraindicated when there is a focal deficit, depressed consciousness, and signs of raised ICP (risk of herniation); LP is reserved for suspected SAH only after a normal CT in stable patients. EEG addresses seizures but would not identify the life-threatening hemorrhage driving this presentation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT:
Left parietal intraparenchymal hemorrhage (~30 mL) with surrounding edema
Adjacent subarachnoid blood
Midline shift 5 mm with early effacement of the left lateral ventricle
CTA: tangle of dilated vessels with an enlarged feeding artery and early-draining vein in the left parietal region (nidus)
No evidence of aneurysm at the circle of Willis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CTA shows a vascular nidus with an enlarged feeding artery and early-draining vein. What is the most likely cause of this hemorrhage?
options: Hypertensive basal ganglia hemorrhage
Ruptured arteriovenous malformation (AVM)
Saccular (berry) aneurysm rupture
Hemorrhagic conversion of an ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: A parenchymal nidus of tangled abnormal vessels with an enlarged feeding artery and an early-draining vein on CTA is the signature of an arteriovenous malformation; rupture of an AVM is the leading cause of spontaneous (nontraumatic) hemorrhagic stroke in children and adolescents. Hypertensive hemorrhage is rare in children and classically occurs in the basal ganglia/thalamus without a nidus. A berry aneurysm produces predominantly subarachnoid hemorrhage and would appear as a focal outpouching at a vessel bifurcation, not a parenchymal nidus, and the CTA showed no aneurysm. Hemorrhagic conversion follows an established infarct, with imaging evidence of prior ischemia rather than a vascular nidus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient has an anisocoric blown right-sided pattern, hypertension with bradycardia, and 5 mm midline shift. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Lower blood pressure aggressively to normal and observe
Start therapeutic anticoagulation
Administer IV tPA
Initiate measures to reduce intracranial pressure (head elevation, hyperosmolar therapy) and emergent neurosurgical evaluation for hematoma evacuation
stages.explanation: The Cushing response (hypertension with bradycardia), anisocoria, and midline shift signal life-threatening raised ICP and impending herniation; immediate ICP-directed therapy (head-of-bed elevation, hyperosmolar therapy with hypertonic saline or mannitol, normocapnia/controlled ventilation) plus emergent neurosurgical evaluation for clot evacuation and AVM management is required. Aggressively normalizing blood pressure can compromise cerebral perfusion pressure in the setting of raised ICP and is not the priority maneuver. Anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated with active intracranial hemorrhage. IV tPA is for ischemic stroke and would be catastrophic in a hemorrhagic lesion. Definitive AVM treatment (surgery, embolization, or radiosurgery) follows stabilization.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization, what is the rationale for definitive treatment of the underlying AVM?
options: Obliterating the nidus (surgery, embolization, and/or stereotactic radiosurgery) prevents future hemorrhage, which carries high morbidity and mortality
AVMs always resolve spontaneously, so no treatment is needed
Lifelong antiplatelet therapy eliminates rebleeding risk
Only blood pressure control is needed long term
stages.explanation: A ruptured AVM carries a substantial risk of rebleeding, and each hemorrhage carries significant morbidity and mortality; definitive obliteration of the nidus — by microsurgical resection, endovascular embolization, stereotactic radiosurgery, or a combination tailored to size, location, and drainage (e.g., Spetzler-Martin grading) — is undertaken to prevent future hemorrhage. AVMs do not reliably involute spontaneously. Antiplatelet therapy does not address the malformation and could increase bleeding risk. Blood pressure control alone leaves the structural lesion in place and does not remove the rebleeding hazard. Eliminating the nidus is what actually reduces the long-term hemorrhage risk.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which acute complication should the PICU team monitor for most vigilantly in the first hours after this hemorrhage?
options: Hypoglycemia
Deep vein thrombosis requiring immediate anticoagulation
Cerebral herniation from expanding hematoma and rising intracranial pressure
Hyperkalemia
stages.explanation: With a 30 mL parenchymal hemorrhage, surrounding edema, midline shift, and early signs of a Cushing response and anisocoria, the dominant acute threat is cerebral herniation from an expanding hematoma and rising ICP; continuous neuro checks, ICP monitoring, and serial imaging are essential. Hypoglycemia and hyperkalemia are not characteristic immediate complications of this hemorrhage. Although immobilized neurocritical patients are at risk for DVT, immediate anticoagulation is contraindicated with active intracranial hemorrhage, so it is not the priority concern in these first hours. Vigilance for neurologic deterioration and herniation drives moment-to-moment management.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured cerebral arteriovenous malformation with intraparenchymal hemorrhage and raised intracranial pressure
pearls: AVM rupture is the leading cause of spontaneous hemorrhagic stroke in children and adolescents.
Thunderclap headache with vomiting and meningismus needs an emergent non-contrast head CT first; never LP before imaging with focal deficits or raised ICP.
A parenchymal nidus with an enlarged feeding artery and early-draining vein on CTA defines an AVM.
Cushing response (hypertension + bradycardia), anisocoria, and midline shift demand immediate ICP-lowering measures and neurosurgical evaluation.

cases.title: A Newborn with a Failing Heart and a Noisy Head
cases.chiefComplaint: Respiratory distress and poor perfusion shortly after birth with a murmur heard over the skull.
cases.history: A term newborn boy delivered at 38 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery develops tachypnea, poor feeding, and signs of heart failure within the first day of life. Apgars were 8 and 9. He was noted to have a loud continuous bruit auscultated over the anterior fontanelle and bilaterally over the skull. He is tachycardic with bounding pulses and hepatomegaly. Prenatal ultrasound late in the third trimester had suggested a 'midline intracranial vascular structure,' but follow-up was incomplete. There is no maternal diabetes, no infection, and no perinatal asphyxia. He is the first child of non-consanguineous parents. No family history of congenital heart disease. No medications or allergies. He has not had seizures yet. Birth weight was appropriate for gestational age. He required escalating respiratory support over hours. There is no dysmorphism. Cardiac echo in the nursery showed a structurally normal heart but high cardiac output with a dilated right heart and reversed diastolic flow in the descending aorta.
cases.exam: Tachypneic neonate in respiratory distress on CPAP. A loud continuous cranial bruit is audible over the anterior fontanelle and skull. Bounding peripheral pulses with a wide pulse pressure. Precordium hyperdynamic; soft systolic murmur. Hepatomegaly 4 cm below the costal margin. Anterior fontanelle soft, slightly full; head circumference at the upper limit of normal with prominent scalp veins. Tone slightly decreased; primitive reflexes present but sluggish. Lungs with bibasilar crackles. No focal seizures observed. Capillary refill 3-4 seconds. No dysmorphic features. Pupils reactive.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A term neonate with high-output heart failure, bounding pulses, and a continuous cranial bruit most likely has which underlying lesion?
options: Large patent ductus arteriosus alone
Coarctation of the aorta
Total anomalous pulmonary venous return
A high-flow intracranial arteriovenous shunt (vein of Galen malformation)
stages.explanation: High-output cardiac failure in the first days of life with bounding pulses, a wide pulse pressure, hepatomegaly, and — crucially — a continuous bruit heard over the skull points to a large intracranial arteriovenous shunt, the vein of Galen malformation. The massive cerebral AV shunting steals systemic output, producing high-output failure and a cranial bruit. A large PDA causes a continuous murmur over the precordium, not the skull, and the echo here showed a structurally normal heart with high output. Coarctation causes differential pulses/hypertension proximally with diminished femoral pulses, not a cranial bruit. TAPVR causes cyanosis and pulmonary venous congestion without a cranial bruit or high-output physiology of this type.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What imaging will best confirm the suspected diagnosis while assessing the brain?
options: Cranial ultrasound with Doppler followed by MRI/MRA of the brain
Plain skull radiographs
Abdominal ultrasound only
Bronchoscopy
stages.explanation: Cranial (transfontanelle) ultrasound with Doppler is an excellent, bedside first step in a neonate, showing the dilated midline venous sac with high-flow turbulent arterial signal; MRI/MRA then characterizes the malformation's angioarchitecture and assesses for brain injury before treatment planning. Plain skull films cannot demonstrate the vascular lesion. Abdominal ultrasound evaluates the liver/hepatomegaly but not the cerebral lesion driving the disease. Bronchoscopy is irrelevant to this vascular diagnosis. Doppler ultrasound plus MRI/MRA is the appropriate pathway to confirm a vein of Galen malformation and to evaluate associated hydrocephalus and parenchymal injury that influence management timing.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Cranial Doppler ultrasound: dilated midline venous structure with high-velocity turbulent arterial flow
MRI/MRA: large median prosencephalic venous sac with multiple arterial feeders (vein of Galen malformation)
Mild ventricular prominence
Echo: structurally normal heart, high cardiac output, dilated right heart, retrograde diastolic flow in the descending aorta
BNP elevated
No intracranial hemorrhage

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of definitive treatment for a vein of Galen malformation causing high-output failure?
options: Immediate open surgical excision of the malformation in the neonatal period
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt as the primary therapy
Staged endovascular embolization to reduce the shunt, with medical stabilization of heart failure first
Cardiac transplantation
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of definitive treatment is staged endovascular embolization to progressively reduce the AV shunt; the neonate is first stabilized medically for heart failure (diuretics, inotropic/afterload support, ventilation) and embolization is timed — often deferred a few weeks when feasible — to balance heart-failure control against the risks of intervening on a small infant. Open surgical excision carries prohibitively high mortality in neonates and is not first-line. A VP shunt addresses hydrocephalus but does not treat the shunt physiology and can be hazardous (risk of hemorrhage) if done before the malformation is addressed. Cardiac transplantation does not treat the cerebral lesion causing the high-output state.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication of an untreated or poorly controlled vein of Galen malformation is most important to anticipate in this neonate?
options: Hyperthyroidism
Progressive high-output heart failure, multiorgan hypoperfusion, and brain injury (including hydrocephalus)
Biliary atresia
Necrotizing fasciitis
stages.explanation: The dominant threats are progressive high-output heart failure with multiorgan hypoperfusion and, in the brain, venous hypertension causing hydrocephalus and potentially ischemic/encephalomalacic injury ('melting brain' in severe cases), all of which worsen prognosis. These complications drive the urgency of stabilization and timed embolization and inform whether aggressive intervention is warranted (a poor neurologic substrate may change the management plan). Hyperthyroidism, biliary atresia, and necrotizing fasciitis are unrelated to the AV-shunt physiology of a vein of Galen malformation. Anticipating cardiac and cerebral deterioration guides the multidisciplinary neonatology, cardiology, and neurointerventional team.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before embolization, which initial medical strategy best stabilizes this neonate's high-output heart failure?
options: Aggressive crystalloid fluid boluses to raise blood pressure
Diuretics, inotropic/afterload support, and mechanical ventilation to manage the failing high-output circulation
Beta-blockade to slow the heart and reduce shunt flow
Indomethacin to close a presumed patent ductus arteriosus
stages.explanation: Medical stabilization of the high-output failure caused by the cerebral AV shunt relies on standard heart-failure support — diuretics to relieve congestion, inotropic and afterload-reducing agents to support the overloaded ventricles, and mechanical ventilation/respiratory support — buying time until staged embolization reduces the shunt. Aggressive fluid boluses worsen volume overload and pulmonary edema in a high-output, congested circulation. Beta-blockade can blunt the compensatory tachycardia that maintains output in a shunt-dependent state and may precipitate decompensation. Indomethacin targets a PDA, but the echo shows a structurally normal heart with high output from cerebral shunting, not a ductal problem, so it would not help. Definitive control comes from embolizing the malformation.

cases.diagnosis: Vein of Galen malformation presenting with neonatal high-output heart failure
pearls: A term neonate with high-output heart failure, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, and a continuous cranial bruit suggests a vein of Galen malformation.
Transfontanelle Doppler ultrasound is a quick bedside clue; MRI/MRA defines the malformation and brain injury.
Staged endovascular embolization is the cornerstone of treatment after medical stabilization of heart failure.
Anticipate high-output failure, hydrocephalus, and parenchymal brain injury; open surgery in neonates carries high mortality.

cases.title: A Jittery Newborn with Twitching on Day One
cases.chiefComplaint: Jitteriness and rhythmic twitching of one arm a few hours after birth.
cases.history: A 1-day-old girl, born at 38 weeks to a mother with poorly controlled gestational diabetes, is evaluated for jitteriness and episodes of rhythmic clonic twitching of the right arm noted at about 4 hours of life. The pregnancy was complicated by elevated maternal glucose; the mother required insulin. Delivery was by cesarean for macrosomia; birth weight 4.4 kg (large for gestational age). Apgars were 8 and 9. The infant has been difficult to feed and appears tremulous; the twitching does not stop when the limb is held. There has been no maternal fever, prolonged rupture of membranes, or other infectious risk. No family history of neonatal seizures or metabolic disease. The newborn screen results are pending. There is plethora and a cushingoid, chubby appearance typical of an infant of a diabetic mother. No dysmorphic features. No medications or allergies. The mother did not use substances. The infant has had no apnea, but the twitching episodes have recurred several times over the last hour.
cases.exam: Large-for-gestational-age, plethoric infant. Tremulous with coarse jitteriness that stops when the limb is gently held, but separate episodes of rhythmic right-arm clonic jerking that do NOT stop with restraint and are accompanied by eye deviation. Tone variable; alternating between irritable and lethargic. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Pupils equal and reactive. No dysmorphism. Hepatomegaly mild. Capillary refill brisk. No rash. Moro and suck present but suck is poor. No bulging fontanelle, no meningismus. Limbs otherwise move symmetrically between episodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How best to distinguish the concerning episodes from benign jitteriness in this newborn?
options: Jitteriness is associated with eye deviation and does not stop with restraint
Seizure activity does not stop with gentle restraint and may have abnormal eye movements/autonomic changes, whereas jitteriness is stimulus-sensitive and stops when the limb is held
Both are identical and cannot be distinguished clinically
Jitteriness always involves only one limb
stages.explanation: Jitteriness/tremor is stimulus-sensitive, has no abnormal eye movements or autonomic features, and characteristically stops when the limb is gently held (it is a tremor, not a seizure). True neonatal seizures do not stop with restraint and may be accompanied by eye deviation, lip-smacking, apnea, or autonomic changes. In this infant the rhythmic right-arm jerking with eye deviation that persists despite holding the limb is seizure, while the coarse tremor that abates with restraint is jitteriness. The other options reverse the features or wrongly claim they are indistinguishable; recognizing that restraint stops jitteriness but not seizures is the key bedside discriminator.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given an infant of a diabetic mother with seizures on day one, what is the most likely metabolic cause to check first?
options: Hypernatremia
Hypermagnesemia
Hypoglycemia
Hyperammonemia
stages.explanation: Infants of diabetic mothers are prone to neonatal hypoglycemia: in utero maternal hyperglycemia drives fetal hyperinsulinemia, and after birth the newborn's persistent high insulin causes a rapid glucose drop, commonly within the first hours of life — exactly this timeline. Hypoglycemia is a leading, immediately treatable cause of early neonatal seizures and must be checked first with a bedside glucose. Hypernatremia is not characteristic of IDMs early on. Hypermagnesemia relates to maternal magnesium therapy (e.g., for preeclampsia), not diabetes, and causes hypotonia rather than seizures. Hyperammonemia from an inborn error typically presents a bit later with lethargy and poor feeding after protein intake, not in the first hours of an IDM.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Bedside glucose 22 mg/dL
Serum glucose 24 mg/dL
Ionized calcium 1.25 mmol/L (normal)
Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.4 mEq/L
Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL (normal)
CBC: hematocrit 64% (polycythemia)
Blood culture sent; CRP normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The glucose is 22 mg/dL with ongoing seizures. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Intravenous dextrose bolus (e.g., 2 mL/kg of D10W) followed by a continuous dextrose infusion
Oral feeding with formula only
Phenobarbital as the first intervention
Intravenous calcium gluconate
stages.explanation: Symptomatic neonatal hypoglycemia with seizures is corrected urgently with an IV dextrose bolus — typically 2 mL/kg of D10W (about 200 mg/kg) — followed immediately by a continuous dextrose infusion to maintain glucose and prevent rebound, since these infants are hyperinsulinemic. Oral feeding alone is too slow and unreliable for a seizing infant with a glucose of 22 mg/dL. Phenobarbital treats seizures but does not address the underlying cause; correcting glucose usually stops glucose-driven seizures and is the priority. IV calcium gluconate treats hypocalcemic seizures, but this infant's ionized calcium is normal, so it is not indicated. Glucose must be rechecked frequently after correction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After glucose correction, the seizures stop and glucose normalizes. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start long-term antiseizure medication for life
Discharge home immediately once one normal glucose is obtained
Begin a ketogenic diet
Continue dextrose infusion with frequent glucose monitoring, wean as feeds are established, and monitor for recurrence
stages.explanation: When seizures resolve with glucose correction, the cause is being treated directly, so the plan is to maintain the dextrose infusion, monitor glucose frequently, and slowly wean the infusion as enteral feeds are established, watching for recurrent hypoglycemia (common in IDMs over the first day or two). Lifelong antiseizure medication is unwarranted for a transient, correctable metabolic seizure. Immediate discharge after a single normal value is unsafe because hypoglycemia can recur as the infusion is reduced. A ketogenic diet is a treatment for certain refractory epilepsies and is inappropriate and dangerous here, where the problem is low glucose. Address polycythemia if symptomatic and continue infection surveillance.

cases.diagnosis: Neonatal seizures due to hypoglycemia in an infant of a diabetic mother
pearls: Jitteriness stops when the limb is held and lacks eye/autonomic features; true seizures persist despite restraint.
Infants of diabetic mothers develop early hypoglycemia from fetal hyperinsulinemia — check a bedside glucose first.
Treat symptomatic neonatal hypoglycemia with an IV D10W bolus (~2 mL/kg) plus a continuous dextrose infusion, not feeds alone.
If seizures resolve with glucose correction, no long-term antiseizure medication is needed; monitor for recurrence as the infusion is weaned.

cases.title: A Five-Day-Old with Irritability and Spells
cases.chiefComplaint: Irritability, increased tone, and brief episodes of twitching beginning on day five of life.
cases.history: A 5-day-old boy, born at term, is admitted for irritability, jitteriness, increased muscle tone, and brief episodes of focal twitching that began today. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery; Apgars 9 and 9; birth weight appropriate. He had been feeding and behaving normally for the first few days and went home, returning now with these new symptoms. The parents have been feeding him a homemade formula and also adding extra cow's-milk-based product. There was no birth asphyxia, no maternal infection, and no maternal diabetes. The maternal pregnancy was unremarkable except that the mother's prenatal vitamin D status was not checked. No family history of neonatal seizures, though a maternal cousin reportedly had 'low calcium' problems as an adult. No medications; no allergies. The infant is afebrile and feeding less today. There is no diarrhea or vomiting. The newborn metabolic screen is still pending. The infant appears hyperalert and startles easily, with occasional carpopedal posturing of the hands noted by the nurse.
cases.exam: Hyperirritable, hypertonic infant who startles markedly to handling. Brief multifocal clonic twitches of the face and limbs that do not abate with restraint. Carpopedal spasm and occasional jaw twitching elicited on examination. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Pupils reactive. No bulging fontanelle, no meningismus, no rash or petechiae. Cardiac exam regular; capillary refill brisk. Abdomen soft. No dysmorphism. Suck present but the infant is fussy. No focal weakness between episodes. Tone diffusely increased. A facial twitch on tapping the cheek is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given late-onset (day 5) seizures with irritability, hypertonia, and carpopedal spasm, which electrolyte abnormality is most likely?
options: Hypoglycemia
Hyperkalemia
Hyponatremia
Hypocalcemia
stages.explanation: Late-onset neonatal seizures (after the first 2-3 days, classically around day 5-7) with hyperirritability, increased tone, carpopedal spasm, and a facial twitch on tapping the cheek (a neonatal Chvostek-like sign) point to hypocalcemia. Early hypoglycemia typically presents in the first hours (as in infants of diabetic mothers), not on day 5 with tetany-like features. Hyperkalemia causes cardiac arrhythmia and weakness rather than this tetanic picture. Hyponatremia can cause seizures but usually with lethargy and a dilutional/SIADH or free-water context, not carpopedal spasm and increased tone. The neuromuscular irritability (tetany) is the classic fingerprint of low calcium.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Ionized calcium 0.72 mmol/L (low)
Total calcium 6.4 mg/dL (low)
Phosphate 9.8 mg/dL (high)
Magnesium 1.6 mg/dL (low-normal)
Glucose 78 mg/dL
Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.6 mEq/L
PTH pending; vitamin D pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The calcium is low and the phosphate is markedly HIGH. Which mechanism best fits this late-onset hypocalcemia?
options: Maternal vitamin D deficiency alone
Excessive renal calcium reabsorption
A high phosphate load (e.g., from cow's-milk/high-phosphate feeds) or hypoparathyroidism causing hyperphosphatemic hypocalcemia
Hyperparathyroidism
stages.explanation: Low calcium with high phosphate (hyperphosphatemia) characterizes late-onset neonatal hypocalcemia from either a high dietary phosphate load — classically cow's-milk-based or improperly prepared formula overwhelming the immature kidney — or hypoparathyroidism (including DiGeorge/22q11 deletion), both of which raise phosphate while lowering calcium. The clue here is the high-phosphate feeding history plus markedly elevated phosphate. Isolated vitamin D deficiency typically lowers both calcium and phosphate (with high PTH) rather than producing hyperphosphatemia. Excessive renal calcium reabsorption would raise, not lower, calcium. Hyperparathyroidism raises calcium and lowers phosphate — the opposite pattern. Checking PTH distinguishes hypoparathyroidism from a phosphate-load mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The infant is having active seizures with an ionized calcium of 0.72 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Oral calcium supplementation only
Slow IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring
IV dextrose bolus
Phenytoin as first-line
stages.explanation: Symptomatic hypocalcemic seizures are treated with slow intravenous calcium gluconate under continuous cardiac monitoring (rapid infusion can cause bradycardia/arrhythmia, and extravasation causes tissue necrosis). This directly corrects the cause and stops the seizures. Oral calcium is too slow for an actively seizing neonate and is used later for maintenance. An IV dextrose bolus treats hypoglycemia, but this infant's glucose is normal (78 mg/dL), so dextrose would not help. Phenytoin is not first-line for a clearly metabolic hypocalcemic seizure; correcting calcium is the priority. Coexisting low magnesium should also be corrected, as hypomagnesemia can make hypocalcemia refractory.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After acute correction, which step best addresses the underlying cause and guides longer-term management?
options: Switch to a low-phosphate (appropriate) formula and check PTH, vitamin D, magnesium, and consider 22q11 deletion testing if hypoparathyroidism is confirmed
Continue the high-phosphate feeds unchanged
Start lifelong antiseizure medication
No further work-up is needed
stages.explanation: Definitive management treats the cause: switching from the high-phosphate feed to an appropriate low-phosphate formula reduces the phosphate load, while checking PTH, vitamin D, and magnesium identifies hypoparathyroidism or vitamin D deficiency; if PTH is inappropriately low (hypoparathyroidism), testing for 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) is warranted, given its association with neonatal hypocalcemia and cardiac/immune anomalies. Continuing the high-phosphate feeds perpetuates the problem. Lifelong antiseizure medication is unnecessary for a correctable metabolic cause. Doing no further work-up misses a potentially important diagnosis like hypoparathyroidism/DiGeorge that changes ongoing care. Maintenance calcium and vitamin D may be needed depending on the etiology.

cases.diagnosis: Late-onset neonatal seizures due to hypocalcemia (hyperphosphatemic; phosphate load vs hypoparathyroidism)
pearls: Late-onset (day 5-7) neonatal seizures with irritability, hypertonia, and carpopedal spasm suggest hypocalcemia (tetany).
Low calcium with HIGH phosphate points to a phosphate load (cow's-milk/high-phosphate feeds) or hypoparathyroidism, not isolated vitamin D deficiency.
Treat acutely with slow IV calcium gluconate under cardiac monitoring; correct coexisting hypomagnesemia, which can make it refractory.
Check PTH/vitamin D and consider 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) testing if hypoparathyroidism is confirmed.

cases.title: Repetitive Jerking Movements Only During Sleep in a Newborn
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report rhythmic jerking of the baby's arms and legs that happens only while she is asleep.
cases.history: A 2-week-old girl is brought to clinic by anxious parents who recorded several phone videos of repetitive jerking movements of both arms and legs that occur only while she sleeps. The jerks are rhythmic, last several minutes, and can recur in clusters during a nap. They stop immediately and reliably when she is picked up and awakened. There is no facial twitching, eye deviation, color change, or apnea, and she feeds vigorously between episodes. She was born at 39 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery after an uncomplicated pregnancy; Apgars were 8 and 9. There were no maternal infections, gestational diabetes, or substance exposure. She is exclusively breastfed, gaining weight well, and has had no fevers. She takes vitamin D drops only; no other medications. No known drug allergies. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with childhood febrile seizures but no neonatal seizures or developmental disorders. The parents are non-consanguineous. She lives with both parents and a healthy older sibling; no smokers in the home.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, alert, vigorous newborn in no distress. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Head circumference at the 55th percentile. Normal tone with symmetric spontaneous movements of all four limbs. Primitive reflexes (Moro, root, suck, grasp) intact and symmetric. Eyes track briefly; red reflex present bilaterally. No dysmorphic features, no hepatosplenomegaly, and skin without neurocutaneous stigmata such as café-au-lait or hypopigmented macules. During the visit she briefly drifts to sleep and demonstrates several rhythmic limb jerks that abruptly cease when she is gently roused. Awake exam between events is entirely normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is most useful in distinguishing this infant's movements from true neonatal seizures?
options: The movements involve all four extremities
The movements occur in rhythmic clusters lasting minutes
The movements stop reliably with arousal and occur only in sleep
The movements began within the first month of life
stages.explanation: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus occurs exclusively during sleep and stops promptly when the infant is awakened — a feature seizures do not share, since epileptic events persist regardless of arousal. That is the single most discriminating clue. Involvement of all four extremities and clustering for minutes can occur in both myoclonus and seizures, so neither distinguishes them. Onset within the first month is true of many neonatal paroxysmal events, benign and pathologic alike, and does not separate them. Demonstrating that gentle arousal abolishes the jerks at the bedside is highly reassuring and is the classic teaching point for this entity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Obtain a routine or video EEG capturing a typical episode
Start empiric phenobarbital and observe response
Order urgent brain MRI with contrast
Send serum and CSF amino acids for inborn errors of metabolism
stages.explanation: When the history is classic, the diagnosis is clinical, but an EEG (ideally video-EEG capturing an event) confirms benign neonatal sleep myoclonus by showing a normal background with NO ictal correlate during the jerks. Empiric phenobarbital is wrong and potentially harmful — antiseizure drugs can paradoxically worsen the myoclonus and expose a healthy infant to sedation. Brain MRI is unnecessary with a normal exam and no red flags. CSF/serum amino acids target metabolic epilepsies, which present with abnormal interictal exams, encephalopathy, or refractory seizures — none present here. A normal EEG during a captured event seals the benign diagnosis.

stages.label: EEG RESULT
stages.content:
Video-EEG (60 min, including sleep):
Background: normal, age-appropriate continuity and sleep architecture
During captured episode: bilateral limb myoclonic jerks recorded
Ictal correlate: NONE — no electrographic seizure or epileptiform discharges
Movements ceased immediately upon arousal
Interictal epileptiform activity: absent

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the EEG findings, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Initiate levetiracetam prophylaxis
Admit for continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
Reassure parents and provide anticipatory guidance with no medication
Refer for genetic testing for early infantile epileptic encephalopathy
stages.explanation: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus is self-limited and requires no treatment; reassurance plus anticipatory guidance is the standard of care. Parents should be told episodes occur only in sleep, do not harm the brain, and typically resolve by 2–3 months and certainly by 6 months. Levetiracetam is unnecessary and inappropriate for a non-epileptic phenomenon. Inpatient cardiorespiratory monitoring is not indicated for a well infant with a normal exam and no apnea or color change. Genetic testing for epileptic encephalopathy is reserved for infants with abnormal EEGs, encephalopathy, or developmental regression — the opposite of this child's reassuring picture.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point about prognosis is most accurate?
options: Episodes carry an elevated lifelong risk of epilepsy requiring annual EEGs
Episodes typically resolve spontaneously by a few months of age with normal development
Episodes indicate cerebral palsy and warrant early physical therapy referral
Episodes will persist into adulthood but are harmless
stages.explanation: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus resolves spontaneously, usually by 2–3 months and almost always by 6 months, with completely normal neurodevelopment and no increased risk of epilepsy. There is no need for serial EEGs — the entity is benign and does not predispose to seizures. It is unrelated to cerebral palsy; the interictal exam, tone, and reflexes are normal, so PT referral is unwarranted. The movements do not persist into adulthood; they are an age-limited phenomenon of early infancy. Clear, confident reassurance prevents unnecessary testing and antiseizure medication exposure.

cases.diagnosis: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus
pearls: Benign neonatal sleep myoclonus occurs ONLY during sleep and stops abruptly with arousal — the key clinical discriminator from neonatal seizures.
Video-EEG shows a normal background with NO ictal correlate during the jerks; the diagnosis is otherwise clinical.
Antiseizure drugs (especially benzodiazepines/phenobarbital) can paradoxically worsen the myoclonus and should be avoided.
It resolves spontaneously by 2–6 months with normal development and no increased epilepsy risk — reassurance is the treatment.

cases.title: Recurrent Sudden Spells of Unsteadiness and Fear in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: A 4-year-old girl has recurrent brief spells where she suddenly becomes unsteady, frightened, and clings to a parent.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl is brought in for recurrent spells over the past 6 months. Without warning she suddenly appears off-balance, refuses to walk, grabs onto furniture or a parent, and looks frightened. She often says she feels like things are 'moving.' Episodes last 1–3 minutes and resolve completely; afterward she returns immediately to normal play. She does NOT lose consciousness, and there is no shaking, tongue biting, incontinence, or postictal confusion. During spells parents sometimes notice she looks pale and her eyes seem to flicker briefly. There are no triggers, no headache, and she is entirely well between episodes — running and climbing normally. Frequency is roughly two to three times a month. She was a full-term infant with normal development; she is fully toilet trained and speaks in sentences. No medications, no allergies. She attends preschool and meets all milestones. Family history is notable: her mother has migraine with aura, and a maternal aunt had 'dizzy spells' as a child that stopped on their own. No family history of epilepsy or cardiac disease.
cases.exam: Cheerful, cooperative preschooler running around the exam room without difficulty. Gait is normal with good heel-to-toe, and she hops on each foot. Cranial nerves intact; extraocular movements full with no nystagmus at rest. No dysmetria on finger-to-nose, normal rapid alternating movements, and Romberg negative. Tympanic membranes normal bilaterally; no spontaneous or positional nystagmus elicited. Strength, tone, and deep tendon reflexes symmetric and normal. No truncal ataxia. Skin without neurocutaneous markings. Fundi sharp without papilledema. The interictal neurologic examination is entirely normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which characteristic of these spells is most consistent with a benign paroxysmal disorder rather than a seizure?
options: The spells are accompanied by a frightened facial expression
There is brief flickering of the eyes during episodes
Consciousness is fully preserved with immediate return to baseline and no postictal state
The spells last 1 to 3 minutes
stages.explanation: Benign paroxysmal vertigo of childhood (BPVC) preserves consciousness throughout, and the child returns instantly to normal with NO postictal confusion or drowsiness — strong evidence against an epileptic seizure, which typically alters awareness and is followed by a postictal phase. A frightened expression simply reflects the distressing vertigo and is nonspecific. Brief eye flickering/nystagmus actually supports vestibular vertigo but does not by itself exclude seizures. A 1–3 minute duration overlaps with both seizures and benign spells, so it is not discriminating. Preserved consciousness with immediate recovery is the cardinal benign feature.

stages.label: DESCRIBED EPISODE & EXAM
stages.content:
Parent video of a typical spell:
Child suddenly stops, reaches for the couch, looks scared
Brief horizontal nystagmus noted, mild pallor
No loss of consciousness, no limb jerking, no incontinence
Duration ~90 seconds, then resumes play normally
Interictal exam (today): completely normal gait, no nystagmus, no dysmetria

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With a classic history and normal interictal exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Diagnose clinically as benign paroxysmal vertigo of childhood; no urgent imaging needed
Obtain an urgent brain MRI to exclude a posterior fossa tumor
Order an EEG to rule out focal epilepsy as the primary test
Refer for tympanometry and audiology as the priority evaluation
stages.explanation: BPVC is a clinical diagnosis when the history is classic and the interictal exam (including gait and cerebellar testing) is normal — no urgent imaging is required. Brain MRI is reserved for red flags such as persistent ataxia, abnormal eye movements between episodes, headache with vomiting, or focal deficits, none of which are present. EEG is not the primary test here because consciousness is preserved and there is no postictal state; it is considered only if features suggest seizure. Audiology/tympanometry is unnecessary in a child with normal ears and no hearing complaints — BPVC is not an otologic disorder.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this child's infrequent episodes?
options: Start daily propranolol prophylaxis immediately
Begin meclizine for vestibular suppression
Reassurance and education; pharmacologic prophylaxis only if episodes become frequent or disabling
Initiate levetiracetam to prevent progression to epilepsy
stages.explanation: For infrequent, brief, self-limited BPVC episodes, reassurance and education are the mainstay; most children need no medication, and the disorder remits spontaneously over months to a few years. Prophylaxis (e.g., cyproheptadine or, in older children, agents used for migraine) is considered ONLY when attacks are frequent or disabling. Routine daily propranolol is overtreatment for sporadic spells. Meclizine is used for peripheral vestibulopathy in adults and has little role in this self-limited childhood syndrome. Levetiracetam is wrong — BPVC is not epilepsy and does not 'progress' to it, though it is associated with later migraine.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement about the long-term significance of this condition is most accurate?
options: It is a precursor associated with later development of migraine
It usually evolves into a generalized epilepsy syndrome
It indicates an underlying inner-ear malformation
It predicts future cerebellar degeneration
stages.explanation: BPVC is considered a childhood episodic syndrome that may be associated with migraine — it is a recognized migraine precursor, and many affected children (often with a strong family history of migraine, as here) go on to develop migraine later in life. It does NOT evolve into generalized epilepsy; preserved consciousness and a normal EEG distinguish it from seizures. It is not caused by an inner-ear malformation — the otologic exam is normal and it is a central episodic syndrome. It does not predict cerebellar degeneration; the interictal cerebellar exam is normal and the disorder is benign and self-limited.

cases.diagnosis: Benign paroxysmal vertigo of childhood
pearls: BPVC: sudden, brief recurrent vertigo with imbalance and fear in toddlers/preschoolers, fully preserved consciousness, and an entirely normal interictal exam.
It is one of the childhood episodic syndromes associated with migraine — look for a strong family history of migraine.
Diagnosis is clinical; reserve MRI for interictal ataxia, abnormal eye movements, headache/vomiting, or focal deficits.
Most cases remit spontaneously; treat with reassurance and use migraine-type prophylaxis only for frequent or disabling attacks.

cases.title: Recurrent Episodes of Severe Vomiting With Well Periods in Between
cases.chiefComplaint: A 6-year-old boy presents with another bout of intense, repeated vomiting identical to several past episodes.
